File.Name: i101d_hc28-013.jpg
Notes: 27655824829 Here is another clutch of tiny insect eggs on the underside of a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. These eggs have already hatched. They are stacked like little hexagons, with six neighbors each. The eggs are tiny, about 150 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 1/2 mm. (See here for how I figure.) I suspect these are the eggs of true bugs in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Redux 2017--My Favorite Theme of the Year" and the sub-theme of "Egg". Hah, I got another photo of insect eggs for the original "Egg" theme back on March 6 - see this photo. I happened to find this clutch in a week when we were asked to reuse a theme from the past year, so I took that as an omen. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2017)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: i102a_i102-005.jpg
Notes:
39431609142 Here are two tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spiders - both shot at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens, and cropped to the same size! By counting pixels, I figure the spider on the right is 4.4 mm and the one on the left is just 1.06 mm. (See this photo for how I figured.) The spider on the right looks large by comparison, but it's really a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (genus Cyclosa) spider. It makes a perfect little orb web and hangs in the center that it decorates with the bodies of its prey. That tiny spider on the left also makes a perfect little orb web and hangs in the center - but this one was so small I could barely see it! It doesn't look like a spiderling. I don't have a name for this tiny spider, but I wonder if its the one responsible for the neat orb webs I've been finding lately that are just a couple of inches across, e.g. in this photo. Hah - it you look close, it looks like the tiny spider has an even tinier prey. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2018)

I had an appointment in town this morning and didn't get home until almost noon when a breeze was starting to kick up. We have a 30% chance of a splash of rain tomorrow, but this is not the storm we've been hoping for. Maybe next week?

File.Name: i102b_i102-024.jpg
Notes: 38753642094 This is an odd galled flower of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). That odd "crook in the neck" is typical. I've found these galls before, see this photo which I reckon settles that it's a galled flower and not an aborted fruit. Also see this photo which shows a gall and a ripe berry at the same time. Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." I guess this is the one, and it's a flower NOT a berry. (Well, it may be the ovary of a flower.) (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2018)

File.Name: i102c_hc31-031.jpg
Notes: 39461893541 Here are two large clumps of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. tomentosum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Hah - the mistletoe seems to have completely overtaken the oak, which appears to be on its last legs. There's a lesson here for parasites not to get too efficient at debilitating their hosts - otherwise they will end up like the mistletoe clump on the left! It was interesting - there's not much of this mistletoe here on San Marcos Pass, though it seem to be common further along the mountains near the top of Gibraltar Road. I don't know why. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2017)
ScientificName: Phoradendron leucarpum; tomentosum; Viscaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron leucarpum; tomentosum; Viscaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i102d_hc27-038.jpg
Notes: 24594990387 There are still nuts on the winter-bare shrubs of deciduous native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They are proper tree nuts with a husk on the outside, which splits to reveal the hard shell inside, which you can crack to expose the nut meat inside of that. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. I have a close shot of the nuts in this photo taken in November. I've never found the nuts so late in the season, and I wonder if some critter that normally eats them is missing this year? The twigs are host to lichens that seem to favor these deciduous shrubs that lose their leaves in winter and let the sunlight in. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in both woodland and chaparral, it has wonderful white flowers that smell like perfume, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting edible nuts! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2017)

File.Name: i103a_i103-021.jpg
Notes:
24618789837 Here's another photo of new catkins on a shrub of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family growing on the pass. The actual flowers aren't open yet, but they are getting closer - see this photo for the actual flowers. The catkins are handsome even before the flowers open. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2018)

I found this shrub growing along a trail that has been blocked since summer by a heavy growth of Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or Squaw Bush (Rhus aromatica aka Rhus trilobata, Anacardiaceae). Really, I could barely get through it! I figured I'd wait until winter to cut a trail, which would be easier when the deciduous shrubs have lost their leaves. A fire work crew beat me to it - they bulldozed a path through the thicket and along the rest of my trail as well. I don't want to go off on a rant, but it's an issue when fire-suppression efforts include the construction of fire lines miles from the fire. Using heavy equipment to clear fire lines and trails and just piling the slash along the route is what happens, but that just opens up a path for erosion and non-native weeds. I'm not mad, I suppose it must be done . . . but it is destructive. Of well, if chaparral has one special quality, it is that it is resilient - it comes back surprisingly fast! (So far, anyway.)

We had interesting cloudy skies today with just a hint of rain, more like virga that was barely making it to the ground. We have a 70% chance of more rain tonight and tomorrow morning, probably not much, but any is welcome. There is a better chance of real rain next week. (Real rain will be a problem too - mudslides after the Thomas Fire.)
CommonNames: Veatch Silk-Tassel; Basket Bush; Skunkbush; Squaw Bush
ScientificName: Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Rhus aromatica; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae
associatedTaxa: Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Rhus aromatica; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae

File.Name: i103b_i103-019.jpg
Notes: 38776568444 I found this interesting gall on a stem of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, Fagaceae). It looks a bit like the green form of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in this photo, but that one grows on Scrub Oak (a white oak) and this one grows on Canyon Live Oak (an intermediate oak) and the different kinds of oaks have different kinds of galls. The foliage of this oak is quite different. I'm not sure, but this might be a gall of the Beaked Spindle Gall Wasp (Heteroecus pacificus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). There are a few images at BugGuide that might be the same, and shape and colors are said to be variable. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2018)
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Beaked Spindle Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Heteroecus pacificus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Heteroecus pacificus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: i103c_i102-002.jpg
Notes: 27707981129 Here is a tiny spider (on the left) sitting by its odd triangular web (on the right). I believe the spider is a Triangle Web Spider (Hyptiotes gertschi) in the family Uloboridae of the Cribellate Orb Weavers. If you look close, you can see that the spider has caught a fly as well as a leaf in its web! The spider is only about 1/4 inch long, but it looks remarkable up close - see this photo. The spider's web is remarkable too. The spider makes this vertical web that is shaped like a pie slice of a normal orb web. It's a cribellate spider that produces a different kind of webbing from non-cribellate spiders, see this photo and this photo for more info. I found this one in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2018)
CommonNames: Triangle Web Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; cribellate spider; Southern Bush Monkeyflower
ScientificName: Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloboridae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloboridae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: i103d_hc28-018.jpg
Notes: 25614379078 This is the web of a tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider on a dead stem of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) with last summer's seedheads. This web is small, just a few inches across. Maybe it's the web of the tiny spider on the left in my [Previous] photo)? I've show these web before, here and here. Well, why not - they're beautiful, so happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2017)

File.Name: i104a_i104-023.jpg
Notes:
38613640975 We had some rain this morning, and these lichens on the oak trees are already revived! On the other hand, think how fast a dry sponge "revives" when it gets wet. I don't know if this is a fair comparison, but it is remarkable how quickly the lichens go from inconspicuous to burgeoning with a bit of rain. We've basically had no rain for the water year starting on September 1. It's the driest water year on record, at least since records started on San Marcos Pass in 1966. (You can see our rain history here.) Wildfire is another consequence of the general dryness. But we finally got some rain today! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2018)

We got just over 4/10 inch of rain today, more than I expected. Hah - by my reckoning, our normal rainfall for September through December comes to 9.86 inches. This year it comes to 0.64 inches - just 6% of normal. We have another pretty good chance of a bigger storm next week, but that's still days away. Still it's a start.

File.Name: i104b_i104-009.jpg
Notes: 24641283997 We had some rain this morning, and these liverworts (Marchantiophyta) were already revived from the dead before noon! Compare this photo of desiccated liverworts taken a couple of weeks ago. I'm pretty sure the liverwort on the left is the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), and the liverwort on the right is the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). We also have several smaller liverworts in our mountains which I'm trying to learn, see my photos here, here, here, and here. I like hunting for these inconspicuous plants that the rain brings. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2018)

File.Name: i104c_i104-001.jpg
Notes: 25638408118 We had some rain early this morning, and I found these mushrooms later in the day. Mushrooms do indeed follow the rains, but they are not that fast - though they did catch my eye on this wet morning. I'm not sure what they are, but they may be a small cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) growing from buried wood under an oak tree, compare this photo. These mushrooms are variable and highly influenced by environmental conditions. Whatever they are, I hope they are a sign of things to come! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2018)

File.Name: i104d_hc31-009.jpg
Notes: 24640773527 This is another shot of "just a fly" (Diptera) working a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) - see this photo from a few days ago. The fly has its proboscis out, though it seems nowhere near long enough to reach the goodies in the flower. I'm not sure what it's going for. I'm also not sure what kind of fly it is, though I don't think it is an ordinary one. Maybe it's another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae)? I have another photo of this fly here, and I'll probably post another one tomorrow. Oh well, insects are scarce right now, and this is a nice shot of its rear end - so HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2017)

File.Name: i105a_i105-007.jpg
Notes:
25657419088 Rain yesterday, termites today! We had a hatch of these winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) this morning arising simultaneously for several miles in the mountains. Here is just one of them paused for a moment on a twig of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) before heading off to find its destiny. These termites arise from bare soil, and not always near a tree or other obvious wood. Maybe it's a Subterranean Termite (family Rhinotermitidae)? See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the swarm. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2018)

It was sunny and warm today, without the cold wind that usually comes after our winter storms. We had about 4/10 inch of rain yesterday - not much, but it's something after a very dry start to the rain year. Our next storm sounds more serious. As it stands, rain is expected from next Monday to Wednesday - with a 100% chance on Monday night! That's an unusual percentage for a storm that's still several days away. Rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches are in the forecast, with the highest amounts across south-facing slopes. That's us. This will be great for the woods, but it could cause some problems below the areas burned by the recent Thomas Fire.

File.Name: i105b_i105-036.jpg
Notes: 27751999429 Rain yesterday, termites today! We had a hatch of these winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) this morning arising simultaneously for several miles in the mountains. They arise from bare soil, and not always near a tree or other obvious wood. Maybe these are Subterranean Termites (family Rhinotermitidae)? It was a small rain, less than 1/2 inch, so I reckon that means it's not so much the rain as the idea of rain that brings out the termites. Maybe this is how they synchronize their swarms with other termite colonies so that they'll all emerge at the same time, thereby increasing the chance of mating with another swarm? We see these termites every year, see this photo from last year for an especially large hatch at home. I don't believe these termites do structural damage. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2018)

File.Name: i105c_hc31-004.jpg
Notes: 39497918942 Here is another shot of "just a fly" (Diptera) working new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) - see this photo from a few days ago. The fly has its proboscis out, though it seems nowhere near long enough to reach the goodies in the flower, so I'm not sure what it's going for. I'm also not sure what kind of fly it is, though I don't think it is an ordinary one. Maybe it's another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae)? I have more photos of the same fly here and here. Insects are scarce right now, so they have to do double duty. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2017)

File.Name: i105d_hc30-028.jpg
Notes: 27751696869 Here is another shot of a handsome Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) working still-fresh flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) - see this photo for another shot of the same fly. Maybe it's the species Scaeva pyrastri in the tribe Syrphini? Most of the buckwheat flowers are finished by now, but the pink stamens show that these flowers still have something to offer insects. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2017)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Scaeva pyrastri; Syrphini
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Scaeva pyrastri; Syrphini

File.Name: i106a_i106-020.jpg
Notes:
24679852027 This is a Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera, maybe the Field Dog Lichen (Peltigera rufescens), growing on a sandstone rock with mosses. This lichen has remarkable root-like rhizines along the edges. You can also see a few "dog teeth" which are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership. I believe these lichens have Nostoc as a photobiont, which is a cyanobacterium rather than an algae. Different kinds of pelt lichens are common in the Oregon woods, but they are quite uncommon in our mountains. I only know them from this one trail, but they almost disappear into a dark crust when dry, so they would be easy to overlook. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2018)

Today's photos are all lichens, and I could post a lot more! Lichens don't suddenly appear from nothing like mushrooms and liverworts, but they do revive after a rain and become more conspicuous. Our next storm will give them another chance to flourish. As it stands, rain is expected from Sunday night to Wednesday. Rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches are in the forecast, with the highest amounts across south-facing slopes. That's us. This will be great for the woods, but it will cause some problems below the areas burned by the recent Thomas Fire.
ScientificName: Peltigera; Peltigera rufescens; rhizines; apothecia; Nostoc; cyanobacterium
associatedTaxa: Peltigera; Peltigera rufescens; rhizines; apothecia; Nostoc; cyanobacterium

File.Name: i106b_i106-037.jpg
Notes: 38838065274 Here are lichens growing on an old acorn cup on a dead branch of what I believe is I believe is Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei) or maybe Quercus wislizeni. It's really surprising - lichens are so slow growing you wouldn't think an acorn cup could provide a habitat for them to settleon, but there's no telling how long this one has been here, and here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2018)

File.Name: i106c_i106-005.jpg
Notes: 24679213577 This is a colony of Pixie-cup lichens in the genus Cladonia growing on a sandstone outcrop in a shady place near home. We have several different kinds of these lichens which which I can't tell apart. Like most Cladonia species, they have a "squamulose primary thallus" - those green "leaves" at the base - and separate erect stalks called podetia. These belong to the same lichen. These lichens don't disappear during the dry time of year, but they do shrivel up and become inconspicuous, so it's a pleasure when they revive after even a small rain. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2018)

File.Name: i106d_i106-011.jpg
Notes: 38837669084 This is a striking colony of Gold-Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) growing with other gray foliose lichens on a mostly-dead twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The orange cups are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the fungus and algae. This lichen is similar to its cousin Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) which lacks the ciliate apothecia, see this photo. Lichens don't disappear during the dry time of year, but they do shrivel up, so it's a great pleasure when they revive after even a small rain. These look like they belong under the sea! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2018)
CommonNames: Gold-Eye Lichen; Coast Live Oak; Powdered Orange Bush Lichen
ScientificName: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; apothecia; Teloschistes flavicans
associatedTaxa: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; apothecia; Teloschistes flavicans

File.Name: i107a_i107-033.jpg
Notes:
27791629729 I turned around today and looked up . . . and there was this striking Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly spreading its wings on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) right in front of me! Sure, it's January in the middle of winter, but that's when I usually find these butterflies out on warm days. It is part of a trio of butterflies that I mostly find in the middle of winter, along the Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) and the (Nymphalis californica). I have managed to photograph the Red Admiral this year, see this photo from the day before Christmas, but I'm still waiting on the other. The California Dogface (Colias eurydice) and Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) are two more butterflies that I see in mid-winter, but I also find them in summer. This photo also has backlight and nice bokeh. I believe the seven-sided polygons are an internal reflection of the seven-sided diaphram in my favorite Nikon 105mm micro lens. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2018)

Our next storm keeps growing in its potential impact. As it stands, rain is expected from later tonight to Wednesday. We're under a NWS High Wind Warning as well as a Flash Flood Watch. Rainfall amounts of 6-7 inches are now forecast for our mountains, with the highest amounts across south-facing slopes. That's us. This will be great for the woods, but it will cause problems below the areas burned by the recent Thomas Fire. There's good info at the California Weather Blog - including this link showing a debris flow after a different wildfire. "Hang onto your hats, it's going to be a bumpy ride!"

(It's also being a bumpy ride at Flickr today. Lots of problems, but I can post OK.)
Locality: It's also being a bumpy ride at Flickr today.  Lots of problems
CommonNames: Mourning Cloak; Coast Live Oak; Red Admiral; ; California Dogface; Painted Lady

associatedTaxa: Nymphalis antiopa; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis californica; Colias eurydice; Vanessa cardui

File.Name: i107b_i107-022.jpg
Notes: 38671718655 I believe this is the nymph of a true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) tucked into a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I've seen other critters use these little boats for shelter - see this photo and this photo. Pretty neat! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2018)

File.Name: i107c_i107-009.jpg
Notes: 27791090899 Look between the gills of these Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) - they are being consumed by fly maggots. This is the same large cluster of these mushrooms that I first showed a couple of weeks ago in this photo. In time, the maggots will turn the mushrooms into a slime mass like this one. I reckon the maggots are basically aquatic and turn the mushrooms into their own liquid environment. Hah, like people, they consume the caps of the mushrooms first and only go for the stems when there's no choice! In the past I've thought the maggots were the young of Drosophilid Flies, but now I believe that they are the maggots of Heleomyzidae flies in the genus Suillia, see this photo. I tried to get a closer shot today, but I don't like it. I'll try again tomorrow. I have a closer shot of other maggots in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2018)

File.Name: i107d_i107-005.jpg
Notes: 24699687817 Here are fungi growing from a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I reckon there are four different kinds on this one leaf - and that's just what I can see! There are no proper mushrooms in the woods yet, other than the Honey Mushrooms in my [Next] photo, but I think they will soon be coming up - especially with real rain coming tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2018)

File.Name: i108a_i108-012.jpg
Notes:
38690385755 This is the kind of little Woodlouse that can roll up in a ball, so I guess it's genus Armadillidium, probably Armadillidium vulgare. Compare the woodlouse that can't roll up in this photo. One difference is the yellowish color swirls on the scales. These critters are terrestrial crustaceans in the suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda, Crustacea. They are known as pill bugs, sow bugs, roly-polys and many more common names. These are critters that the rain brings out, and we've (finally!) been having some rain lately. I like their bumpy little eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2018)

We've had some wind and light rain today, but only about 3/10 inch. There is heavy rain expected later tonight and tomorrow - maybe as much as 7-9 inches! But I get nervous when there is this much hype around a storm-to-be. We'll see what happens.
ScientificName: Armadillidium; Armadillidium vulgare; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea
associatedTaxa: Armadillidium; Armadillidium vulgare; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea

File.Name: i108b_i108-017.jpg
Notes: 25715760678 Here are pretty new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. I got this photo in the wind and light rain today. This is the second time these shrubs have flowered - I also found them back in October, see this photo. The shrub with flowers today also has green fruit from the earlier flowering. I'm not surprised that these shrubs are confused by the weather - I am too! I found this shrub growing along Highway 154 today on the lower edge of what I consider to be San Marcos Pass - from the Trout Club to the Christian Camp, and Lizard's Mouth to Knapp's Castle. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2018)

File.Name: i108c_i107-037.jpg
Notes: 39585775711 Here are two more shots of the Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly spreading its wings on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that I showed yesterday, here. It's a butterfly that I usually find on warm days in the middle of winter. They are a rich brown on top, but they are quite dark on the undersides of their wings, and pretty much disappear when perched on a tree trunk. I'll bet that's where this one is in the rain today. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2018)

File.Name: i108d_i105-015.jpg
Notes: 25710661898 Here are new flowers on a shrub of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family - done as a double exposure for this week's theme for the Macro Mondays group. I wasn't enthusiastic about the theme, as I really regard double exposures as gimmicky. But I found the setting on my Nikon camera - hardware, not software - and I gave it a try. I kind of like this double shot of the early flowers, so here it is. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2018)

File.Name: i109a_i109-008.jpg
Notes:
25734542978 This is looking at a branch of native White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia, Betulaceae) tree, with San Jose Creek in the background. The creek is flowing but not rushing - compare this photo from a year ago. The difference is that this was our first real storm of the year, whereas last year we had more normal rain to get the ground prepared before the "big storm" came. I got this photo today from the bridge on West Camino Cielo Road after the good rain last night and this morning. I realized too late that I only had my 105 mm macro lens which is too long for wide shots. Of well, I made the best of it by shooting small things like these new flowers on the alder. These are mostly male staminate catkins, but you can also see a few female pistillate cones in the lower right. Note that the deciduous tree still has some green leaves. I reckon the trees are confused in this odd weather year. The dead-looking brown plants in the background are a native Sedge in the Cyperaceae plant family, maybe the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae). I hope it's just resting and not dead. It's a pleasure to see water in this creek today, though our creek at home is still dry. I guess that means that all the rain soaked in, which is good. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2018)

I was disappointed at first this morning when I found only 2-3/4 inch of rain in the gauge. It was up to four inches by the end of the day, but still less than the 6-8 inches in the forecast. It's a good thing we didn't get the expected rain. As it is, many houses are washed away by mudflows, Highway 101 is closed, 13 people are confirmed dead, rescues and searches are still underway even at this hour. Monteceito is a mess. As a local newscaster said on TV today, it's hard to get your head around this kind of destruction happening right at home - but still out of sight. As during the Thomas Fire, our local KEYT Channel 3 TV has had continuous coverage with no commercial breaks. They deserve awards.
CommonNames: White Alder; Sedge; Santa Barbara Sedge
ScientificName: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae
associatedTaxa: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae

File.Name: i109b_i109-005.jpg
Notes: 25734243518 Here are new catkins of a native White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia, Betulaceae) tree growing by San Jose Creek - with raindrops. I got this photo today from the bridge on West Camino Cielo Road after the good rain last night and this morning. Julie and I drove here to see if the creek was going - it is - but I realized too late that I only had my 105 mm macro lens which is too long for wide shots. Of well, I made the best of it getting a photo of these new flowers. These are the male staminate catkins, but you can also see a few female pistillate in the lower right of my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2018)

File.Name: i109c_i108-001.jpg
Notes: 39575416992 Here are the first open flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, related to sassafras and avocado, that I've seen this year. This tree is growing in a shady place where the sun never shines at this time of year on a rainy and windy day - I did my best for color and contrast. The flowers are surprisingly intricate, see this photo for a close look. The flower buds form early, and then the trees wait to actually open their flowers. It's interesting that this tree picked the first real rain of the year to open. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking - but don't use too much! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2018)

File.Name: i109d_i108-006.jpg
Notes:
27827372709 When good mushrooms go bad . . .

These are Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that are being consumed by fly maggots. This is the same large cluster of these mushrooms that I first showed a couple of weeks ago in this photo. In time, the maggots will turn the mushrooms into a slime mass like this one. I've been trying to get a closer photo of the maggots, but they are hiding inside the mushroom caps on these rainy days. For more info, see my description for this photo from a couple of days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2018)

File.Name: i110a_i110-017.jpg
Notes:
39594412432 I pulled away a piece of bark from a standing-dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree today, and found this beetle on the inside surface. It's fair sized, maybe 3/4 inch long. I believe it is a Green Bark Beetle - species Temnoscheila chlorodia in the family Trogossitidae of Bark-gnawing Beetles. The fantastic color is iridescent, flashing between green and blue, but mostly green. They are said to be predaceous, foraging under the bark of dead trees. You can see some webbing and frass at the bottom of my photo, but I'm not sure if it belongs to this beetle or its prey. I put the piece of bark back where I found it after I got my photo. What a beauty! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2018)

We had a good rain over the last couple of days, but we're still coming out of an unusually long summer drought that lasted into winter. Our four inches of rain yesterday brings out total for the water year (since September 1) to 4.6 inches - just 36% of normal. *Sigh* Fire and flood were just too close together, with the result that our moderate rain brought death and destruction to the cities below the Thomas Fire last month.
Locality: since September 1) to 4.6 inches - just 36% of normal.  *Sigh*  Fire and flood were just too close together

File.Name: i110b_i110-019.jpg
Notes: 38915635714 This is a brand new earthstar "mushroom" just coming up in the woods along our road. I added scare quotes because these fungi are not at all fleshy. I guess it is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus), though it seems larger and more patterned that that species usually is, and it doesn't seem to have an apical pore. These fungi are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make a star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity. The inner puffball is ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. This one is growing in the same place where I found a similar one last year, see this photo, so I guess they are perennial. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2018)

File.Name: i110c_i110-008.jpg

I guess they are some kind of jelly fungi (Tremellales and allies) - something like Witch's Butter (Tremella aurantia) that can dry up into almost nothing, photo here. The same fungi were here last year, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; jelly fungi; Witch's Butter
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tremellales; Tremella  aurantia

File.Name: i110d_i110-030.jpg
Notes: 25751781898 Here are translucent fungi growing with lichens on a dead twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I guess they are some kind of jelly fungi (Tremellales and allies). I don't know anything about them, but they are a sign that real mushrooms are on their way. Hurrah, we finally got some rain after an unusually long summer drought that lasted into winter. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2018)

File.Name: i111a_i111-004.jpg
Notes:
39611518432 Here is a closer shot of the spider in my [Previous] photo in the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, though I'm pretty sure it's a male with those enlarged palps. My first thought is that it's a Cobweb Weaver spider in the Theridiidae family - but it does have spiny legs, and my book says these spiders have few or no spines. This is a native cactus, maybe one we collected in Baja many years ago? See this photo of our garden, on the left with pink flowers. I've found a number of insects and spiders on the cactus fruit, especially crickets - see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2018)

What a difference a day or two makes. The weather has turned mild and dry. I still haven't found any proper new mushrooms, though I have to think they're stirring under the oak mulch. I haven't found any Banana Slugs yet either, and I'm worried about them. We're still coming out of an unusually long summer drought that lasted into winter this year. It will be interesting to see what happens next - do plants and insects keep the same relative schedule with a late start, or will they just skip the early ones that were left behind?

File.Name: i111c_i111-014.jpg
Notes: 27862925849 I believe these are nymphs of a true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) tucked into a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). File.Name: i112a_i112-011.jpg
Notes:
25786581888 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) on the cap of a Honey Mushroom (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that are being consumed by fly maggots. If you look at my [Previous] photo (here), you can see this same fly in the top-center. The white isn't snow or a spore deposit - it has a texture, and I believe it is a mold growing on the mushroom cap. I believe the flies are Drosophilid or Lauxaniid flies or something similar. The flies seem smaller than the maggots, so maybe they are something else - but maybe not. I was looking for a fly to photograph this morning for the Fly Day Friday group, and here it is - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2018)

I did find a few tiny Mycena mushrooms in the woods today, but I only found a few. These tiny mushrooms look better in groups like this. Maybe tomorrow, but the woods are already starting to dry out. There's moisture in the ground, but the air is dry. The good news is that after the recent rain that caused all the flooding the 281,893-acre Thomas Fire is now officially fully contained.
ScientificName: Diptera; Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Mycena

File.Name: i112b_i112-009.jpg
Notes: 39658552621 Here's another photo of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that are being consumed by fly maggots. You can see a few maggots swimming in the slime, but most are hidden inside. The barely-recognizable mushrooms are also being invaded by both white and dark molds. If you look close, you can see a couple of adult flies (Diptera) on the cap to the right. (Look for the orange eyes!) I believe they are Drosophilid or Lauxaniid flies or something similar. The flies seem smaller than the maggots, so maybe they are something else. See my [Next] photo for a close shot of the fly in the top-center. Isn't decomposition wonderful! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2018)

File.Name: i112c_i112-025.jpg
Notes: 38761116255 Here is a basketball-size colony of pleurotoid mushrooms growing from the trunk of a dead hardwood tree by San Jose Creek today, maybe a White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia, Betulaceae) tree. Pleurotoid fungi have a lateral stalk or no stalk at all, and take their name from the Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus). Maybe that's what they are, but maybe not - it's a common form. I found similar mushrooms last spring on the same tree, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2018)
CommonNames: pleurotoid mushrooms; White Alder; Oyster Mushroom

File.Name: i112d_i112-004.jpg
Notes: 39627140862 I believe these are the early stage of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). I found them on rotting wood from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. They look like insect or snail eggs, but I believe something different will come from them, see this photo for a bit more info. Maybe this photo shows the plasmodia stage of a genus Stemonitis before they form sporangia, see this photo? Or maybe they really are insect or snail eggs! Time will tell - I'll keep an eye on these. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2018)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Stemonitis

File.Name: i113a_i113-008.jpg
Notes:
25804517878 This is a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) with an usually long abdomen. It seemed to be patrolling this patch of sandy soil and oak leaves, maybe looking for prey. There are many different kinds so I'll leave it at that. You can see the fly's spurious vein on its upraised wing if you know what to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2018)

The woods are already starting to dry out after our good rain last week. There's still moisture in the ground, but the air and the creeks are dry. This dry spell must be good news for the cleanup work after the mudslides in Monteceito that took lives last week. We're still coming out of an unusually long summer drought that's lasted into winter this year, and it's an open question whether it will be another drought year. It's not looking so good right now - we stand at just 4.68 inches for the rain year that started on September 1, just 35% of normal.

File.Name: i113b_i113-017.jpg
Notes:
38778703535 I was surprised to find this early caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a stem of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. I guess I shouldn't be surprised as I found the first on last year on January 5. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example in this photo. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2018)

There might actually be a conflict here between the early caterpillars that respond to warmth and the as yet undeveloped shrub that responds to moisture. Since we've had such an unusually long summer drought this year with our first rain only last week, the caterpillar has emerged to find its food plant with no new leaves to munch on. I hope it does OK.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: i113c_i113-030.jpg
Notes: 24807123687 Here are new mushrooms after our first good rain of the year last Tuesday. I believe they are the mushroom formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila), now more properly called Gymnopus dryophilus - though I suspect the common name will stick. Hah, I found the first ones last year on October 21, but we've had an unusually long summer drought this year that's lasted into winter. This mushroom comes up early; under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae); in groups; has white spores; and copious white mycelium with root-like rhizomorphs at the base, visible at the base of the mushroom on the right. Now we just need some more rain to keep the season going as it's already starting to feel dry. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2018)
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i113d_i113-003.jpg
Notes: 27897144899 I thought so - the egg-like white mass I showed yesterday in this photo is definitely a colony of Slime Molds (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) and not snail eggs. Here they are today. I believe this photo shows the sporangia of a genus Stemonitis slime mold. Hah - they look like little hot dogs on a stick! I found these on well-rotted wood from a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. They're small. It's remarkable how quickly they develop - this change was literally overnight. I'll try to get another photo tomorrow if they are releasing spores. See this photo for a bit more info about these fascinating creatures. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2018)
ScientificName: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Stemonitis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i114a_i114-014.jpg
Notes:
38798748805 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on the windshield of Julie's car this morning. I just got down the hill from photographing the mushrooms and slime mold two photos back, and I got this photo without realizing that my camera was still set at f/22 - but it worked! This fly looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. Hah - there's probably a specialized name for every one of those bristles! (I just looked it up - the arrangement of bristles on an insect or mite is called chaetotaxy, see Wikipedia!) (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2018)

Warm weather continues, and the woods are starting to dry out after our good rain last week. Our next chance of rain comes next Thursday or Friday, but it doesn't sound very exciting.

File.Name: i114c_i114-013.jpg
Notes: 38986820094 I found these gorgeous little mushrooms growing on the same well-rotted fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) where I found the slime mold in my [Previous] photo. They're small, the caps less than 1/2 inch across. I'm not sure what they are, but one possibility is the Yellow-stemmed Pluteus (Pluteus lutescens aka P. romellii). One problem with that is that there's no sign of the pink-colored spores of that species, but these caps are new and maybe the spores haven't developed yet? Another possibility is that they are the atypical Psathyrella uliginicola aka Coprinopsis uliginicola or C. lignicola, seethis photo, but they are too small and the yellow stems seem wrong. Whatever they are, they are handsome mushrooms.
Locality: Pluteus lutescens aka P. romellii). One problem with that is that there's no sign of the pink-colored spores of that species

File.Name: i115a_i115-013.jpg
Notes:
38818286005 This photo shows Liverworts (Marchantiophyta) growing with mosses on bare ground in once-burned chaparral near Lake Cachuma. I believe there are actually three different kinds of liverworts in this photo! he large colony in the center is the Hairy Crystalwort (Riccia trichocarpa). It's interesting how they all seem to be growing from one central place - where the colony started? If you look close, you can also see a different liverwort with flat leaves without hairs around the sides of my photo. (I'll add a note.) I'm not sure, but I think this is a different Riccia species, maybe the Black Crystalwort (Riccia nigrella). Finally, if you look in the top-left, you can see something that looks like moss - but isn't. I believe that is a genus Fossombronia liverwort. They have a ruffled thallus that looks like lettuce, and sometimes produce tiny lollypop-like sporophytes, see this photo. It's only been a week since our first real rain - compare this photo of the same plants from a year ago. I like these secretive little plants that hide in plain sight! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2018)

I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I stopped by Lake Cachuma and found these liverworts. I did have a photo for the Macro Mondays group with the theme of "My Favorite Read (Fiction)" - a photo recognizing Terry Pratchett's fine diskworld books. Damn, our internet connection went down this morning between uploading the photo and writing the description. It didn't come back until almost 5:00 p.m. - which makes me think it was some kind of scheduled maintenance without the schedule. I missed the 4:00 PST/12:00 GMT/UTC deadline for the group, so I deleted the photo. (The group is strict about that!) I put some thought into this week's assignment, but there's always next week.
Locality: The group is strict about that!)  I put some thought into this week's assignment

File.Name: i115b_i115-004.jpg
Notes: 24846861507 This is another little spider in the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus. Compare this photo of a different spider in a similar fruit. About all I know for sure is that they are different kinds of spiders. My first guess would be that this is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae), maybe genus Xysticus or Ozyptila. If the spider look a little fuzzy, it's because I'm shooting through some webbing over the top of the fruit. It looks like it has a regular nest in there with broken cactus spines - but do spiders do that? I also wonder what the spider will do when it rains - its house will become a pool! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2018)

File.Name: i116a_i116-026.jpg
Notes:
39025581624 I found both of these different Assassin Bugs (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) today on leaves of two different Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). On the left is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), no doubt the most common of these predators in our mountains. It's in a defensive pose with front legs raised. The one on the right is more interesting - and more gnarly! It is probably the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes). These insects are in the same family, but they look quite different despite a family resemblance. If you look close, you can see that they both have an impressive spike-like proboscis for subduing their prey. I got these photos on a cloudy day under the trees with a breeze. The photos have problems, but maybe they work as a diptych. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2018)

Today was ten degrees cooler than yesterday, mostly cloudy, with a breeze from the south. It felt like rain - but nah. Our chances of rain later this week and next week seem to be shrinking as well. Bah!

File.Name: i116c_i116-004.jpg
Notes: 25862531678 Here is another photo of the tiny spider which I now believe is a Triangle Web Spider (genus Hyptiotes) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers. In particular, I believe it is Hyptiotes gertschi, see my photos here and previous to that. I found this one in a winter-bare shrub of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). This spider is small, only about 1/4 inch long to the tip of its toes. What is most peculiar is that it seems to be clutching the broken ends of its web fore and aft! See this photo for another shot. I guess it moves by producing new webbing and reeling in the old as it goes, but does that mean it can only move forward?? Hah - it looks like a furry octopus! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2018)

File.Name: i116d_i116-019.jpg
Notes: 24864773327 Here is a dead-looking plant of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) greening up in the canyon - again. These shrubs started to green up a couple on months ago, see this photo taken on November 7. But then our usual summer drought lasted into winter this year, not really ending until a week ago in the storm that caused all the flooding in town. The plants were looking quite dead, but they are starting to show signs of new life again after the rains. The new leaves look succulent, but I believe they will become sharp spines in time. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2018)

File.Name: i117a_i117-053.jpg
Notes:
39721731922 Here is yet another spider in the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus - a different one, compare my [Previous] two photos! BTW, the cactus fruit just are different colors, sometimes red and sometimes purple. I'm not sure what kind of spider this one is, maybe a Wolf Spider (Lycosidae) - or maybe Agelenidae? Its legs are confusing. I come up one short when I try to count them, so maybe it's missing one. I can see one iridescent eye peering out between the legs on the right. I don't know why I find these spiders in the cactus fruit, but they like it here! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2018)

There's a 20% chance of rain on Thursday night/Friday morning, then it's expected to turn windy and cold. Hah, there's even a report of snow down to 2500 ft. which includes us! I'm not impressed with the odds, but we'll see what happens.

File.Name: i117b_i117-008.jpg
Notes: 39753304331 Here is a different spider in the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus. I'm not sure what kind of spider this is, though I'm pretty sure it's a male with those enlarged palps. My first thought is that it's a Cobweb Weaver spider in the Theridiidae family. I've seen this spider before, see this photo - but on a different fruit. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos for different spiders in the same place! For some reason, they like it here. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2018)

File.Name: i117c_i114-003.jpg
Notes: 39753070451 This photo shows webbing across the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus - with the spider underneath! (Try leaning back a bit to see the spider.) I believe this is the same kind of spider I show in a different cactus fruit in this photo, maybe a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the genus Xysticus or Ozyptila. This seems like it might be a permanent nest, but the spider was gone the next day. It also seems a surprising place to find a spider, but see my [Next] two photos (here and here) which show different kinds of spiders in the same place! For some reason, they like it here. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2018)

File.Name: i117d_i114-010.jpg
Notes: 39752932341 I believe from its shape that this is a little Long-Jawed Orb Weaver (Tetragnathidae) spider on what's left of its web, maybe genus Metallina. I say "what's left of its web" because I clumsily took out the other half with my tripod leg. D'oh! My photo does show half its web, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2018)

File.Name: i118a_i118-012.jpg
Notes:
39771862561 This is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (genus Cyclosa) spider hanging in the center of its web with bits of its "trashline" visible. These spiders decorate their perfect webs with the bodies of their prey to make up the "trash line" of the web. They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. This is a nice shot of the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2018)

Hah - I've posted four photos today with the inscription "HBBBT" for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but that group has a limit of two photos. Oh well, it's been a good week for butt shots - I could have posted four more! The weather might get interesting over the next couple of days - or it might just get nasty. There's a slight chance of rain tonight and tomorrow but wind and cold will steal the show. Oh well, I have a newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to put together this weekend. If it's cold and windy then all the better for being tied to my computer all day!

File.Name: i118b_i118-021.jpg
Notes: 39739938652 This is a bristly little fly (Diptera) on a still-damp sandstone outcrop that's covered with liverworts (Marchantiophyta) which I'm pretty are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). This fly looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. It was definitely interested in the liverworts, walking around on them for some time. This is also a nice shot of the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2018)

File.Name: i118c_i117-034.jpg
Notes: 24901743357 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) working a new flower of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I'm also not sure what kind of fly it is, though I don't think it's an ordinary one since here it is visiting native flowers several miles from people. My first thought at the time was that it's the same kind of fly as this photo which I found working a different native flower, but now I'm not so sure. This is also a nice shot of the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2018)

File.Name: i118d_i116-015.jpg
Notes: 39739561622 This is a little Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) trying to hide in a sheltered place between dead leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it's a Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens), see this photo for another shot of lots of them. We used to have congregations of thousands of these in our canyon, but I've only been seeing singletons during the drought years since 2012. This is also a nice shot of the beetle's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2018)
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; Coast Live Oak; Convergent Lady Beetle

File.Name: i119a_i119-001.jpg
Notes:
24918763927 I found these Agaricus mushrooms growing in the oak mulch on the edge of the garden today. They look delicious, but I believe they are the ill-tasting Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis). I nibbled a piece. It tastes good at first, but soon gets a nasty phenol-like aftertaste that's hard to spit out. This one is toxic, causing gastrointestinal upset. It has a felt-like partial veil ring on the stem which is distinctive, see this photo. We have very similar mushrooms in the woods that are truly delicious - maybe here and here. I find our woodland Agaricus mushrooms to be a confusing group! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2018)

The good news is that there are a few mushrooms - and look at the raindrops of them! We did get a splash of rain today, but only a few hundredths of an inch. And I found these mushrooms - it must be a coincidence! It was drizzly and calm this morning, then it turned windy and cold.

File.Name: i119b_i119-007.jpg
Notes:
25916312498 Here are two different shots of the same colony of Slime Mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) in the woods. I got the photo of the right yesterday and the photo on the left today - it's remarkable how fast they develop! I believe these are the genus Stemonitis slime mold. They are growing on the same on a well-rotted fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) as the slime molds I've been showing here, here, and here. (I'll post another photo of that one tomorrow to continue the series.)

This one today is going through the same progression. On the right, you can see the plasmodium of the slime mold forming into stalks and becoming the spore-bearing sporangia on the left. Look how you can see the stalks right through the material on the right - like life itself arising from the primordial ooze! But that's not what's happening - see this photo for a bit more info about these fascinating creatures. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2018)
ScientificName: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Stemonitis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Stemonitis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i119c_i117-029.jpg
Notes: 39756840292 This is another shot of "just a fly" (Diptera) working new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The fly is so dark that I had to over-expose a bit to bring out some detail - which worked, and I like the painterly effect on the flowers in the background. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, though I don't think it's an ordinary one since here it is visiting native flowers several miles from people. I have another photo of the same fly a few photos back, here. My first thought at the time was that it's the same kind of fly as this photo which I found working a different native flower, but now I'm not so sure. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2018)

File.Name: i119d_i113-014.jpg
Notes: 39078204494 Here is a handsome little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on a still-damp sandstone outcrop that's covered with liverworts (Marchantiophyta) which I'm pretty are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). I have another photo of a different kind of fly that was interested in the same liverworts two photos back, here. It's interesting how some of these Syrphids rest with their wings together like this one, and others rest with their wing spread more open. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2018)

File.Name: i120a_i119-011.jpg
Notes:
39774279732 This is a collage of photos of the same Slime Mold (Myxogastria, Myxomycetes) in the woods over the last week. I believe they are the genus Stemonitis, all shot on the same well-rotted fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I got the photo on the left of the egg-like plasmodia a week ago on January 12, here. Then the plasmodia ripened into chocolate-colored "hot dogs on a stick" the next day on January 13, here. Then the new sporangia started releasing spores on January 14, here. Then I skipped a few days as the sporangia didn't change much, but by January 19 yesterday, you can see that the sporangia (with raindrops!) have been releasing their spores - "flying Mother Nature's Silver seed to a new home." It's a neat progression that shows how these microscopic critters get from one decomposing branch to another fresh one. See this photo for a bit more info about these fascinating creatures. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2018)

Just these two photos today, as I'm computer-weary after working all day on the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society all day. I'm just about done. I'll send it out to a few society board members for review and comments tomorrow and hopefully get it to the printers on Monday. It was cold and too windy for photos today anyway as our small storm blew through, good timing.
ScientificName: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Stemonitis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Myxogastria; Myxomycetes; Stemonitis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i120b_i119-015.jpg
Notes: 38906715925 Here's another photo of the mushrooms in my [Previous] photo which I believe are the ill-tasting Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis). They came up in the oak mulch on the edge of our garden in a drizzle yesterday. You can see the thick felt-like partial veil ring on the stem on the left which is distinctive. The gills start pallid and covered by a veil (right) but become chocolate brown and exposed (left) with time. I nibbled a piece. It tastes good at first, but soon gets a nasty phenol-like aftertaste that's hard to spit out. This mushroom looks delicious, but it is toxic, causing gastrointestinal upset. We have similar mushrooms in our woods that are delicious - but avoid these. Like I said, the ring is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2018)

File.Name: i121a_i121-002.jpg
Notes:
25954172048 This is a little Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). In fact, this is the same dead leaf I showed in this photo a few days ago of what is probably the same crittter. Like me, these bugs don't travel much! These predators don't necessarily prey on leafhoppers, but they do prey on tiny things like this photo shows. You can see its spike-like proboscis, but these critters are slow rather than fast. Look at the shutter speed of this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2018)

It was still cold today, but the wind finally died down. I sent my newsletter draft out to a few board members for review and comments, and I gave them until noon tomorrow to respond. Then I took a walk with my camera, always a pleasure after a full day at the computer. I'll get it off to the printers on Monday and be done with it. What can I say, the volunteer work we do for our non-profits is important.
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: i121b_i121-005.jpg
Notes: 38928099645 This is a bristly little fly (Diptera) perched on a narrow leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). This fly is quite small, smaller than a house fly. It's bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. Maybe this is the same kind of fly as this photo of a different fly on nearby liverworts? (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2018)

File.Name: i121c_i121-013.jpg
Notes: 39795084092 I found this small spider tucked into a place where several leaves come out on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree today. The eyes are interesting and I hoped they would tell me what kind of spider it is using the useful guide to Spider Eye Arrangements at BugGuide, but I still can't decide - Theridiidae and Araneidae are both contenders. Whatever it is, it's an impressive spider. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2018)

File.Name: i121d_i118-026.jpg
Notes: 39794853682 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders (photo here) that I find in the fall, though it's much smaller. See this photo for another one I was able to measure at less than 1/10 inch. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis. In a comment to this photo, Alice Abelia says to look at "the way the abdomen dips between the two humps" - but I can't really see if there's a dip or not. These aren't the best photos, but I want them as a record since this is the first one of these spiders I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2018)

File.Name: i122a_i122-010.jpg
Notes:
39137216894 Here is the first new flower of Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family that I've seen in our mountains this year. (I have trouble spelling this one - it's "Fuchsia" not "Fuschia".) This is just about when I've found the first flowers in previous years, see this photo for example - but what an odd rain year this has been! Our usual summer drought lasted through autumn into winter this year, not really ending until the storm that caused all the flooding in town two weeks ago. We've still only had about 30% of normal rain. The natives must be confused about what season it is, but this shrub is right on schedule. See this photo for what these shrubs can do! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2018)

I finished the SYVNHS newsletter today and delivered it to the printer. Someone else else will pick it up, so my work is done. I did get out for a few photos today. It will be nice to get back to my photos full-time, but the volunteer work we do for our non-profits is important too!

File.Name: i122b_i122-007.jpg
Notes: 24976298287 Here are new flower buds on a shrub of native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family. This plant is uncommon in our mountains, and I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines. What interesting pastel colors the flower buds have! The foliage is holly-like and beautiful - though very thin, like paper, not what its looks like. These plants haven't flowered for a few years of drought, but I reckon they are responding to last year's good rains. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2018)

File.Name: i122c_i119-036.jpg
Notes: 25973562438 I believe these are odd galled flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). That odd "crook in the neck" is typical, see this photo of a different gall on the same shrub. I've found these galls before, see this photo which I reckon settles that it's a galled flower and not an aborted fruit. Also see this photo which shows a gall and a ripe berry at the same time. Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." I guess this is the one, and it's a flower NOT a berry. (Well, it may be the ovary of a flower.) I got this photo (and my [Previous] one of a different gall) on a drizzly day - perfect for this subject. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2018)

File.Name: i122d_i119-029.jpg
Notes: 39840614731 This is a beautiful speckled gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The pointed beak of these galls is distinctive. It's about the size of a grape. I have another photo of the same gall from a month ago here. These galls are the nests of specialized wasps, but they are made by the oak in response to the "sting" of the wasp. The larva of a wasp lives inside until it's time to emerge, mate, and start another gall. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Speckled". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2018)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: i123a_i123-015.jpg
Notes:
28086031009 This is a gorgeous little +Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's only about 800 pixels long in my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 3.1 mm long - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) I don't notice these insects very often, but I'm always surprised how handsome they are when I see then up close. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2018)

Somehow I managed to injure my knee - it's painful to walk or bend my knee. I still got out to my usual place in the canyon, but I stayed there. I wanted to get a photo of the cute little furry Bee Flies (Bombyliidae, Diptera) that have just started to appear. I couldn't get low enough, but see this photo from last year. Darn, I also saw the cute little diurnal (daytime) moth which I believe is Pyrausta dapalis, see this photo. I hope my knee gets better soon - I'm missing photos!
Locality: daytime) moth which I believe is Pyrausta dapalis
CommonNames: +Leafhopper; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Bee Flies

File.Name: i123b_i123-006.jpg
Notes: 25992089988 This is another "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). "Just a fly" - but there sure are a lot of different kinds of them. Compare the fly in my [Previous] photo and look at the antennae and bristles - they are clearly different. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2018)

File.Name: i123c_i123-026.jpg
Notes: 39865605821 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). "Just a fly" - but there sure are a lot of different kinds of them. Compare the fly in my [Next] photo and look at the antennae - they are clearly different. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2018)

File.Name: i123d_i118-027.jpg
Notes: 25991862108 I'm pretty sure this is a tiny Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka F. pyramitela, Linyphiidae) perched on the upper bowl part of its web. You cab see a few of its tiny prey in the web - which I guess are tiny flies. It's interesting that the spider is below the webbing and the flies are above! I have another shot showing a web here. It's a surprisingly small spider for such a large web, maybe 3/16 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2018)

File.Name: i124a_i124-029.jpg
Notes:
38984980345 I found this tiny ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) taking nectar from new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry aka Islay (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. I believe the ant is an Acrobat Ant in the genus Crematogaster of the subfamily Myrmicinae with an articulated abdomen and thoracic spines - barely visible in my photo. (It's one of the few ants I can recognize.) The individual flowers look like something under the ocean. This is the second time these handsome shrubs have flowered this year - I also found them back in October, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2018)

My knee was feeling better today, but still sore - I could walk but not stoop. It was breezy this morning, with wind from the south. I think of that as our rain wind - our winter storms come down from the north, but they pull in air from the south as they move down. We do have a 50% chance of light rain later tonight - not much rain, but it will keep the seedlings alive for another week. This is being a hard year for our flora.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: i124b_i123-007.jpg
Notes: 39851604072 This is webbing between leaves on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I believe it's the web of caterpillar and not a spider. If you look close, you can see insect frass (poop) caught up in the webbing and spiders don't make that stuff. I have other photos of similar webs here, here, and here. I guess this is the work of a little moth caterpillar, but I have yet to see it. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2018)

File.Name: i124c_i119-018.jpg
Notes: 39173268974 Here is a network of raindrops on a spider web. It wasn't much of a rain last Friday when I got this photo - just a few hundredths of an inch - but that light rain was just right to hang on the spider webs. I guess this is the web of a Sheetweb Weaver (Linyphiidae) or a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae). Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2018)

File.Name: i124d_i113-023.jpg
Notes: 39173007314 Here is a mating pair of bristly little flies caught in the act. Look at the legs of the one "on top" (to the right) - what an interesting position! These flies are bristly enough to be a Tachinid Flies (Tachinidae), but who knows. They're on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I forgot to post this one last week, so here it is - Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2018)

File.Name: i125a_i125-004.jpg
Notes:
39868741842 I found this California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the woods today, the first one I've seen this year! I've actually been worried about them since our usual summer drought went on and on this year, not really ending until the storm that caused all the flooding in town two weeks ago. Somehow these slugs manage to survive, and I sure don't know how! There seems to be some confusion about their proper scientific name, but the Oregon Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) look quite different, with greenish colors and a more pronounced ridge on their back, compare this photo. I found this one with new seedlings. Wikipedia reports that they are the second-largest species of terrestrial slug in the world, growing up to 10 inches long - but this one was smaller, only three or four inches long. It's still a handsome slug. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2018)

We didn't get enough rain to measure last night, but the drizzle and mountain fog were enough to bring out this banana slug. It was really too windy for my usual kind of photos today. We get wind from the south as a storm approaches, and wind from the north after it passes. The chilly winds were blowing from the north today.

File.Name: i125b_i124-043.jpg
Notes: 39901343261 I found this "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) working new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry aka Islay (Prunus ilicifolia, Rosaceae) yesterday. There are a number of these red and black bugs which I always get confused, but I believe this one is a Redcoat Seed Bug (Melanopleurus belfragei, Lygaeidae). I usually find this species towards the end of summer, and it seems odd to find it now. I have a list of other "red bugs" in my description for this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2018)

File.Name: i125c_i123-011.jpg
Notes: 28120541399 This is another "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) - showing its rear end. This fly looks similar to he fly in my [Previous] photo, but it seems to be more hairy. I'm pretty sure this is the same fly as this photo taken in the same shrub. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but this is a good butt shot - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2018)

File.Name: i125d_i123-031.jpg
Notes: 39900968931 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - showing its rear end. That part of the fly looks like the fly in my [Next] photo, but it doesn't seem as bristly. Maybe this one is a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae, though it has no sign of iridescent colors. Whatever the fly is, this is a good butt shot - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2018)

File.Name: i126a_i126-036.jpg
Notes:
28139056039 Here is a handsome little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) with proboscis out on a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I wouldn't think the fly could get deep enough into the flower with its proboscis, but apparently it does. This is another Syrphid that perches with wings closed, see this photo. Did you notice that tiny wasp on the flower in the center? Hah - the fly is tiny, the wasp is really tiny! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2018)

I was afraid that the Bigberry Manzanita would skip flowering on the pass this year, but here it is - flowering late, but still flowering. I was afraid that the Banana Slugs would disappear, but I found one yesterday. Gooseberries are starting to flower, and I've been trying (and failing) to get a photo of the first new butterflies. Our usual summer drought went on and on this year, not really ending until the storm that caused all the flooding in town a couple of weeks ago. It's not a normal rain year in the woods, but life is trying to go on
Locality: and failing) to get a photo of the first new butterflies.  Our usual summer drought went on and on this year
