File.Name: i126b_i124-035.jpg
Notes: 26045962488 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) working new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry aka Islay (Prunus ilicifolia, Rosaceae). The fly look pretty ordinary, maybe a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae, though it has no sign of iridescent colors. It's interesting that it's working native flowers in the woods, though I found it along the highway, not in the wilderness. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2018)

File.Name: i126c_i126-002.jpg
Notes: 28138721089 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on glandular flower buds of of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). The spider sure looks like genus Mecaphesa, which I seem to find in both spring and fall - compare this photo. This spider really is tiny - It's only about 800 pixels long in my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 3.1 mm long - about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Yeah, it's a nice "butt shot" for BBBT on the left, but next Thursday is a long ways away. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2018)

File.Name: i126d_i126-016.jpg
Notes: 25047010467 This is a new plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family, coming up mushroom-like under oaks and a shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). The red color belongs to leafy bracts where the real flowers will appear - but there are no flowers yet. (Do I see a pistil sticking out on the bottom-left?? For more flowers, see this photo from last year.) These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely manzanita in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. I only saw a few plants today, but I was surprised to find even them. It's being a tough year for annuals and perennials with such late rain. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2018)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria

File.Name: i127a_i127-035.jpg
Notes:
39937496541 These are the light-trails of tiny flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams. You can see the steady beating of their wings as little blips in their trails that makes them look like little centipedes! My exposure time was 1/13 second, and I count about 50 beats in that time, so they are beating their tiny wings 650 times a second - really fast! (Is that possible??) I guess they are tiny Midges in the family Chironomidae, see this photo. I assume this is a mating thing. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2018)

These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so. Then the trick to getting these photos is to slow your camera down. I got this photo at 1/13 sec @ f/20 @ ISO 100, on a tripod of course. Some post-processing to bump up the contrast helps. In some years, we have massive mating flights of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees - see this photo (and [Previous] to that) from Christmas Day, 2009. (This photo is at Fotolog before I started using my Flickr account - it goes back a few years!) The bird photos by photographer Xavi Bou look similar, follow this link.
Locality: This photo is at Fotolog before I started using my Flickr account - it goes back a few years!)  The bird photos by photographer Xavi Bou look similar

File.Name: i127b_i127-027.jpg
Notes: 28157146459 This looks like the work of a terrestrial caddisfly, but I believe it is the nest of a Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae, maybe genus Dianthidium. It's located about two feet high on an old stem of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae). This one appears to have three "lumps" - which I guess are three cells. I've found something similar that is much larger, see this photo. This is a lot of rocks to carry to the branch! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2018)
ScientificName: Megachilidae; Dianthidium; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Megachilidae; Dianthidium; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: i127c_i127-020.jpg
Notes: 39226347314 Here is another a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a dead twig covered with lichen. The spider sure looks like genus Mecaphesa, which I seem to find in both spring and fall - compare this photo which I just posted yesterday. I wonder if they hibernate in protected places (like these lichens) for the winter? It was a notch warmer today, which may explain why it's active. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2018)

File.Name: i127d_i126-027.jpg
Notes: 28156701249 Here are two more shots of the same handsome little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) in my [Previous] photo. The fly is engaged with new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I wouldn't think the fly could get deep enough into the flower with its proboscis, but apparently it does. This is one of the first shrubs to flower in our mountains - in early October this year, see this photo. It's really just starting to flower in earnest, at least down here on the Pass. It's an important shrub for insects and hummingbirds when not much else is flowering. It's important for nature photographers too! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2018)

File.Name: i128a_i128-020.jpg
Notes:
28177811639 Here are photos of first flowers of two different Gooseberry (Ribes) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae plant family that grow in our mountains. Both shrubs have spines which distinguish them from the spineless Currants in the same genus. On the left is a new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) which is distinguishable by its glandular petiole and ovary - and leaves too. I got this photo yesterday near home. On the right is the Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) which is distinguished by its mostly glabrous petiole and ovary - and foliage too. I got this photo today. There are other differences between these two, like the color of the anthers, but I'm not sure that's consistent. We also have the Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) with spines but different flowers in our mountains. I found the first flowers a few days ago, see this photo.

All of these spiny gooseberry shrubs flower about the same time at the end of January, see this photo of all three species last year from February 1. These shrubs are right on schedule despite our odd rain year, see my [Previous] two photos for some discussion. (San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 October 2017)

After a week of chilly weather, it warmed up today to nearly 80∞. That's too warm for this time of year, no wonder the plants are confused! At least it was not windy. We don't get those Santa Ana devil winds here in the Santa Barbara Mountains because we don't have a path to the interior deserts where they originate. It did warm up today even without the winds.
Locality: San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro Saddle
CommonNames: Gooseberry; Currants; Bitter Gooseberry; Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry
ScientificName: Ribes; Grossulariaceae; Ribes amarum; Ribes californicum; Ribes speciosum
associatedTaxa: Ribes; Grossulariaceae; Ribes amarum; Ribes californicum; Ribes speciosum

File.Name: i128b_i128-001.jpg
Notes: 26084353148 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - up in the mountains this time and not on the lower slopes. Last year I found the first flowers on January 24, so it's curious that these shrubs are right on schedule. Other native plants are not on schedule - see my [Previous] photo for an extreme example, and I'm afraid that many annuals and perennials won't flower at all if the rains don't continue. Our usual summer drought went on and on this year. It seems that some plants flower independently of the rain (maybe they are keyed to the sun?), while other plants flower depending on when the rains some. It's interesting to track what happens. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2018)

File.Name: i128c_i128-005.jpg
Notes: 39956882311 This native shrub of Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family still has a few red berries - see my Christmas photo - and it also has new flowers! The flowers don't usually appear until mid-summer, so this one is really out of sync. And it's not just this one plant, I've seen other toyon shrubs with flowers. I believe our native shrubs are getting confused by the odd weather we've been having - and I can't blame them. Our usual summer drought went on and on this year, not really ending until the storm that caused all the flooding in town a few weeks ago. On the other hand, some shrubs are keeping their normal schedule - see my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2018)

File.Name: i128d_i126-011.jpg
Notes: 25085525977 This is the furry foliage of native Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum) in the Asteraceae plant family - without ashes from the Thomas Fire. Compare this photo of a similar plant taken a month ago with the leaves covered with ashes from the wildfire. Hah, the Fabulous Foliage group admin didn't like that submission, and I hope this one will be acceptable. The plants get yellow flowers in mid-summer, see this photo, but the pretty foliage pretty much disappears by then. These are plants of cool rocky places, and I found this one growing from a crack in the sandstone bedrock. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2018)

File.Name: i129a_i129-034.jpg
Notes:
26104367378 This is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a surprising flower head of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that (mirabile dictu) still has flowers. I believe from the spider's general hairiness that this is another genus Mecaphesa spider. I don't even need to see the palps to recognize that this is a male. It's smaller but with longer legs that the female in my [Previous] photo. This is real sexual dimorphism - meaning that males and females look totally different. I found these two spiders in different parts of the same patch of buckwheat, but that's no guarantee that they are the same kind. These males seem less common than the ladies, but maybe that's just because they are harder to see. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2018)

It's been disturbingly warm. We were in the low 70s almost all night, and got up into the low 80s during the day. At the same time, there were enough high clouds that I had to adjust my white balance. *Sigh* There's no rain in the forecast for the next ten days. We're down to 27% of normal rainfall for the year.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; mirabile dictu; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; mirabile dictu; Mecaphesa

File.Name: i129b_i129-031.jpg
Notes: 39266161374 This is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a surprising flower head of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that (mirabile dictu) still has flowers. I believe from the spider's general hairiness that this is another genus Mecaphesa spider. I've been finding a few of these lately, see here and here. I believe that this one is a female. See my [Next] photo for a very different male. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2018)

File.Name: i129c_i129-023.jpg
Notes: 39944940872 I found both of these tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (genus Cyclosa) spiders hanging on their webs today in different places in he woods. These spiders decorate their perfect webs with the bodies of their prey to make up the "trash line" of the web - though the spider on the left seems to have a late start. They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. These spiders look different. I'm not sure if they are different species, but I am pretty sure they are different genders. You can tell by its swollen palps that the one on the right is a male. The palps of the one on the left are harder to see, but maybe I can make out a non-swollen female palp? (I'll make notes.) Both spiders have similar CD-size webs. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2018)

File.Name: i129d_i128-035.jpg
Notes: 28190997329 Ice and fire - and a bit of acetone. My idea was to dip an ice cube into a solvent and get a photo of it burning. I tried a shot of gin at first, but it needed more kick to burn. Then I tried acetone. That burned just fine, but the flame didn't last. I did manage to get this photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Flame". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2018)

File.Name: i130a_i130-009.jpg
Notes:
25124608957 Here is a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - almost certainly Largus californicus - working new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry aka Islay (Prunus ilicifolia, Rosaceae). It seems to be looking right at me with a slightly annoyed look. These are fairly large bugs that I seem to find year-round. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2018)

It was mostly overcast today with high clouds. I was still quite warm, not quite to the 80s. Despite the clouds, there's no rain in the forecast for some time.
CommonNames: Bordered Plant Bug; Holly-leaved Cherry; Islay
ScientificName: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae

File.Name: i130b_i130-026.jpg
Notes: 28216015879 Here are the first actual flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family that I've found in the mountains this year. The catkins form early on he shrubs but take their time actually forming flowers. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this photo shows the male, pollen-bearing, staminate plants. It usually takes a while before the female, seed-bearing, pistillate flowers appear on different shrubs. They are handsome shrubs in flower with the (mostly) dangly catkins. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2018)

File.Name: i130c_i130-032.jpg
Notes: 39994719901 Here are opening flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, related to sassafras and avocado. The flowers come in little bundles with a papery covering. In this photo both the bundle and the individual flowers within are opening. The flowers themselves are surprisingly intricate, see this photo for a close look. I found the first flowers a few weeks ago (here), but they stalled and are just starting to appear more generally. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2018)

File.Name: i130d_i130-001.jpg
Notes: 39994549041 This is a tiny spider on its web with prey. Is the prey a psyllid (Psylloidea)? (Compare this photo at BugGuide.) The spider looks like it might be another Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae), but it look different from the ones I showed yesterday, with a less developed bump on its abdomen. Maybe it's a juvenile? It really is small, about 680 across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) I found this scene in the cactus patch of our garden, the web strung between two cactus pads. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2018)

File.Name: i131a_i131-018.jpg
Notes:
28234873619 I found this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in a narrow gap between two leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Sure, it's looking at me. There's something unnerving about making eye contact with such a tiny critter, but here it is. There are many kinds of these little jumpers that I can't tell apart, and this is one of them. Isn't it cute! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2018)

The NWS Forecast Discussion this morning said that "The weather pattern is relentlessly unchanging through the xtnded period." Later on they added "Really not seeing much change in this pattern at least through next weekend as well so it`s looking likely that the first 10 days of February will be dry and quite possibly longer." The weather is warm and beautiful but dry dry dry. For some reason the chorus frogs have been really going at all night it in our outdoor bathtub water garden. Go figure.

File.Name: i131b_i131-031.jpg
Notes: 39304075404 Here is a small spider casting a big shadow under a leaf in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). From the markings, this may well be a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes of the Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spiders. From another photo, I believe this spider has the swollen palps of a male. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Hammock Spider; Sheet Web Weaver

File.Name: i131c_i131-011.jpg
Notes: 40012891251 Here are two shots showing a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) on its web, both near and far. It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders (photo here) that I find in the fall, though it's much smaller. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis. In a comment to this photo, Alice Abelia says to look at "the way the abdomen dips between the two humps" - so maybe this one with a dip is A. montereyensis. I believe that this one is male with those swollen palps. These spiders make small, perfect orb webs about the size of a CD-disk or a bit larger. They look like the webs of Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa) spiders without the trashline - but maybe they are a bit more widely spaced? This photo shows the web as well as the spider, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2018)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Coast Live Oak; Trashline Orb Weaver
ScientificName: Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis; A. montereyensis; Cyclosa

File.Name: i131d_i131-004.jpg
Notes: 39115064225 Here is a tangled spider web catching the sunlight just right. I'm shooting up so the webbing is really a more or less flat sheet rather than the pyramid it looks like. I found this web in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I guess this is the work of a Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae, though it's not as well organized as the web of the Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis) in this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2018)

File.Name: i201a_i201-003.jpg
Notes:
39322912184 This is a little spider trying to look inconspicuous among cactus spines in the garden this morning. I guess from its web that this is another Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider. I can't see its dorsal surface, but this may be the same kind of spider I showed yesterday in this photo - a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2018)

We're stuck in a quiet weather pattern that's warm and calm. Really, it's perfect weather for being outside - but it's all wrong for this time of year. We're just at 26% of normal rainfall, and we may be about to loose the seedlings that came up after our one and only rain earlier this year. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, we're back in "D2 (Severe Drought)" conditions. There's more discussion at the California Weather Blog. One observation I can make is that I've been finding more spiders than insects lately - interesting!

File.Name: i201b_i131-024.jpg
Notes: 25161771057 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a brand new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). It's the first flower I've seen this year on his plant - isn't it remarkable that the spider found it almost as soon as it opened! The light was fading into shade as I got this photo, so I still need to get a better photo of the flower. Hah - it seems like I just posted this photo of a plant starting to show signs of new life. It's also remarkable that it's flowered as soon as it has. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2018)

File.Name: i201c_i129-010.jpg
Notes: 28253432819 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) disappearing over the edge of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This might be the same kind of spider I showed yesterday in this photo. (You can't see its abdomen in that photo, but I can in another shot.) HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2018)

File.Name: i201d_i126-020.jpg
Notes: 26159589168 Here is the lovely nymph of a Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). This might be the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring. It's tiny, about 800 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm long, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) That's not counting its tail - which makes it look jet propelled - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2018)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: i202a_i202-035.jpg
Notes:
39339797574 Here are two shots of a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) - captured in the air. The fly is probably genus Bombylius, maybe the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major). I've been trying without luck to get a photo of these furry flies at rest, so I tried something different today and got these photos as a fly hovered nearby. Yeah, cropped too tight - but still pretty neat. You can see through its hairs - it's a fat furry fly with a skinny little fly inside. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2018)

Today is Groundhog Day, midway between solstice and equinox. The days are getting longer and the sun is a bit higher, we are starting to get a bit of sun in the afternoon on our upstairs porch. The old story goes that if a groundhog sees it's shadow on this day, then we're due for six more weeks of winter - until the equinox. Well, if any gopher stuck his head out today, he did see his shadow very clearly - which means six more weeks of winter. Hah - you call these 80∞ days winter?!
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Greater Bee Fly; Groundhog Day

File.Name: i202b_i202-017.jpg
Notes: 40050531751 This is another shot of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a brand new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). I posted another photo of this spider and flower yesterday - taken the day before - but this photo shows the flower better. It's the first flower I've seen this year on his plant - isn't it remarkable that the spider found it almost as soon as it opened, and that it's still there after two days. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2018)

File.Name: i202c_i202-037.jpg
Notes: 39151603225 Here are the first open flower catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today. These shrubs are dioecious with male and female flowers on different plants, and these are male pollen-bearing staminate catkins on his plant. The catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many different insects. You can see a couple of ants in my photo that are tending aphids - visible under the leaf above the flower and elsewhere, see this photo. Yes, these plants are confused by the odd weather we've been having this year - they are flowering when they still haven't dropped their old leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2018)

File.Name: i202d_i127-014.jpg
Notes: 40017243022 This is another "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This might be the same kind of fly I show in this photo. This fly looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2018)

File.Name: i203a_i203-020.jpg
Notes:
40069462371 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) - though I'm not sure what kind of prey it will find here. I believe from the spider's general hairiness that this is another genus Mecaphesa spider. I've been finding lots of these spiders since the weather turned warm this week. I dig those crazy multi-colored eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2018)

I really have been getting more photos of spiders than insects lately. I suspect it's just that insects are still scarce which makes them harder to find and photograph, so I'm looking closer and finding more spiders instead. Our run of nearly 80∞ days continues - and will probably continue all next week. That makes up the first half of February, which should be our rainiest month.

File.Name: i203b_i203-028.jpg
Notes: 39358404864 This is the same little Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I show in this photo, probably Araneus montereyensis. In that photo, I show the spider on its web in a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but it usually sits here under a nearby leaf. I was watching today - it runs out to its web if a tiny critter so much as bumps into it. I could hardly see the spider in the shade under the bright sunlight today, but I tried getting a photo anyway with the exposure kicked up a stop or two. This is a flawed photo, but it came out better than I expected. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2018)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus montereyensis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus montereyensis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i203c_i203-025.jpg
Notes: 39358273164 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) warming up on leaf cluster of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). These wasps look threatening enough, but I've never been stung, even by the ones that nest under our eaves at home. These wasps should be dormant right now, but they come out on warm days like this - it almost got up to 80∞ today, like it has all week. Insects are still scarce, but they are stirring. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2018)
CommonNames: Western Paper Wasp; Southern Bush Monkeyflower
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: i203d_i203-016.jpg
Notes: 40035924422 It's surprising to see this new foliage of native perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family coming out in the canyon. It's been so dry in this drought year (just 25% of normal) that I expect many annuals and perennials will skip flowering this year - but I expect the weeds will manage to flower. This is one way that the weeds get ahead of the natives. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2018)

File.Name: i204a_i204-028.jpg
Notes:
39191058795 Here are new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, related to sassafras and avocado. The flowers themselves are surprisingly intricate. Look close at that uppermost flower on the right. There's a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with four anthers each, with two orange glands at the base of each, finally surrounded by an outer circle of six more fat stamens with four anthers each. The whole thing is contained within a circle of (usually) six sepals/petals. They are strange and wonderful flowers for such a common tree in our California woods. If you haven't looked close at the flowers of this tree, I recommend you do so. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2018)

It did get up to 80∞ today, with nothing but more of the same in the forecast for the next week - but maybe not quite so warm. It was a bit breezy today with winds from the south. I believe this is the work of a little eddy that forms in the channel as the ocean winds come around Point Conception. It's got nothing to do with rain.

File.Name: i204b_i204-010.jpg
Notes: 39190799545 Here is a shrub of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family draped with unopened flower catkins - like tinsel on a Christmas tree! See my [Previous] photo for an open flower on a different shrub. The color of these unopened catkins looks kind of unwholesome compared to that other photo, and I hope the catkins will finally open. This shrub is flowering heavily after the good rains last year, but next year is likely to be different after our paltry rains this year - so far at least. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2018)

File.Name: i204c_i204-003.jpg
Notes: 28308265559 This is looking close at opening flowers of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this photo shows a male, pollen-bearing, staminate plant. Both male and female flowers are organized into catkins. Look on the left - the new stamens are enclosed in little cages, like a royal scepter! Most of the plants in our mountains still have unopened flowers, see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2018)

File.Name: i204d_i204-026.jpg
Notes: 40056127992 This is a flowering branch of Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family which is distinguished by its non-glandular, mostly hairless flowers and foliage. For some reason we don't have this one in our canyon at home - it is replaced by the glandular Bitter Gooseberry (R. amarum), see this photo on the left. Canyon Gooseberry is the host plant for the Tailed Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly, see this photo. I've never seen the butterfly in our home canyon either. I know these two gooseberries when I see them, but I don't understand their distribution. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2018)
CommonNames: Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Bitter Gooseberry; Tailed Copper

File.Name: i205a_i205-005.jpg
Notes:
25235993657 This is a striking colony of Gold-Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) growing on a dead twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). That's an evergreen shrub, but for some reason lichens are especially drawn to it. The orange cups of this lichen are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the fungus and algae. This lichen is similar to its cousin Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans) which lacks the ciliate apothecia, see this photo. Lichens don't disappear during drought, but they do shrivel up. These look like they belong under the sea! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 5 February 2018)

I drove out to the Santa Ynez Valley today to pick something up, but it turned out I can't get it until next week. Oh well, I did stop for a couple of photos on the way. I'm also on call for jury duty this week. I have to call in every night to see if they need me - so far I'm not needed. I've served on three juries so far. I understand that the service is a duty of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos!

File.Name: i205b_i205-009.jpg
Notes: 26233669738 This is new foliage of a perennial California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, arising mushroom-like from the ground. They remind me of new rhubarb stalks. (It's actually growing from the edge of a woodrat nest - I guess they don't taste very good!) These plants are fire-followers, but they sometimes linger for decades, as at this place near Lake Cachuma. What's disturbing is that this is the only plant I've found this year, in a place that should have a dozen plants or more. The flowers usually appear soon after the leaves, see this photo. I hope we get flowers in this drought year, but I won't bet on it. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 5 February 2018)

File.Name: i205c_i201-016.jpg
Notes: 26228079198 Here are a pair of old calyx cups on a stem of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina, Lamiaceae). The cups look like tattered paper lanterns. This is the original color image that changed to monochrome with just a hint of color in my [Previous] photo for the Macro Mondays group with the theme of "Monochrome". To be honest, I think I prefer this color image with just a trace of orange. It's pretty close to monochrome anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2018)

File.Name: i205d_i201-016.jpg
Notes: 40069349592 Here are a pair of old calyx cups on a stem of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina, Lamiaceae). The cups look like tattered paper lanterns. I got this as a color photo and then used software to change it to monochrome with just a hint of color. I'll post my original color image as my next photo - it's almost monochrome already but not quite. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Monochrome". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2018)

File.Name: i206a_i206-007.jpg
Notes:
39227743835 Here is a handsome Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly spreading its wings to catch some sun on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) down by the creek. That's where their willow host plants grow. There were a pair of these butterflies flying around today, and I hope they start a new generation in our willows. I see these butterflies every time I go by the creek, but I don't get many photos. Hah - a couple of times one has landed on my head or my camera tripod! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2018)

I'm on call for jury duty this week. I have to check in every night to see if they need me the next day - and I'm still not needed tomorrow/Wednesday. I've served on three juries so far. I understand that the service is a duty of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos!

File.Name: i206b_i206-056.jpg
Notes: 26252633048 This is a handsome little diurnal (daytime) Moth (Lepidoptera) working a catkin of new female pistillate flowers on native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today. The male staminate willows are also flowering, see this photo from a few days ago. Willow catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many different insects. If you look close, you can see that this moth's proboscis is busy with the flowers. Hah - the flower is in sharp focus, the moth is blurry because it's moving so fast over the flowers! Maybe I've seen this moth before on the willows, see this photo. I'll try to get a sharper photo tomorrow, and maybe I can figure out what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2018)

File.Name: i206c_i206-015.jpg
Notes: 39414595934 These two spiders aren't mating yet, but they sure are thinking about it! That's the male on the left with those swollen palps. It's interesting that the female on the right is smaller than the male - that seems to go against the usual rule. I'm pretty sure these are Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes of the Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spiders, see this photo. If you look close, you can see a thin sheet of webbing over the leaf, which belongs to a small tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I watched them for some time hoping they would get it on, but they stayed apart until I finally let them be. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2018)
CommonNames: Hammock Spider; Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: i206d_i206-040.jpg
Notes: 40126377101 This is a really tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in its nest in an old seedhead of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider is at one end of a small orb web - I'll post a shot of it tomorrow for the Web Wednesdays group, but it looks just like this different web in the same kind of shrub. The spider is tiny, about 480 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.8 mm long, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It has interesting markings. It night actually be the same kind of spider as the one on the left in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2018)

File.Name: i207a_i206-021.jpg
Notes:
28363567889 This is looking close at the male spider that I showed with it's likely mate in this photo yesterday. I'm pretty sure this is a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes of the Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spiders. My photo shows the spider's very male palps at the front of its head, and I don't think I've ever seen palps quite so swollen. Palps are actually the primary sex organs for male spiders. I have more info about spider love at this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2018)

Yeah I did get some new photos today, but it was breezy today and I like these better. I'm on call for jury duty all week. I have to check in every night to see if they need me the next day - and I'm still not needed tomorrow/Thursday. Only one more day to go! (But I've been snagged on the last day before.) I've served on three juries so far. I understand that the service is a duty of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos!
Locality: But I've been snagged on the last day before.)  I've served on three juries so far.  I understand that the service is a duty of citizenship

File.Name: i207b_i206-045.jpg
Notes: 26270004258 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) squatting in the center of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). This is a different shrub than the one I showed in this photo a few days ago. There are still not a lot of flowers, but at least they are spreading in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2018)

File.Name: i207c_i206-034.jpg
Notes: 39431756854 This is the web of the really tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider that I showed yesterday in this photo. The web is strung between old stems of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The spider has its nest on top of the old seed head to the right. Note all the extra webbing around its nest - in part to transfer the vibrations of prey from web to nest. The web is smaller than it looks, only a couple of inches high - small but perfect. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2018)

File.Name: i207d_i203-010.jpg
Notes: 25271415007 This is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). These spiders build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. I believe there is usually a line or two that connects them so that the spider can sense activity. The spider is no doubt in its retreat. I didn't get a photo of the spider this day, but I have others, e.g. here, here, and here. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2018)

File.Name: i208a_i208-042.jpg
Notes:
39449285084 Here are two shots of a striking wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working new pistillate flowers on a catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. I guess this bee has also been working the male staminate flowers on a nearby tree as well - it's pretty well dusted with pollen. It was moving fast on the catkins, hard to photograph. I'm not sure what kind of bee this is, and I don't want to guess as I've wrong so many times before. The bee looks pretty distinctive - if you recognize it, please let me know. (I can't resist - could it be genus Andrena? See the Dark-tailed Andrena (Andrena fuscicauda) at BugGuide.) Whatever it is, it's a gorgeous bee. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)

D'oh - they got me! Just when I thought I might get away with my call for jury duty this week, I have to report for my duty on Friday morning at 8:00 am. (That seems kind of brutally early to actually be at the office in Santa Barbara!) Oh well, the odds are still stacked against actually serving on a jury. I've served on three juries so far. I understand that the service is a duty of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos!

File.Name: i208b_i208-021.jpg
Notes: 40160338961 Here is an ant with its flock of aphids on a flowering staminate tree of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. The ants are being smart. They've been tending their aphids on the old leaves with are still on this small tree, see this photo. But these trees are deciduous, and I've been wondering what they will do when they finally drop their leaves - aphids and all. But my photo shows that the ants have solved this problem by moving their aphids to the new leaves which are just starting to appear. There are aphids on the base of the catkin too, but not so many - because the catkins will drop too? Hah - the ant is blowing a bubble! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)

File.Name: i208c_i208-054.jpg
Notes: 25288272067 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) working new pistillate flowers on a catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today. The fly look pretty ordinary, maybe a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae, though it has no sign of iridescent colors. It's interesting that it's working native flowers in the woods. Hah - if this fly's posterior looks familiar, see this photo from a couple of weeks ago. This is a good shot of the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)

File.Name: i208d_i207-018.jpg
Notes: 40127391502 Here are two shots of a striking little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I believe it is species Pselliopus spinicollis. I've seen these before on manzanita flowers, see this photo. It really didn't want to pose for me, and I was lucky to get these two shots which are cropped pretty tight. Half of this photo is a fine butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2018)

File.Name: i209a_i209-004.jpg
Notes:
39278905695 Here is another caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly transferring between two boat-like seed capsules of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I've seen this before this year, see this photo from last month. I've seen other critters use these little boats for shelter - see this photo, this photo, and this photo. Actually the monkeyflower is slow to develop new leaves in this drought year, so the caterpillars are transferring to a different host plant - the native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) which is now starting to leaf out. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2018)

I reported for Superior Court jury duty today. We didn't learn much about the case except that it will take a month to settle on a MWF schedule with TT off. The judge wanted to give people a chance to ask for a deferment. I couldn't think of any good reason why I couldn't serve - except that I want to keep posting these photos, of course - so I report back in two weeks for actual jury selection. With days off, I figure I'll be able to keep up the photos. To tell the truth, I was shocked by how many people wanted to ask for a deferment - which just puts it off, it doesn't excuse you. Oh well, I got to leave early without listening to everyone's excuses, and even got this photo. Hey - there's a chance of rain next week!

File.Name: i209b_i208-049.jpg
Notes: 40144366722 I'm not sure what kind of fly this is, but it's a handsome one with that orange color and translucent body. Like the fly in my [Previous] photo, this one is working a pistillate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family, going for the nectar produced by the dark glands at the base of the actual flowers. Willow catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many different insects - including this fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)

Notes: 25305211227 I'm not sure what kind of fly this is - but I don't think it's "just a fly" like so many flies are. Maybe I can see a "spurious vein" on it's wing, so maybe it's a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera)? Maybe not. This one is working a pistillate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family, going for the nectar produced by the dark glands at the base of the actual flowers. Willow catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many different insects - including this fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)

File.Name: i209d_i208-037.jpg
Notes: 39278421155 In between the time when I got the two photos of the gorgeous wild bee in my [Previous] photo (here), I heard a little thump and saw a blur through my camera viewfinder - and I snapped a photo. My photo tells all, even with the blur. That's my bee on the bottom, which I figure is female with that wide abdomen. The smaller male on top must have dived in for a quickie and then immediately left. I never actually saw it, it all happened so fast, and I'm not even sure if it was consummated . The bee on the bottom went on with its business as if nothing had happened. I don't know if this episode was rape, assault, or normal mating behavior! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)

File.Name: i210a_i210-026.jpg
Notes:
40162413282 This is a worn California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly perched for just a moment in the oak leaves along the road. These butterflies overwinter as adults and are active on warm winter days like we've been having lately. I've been seeing them for some time, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph this season. I don't usually find these at a other time of year, but there are a few like this fine specimen. I also found a horde of caterpillars on a Ceanothus shrub last year that are probably these - see this photo. Other overwintering butterflies are the Mourning Cloak (here), Red Admiral (here), and the California Dogface (here). It's always a treat to find one of these butterflies on a winter day. I have been seeing a few new butterflies, but I haven't got any photos yet - Pacific Azure, Umber Skipper, and Funereal Duskywing that I recall. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2018)

Today started sunny and warm, but it started to change around noon with haze and a cool breeze from the south. My first thought was "marine layer" - and sure enough, marine layer fog started spilling over the mountains. The temperature dropped 20 degrees into the low 50s. We've been locked into the same beautiful but dry weather pattern for so long that I figure any change is for the better. And there is rain in the forecast for next week!

File.Name: i210b_i210-024.jpg
Notes:
28414950449 I believe this is another "really tiny orb-weaver" (Araneidae) spider like I showed in this photo a few days ago. It has a perfect orb web like the one in this photo, except that this one is in a dead shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) rather than Black Sage like the other one. There's a little gnat/midge in there for scale. This one appears to be male with enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2018)

Now that I look at this, I wonder if it could be a really tiny Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), one that is just starting to decorate its retreat with the bodies of its prey? Compare this photo. It does have a perfect little orb web off to the side. I suppose time will tell.

File.Name: i210c_i210-011.jpg
Notes: 39296571975 I believe this little spider is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver in the Tetragnathidae family, probably Metellina mimetoides. It has a humped back, very long front legs, and It spins a small perfect orb web. We have several different small spiders that make perfect little orb webs besides this one: Araneus bispinosus and A. montereyensis, see this photo; Trashline Orb Weavers (genus Cyclosa), see this photo; and the really tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in my [Next] photo, also see this photo. No doubt we have more - these spiders are all tiny, and many are shy and reclusive. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2018)

File.Name: i210d_i210-014.jpg
Notes: 40194447831 These are little galls on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I believe they are galls of the Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) flies in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. Usually the tiny galls form under the leaves, but one of these in the back is on top. It's hard to see in this photo, but the galls are open on top, with the openings filled with hairs. I seem to be seeing more of these galls than usual this year, but that might just be a result of looking closer because there are fewer insects in this drought year. I've been finding more spiders than usual for the same reason. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2018)

File.Name: i211a_i211-014.jpg
Notes:
39316434425 This is looking close at new flowers of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing up here in the mountains. I found the first flowers a couple of weeks ago, see this photo, and they've been spreading. The individual flowers are intricate, with a blue nectar disk at the base. In a good year, the shrubs cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains with flowers, like snow - see this photo from a few years ago, gazillions of individual flowers. I don't expect that in this drought year, but it sure is nice to see any new flowers! We have several different white-flowered Ceanothus species that have similar white flowers, but the foliage is different. This one is unique in having (mostly) alternate leaves and large seed capsules - but see this photo. I was disappointed that there weren't many insects on the flowers today, but that's how it is this year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2018)

I was hoping for at least some clouds today, but it was sunny and pleasant. Some clouds did start moving in this evening. We do have a chance of rain for the next few days, but it doesn't sound too serious - it won't cause new mudslides. We've only had 23% of our normal rainfall this year, essentially in one storm in early January, but this isn't the storm we need.

File.Name: i211b_i211-022.jpg
Notes: 25342991487 Here is a branch of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family draped with new flower catkins - like tinsel on a Christmas tree. The flowers look so airy and fluffy - beautiful in their own way. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and this photo shows a male, pollen-bearing, staminate plant. Both male and female flowers are organized into catkins. The shrubs seem to be flowering heavily after the good rains last year, but next year is likely to be different after our paltry rains this year - so far at least. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2018)

File.Name: i211c_i211-027.jpg
Notes: 39502544244 Here is native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family running along a wire fence. Hah - this plant seems to be pre-adapted to fencing! The vines have exuberant new growth, coming from the enormous "man-root" of the plant, see Gerry Queener's photo of a related species here. We also have this plant's cousin Marah fabaceus which grows more in the backcountry and has smaller more yellow flowers. It's confusing where they overlap, but M. macrocarpus has 8-12 smaller seeds in its spiky fruit, and M. fabaceus has just four larger ones. See this photo for the difference. Both of these plants are monoecious with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. My photo mostly shows the plentiful male staminate flowers. The female pistillate flowers are more solitary and hard to find before they turn into fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2018)
ScientificName: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; monoecious
associatedTaxa: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; monoecious

File.Name: i211d_i207-026.jpg
Notes: 26341232788 Here are the first new flowers of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. I only found the first signs of the plants a couple of weeks ago, see this photo from January 26. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely manzanita in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. There are only a few plants in this drought year - in fact, these are the first flowers I seen any any herbaceous plant (not a shrub or tree) this year. We've only had 23% of our normal rainfall, essentially in one storm in early January. It's being a tough year for annuals and perennials with such late rain, but life does go on. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2018)

File.Name: i212a_i212-012.jpg
Notes:
26361661298 Here are new catkins and foliage of native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata, Fagaceae) growing in the Santa Ynez Valley. The deciduous trees are just starting to get flowers and leaves, compare my [Previous] photo. I had to drive around for a while to find a tree that was both leafing out and accessible. If you look close, you can see a succulent gall on the catkin at the base of the leaf stalk that goes off to the right, I'll make a note. Apparently this is the gall of an undescribed species of Cynipid Wasp, see this photo at BugGuide and the comment by Joyce Gross. These galls seem to be locally common. I'll get a closer photo when I can get closer to a flowering tree. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2018)

Our latest stormy weather has turned out to be a bust, bringing just wind and chilly conditions. This could turn out to be our driest year ever if we don't get some real rain soon. We stand at 4.68 inches of rain in this water year, only 22% of normal, all essentially from one storm in early January. The driest year that I can find in the record is 10.90 inches in 2006-7. Our normal rainfall is 34.14 inches. Yipes!

File.Name: i212b_i212-003.jpg
Notes: 38422873970 Here is a group of classic "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata, Fagaceae). True to their name, these oaks only grow in the valleys, but the same gall wasps make galls on the Scrub Oaks (Quercus berberidifolia) in the mountains. Apparently, these galls are made by wasps that are completely parthenogenetic - neither males nor an alternate generation is known. Notice the exit holes which show that the galls are polythalamous - containing many larvae. I have a photo of the larvae here. There are also a few old galls on the left that may belong to the Flat-topped Honeydew Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis eldoradensis). This deciduous tree is still winter-bare, unlike the tree in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2018)
CommonNames: California Gall Wasp; Valley Oak; Scrub Oaks; Flat-topped Honeydew Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; polythalamous; Disholcaspis eldoradensis

File.Name: i212c_i212-013.jpg
Notes: 39335982055 Here are the first pollen-bearing staminate catkins that I've seen on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this year. It was windy when I got this photo, so it's not quite sharp - but the wind is good for blowing the pollen around . . . and causing hay fever too. The trees are just starting to flower. They are quite beautiful when fully draped with catkins, but I don't know if that will happen in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2018)

File.Name: i212d_i209-013.jpg
Notes: 39516640934 This is a willow catkin in the mouth of a small blue bottle. It's a pistillate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family. That's what I had at hand, and it looks like something under the sea, strange and mysterious. The mouth of the bottle is 1/2 inch. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "In a Bottle". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2018)

File.Name: i213a_i213-010.jpg
Notes:
39357499605 Here are new flowers on a shrub of native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family. The flowers look like buds - but I'm pretty sure this is all there is. I have another photo from a month ago here. It's an uncommon plant in our mountains, and I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines. The foliage is holly-like and beautiful - but very thin, like paper, not what its looks like. These plants haven't flowered for a few years of drought, but I reckon they are responding to last year's good rains. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2018)

Clouds did move in last night and we had a bit of rain - just 6/100 inch, but that's more than nothing. It was cloudy all day, and there was even some thunder this afternoon, but mostly it was cold and windy. I did get out for a drive and a walk today, and got a couple of photos. It's also a good day to post a couple of last week's photos before they get stale.

File.Name: i213b_i213-021.jpg
Notes: 40210529142 This small tree of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family is slowly succumbing to (I guess) an infestation of Western Bark Beetles (Dendroctonus brevicomis, Scolytinae or Curculionidae, Coleoptera). You can see a totally dead tree behind it on the right, and you can also see a Gray Pine aka Digger Pine (Pinus sabiniana) behind it on the left that is totally unaffected. The beetles or the drought or whatever it is have killed about half of the pines in this place. It's frustrating because all you can do is watch the trees die. This has been going on for some time, see this photo from 2014. *Sigh* The tree is dying, but it's also quite beautiful, on this gray day, with that shaggy patchwork of different colored needles. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2018)
CommonNames: Coulter Pine; Western Bark Beetles; Gray Pine; Digger Pine
ScientificName: Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae; Dendroctonus brevicomis; Scolytinae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Pinus sabiniana
associatedTaxa: Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae; Dendroctonus brevicomis; Scolytinae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Pinus sabiniana

File.Name: i213c_i207-036.jpg
Notes: 25383888847 A week ago, I got this photo of a little diurnal (daytime) moth working a pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow. I went back the next day hoping to get a sharper photo of the moth, and I found this scene instead - it's an ant taking one of the moths apart. I didn't see it, but I'll wager that the ant caught the moth on a willow flower - see this photo from last year of an ant attacking a fly! The ants are tending their aphids on the willow catkins, and they do not like other insects getting close. I'm still not sure what kind of moth this is - or was. This photo shows its orange underwings. Maybe I've seen these moths before on the willows, see this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2018)

File.Name: i213d_i206-030.jpg
Notes: 26382116518 This caterpillar looks like a stick! This is a well-camouflaged looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae). (Maybe it's a vine on the gooseberry?) Geometrid caterpillars are distinctive with a base of just two pairs of prolegs. These caterpillars have great camouflage - they stand out straight like little twigs. I've found them on the gooseberry before, see this photo from last year. I don't know what that hair is. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2018)
CommonNames: looper; inchworm; Geometrid Moth; Bitter Gooseberry

File.Name: i214a_i214-028.jpg
Notes:
40229375312 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It didn't want to look at me in that classic jumping spider pose, but this pose is just fine - and you can see at least one each of its four pairs of eyes. I've been finding the same kind of spider lately, for example this photo. There are many kinds of these little jumpers that are hard to tell apart - maybe this one is genus Pelegrina? (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2018)

The weather today had a little bit of everything - sun, clouds, showers, wind, calm, warmer, colder - it was an interesting weather day! We ended up with about 0.15 inch of rain over the last two days. That's not the rain we need in this drought year, but it is something.

File.Name: i214b_i214-008.jpg
Notes: 25402731507 Here are new fronds of native Coffee Fern (Pellaea andromedifolia) in the Pteridaceae plant family, with a few raindrops on he lower part of he fronds. I guess the name comes from the color of the stems. They've had a tough time in this drought year, but this one is trying. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2018)

File.Name: i214c_i208-026.jpg
Notes: 40228824782 This is a little web-spinning caterpillar out of its nest between the leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. Here it is under a thin layer of webbing on a leaf that it appears to be skeletonizing - at least it's eaten the top layer. I figure the caterpillar's nest in back in the shadows where I can make out some frass. I've posted a number of photos of the work of these caterpillars: here, here, here, and here. This is the first time I've managed to catch a caterpillar. Of course I still don't know what it is - how many caterpillars make obvious webbing?? This caterpillar is a web spinner, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)

File.Name: i214d_i208-009.jpg
Notes: 39375813045 This is the web of a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider in an old seed head of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek. The web comes in layers! It might be the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis) - though the middle layer isn't really shaped like a bowl. (This matches the description of the related spider Neriene digna.) I like the nearly monochrome colors of this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Mugwort; Bowl and Doily Spider
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Frontinella communis; Neriene digna
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Frontinella communis; Neriene digna

File.Name: i215a_i215-024.jpg
Notes:
38481744840 I believe this photo shows a mating pair of tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera ) insects. Their nuptial bed is the nectar disk of a flower of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. These critters look different from one another, but it's one nice feature of mating shots that they must be the same species! Psyllids of several different genera are said to occur on Ceanothus species - Ceanothia, Nyctiphalerus, Euglyptoneura, Pexopsylla, and Cacopsylla. I won't try to guess which one my photo shows, but there's more info at BugGuide. These critters are tiny, about 400 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.6 mm long, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) That number doesn't include foreshortening or the bend of the abdomen, so 2 mm might be a better estimate. Really tiny! I've also found pysllids on Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata, Anacardiaceae) and near Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) - see this photo and this photo. I don't really understand this insect group, so I'm always happy to find a new one. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2018)

Today was clear and warmer - though there's still a breeze, and it's still chilly at night. I decided to go for a walk through Kinevan Canyon, though I really didn't find much to photograph except that great honeysuckle vine in my [Previous] photo. Then I went further along West Camino Cielo to get this photo. It seems that I really have to hunt for insects to photograph right now.

File.Name: i215b_i215-008.jpg
Notes: 40291412261 This is a remarkable rope-like vine of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's about two inches in diameter, made up of a number of separate stalks that are entwined to make one. I suspect this once grew up into an oak tree that is no longer there. I found this along the shady road in Kinevan Canyon, so it's possible that a tree was cut down - though it's surprising that the vine survived intact. Look - the vine has a green sprout! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2018)

File.Name: i215c_i208-035.jpg
Notes: 39579877274 Here is the rear end of the striking wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) I showed a week ago in this photo. The bee is working new a pistillate catkin of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. I guess this bee has also been working the male staminate flowers on a nearby tree as well - it's pretty well dusted with pollen. Maybe the bee is genus Andrena? See the Dark-tailed Andrena (Andrena fuscicauda) at BugGuide.) Whatever it is, it's a gorgeous bee - even from the backside! HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Arroyo Willow; Dark-tailed Andrena

File.Name: i215d_i214-025.jpg
Notes: 40291229351 This is the rear end of the tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in my [Previous] photo (here). it's trying to avoid me on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). There are many kinds of these little jumpers that are hard to tell apart - maybe this one is genus Pelegrina? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2018)

File.Name: i216a_i216-002.jpg
Notes:
39411987605 These are twining tendrils of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I was thinking of this for the Macro Mondays assignment this week with the theme of "Fasteners" - see the description here. The tendrils do indeed fasten the plant to other plants (and itself), but the group describes a fastener as "a device that attaches something firmly to something else". I don't believe this counts because the tendrils are part of the things attached and not something extra, so I have to think of something else - but this is still a nice photo to post here! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2018)

Like yesterday, today was clear and warm - though there's still a breeze, and it's still chilly at night. I took my usual walk in the canyon. It seems that I really have to hunt for subjects to photograph right now.

File.Name: i216b_i216-007.jpg
Notes: 39597603494 Here is the spiky caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly munching a leaf of native Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). I believe this is the preferred host for these caterpillars. I find the earliest caterpillars on Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) which is evergreen, but they seem to prefer the deciduous penstemon when they have a choice. This plant is just leafing out now. I also find a few caterpillars on other plants like Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). See this photo for a list of host plants. All these plants used to be considered as "scrophs" - members of the old Scrophulariaceae plant family. That family has now been split up, but there is still some affinity. It sure is hard to get a sharp photo of of these spiky caterpillars! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Bird's Beak
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: i216c_i214-001.jpg
Notes: 38499436000 I found this tiny fly (Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). Hah - it looks like its body has been placed on the wrong chassis! My first thought was that it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae. My second thought is that its proboscis is too short for that, so maybe it's a Long-legged Fly in the family Dolichopodidae. Now Thijs de Graaf suggests below that it's a Hybotid Dance Fly (Hybotidae) in the family Hybotidae. I've seen similar flies before, see this photo. All three fly families are related in the superfamily Empidoidea, and I believe they are all predators of even tinier insects. This fly really is small - less than 1/8 inch. I got the photo at 1:1 magnification, but its body isn't straight enough to measure it by counting pixels. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2018)
CommonNames: Canyon Sunflower; Dance Fly; Long-legged Fly; Hybotid Dance Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Empididae; Dolichopodidae; Hybotidae; Hybotidae; Empidoidea
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Empididae; Dolichopodidae; Hybotidae; Hybotidae; Empidoidea

File.Name: i216d_i210-003.jpg
Notes: 26436595698 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) down by the creek. You can tell from the yellow pollen in its beard that it's been snuffling around in the pollen-bearing willow catkins nearby. The fly look pretty ordinary, but I won't try to guess what kind it is. It's interesting that it's working native flowers in the woods. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2018)

File.Name: i217a_i217-036.jpg
Notes:
39617847764 This is the first new flower I've seen his year on a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. The flowers look a lot like the manzanita flowers in my [Previous] photo - both are in the same plant family, though these are puffier and more oblate. This might be my favorite tree in our mountains. Hah, see my Naked lady Madrone photo of a tree that looks like a lady. It's one of my most viewed photos, but probably just because its name pops up in so many searches! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2018)

Like yesterday, today was clear and warm - though it was breezy, and it's still chilly at night. It seems that I really have to hunt for subjects to photograph right now.

File.Name: i217b_i217-017.jpg
Notes: 39431042245 Here are photos of the two kinds of manzanita shrubs in the Ericaceae plant family that grow in our part of the Santa Ynez Mountains. (The manzanitas get more complicated as you go west towards Lompoc where there are several more species.) On the left is Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) which has just started flowering. It's named for the California botanist Alice Eastwood, not for Clint. On the right is Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) which is one of our earliest flowering shrubs, see this photo from early October. It's still flowering. All manzanita flowers look pretty much the same - but there are other differences. The shrub on the left has shaggy white hairs on the stems, while the one on the right is finely glandular. A bigger difference is that the one on the left comes from a burl and stump-sprouts, while the other one doesn't. There are more differences in the flowers and fruit. These are the first flowers I've seen this year on Eastwood Manzanita. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2018)
ScientificName: Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: i217c_i217-031.jpg
Notes: 40328400141 Here are new flowers of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family, taken with the sun behind. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, manzanita or chamise in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to the roots of their host species. You can see the anthers inside the flower tubes with the backlighting. There are only a few plants in this drought year, but this one is making up for it with exuberant flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2018)

File.Name: i217d_i217-027.jpg
Notes: 39616832954 Here are a pair of the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. The basal leaves will completely shrivel up by the time they flower, leaving a bare asparagus-like stalk a foot or two feet tall with tiny orchid flowers - see this photo of last year's flowers on a different plant. You can see last year's flower stalk by the plant on the right in this photo. These orchids are growing in a mulch of oak and manzanita leaves in a place where the sun never shines, and white balance is what it is. If you look at that mossy rock, you can appreciate how dry the woods are in this drought year. Really, I was surprised to find these orchids at all, but I guess they are growing from last year's roots, and we did get decent rain last year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2018)

File.Name: i218a_i218-063.jpg
Notes:
39638557384 I found this furry little fly on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) down by the creek today. I guess from its long proboscis that it's a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) - but that's just a guess. You can tell from the yellow pollen on its leg that it's been hanging around the pollen-bearing willow catkins nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)

We actually have a forecast for snow tonight and tomorrow morning - that is, IF we get any precipitation at all. It didn't feel like rain/snow today - it was sunny, in the 60s - but that could change. Snow or not, it's expected to be frosty cold tonight - and windy too. We under both a NWS Winter Weather Advisory and a Wind Advisory tonight. It's the wind that I don't care for.

File.Name: i218b_i218-008.jpg
Notes: 26478556588 This little fly looks threatened by the looming spider, but it's actually in a safe place. The spider is a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae), and it won't leave its web - even for the juicy fly! I'm not sure what kind of fly this is, but I've been seeing quite a few of them lately. It's quite bristly. I found this drama on a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree down by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: i218c_i218-025.jpg
Notes: 25477256387 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) lounging in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I believe from the spider's general hairiness that it is another genus Mecaphesa spider. I've seen quite a few other critters use these little boats for shelter - see this photo, this photo, this photo, and this photo. This spider looks almost relaxed, but I'll bet it isn't - after I disturbed it getting my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Mecaphesa
