File.Name: i218d_i218-044.jpg
Notes: 38539421260 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) which is just starting to flower. What struck me at the time was how dark the spider is - I was actually surprised by how brown it looks in the photo, but at least it's a dark brown. I don't know what kind of jumping spider it is - and with 119 described species in California, I don't want to guess! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)

File.Name: i219a_i219-010.jpg
Notes:
39473665555 Here are the first new flowers of native Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. The brilliant red color is mostly from petal-like bracts, but the actual flower tubes are also quite red - whereas the flower tubes of the Woolly Paintbrush (C. foliolosa in my [Previous] photo are green. Flowers and bracts are also quite glandular, even below the flowers - the leaves feel sticky, almost slimy. It's curious that the Woolly Paintbrush in my previous photos are late this year, while this one seems early. Our ongoing drought is having a real impact this year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2018)

We didn't get any rain or snow last night, but we've had chilly wind today with 40 mph gusts. It was really no day for new photos, but I went for a drive up East Camino Cielo and found new flowers growing in sheltered places. It will surely freeze tonight . . . and there won't be any frost because it's so dry!
ScientificName: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; C. foliolosa
associatedTaxa: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; C. foliolosa

File.Name: i219b_i219-011.jpg
Notes: 39473376085 This is a flower head of Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - the only plant I've found in flower in this drought year. This plant was growing at the base of a shrub of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family. and I had to coax the flower out to get a photo. It was impossible to include foliage with the flowers in this shot, so I added a separate photo of a whole plant without flowers, see my [Previous] photo. We have several different Paintbrush species in our mountains - this is the only one that is a woody sub-shrub with white-woolly leaves. If you look close, you can see the real tubular flowers, not just the brilliant red bracts that first get your attention. If you compare my [Next] photo of a different species, you can see that the flower tubes of this one are green, but those of the other one are red. It's disturbing not to find more flowers this year as this is usually one of our first native plants to flower, usually before the new year. Our ongoing drought is having a real impact this year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2018)
ScientificName: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae; Cercocarpus betuloides; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae; Cercocarpus betuloides; Rosaceae

File.Name: i219c_i219-014.jpg
Notes: 39473119615 This is what all but one of the plants of Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family look like this year - no flowers, though you can see some of last year's seed capsules. I did find one plant flowering today, see my [Next] photo. This is a small woody sub-shrub, usually just a foot or two high, and often less. My photo shows its typical habitat. It's disturbing not to find flowers this year as this is usually one of our first native plants to flower, usually before the new year. Our ongoing drought is having a real impact this year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2018)

File.Name: i219d_i219-016.jpg
Notes: 40322646012 This single chain link is fastening one padlock to another on the gate to Knapp's Castle and the Snyder trail in the Santa Barbara mountains. Hah - if you look close, you can see me getting the photo with my tripod on the shackle of the lock on the right. That sunburst just happened. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Fasteners". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2018)

File.Name: i220a_i220-026.jpg
Notes:
40347406422 Well I'll be darned . . . I noticed this large White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata, Sphingidae) moth hanging on to dead stems under a plant of native Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). It was a cold day, and it didn't want to move. I think of these moths as being active in mid-summer, but I checked my records and I've photographed them in every month from March to July - though mostly in July. I've seen the caterpillars from March to May. I was surprised to find this one in February - is it an overwintering adult or is it just early? (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2018)

Today was sunny but cold - in the 20s last night and only in the 40s today. This will be another chilly night. On the other hand, the chill is probably the only reason I found this wonderful moth today!
CommonNames: White-Lined Sphinx Moth; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i220b_i220-004.jpg
Notes: 40392030071 This is a tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a new leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae), the first I've seen this year. It's not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It might be the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), see this photo. This one really is tiny - just 840 pixels long in his 1:1 macro shot, , which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's antennae are much longer than the nymph is. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2018)
CommonNames: Katydid; Canyon Sunflower; California Angle-winged Katydid

File.Name: i220c_i218-058.jpg
Notes: 25519719947 This is a new colony of aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on new growth of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). Of course I will call them Mugwort Aphids, but I have no idea if they only occur on Mugwort. They do seem distinctive with those longer-than-usual antennae. I didn't notice any ants tending these aphids. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)

File.Name: i220d_i220-015.jpg
Notes: 40346525202 This is another tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that has been mined and killed by one of the web-spinning caterpillars that I've been showing in the oak trees, see this photo. The webbing in this photo is made by the caterpillar, not the spider. It's about 1,000 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.9 mm, a bit more than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's hard to see, but this one might be a male with enlarged palps. What handsome gold colors it has. I don't know what kind of jumping spider it is - and with 119 described species in California, I don't want to guess. It doesn't look as dark, but could this be the same kind of spider as this photo? (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2018)

File.Name: i221a_i221-016.jpg
Notes:
39515091135 Here are new flowers of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing up here in the mountains. The individual flowers are intricate, with a blue or yellow nectar disk at the base. In a good year, the shrubs cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains with flowers, like snow - see this photo from a few years ago, gazillions of individual flowers. I don't expect that in this drought year, but it's nice to see any new flowers. We have several different white-flowered Ceanothus species that have similar white flowers, but the foliage is different. This one is unique in having (mostly) alternate leaves and large seed capsules - but see this photo. I'm still not finding many insects on the flowers, but that's how it is this year. Maybe that's a tiny Psyllid insect below the flowers? See this photo. I'll make a note. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2018)

Today was sunny but cold again - in the 20s last night, though we did get into the 50s today. It was breezy today, and chilly winds are expected for the rest of the week. This morning there was a 20% chance of a shower tomorrow, but that has disappeared from the forecast tonight. Cold and wind - bah!

It's curious . . . while it's cold here in California, I've just been reading that it's uncommonly warm in the arctic - see this article from the Washington Post.

File.Name: i221b_i218-001.jpg
Notes: 39514862005 This is the web of a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree down by the creek. I believe it's the web of a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes, I'll have a photo tomorrow. You can see that the web has a sheet below, with scaffolding above to support the sheet and to knock down flying insects into it. The spiders hang upside down under the web. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak; Hammock Spider

File.Name: i221c_i208-017.jpg
Notes: 40366489292 Here is the perfect web of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) - with the tiny spider hanging bottom-up in the center. The web is about the size of a CD-disk or a bit larger. The web is actually a spiral - if you look close, you can see the start of it towards the center and follow it around. You can also see the "trash line" going through the center of the web made up with the bodies of their prey. Yeah, I got this photo two weeks ago, but it's still a good photo - so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2018)

File.Name: i221d_i217-013.jpg
Notes: 40410736061 Here is a pair of tiny mating beetles on new foliage of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). I believe they are Flea Beetles in the group Alticini, Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera. If you look close at my photo, you can see that these beetles have enlarged femurs on their hind legs - yep, they can jump like fleas! They are a bit over a bit over 1/10 inch long, see this photo for the details. This shrub is a particular favorite of theirs - I noticed the damage to the leaves before I noticed the beetle. Yeah, the photo is not quite sharp, but maybe the soft focus is appropriate for the subject. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2018)
ScientificName: Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Alticini; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Alticini; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: i222a_i222-022.jpg
Notes:
40387295832 Here is a little web-spinning caterpillar out of its nest munching a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I have another recent photo of a similar caterpillar here. Here it is under a thin layer of webbing on a leaf that it appears to be skeletonizing - at least it's eaten the top layer. I've posted a number of photos of the work of these caterpillars: here, here, here, and here. I don't know what moth this caterpillar will become. How many caterpillars make obvious webbing like this?? (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2018)

It was cold and breezy today, and the chilly winds are expected to continue for the next few days. It won't bother me tomorrow anyway - I report for Superior Court jury duty early tomorrow/Friday morning. This is just the jury selection phase, so the odds are still stacked against actually serving on a jury. I've served on three juries so far. I understand that the service is a duty of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos!

File.Name: i222b_i222-035.jpg
Notes: 39534631965 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This might be the same kind of fly I show in this photo and this photo. This fly looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but they are also quite common in this place by the creek, so maybe they are just particularly bristly flies in the family Muscidae. This is also a nice shot of the fly's bristly rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2018)
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; Muscidae

File.Name: i222c_i222-010.jpg
Notes: 40430277501 Here are female (left) and male (right) Crab Spiders (Thomisidae) lounging in different boat-like seed capsules on the same Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) shrub. I believe they are genus Mecaphesa. Look at their palps - those little leg-like appendages on the sides of their faces. The male spider's palps are swollen and carry the copulatory organs. Male and female spiders were about a foot apart, the male a bit smaller. I got both of these photos today, but I have another photo of the same female in the same seed capsule from a few days ago here. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2018)

File.Name: i222d_i218-012.jpg
Notes: 26559052208 This is a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider hanging on its web in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree down by the creek. I believe it's a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. I have a photo of a similar but different web in the same tree two photos back, here. It certainly shows the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Coast Live Oak; Hammock Spider

File.Name: i223a_i222-040.jpg
Notes:
39552287535 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's another very dark spider, like the ones in this photo and this photo - maybe even darker with that black patch on its head. It's tiny, about 1300 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 5.1 mm long, less than 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I don't know what kind of jumping spider it is - and with 119 described species in California, I don't want to guess! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2018)

"We're going to need some more jurors." The judge decided this at noon today and excused us until Monday afternoon. I'm involved in a jury selection process and still waiting. I got home early, but the chilly wind was already acting up, so I stayed inside. I understand that jury duty is part of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos - but not in the chilly wind! Our NWS Wind Advisory runs out tonight, and I'm hoping tomorrow will be calm.

File.Name: i223b_i218-023.jpg
Notes: 25577091307 Here's another photo of the little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) curled up like a cat in a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I've shown this spider before - in this photo and this photo. She's a lady as shown by her slender palps - see the second link for a comparison, she's on the left. I've found lots of other critters using these little boats for shelter - see the first link for a list. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: i223c_i220-006.jpg
Notes: 39737449854 I believe this is a Hybotid Dance Fly in the family Hybotidae on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) - see Thijs de Graaf's comment to this photo. My first thought was that it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae or a Long-legged Fly in the family Dolichopodidae. It turns out that all three fly families are related in the same superfamily Empidoidea, and I believe they are all predators of even tinier insects. This fly really is small, about 700 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.7 mm long, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) These flies run around a lot, and it took some patience to get a photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2018)

File.Name: i223d_i218-004.jpg
Notes: 40404070332 This is another shot of "just a fly" (Diptera) balancing on the edge of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks a little like the fly I've been showing, e.g. two photos back in this photo - but looking closer, I think it's different. It is just as bristly, but this one is smaller and its face is different. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2018)

File.Name: i224a_i224-016.jpg
Notes:
39757295324 This is another fly in the woods that is definitely not "just a fly" - what pretty colors it has. The fly is sparsely on its abdomen, though it's hard to see in this photo. One possibility is that it's an atypical Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) like the species Euclytia flava - see BugGuide. I really don't know, but what a handsome fly it is. I found it perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - a moment later it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2018)

It takes a commitment like work or jury duty to appreciate days off! It's not "Fly Day Saturday" - but I mostly found flies in the woods today. It was warmer - mostly because the chilly winds died down. There was still a breeze, but not the gale-force winds we had yesterday. We do have a chance of rain on Monday. There's a rumor of real rain later in the week.
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Euclytia flava; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

Notes: 40423731102 I don't know what kind of fly this is, but it's definitely not "just a fly". It has patterned wings and remarkable orange eyes. It's small, about 1300 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.1 mm long, less than 1/4 inch. I found it perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - a moment later it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2018)

File.Name: i224c_i224-010.jpg
Notes: 25596238157 This is another photo of the "just a fly" (Diptera) I've been showing that might be a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) - see this photo and Thijs de Graaf's comment. This time the fly is on a narrow leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I like photographing these flies - they let me get close, and they have no much structural detail. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2018)

File.Name: i224d_i224-026.jpg
Notes: 39571284175 This is another shot of "just a fly" (Diptera) putting its feet up on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. That yellow pollen on its feet suggests that it's been visiting the pollen-bearing willow catkins nearby. The fly look pretty ordinary, maybe a Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae, though it is bristly and has no sign of iridescent colors. It's interesting that it's been visiting native flowers in the woods, but I've seen this before - e.g. in this photo on wild cherry. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2018)

File.Name: i225a_i225-044.jpg
Notes:
25617496937 This is a handsome male Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched on a nearly dead leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). For some reason, the butterfly liked this spot. I saw him there, moved my chair over, and got the photo. Later in the day, I found the butterfly in my [Previous] photo trying to warm up in a nearby shrub. This butterfly was actively chasing other butterflies - including what I'm pretty sure was a female. (The ladies have a slightly different pattern of orange and black on their wings that's actually more elaborate.) I found these butterflies in a place where their host plant grows - native Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. This is the first of the new butterflies that I've managed to photograph his year. I saw one yesterday and went back to the same place to get this photo today. I wish it always worked like that! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

If I had my druthers, I'd go back to this spot tomorrow to get a photo of the female. *Sigh* I have to report for jury selection again. Oh well, here's a chance of rain on Monday anyway - even a chance of snow tomorrow night when we're under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory. There's still a rumor of real rain later in the week!
CommonNames: Sonoran Blue; Mugwort; Southern California Dudleya
ScientificName: Philotes sonorensis; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae
associatedTaxa: Philotes sonorensis; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae

File.Name: i225b_i225-046.jpg
Notes:
40487913031 I was confused by this Blue (Polyommatinae) butterfly until it opened its wings . . . and I realized instantly that it's a male Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis), see my [Next] photo of a different specimen that I also found today. This one was parked with its wings facing the sun like little solar collectors, trying to warm up on a chilly day. It's curious that it's resting on a dead twig of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) that's decorated with webbing and the shed skin of a caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. I saw the first of these butterflies yesterday and went back to the same place to get this photo today. I wish it always worked like that! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

This is the first of this year's butterflies that I've managed to photograph. I've seen a few more - Pacific Azure, Western Tiger Swallowtail, Pale Swallowtail, California Sister, and Umber Skipper - but I've only seen one or a few of each, always in passing. Hah - if I see ten butterflies, I might get a photo of one of them. If I only see one butterfly, the odds are against me. I'll keep trying!
CommonNames: Blue; Sonoran Blue; Climbing Penstemon; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Philotes sonorensis; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Polyommatinae; Philotes sonorensis; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: i225c_i225-005.jpg
Notes: 39592034835 I found this Moth (Lepidoptera) today on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm not sure if the moth is nocturnal or diurnal - though it did fly off after I pestered it some. It's a handsome moth with markings like a fine rug. I have no idea what kind of moth it is. You can also see the "stellate hairs" of the oak leaf - like little stars. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

File.Name: i225d_i225-026.jpg
Notes: 39777352164 I found this dragonfly in the woods today - hunting and not just shivering in the cold like I usually find them in winter. I believe from the yellow racing stripes that this is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. It's not the best photo - overexposed and cropped too tight - but this is the first of these hunters that I've seen in the new year, so here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

File.Name: i226a_i226-002.jpg
Notes:
40466231542 Here is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) resting for a moment on a leaf of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae). Maybe it's the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be the common species in central California, see BugGuide. I believe this is a fly that I usually find hovering "motionless" in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2018)

I didn't have to report for jury duty until afternoon today, so I had some time in the garden this morning. It was breezy, but I got this one photo before I had to think about leaving. There was a bit of rain as I came home! My impression is that this will be another moisture-starved storm bringing more wind than rain tonight - even a chance of snow as we're under a NWS Winter Weather Advisory. There's still a rumor of real rain later in the week. My next jury day is Wednesday - a full day this time.

This is another fly that looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. That little bit of gold on its back suggests that it's been visiting the pollen-bearing willow catkins nearby. (They are almost done but not quite.) (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

Notes: 39798175334 This is a small fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) down by the creek. This is another fly that looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. This fly is noticeably smaller than the other bristly flies I've been showing lately, including my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

File.Name: i226d_i224-038.jpg
Notes: 40501192691 I found this tiny fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. My first thought was it is a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) because of that stout proboscis and humped back - maybe genus Geron, though it doesn't seem as furry as the photos at BugGuide. But looking closer, I see that its proboscis sticks out straight and is flattened side to side - which suggests it is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, and I think that's the better guess. It's small, about 1170 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.6 mm long, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Less Than An Inch" - and it certainly does qualify. The entire photo as cropped is 2,356 pixels across, which comes to about 3/8 inch. Most of my photos would probably satisfy this condition. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2018)

File.Name: i227a_i227-004.jpg
Notes:
26656949288 This pretty moth is diurnal - active in daytime - but it was grounded by the frosty weather this morning. I found it on a leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae). I'm pretty sure this moth is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. It does have a snout! They also have brilliant red "underwings" that make them easy to spot in the air, but you can just see a hint of them here as red patches on either side of the rear of its abdomen. This moth is quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. I've been trying all winter to get a photo of one, but it took a frosty morning and the good fortune to notice it. I have another photo that shows more of the underwings. If you look close below its wing, you can see a little forest of stalked glands on the sage leaf that contain the pleasant odor of these leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2018)

We did get a bit of rain last night, but only 6/100 inches - just enough to bring the liverworts back from the shadows again - but only for a couple of hours, see this photo. It was cold again today, and at last we had enough moisture to see the frost! We do have a 100% chance of rain on Thursday/Friday, with up to three inches expected in the mountains. That's enough that there will be evacuation orders below the burn areas. I have jury duty on Wednesday and Friday - if I can drive down the mountain in the rain!
ScientificName: Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: i227b_i227-018.jpg
Notes: 38717578520 I'm pretty sure this is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the genus Mecaphesa. The yellow color is unusual but not unheard of - see this photo. I believe these spiders are color-shifters, but it's interesting that I found this one on leaves of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) with nothing yellow in sight. Go figure. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2018)

File.Name: i227c_i225-001.jpg
Notes: 40527247431 Here are two spiders that are each carrying a passenger! The spiders are a Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on the left and a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the right. The passenger in each case is in the space between the abdomen and the cephalothorax. I wouldn't be surprised to find mites on a spider, but this looks more like a six-legged insect. Could it be a louse? (Do spiders even get lice??) Those were my first thoughts, but I just now found this photo at BugGuide. Could my spider be the larva of a mantidfly in the family Mantispidae of the Neuroptera? I have found an adult mantidfly here, so I know they're around - see this photo. Any info about this will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

File.Name: i227d_i227-019.jpg
Notes: 39816685814 Here are two shots of the same patch of liverworts (Marchantiophyta) taken just one hour apart as the plants go from green to dormant on a sandstone outcrop. I'm pretty sure they are the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla). They were completely dried out yesterday, but we had a bit of rain last night - not much, just 6/100 inch - but that was enough to revive the plants. That's the photo on the left, taken at 11:02 this morning. I went on with my walk and came back an hour later to get the photo on the right, at 12:07. That hour was enough time for the liverworts to revert to their desiccated state. With better rain, the sandstone rock would hold enough moisture to support the liverworts for weeks - but not in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2018)

File.Name: i228a_i225-057.jpg
Notes:
25675586957 This is the moment when I realized that the butterfly in this photo was in fact a male Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly. The butterfly was sitting with wings closed facing the sun like a living solar collector, trying to warm up on a chilly day. At first I was confused, but all was made clear when it opened his wings. It's curious that it's resting on a dead twig of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) that's decorated with the shed skins of caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

Just two photo today - I had jury selection all day, and I'm tired. I've been advanced to the jury box, but there's still a good chance that I'll be excused as the attorneys exercise their right of peremptory challenge to reject potential jurors without stating a reason. Our rain forecast might complicate things with several inches of rain expected on Thursday night - with my next jury day on Friday morning. We'll see what happens. I'm free to get photos tomorrow/Thursday - IF I can get outside without getting soaked.

File.Name: i228b_i225-062.jpg
Notes: 39650917075 Here is webbing and a neat "web bridge" between two partly-skeletonized leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Note the frass. This is the webbing of the web-spinning caterpillar which I show sprawled across the same little web-bridge in this photo - which you really should see to appreciate my photo here. Try flipping between the photos. I'm intrigued by these webs that are not made by spiders, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

File.Name: i301a_i301-022.jpg
Notes:
39670686905 Here are old and new flowers on a branch of Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. This shrub is distinguished by its non-glandular, mostly hairless flowers and foliage. For some reason we don't have this one in our canyon at home - it is replaced by the glandular Bitter Gooseberry (R. amarum), see this photo. Many of these shrubs have stopped flowering now, but others are just getting started. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 March 2018)

Some storms creep in on little cat feet, other storms barge in like a moose in a bar! This has been a moose storm so far - lots of wind and bluster from the south, but only light rain so far. It was a pretty crummy day for new photos, but I went for a drive up East Camino Cielo and got a few. The real rain is expected after midnight tonight, maybe two or three inches. Places below the wildfire burns are under mandatory evacuations - I'm guessing people will comply this time, after the debris flows from the last real storm in January. There's already talk of closing Highway 101. I'm hoping the court calls to cancel my jury duty tomorrow morning.
CommonNames: Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Bitter Gooseberry

File.Name: i301b_i301-016.jpg
Notes: 38755342020 Here are the first new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You can make out all the key characters in this photo. The leaves are opposite and toothed, white-tomentose underneath, with persistent corky stipules at the base of the leaves that look like little chocolate kisses, and white flowers that come from woolly-looking bracts. We have several different white-flowered Ceanothus species that cover the mountains, at least in a wetter year - Ceanothus megacarpus along the south-facing slopes, Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing slopes, and this Ceanothus crassifolius on the summits. They all have white flowers that look similar, but the foliage is different. I found the first flowers last year on March 4, so they're right on schedule - despite the drought this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2018)

File.Name: i301d_i225-061.jpg
Notes: 39670120225 I got this beautiful blue butterfly shot of a male Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly a moment after the shot in my [Previous] photo, here. It's also a pretty good shot of the butterfly's behind, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

File.Name: i302a_i224-022.jpg
Notes:
39873607224 This is another bristly fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. This is another fly that looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) - e.g. here, here, here, and here. I've been finding lots of these bristly flies lately! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2018)

Just two photo today - I had jury selection all day again, and I'm weary. I'm still in the jury box , but there's still a chance that I'll be excused as the attorneys exercise their right of peremptory challenge to reject potential jurors without stating a reason. I guess I'll know one way or the other on Monday.

We had good rain last night with 3-1/2 inches up here on San Marcos Pass! That's enough rain to cause problems, but it all seemed to soak into the ground with no flooding or mudslides, even below the wildfire areas that were devastated in January. Good rain, but we're still at just 8.4 inches of rain for the water year - just 32% of normal. The rain will help the trees and shrubs, but this is still on track to be the driest year ever with the record being 10.90 inches in 2006-7. Our normal rainfall is 34 inches. We need another "March Miracle" storm to make up the difference. In the meantime, it will be a treat to get out in the soggy woods with my camera tomorrow!

File.Name: i302b_i225-024.jpg
Notes: 26712278988 This is a small fly (Diptera) on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It kept running around in little circles. This is another fly that looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but it's smaller than the other candidates I've been showing lately - e.g. here, here, here, and here. I've been finding lots of these bristly flies lately! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2018)

File.Name: i303a_i303-015.jpg
Notes:
26731732098 We're having banana slug weather! I found this hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the woods today. There seems to be some confusion about their proper scientific name, but the Oregon Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) look quite different, with greenish colors and a more pronounced ridge on their back, compare this photo. I actually found two of these today - this large one and another smaller one that's only the size of a crayon. I really consider it a miracle every time I find one after a long drought. Somehow these slugs manage to survive, and I sure don't know how. I regard them as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2018)

Today was overcast with enough showers to bring our rain total up to 4.1 inches for the storm - as much as we got in the January rains that caused debris flows that devastated Monteceito, but this time it all soaked in. Good rain, but we're still at just 9.0 inches of rain for the water year - just 33% of normal, and still less than the driest year on record with 10.9 inches in 2006-7. Our normal rainfall is 34 inches. We need another "March Miracle" storm to make up the difference to get us out of last place, but it was still a pleasure to be out in the drizzle today!

File.Name: i303b_i303-022.jpg
Notes: 39708159705 This is a tiny spider on an old seedhead of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). I'm pretty sure that the spider is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae), though it's not in its usual retreat - see this photo and compare the markings on the spider's back. I did find the remains of a retreat a few inches away, so maybe the spider was displaced by yesterday's rain? (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2018)

File.Name: i303c_i303-034.jpg
Notes: 25732531417 This little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) has found a place out of the rain today under a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). Maybe it's the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be the common species in central California, see BugGuide. It kept trying to lean over to the side of the stem away from me, but it didn't fly off when I nudged it back. I guess it knows it has a good spot and doesn't want to leave! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2018)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Syrphus opinator
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Syrphus opinator

File.Name: i303d_i303-002.jpg
Notes: 25732362477 Here is the cute little nymph of a Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a cactus spine in the garden. It looks like it might have wings, but I believe it's still a late-instar nymph because of those white hairs coming from its tail - which makes it look jet propelled! I believe this is the nymph which becomes the adult in this photo which I find in the spring - genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. It's tiny, about 850 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) What a face! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2018)

File.Name: i304a_i304-029.jpg
Notes:
39729636455 Here are new flowers on a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see a couple of tiny thrips (order Thysanoptera) insects standing in the mouth of the open flower on the left. I believe these are Predatory Thrips in the genus Aeolothrips of the family Aeolothripidae, see this photo for lots of them. I usually find these thrips on similar manzanita flowers, but I've seen very few this year - I'm happy to find them here. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2018)

It was sunny and calm today with moments of overcast and breeze - a nice day, though I prefer the soft light that we had with the overcast yesterday. Tomorrow will be another day in the jury box, and maybe I'll find out if I actually on the jury or not. I understand that jury duty is part of citizenship, but I'd rather be out getting new photos!
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: i304b_i304-023.jpg
Notes: 25754417697 Here are new mushrooms in the woods after our good rain on Thursday and Friday. I believe they are the mushroom formally known as the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila), now more properly called Gymnopus dryophilus - though I suspect the common name will stick. This mushroom comes up early; under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae); in groups; has white spores; and copious white mycelium with root-like rhizomorphs at the base, visible at the base of the mushroom I've picked. I'm not sure if we'll get more mushrooms, even if we get more rain - it may be that there time is done, see my comment to this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2018)
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Gymnopus dryophilus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i304c_i304-041.jpg
Notes: 39728990945 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a leaf of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae). It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spiders (photo here) that I find in the fall, though it's much smaller. This spider really is tiny, about 940 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.7 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's comparable to the spider in my [Previous] photo which is another orb weaver that lacks the "humps" on it's back. I believe this one is Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis. In a comment to this photo, Alice Abelia says to look at "the way the abdomen dips between the two humps" - but I can't really see if there's a dip or not. Looking at its palps, maybe this one is male? (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2018)

File.Name: i304d_i304-042.jpg
Notes: 39728582265 This is another shot taken today of the spider I show two photos back, here. I'm pretty sure it's a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). These are orb-weaving spiders that make a proper orb web and then build a little retreat off to the side - see this photo and this photo. This one's orb web was completely washed away and its retreat was damaged by our good rain two nights ago - now it's a displaced creature. When I found it yesterday, it was a few inches away on a seedhead of the same plant of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) where it's nest is. Sort of like Trashline Orb Weaver spiders, they decorate their retreat with the bodies of their prey, and you can see some assorted insect body parts off to the side. This spider is tiny, about 1050 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.1 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) That's a seed of the composite plant behind the spider. I actually used my on-camera flash for this photo because of the contrasty sunlight and breeze today. I don't like using flash, and I prefer yesterday's photo taken without flash on a cloudy day - but sometimes you don't have a choice. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2018)

File.Name: i305a_i303-045.jpg
Notes:
40644896021 This is another shot of the same hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) that I showed two days ago in this photo. It's eating something, probably a fallen Toyon berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae). It's gumming it, but if you look close at its head you can see its actual "slug teeth" - see this photo for a closer look. I went back looking for these slugs on the next day, and didn't see them. That's just two days this season then I've found them - the other day is here. Somehow the slugs manage to survive, but two days a year doesn't seem like much of a life. By the way, the green seedlings in this photo are almost all weedy annuals - e.g. the parsley-like leaf by the slug's rear end is the nasty Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis, Apiaceae) whose sticky seeds ruin your socks later in summer. That's something that happens in a drought year like this - native annuals hardly appear, but weedy annuals do make seeds and spread. This extended California drought might permanently change the plant makeup our natural areas. It's always interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2018)

No photo for the Macro Mondays group this week with the theme of "Citrus". I thought about native Turpentine Broom (Thamnosma montana) in the same Rutaceae plant family as citrus - see this old photo (at Fotolog). That's in the right family, but it doesn't grow around here anyway. Then I thought about all the dried up orange peels I've found along trails that could make a nice photo with a message - don't drop your darn orange peels, they don't decompose! - but I didn't feel like looking for one. Then I thought of the lime in a G&T, but that's too much like product photography - not for me, and better to drink one!

Just two photo again today - I had jury selection all day, and I'm weary. I'm still in the jury box, and I think they're getting close to an acceptable jury. They need more jurors again. I believe I'll know one way or the other on Wednesday.
Locality: at Fotolog).  That's in the right family
CommonNames: California Banana Slug; Toyon; Hedge Parsley; Turpentine Broom

File.Name: i305b_i303-017.jpg
Notes: 26773148248 This is the empty shell of a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda), no doubt a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. I found several of these empty shells after the rain on Friday/Saturday. Snails don't shed their shells, and I really can't think why I would find more shells after the rain - except that I was looking for live snails and maybe just noticed these empty shells that were always there. This is NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails that are so common in the lowlands. These are creatures of the winter rainy months, and I do find living snails in the mountains now and then, see this photo. They do have predators, including the the Snail Eater Beetle (Scaphinotus, Carabidae, Coleoptera) in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2018)
CommonNames: Land Snail; Shoulderband Snail; Garden Snails; Snail Eater Beetle
ScientificName: Gastropoda; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae; Scaphinotus; Carabidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Gastropoda; Helminthoglypta; Helminthoglytidae; Scaphinotus; Carabidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: i306a_i306-039.jpg
Notes:
25793559137 Here is a gorgeous female Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched for just a moment on a half-dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). It's interesting that I found a male on a similar half-dead stem in this photo last week. They seem to like mugwort, though their host plant is the native Dudleya that grows nearby. This is still the first of the new butterflies that I've managed to photograph his year, now both male and female. It's interesting that the females are more colorful than the males, which reverses the usual pattern with ducks and most other butterflies that the males are more brightly colored than the females. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2018)

Lawyers and doctors, oh my! I only have jury duty on MWF, but I had a doctor appointment this Tuesday morning. Everything's fine, but I used up my morning photo time. Fortunately, I don't have to go in for jury duty until after noon tomorrow. They need more jurors again. I believe I'll finally know whether I'm on the jury by this time tomorrow. There is a rumor of more rain this weekend, but the details are still fuzzy.
ScientificName: Philotes sonorensis; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Dudleya
associatedTaxa: Philotes sonorensis; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Dudleya

File.Name: i306b_i306-013.jpg
Notes: 40621872802 Here is another colony of large aphids on a stem of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek. I believe these are Giant Willow Aphids - species Tuberolachnus salignus, Lachninae, Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. See this photo for more info. Hah - the tubercle on the aphid's back looks like a satellite radio antenna! You can see an out-of-focus ant in the background - looking out for its herd. I've been seeing the aphids on this willow for a couple of months. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2018)
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Tuberolachnus salignus; Lachninae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: i306c_i306-017.jpg
Notes: 38853498150 You can tell two things from this tiny spider photo right off - first, the spider's swollen palps show that it's a male; and second, that out-of-focus bump on the end of the spider's abdomen shows that it's a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider. I first noticed this spider when it ballooned out of nowhere onto my camera lens! Then it ballooned away to this dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) and stayed for a few minutes. The spider was moving almost constantly, hard to photograph. I assume it's looking for a mate? This male spider seems darker than the usual trashline spiders I find, like this one, but I've seen them before - see this photo from two years ago. I'm not sure if they're a different species. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2018)

File.Name: i306d_i306-036.jpg
Notes: 39768391595 Here is new growth on a stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) - with one of the empty boat-like seed capsules that many critters like to lounge in, see my comment on this photo. The stem is swollen beyond normal, and I believe this is the work of a Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaci) in the family Cecidomyiidae, Diptera. It's interesting how the leaves appear different on the galled and ungalled stems, with the leaves from galled stems slightly more ornate with extra lobes. I can tell at a glance which stems are galled. This is more obvious in this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2018)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Neolasioptera diplaci; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Neolasioptera diplaci; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: i307a_i307-001.jpg
Notes:
39972707194 I guess tick season is upon us - I found not one but two tiny ticks (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) in the woods today. Isn't it curious how they fold their legs. The one on the left in on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae), and the one on the right is on a native grass stem, probably Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta, Poaceae). I believe these are both Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus), which is said to be the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast. (What, me worry?) These ticks are small little devils, about 850 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I haven't been ticked yet this year, but I suspect it won't be long. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2018)

Just two photos today. My jury duty didn't start until after lunch, so I took a short version of my usual walk this morning and got a few photos. *Sigh* Our jury has now been accepted, so I'm committed for the duration - the rest of the month for sure. There's still talk of more rain this weekend!
CommonNames: ticks; Canyon Sunflower; Small-flowered Melic; Western Black-legged Ticks

File.Name: i307b_i307-009.jpg
Notes: 39972511534 They're hard to see, but here are three neat orb webs - all made by the same tiny spider that I show in this photo, which I'm pretty sure is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). I found these webs in a dead plant of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). These orb-weaving spiders make a proper orb web(s) and then build a little retreat off to the side - see this photo and this photo. This one's orb web was completely washed away and its retreat was damaged by our good rain last weekend - now it's a displaced creature, but it's coming back! I'll post another photo of the spider in its retreat tomorrow. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2018)

File.Name: i308a_i308-036.jpg
Notes:
39805865425 Crash landing! I believe this is a winged ant that has crashed into a very spiny cactus plant in the garden. I can't see if the ant is actually skewered, but I didn't look too close. The ant looks quite dead - but that seems to be what these winged ants do. Be careful where you land! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2018)

Our jury has now been accepted, so I'm committed for the duration - the rest of the month for sure. I have jury duty on MWF, but that still leaves four days out of each week for new photos - could be worse. There's still talk of more rain this weekend, and maybe a stronger storm late next week. This might be enough to bring us out of last place in yearly rainfall. (We stand at 9.00 inches; the driest year on record is 2006-7 with 10.90 inches. Our normal rainfall is 34 inches.)

File.Name: i308b_i308-029.jpg
Notes: 39990666264 This small spider has captured wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera). Maybe the bee is genus Halictus? I found it spinning slowly around and around, hanging from a thread than comes from a small orb web up above. The web was in a flowering shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae), which no doubt attracted the bee. I'm pretty sure the spider is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae), Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis - see this photo for more info. These spiders have a pair of humps on their back , and one of the humps is visible as the point on the bottom. It's a nice shot of the spider's spinnerets - in fact, I reckon this counts as a double butt shot of both bee and spider - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Eastwood Manzanita; Orb-Weaver Spider
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Halictus; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Araneus; Araneidae; Araneus bispinosus; Araneus montereyensis

File.Name: i308c_i306-019.jpg
Notes: 39990486014 This is another shot of same male Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider that I showed two days ago in this photo - this time showing the "butt end" of the spider. This was a "free range spider" not associated with a web, and I reckon it was on the prowl for a mate. I found it on a dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). The spider was moving almost constantly, hard to photograph. This one seems darker than normal, but they always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2018)

File.Name: i308d_i307-015.jpg
Notes: 40657755012 Can you see the spider? Look to the left of center, towards the top. This is the Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) that I've been showing lately - the spider in this photo and its multiple webs in this photo. In this shot, the spider is in what's left of its retreat after it was damaged by the rain last weekend. Sort of like Trashline spiders, they decorate their retreat with the bodies of their prey, and you can see some assorted insect body parts scattered about. If you look close, you can see something strange - its rear leg towards the top of my photo appears disconnected. I have no idea what this means. I got this photo yesterday on an overcast morning with nice even light. I tried to retake it in the sunshine today, but the contrasty light didn't work at all. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2018)

File.Name: i309a_i307-018.jpg
Notes:
38907814500 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I love those palmate "lupine leaves"! Other plants in the mountains don't even have flower buds yet, a sign of the odd water year we're having. Before our rain last week, we'd only had one real rainstorm for the season, and the native plants were hurting. The situation is a little better right now and might get better with more rain in the forecast tomorrow and next week. In the meantime, it's interesting to see how some plants are going and others aren't. These photos and comments are my field notes - though it's hard to show the plants that aren't flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2018)

My jury duty is happening on MWF - but we get next Monday off which will give me more photo time and a couple of days to get used to getting up early for Daylight Savings Time. We have a 100% chance of rain on Saturday/Sunday and maybe a stronger storm late next week.

File.Name: i309b_i304-031.jpg
Notes: 38907666770 This is "just a fly" perched on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I don't know what kind of fly it is, though I dig those crazy iridescent colors on its wing - like an oil slick without oil. I didn't find many flies this week, unlike last week when they seemed to be everywhere. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2018)

File.Name: i310a_i310-023.jpg
Notes:
40027276834 This pretty moth is diurnal - active in daytime - and here it is working new flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure this moth is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth, family Crambidae in the order Lepidoptera. It does indeed have a snout! They also have brilliant red "underwings" that make them easy to spot in the air, but you can just see a bit of them here as red patches on either side of the rear of its abdomen. This moth is quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. I overexposed it a bit in this photo, but that turned out to be just right to show its face. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 March 2018)

Light rain started at home this morning, so I decided to drive to the Santa Ynez Valley to do some errands that I had to do sometime and may as well do on a rainy day. I made my usual stop near Lake Cachuma and got a few photos before it started raining in earnest. It's nice to see some new flowers in the woods, even though they're late - like the rains have been this year. As I write this early on Saturday evening, we've had about 1/2 inch of steady light rain - an inch or two total is expected. There's another storm in the forecast for Thursday/Friday that may be more impressive - and more dangerous for the burn areas.
ScientificName: Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: i310b_i310-019.jpg
Notes: 40027097314 This is looking into a new flower of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family - the first open flower that I've seen this year. This is a month later than I found the first flowers last year, but it's been that kind of year. Usually the flowers point down towards the earth, but I propped this one up with a stick for a photo. They are one of my favorite wildflowers, probably long-lasting fire-followers in this place that burned about 15 years ago. They are a true native that's related to the garden plants. I've read that the perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb - I'd rather find the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 March 2018)

File.Name: i310c_i310-018.jpg
Notes: 40737184251 This are the first new flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae plant family. The stems and leaves are almost glabrous, with just a bit of "peach fuzz" on them. See this photo to compare with the species. This variety is not recognized in the Jepson manual except as a synonym for the species, but maybe it should be? It seems distinct and localized, and it is recognized in my old Munz manual. I found the first flowers on January 19 last year, so they are nearly two months late this year. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 March 2018)

File.Name: i310d_i310-011.jpg
Notes: 25865719267 There are finally new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family in the woods. I got this photo in drizzle this morning - before it turned to real rain later in the day. They are beautiful flowers, more elegant than our usual yellow "wild mustards" that are non-native. They were flowering at the end of December last year, but the rain and the flowers have been unusually late this year. I found the first flowers a week ago, but they were damaged by the rain last weekend. These are the first ones that are ready to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2018)

File.Name: i311a_i311-038.jpg
Notes:
40048248184 Another rain, another banana slug! I guess this is the same hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) that I found after our rain a week ago, see this photo and this photo. The green seedlings with lobed leaves that the slug is crawling through are mostly native Bowlesia incana in the Apiaceae plant family. There's also some non-native (but ubiquitous) Chickweed (Stellaria media, Caryophyllaceae) in there. There's something remarkable behind the slug's eyestalk on the right that appears to be a tiny snail! I'll add an insert, but it still might not be visible in the lower-quality jpeg that Flickr shows. Maybe the snail is a Pinhead Spot Snail (Paralaoma servilis)? (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2018)

We got about an inch of steady light rain last night - on the low side of the expectation but still welcome. There's a chance of rain in the forecast for every day next week after Monday, leading up to a storm on Thursday/Friday that might be more impressive - and more dangerous for the burn areas. I like the way March is going! It sounds like the next storm(s) will be enough to at least bring us out of last place in yearly rainfall. We stand at 10.05 inches, and the driest year on record is 2006-7 with 10.90 inches. Hah, our normal rainfall is 34 inches.
Locality: s) will be enough to at least bring us out of last place in yearly rainfall.   We stand at 10.05 inches

File.Name: i311b_i311-010.jpg
Notes: 25887288587 Here are a pair of spiky caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a dead-looking stem of native Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) - with raindrops from our storm last night. We got about an inch of rain, and it doesn't look like these caterpillars moved at all! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i311c_i310-007.jpg
Notes: 38947333190 This is a beautiful little yellow-stemmed mushroom, no doubt the same kind as I show in this photo from a couple of months ago. In fact, I found it on the same well-rotted fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) where I found the others. It's small, the cap is the size of a dime. I'm not sure what it is, but one possibility is the Yellow-stemmed Pluteus (Pluteus lutescens aka P. romellii), though I don't see the pink-colored spores of that species. (Maybe there is just a hint of pink spores on the far side of the stem?) Whatever it is, it's a handsome mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2018)
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pluteus lutescens; P. romellii

File.Name: i311d_i310-004.jpg
Notes: 25886860837 I found another cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) growing from a tree stump in the woods yesterday. The gills look orange-staied, but these mushrooms are definitely white-spored - you can see a thick deposit of fallen white spores on one of the lower caps. I don't know what these mushrooms are doing out now - I usually find them in autumn. Everything in the woods is a little confused right now. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2018)

File.Name: i312a_i312-026.jpg
Notes:
40068895564 This is the tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on an old leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). It's not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It's probably the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), see this photo. This one really is tiny - just 800 pixels long in his 1:1 macro shot not counting its antennae, which comes to 3.1 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Its antennae are much longer than the nymph is. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2018)

No jury duty today, so I went out looking for photos - but it was a pretty crummy day with increasing clouds and steady wind from the south. Those are our usual rain signs, and there is already some light rain as I write this. This storm seems to be growing in stature, and we're now under a NWS Flash Flood Watch for Tuesday morning when the heaviest rain is expected. The areas below the Thomas Fire burn are now under mandatory evacuation orders. We have additional storms lined up in the forecast for Wednesday and Friday nights - just what we need in this rain-starved year. There's new info at the California Weather Blog.
CommonNames: Katydid; Scrub Oak; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: i312b_i312-023.jpg
Notes: 25907024157 I found this "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) today. There are a number of these red and black bugs which I always get confused, but I believe this one is a Redcoat Seed Bug (Melanopleurus belfragei, Lygaeidae) - see the treatment at BugGuide. I usually find this species towards the end of summer, and it seems odd to find it now - but I also found it in January, see this photo. I have a list of other "red bugs" in my description for this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2018)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Melanopleurus belfragei; Lygaeidae

File.Name: i312c_i312-010.jpg
Notes: 25906712837 Here are the first flowers of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This shrub is in the same family as the Ceanothus shrubs I've been showing, but the 4-merous flowers are inconspicuous rather than showy. The plain flowers are followed by red berries, see this photo. The leaves appear to have glands on the teeth. (I've noticed this on Ceanothus oliganthus in the same family - see this photo.) It's curious that I found the first flowers on March 13 last year - almost the same day. Native annuals and perennials are running late this year because of the late rains, but the trees and shrubs are just about normal. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2018)
ScientificName: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus oliganthus
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus oliganthus

File.Name: i312d_i312-034.jpg
Notes: 40777568061 Here are several tiers of of flowers of Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family doing what they do best - hanging along horizontal branches to guard their nectar against any critters except hummingbirds. I've been seeing these flowers for some time (see this photo), but they are really just coming into their own now that we've finally had some rain. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2018)

File.Name: i313a_i313-002.jpg
Notes:
40088255804 Here is a tiny spider lying hanging upside-down from its web in the cactus patch of our garden this morning. I believe it's a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider - maybe a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis), though I didn't pay enough attention to its web. (Well, it was raining at the time!) This spider is small - just 855 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The raindrops are a nice bonus! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2018)

Instead of the hard rain that was expected (and feared) today, we had soft rain - lots of light rain that never stopped today. We've had about 3/4 inch so far, and that's a lot of drizzle! This brings our rain total up to 10.86 inches for the rain year - which I'll call a dead tie with the 10.90 inches of the driest year on record. It's still coming down tonight, so our last place status will cartainly fall with additional rain in the forecast. I can remember years that started wet but ended dry, but this is the odd year that starts dry and ends wet. I should have jury duty tomorrow/Wednesday, but it's been postponed until Friday. I'm glad - I'm looking forward to getting out in the soggy woods tomorrow!
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Bowl and Doily Spider

File.Name: i313b_i313-019.jpg
Notes: 25926035097 Well I'll be darned - I found a Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus) mushroom in the woods today during my walk in the light rain today! Here it is as I found it, hidden under the oak leaves, in the same place I found this one last year. (Always remember where you find them - they will be back!) These are large mushrooms, the size of your open hand, with blunt ridges instead of proper gills. I didn't pick this one - partly because it's soggy and will be better if it dries out for a day or two, but also because I don't like picking the only one of anything I find. I'm still happy to find this mushroom - for the table of course, if I collect it, but also because it's a sign that the fungus mycelium has survived six years of drought in the ground as mycorrhiza on the roots of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The green seedlings are mostly non-native (but ubiquitous) Chickweed (Stellaria media, Caryophyllaceae). The mushrooms are edible and delicious, a local favorite. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2018)
ScientificName: Cantharellus californicus; mycelium; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Stellaria media; Caryophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Cantharellus californicus; mycelium; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Stellaria media; Caryophyllaceae

File.Name: i313c_i313-009.jpg
Notes: 40797294741 There are new mushrooms in the woods! It's interesting that these are mushrooms that I usually find coming up with our first rains in autumn - but here they are in (almost) spring. They are all growing in the woodland mulch under mostly Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods near home with raindrops from the late-season storm today. I'm pretty sure the mushroom on the left is Mycena californiensis with a stem that "bleeds" when broken - see this photo and this photo. I'm sure the mushroom on the right is another Mycena, but it looks different. The one in the center is an odd one - a toothpick-thin Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) fungus. It seems odd to find just one, but I'll bet I find a forest of them tomorrow - all of these are fast-growing mushrooms, see this photo for a group shot. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2018)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mycena californiensis; Mycena; Macrotyphula juncea
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mycena californiensis; Mycena; Macrotyphula juncea

File.Name: i313d_i313-012.jpg
Notes: 40797063011 This is a vine of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) with new growth climbing over a small Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree, with the dark woodland of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees behind. I got this photo from our covered porch in the rain today - maybe you can see some raindrops in the dark area on the right. I've shown this scene before when I needed a rain photo, but never at this time of year - see my photos here, here, and here. The tree somehow puts up with being smothered. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2018)
CommonNames: Chaparral Clematis; Madrone; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: i314a_i314-038.jpg
Notes:
25944988427 This is a bee-size and bee-like Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably in the genus Abia. One sign that it's a wasp and not a bee is that it has no hairs on its legs. It also has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that. This one is on a narrow leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) - it's cold and wet and wasn't moving much. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2018)

We did get more rain last night, bringing our total for the storm to 1.07 inch and our season total to 11.12 inches -surpassing the driest year on record (2006-7) with 10.90 inches. The next driest year is 2013-14 with 12.05 inches. There is more rain in the forecast this week and next. I did find a few more mushrooms in the woods today, but just a few. I suspect their time is past - despite the rain.
Locality: 2006-7) with 10.90 inches.  The next driest year is 2013-14 with 12.05 inches.  There is more rain in the forecast this week and next.  I did find a few more mushrooms in the woods today

File.Name: i314b_i314-011.jpg
Notes: 39920900225 This is a gorgeous little spider on a dead seedhead of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). If you know the plant, you can see how tiny this spider is - the whole disk of the flower is about the size of a dime. The spider is tiny - just 925 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.6 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I only noticed it because I've been automatically checking the seedheads after finding this spider on a different plant - but this one has different markings from that Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira). This one does look like another Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae), though it lacks the "humps" of our usual tiny species Araneus bispinosus and Araneus montereyensis - see this photo. I don't know what it is, but it sure is a handsome spider. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2018)

File.Name: i314c_i314-024.jpg
Notes: 39920598715 Here are tiny raindrops on a spider web after light rain last night - with a spider! I'm pretty sure this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider sitting in the funnel opening to its web. The spider sits in the mouth of the funnel and zips in and out very quickly. I got this photo this morning, after light rain and drizzle last night - just right for decorating these webs. The small drops on the web with scattered larger drops seems to be distinctive - and different from the Sheetweb Weaver (Linyphiidae) web in my [Previous] photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2018)

File.Name: i314d_i311-010.jpg
Notes: 25944159987 Here is a network of raindrops on a spider web after light rain a few days ago. I believe this is the web of a Sheetweb Weaver (Linyphiidae). It takes the right kind of rain to decorate a web like this - if it rains too hard, the drops will all fall away. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2018)

File.Name: i315a_i315-031.jpg
Notes:
39938814475 Here are two shots of tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera ) insects on Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. They really are tiny - the one on the left is about 615 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's interesting that the one on the right is by an odd galled leaf on the shrub. I'm don't know if there's a connection, but it's interesting that the gall is not described Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I haven't been able to find any information connecting psyllids with this shrub, but there are species in several genera that occur on genus Ceanothus in the same Rhamnaceae plant family - Ceanothia, Nyctiphalerus, Euglyptoneura, Pexopsylla, and Cacopsylla. I have found psyllids on Ceanothus megacarpus, see this photo. Maybe they are the same species? (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2018)

We actually got another 6/10 inch of rain last night; and we have a 100% chance of more rain tomorrow that could bring another inch; and there is more rain in the forecast for next week. These March rains are definitely helping bring the woods back to life. Maybe it's not a "March Miracle" like we had in 1991 with 21.56 inches(!), but meteorologist David Swain says at the California Weather Blog that it's more of a "March Mitigation" - rains that help mitigate the lack of rain in the early part of the rain year. It's still one of our driest years - but maybe not a disaster.

File.Name: i315b_i315-013.jpg
Notes: 39938692405 This is the larva of a Trirhabda beetle on a furry leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. This is the grub that becomes the adult in this photo. They are a wonderful iridescent green/blue, but I had trouble capturing the color. These critters are in the group of Skeletonizing Leaf Beetles (Galerucinae, Chrysomelidae, Coleoptera), which describes what the larvae do to the foliage. They definitely inflict some damage! I believe this one is Trirhabda eriodictyonis. The species name shows it's affinity for this shrub. Hah - the leaf looks like a sweater up close! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2018)

File.Name: i315c_i311-009.jpg
Notes: 40791164432 This is the rear end of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) - probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo for more info. It's under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), which seems to be a favorite place of theirs. This spider is tiny - about 645 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.5 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) These spiders have humps on their back which become the points in my photo. This certainly counts as a "butt shot" - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2018)
