File.Name: i315d_i314-005.jpg
Notes: 40123873694 Here is another photo of the same Cimbicid Sawfly that I show in my [Previous] photo taken earlier in the morning and still bedecked with raindrops from the storm overnight. It's alive - just cold and wet. It's perched across the same narrow leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) as that other photo, though the wasp managed to turn itself around in that photo. It's wonderful how the raindrops act like little magnifying glasses showing different bits of the wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2018)

File.Name: i316a_i315-007.jpg
Notes:
26980110928 This is a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I believe these flies cause galls on the flower buds of this plant - see this photo and this photo - and there was a flower bud nearby. This isn't the best photo as I'd rather show more of the fly - but those eyes . . . HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2018)

Just two photos today as this was a jury day - I'm tired. It didn't rain all morning, and I was beginning to think our 100% chance of rain today was a 100% chance of nothing. But it finally did start raining as I got out of my jury today. Now we're up to 4/10 inch, and it's still coming down. I like the way our March rains are going - lots of light rain that is all soaking in where it can do some good.

File.Name: i316b_i311-022.jpg
Notes: 40808589582 Here is a chubby little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It was a chilly day after an inch of rain the night before - this fly was trying to warm up and not moving much, and I managed to get close. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2018)

File.Name: i317a_i317-039.jpg
Notes:
25997349097 I believe this is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda), no doubt a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae. I found it under a board today, and turned the board over to get a photo. The snail took off - moving at a "snail pace" but still to fast for the low light at the time. Finally it got to the end of the board and stopped to explore . . . and I got my photo. This is a native snail and NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails that are so common in the lowlands. I often find their empty shells (like this), but I rarely find the living snails. I believe they are mostly nocturnal and stay out of sight. I happened to find this on a Saturday - so Happy Snail Saturday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2018)

We got a bit more rain from our storm last night, but our rain today never materialized. No problem. We now stand at 12.20 inches for the rain year - which beats the second driest year record of 2013-14 with 12.05 inches, bringing us further out of the hole into third place! I don't really care about records, but these late rains might save some trees. It's still one of our driest years - but maybe not the disaster it was starting to feel like. There's more rain in the forecast for next week, maybe a more serious storm. Santa Barbara has already issued a "Pre-Evacuation Advisory" for residents below the burn areas, info here.
CommonNames: Land Snail; Shoulderband Snail; Garden Snails

File.Name: i317b_i317-003.jpg
Notes: 39059261610 At first I took this for a leafhopper (Cicadellidae), but looking closer I can see that it's surely a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera). It's different from our usual planthoppers in the family Tropiduchidae, but maybe it's a Cixiid Planthopper in the family Cixiidae. It has antennae below the eyes, interesting wings, and an impressive proboscis. This one is small, about 1300 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.1 mm, about 2/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's perched on a narrow leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) - the same plant where I got this photo of a Cimbicid Sawfly. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2018)

File.Name: i317c_i317-016.jpg
Notes: 39059144420 I believe this is a cute little Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). There are many different kinds of these weevils, so I'll leave it at that. This one is small, about 800 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Needless to say, this is a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2018)

File.Name: i317d_i317-012.jpg
Notes: 39058963200 Here are two more shots of the tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) that I showed a few days ago in this photo. It's on the same dead plant of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae), though it's moved from a seedhead to the stem. It looks like a tiny genus Araneus spider, but I believe these shots show convincingly that this spider does not have the "humps" on the abdomen of our usual tiny species Araneus bispinosus and Araneus montereyensis - see this photo. It's more colorful too. The spider is tiny - just 900 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I don't know what it is, but it sure is a handsome spider. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2018)

File.Name: i318a_i318-019.jpg
Notes:
39996302885 Here is a real elfin forest of tiny Mycena mushrooms growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I'm pretty sure they are Mycena californiensis with a stem that "bleeds" when broken - see the broken mushroom on the lower-right edge of my photo, and this photo for a closer look. This was to be my photo for the Macro Mondays group with the theme of "Once Upon A Time" - but it was turned down. Maybe I could put little plastic elves in there . . . Nah! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2018)

It was trying to be sunny today, but there were increasing high clouds and an annoying wind from the south - both of which are rain signs. There is pretty high confidence for a "significant storm" later this week on Tuesday-Thursday. We seem to be in the thick of it - "The focus seems to be on Santa Barbara and Ventura Counties where the best upslope flow and the persistent AR aims for the highest amounts." The forecast now calls for 4-6 inches in he mountains - but forecasts have a way of going south as the storm approaches, so I'll wait and see. Santa Barbara has already issued a "Pre-Evacuation Advisory" for residents below the burn areas, more info here and here.
ScientificName: Mycena; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mycena californiensis
associatedTaxa: Mycena; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mycena californiensis

File.Name: i318b_i317-032.jpg
Notes: 39995881175 Here are a pair of Slippery Jack (Suillus) mushrooms growing under native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family. These have pores rather than gills, slimy caps, gland-dotted stems, and no sign of a veil. There are many different kinds - none are poisonous as far as I know, but they are soft and slimy which does not sound very appetizing. This is the only Bolete-type mushroom of any kind that I've found this year. Our rains have been so late this year that the time for mushrooms is past - despite the moisture in the woods right now. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2018)

File.Name: i318c_i314-002.jpg
Notes: 27018952538 I found this very pallid Cortinarius mushroom in the woods growing in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. It clearly has a bulbous base, which places it in the subgenus Bulbopodium. You can see the cobwebby remains of the partial veil (the cortina) on the stem, stained rusty-brown from falling spores. I believe this is Cortinarius regalis. Cortinarius mushrooms are usually yellow or lavender, at least when fresh, but this one is different. This is the only Cotinarius mushroom of any kind that I've found this year. Our rains have been so late this year that the time for mushrooms is past - despite the moisture in the woods right now. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2018)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius regalis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius regalis

File.Name: i318d_i314-026.jpg
Notes: 40847113762 This is a plain white Russula mushroom growing under (mostly) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I guess it is the Boring White Russula (Russula albidula), as author David Arora calls it - but there are other white Russulas like Russula cremoricolor just to confuse us. This one is creamy white, with a viscid cap after a rain, and a peppery taste that's not unpleasant - at least in a small nibble. One test for Russulas as a group is to try breaking the stem - if it snaps like chalk, it's a Russula. Really! This one is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). There are a couple of blades of dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) framing the mushroom. I've only found a few of them this year. That makes sense. Our rains have been so late this year that the time for mushrooms is past - despite the moisture in the woods now. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Boring White Russula; Coast Live Oak; Roundfruit Sedge

File.Name: i319a_i318-031.jpg
Notes:
39100759920 I believe this is an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa, Ericaceae). You can see the fly's long proboscis on the left, with a different curve than the legs. I believe this fly is tribe Phthiriini - see this photo at BugGuide - and maybe genus Neacreothrichus. It seems to have something stuck in its beard - just like me! It was working the flowers very deliberately, letting me get this photo despite the wind this day. I saw other interesting flies and butterflies on that day - including a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly - but they were not so obliging. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2018)

Just two photos today as this was a jury day - I'm tired. I'm free for photos tomorrow - if the rain holds off. We're under a NWS Flash Flood Watch from Tuesday to Thursday with 100% chance of rain each day and night! This is said to be the largest storm of the season with 5-10 inches of rain in the foothills and mountains - but it's an atmospheric river type of event, so everything depends on the exact orientation of the storm. I've seen this before - a difference of twenty miles can make a big difference in total rainfall. Santa Barbara has issued evacuation orders for for residents below the burn areas starting at noon on Tuesday.

Hah - this will be the first storm of spring! The vernal equinox happens on Tuesday morning, and tomorrow is the first official day of spring. Happy equinox! The seasons change, life goes on . . .
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Eastwood Manzanita; atmospheric river

File.Name: i319b_i318-013.jpg
Notes: 39100528070 Here are new galls and old leaves on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I believe these are the galls of the Fuzzy Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera. The gall of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) is said to be similar - but restricted to Blue Oaks (Quercus douglasii). These are "detachable galls" that often stay attached to the stems for years and turn black with fungi. It's curious that these galls are common in some years but skip other years. The galls are stem galls. These scrubby oaks are "semi-deciduous" - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring, soon. The gall wasps are attuned to this schedule, and leaf galls won't start until the new leaves appear. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2018)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Fuzzy Gall Wasp; Round Gall Wasp; Blue Oaks

File.Name: i320a_i320-014.jpg
Notes:
39120398350 I had a photo of the whole California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus) in the top-left and didn't realize what it was looking at until I got it on my computer screen - there's another baby banana slug right in front of it! Needless to say, I changed my crop. The baby slug is cute! The infant is a different color, more like the green Pacific Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) I find in the Oregon woods - like this photo. It also has a more distinctly ridged back like the Oregon slugs. This may be why there's still some confusion about the proper scientific name of our California slugs. The big orange slugs are easy to notice, but I'll sure have to look closer to spot the little ones. This photo sure looks like it shows maternal behavior, but it couldn't be . . . could it? (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2018)

Rain is just starting as I write this in the evening, with just 1/4 inch so far. I expect a lot more - but how much? We have 5-10 inches in our forecast - but it's an atmospheric river type of event, so everything depends on the exact orientation of the storm. I've seen this before - a difference of twenty miles can make a big difference in total rainfall. Santa Barbara has issued evacuation orders for for residents below the burn areas. Darn, I have jury duty early tomorrow morning. I hope I can get there safely.
CommonNames: California Banana Slug; Pacific Banana Slugs; atmospheric river

File.Name: i320b_i320-042.jpg
Notes: 39120152700 This is a gorgeous little moth on a narrow leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). I assume it's nocturnal as it was bedded down in daytime - though it was a pretty gloomy day with rain threatening. Its scales look like bird feathers, especially that fringe on its tail. It's about 1/2 inch long. I don't know what kind of moth it is, but it's a handsome one - though I'm not sure I'd recognize it with wings open! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2018)

File.Name: i320c_i320-019.jpg
Notes: 40887850072 This spider has a little wasp for prey. The spider is a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) spider hanging on its web in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree down by the creek. I believe it's a Hammock Spider in the genus Pityohyphantes. The spider's prey is another Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera), see my [Previous] photo. It's a wasp with no sign of a "wasp waist" - like all sawflies. This one was still kicking in the grasp of the spider - moving its head back and forth. Creepy. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2018)

File.Name: i320d_i320-020.jpg
Notes: 39119864650 I believe this is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's a wasp with no sign of a "wasp waist" - like all sawflies. There are many different kinds. This one is rather pretty with those yellow colors, others are all black like the unfortunate one in my [Next] photo. Some of their larvae become real pests of our oak trees, see this photo, but I don't know which ones. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2018)

File.Name: i321a_i320-025.jpg
Notes:
26075427967 Here is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider on its web. You can see a bit of its "trash line". These spiders decorate their perfect webs with the bodies of their prey to make up a line along the midline of the web, like a once-living stabilimentum, that thicker white webbing behind the spider. They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. This is a nice shot of the spider's web for Web Webnesday, but it's also a nice shot of the spider's rear end for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday. I reckon that's legal - today is still Wednesday by my PDT local time but it's also Thursday by Flickr/GMT/ UTC time - so HWW and HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2018)

Just two photos today as this was a jury day - I'm tired. We did get about 2-1/4 inches of rain today, but it was mostly gentle rain and not the deluge I was expecting - but this storm's not over. It sounds like the worst/best of the rain is moving north towards San Luis Obispo tonight. Then it's expected to move back south towards us tomorrow, so the heaviest rains are still coming. I hope.

File.Name: i321b_i320-014.jpg
Notes: 27076781698 This is the photo of the "whole slug" that I was working on when I first noticed the baby banana slug that's more prominent in my [Previous] photo. The baby slug in in the bottom-right corner, I'll make a note. I never noticed it when I got the photo. These are both California Banana Slugs (Ariolimax californicus) - but it's interesting that the baby slug is more green like the Pacific Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) that I find in the Oregon woods - like this photo. I went back and look at all the photos I took of this orange slug, and the baby is only really visible in this photo, then it looks like the big slug rolled right over it - so much for maternal behavior! I really consider it a miracle every time I find one after our long droughts. Somehow these slugs manage to survive in our mountains, and I sure don't know how. I regard them as environmental indicators of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2018)
CommonNames: California Banana Slugs; Pacific Banana Slugs

File.Name: i322a_i322-004.jpg
Notes:
40962184921 This is looking up San Jose Creek from the bridge on West Camino Cielo road - in the rain today. Looking good! The creek looks respectable, but I bet it was higher at the height of the rain today. It's remarkable how quickly the water in the creeks drops when the heavy rain stops, but watch out for the floods! That's a native White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia, Betulaceae) tree leafing out in the foreground, a California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) on the left, and an unusual Incense Cedar (Calocedrus decurrens, Cupressaceae) tree on the right. I believe the cedar was introduced long ago, though they've been seeding and proliferating in Kinevan Canyon, and they are native in the backcountry. There's also a Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum, Aceraceae) tree in the background. I think that leaning tree in the background has just fallen - compare this photo from last year. Sure, the water is muddy - like liquid sandpaper! By a happy coincidence, today on March 22 is International World Water Day - there's more info here. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2018)

It was a good rain, but not a great rain - we ended up with 5 inches, on the low end of the spread of 5-10 inches of rain in the forecast. It was mostly pretty gentle but continuous, so there were no problems with debris flows below the burn areas, and evacuation orders have been lifted. I'd like to get out into the woods tomorrow, but it's a jury day. This weekend will be good too.
CommonNames: White Alder; California Bay; Incense Cedar; Bigleaf Maple; International World Water Day

File.Name: i322b_i322-019.jpg
Notes: 26089759787 This waterfall along our driveway rarely flows, but here it is! It falls about 20 feet total, more than this photo shows. It's a small drainage and it takes some heavy rain to get it working. We've had two inches of rain in the past 12 hours (and 5 inches in the past two days!) - enough to get our waterfall going. I got this photo as the rain was starting to wind down today - it was cranking earlier, but it was raining too hard to get there with my camera. The woodland around the waterfall shows Coast Live Oak and California Bay trees, with an understory of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) and Poison Oak and Wild Blackberry and still-dormant Nuttall's Snowberry. The woods are looking pretty bare because the snowberry is still leafless and the blackberry has been dying back during our six years of drought. Here's another photo from last year and an old photo from 2011 at Fotolog. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; California Bay; Coastal Wood Fern; Poison Oak; Wild Blackberry; Nuttall's Snowberry

File.Name: i322c_i322-012.jpg
Notes: 40961634311 Here are the first new flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family - with raindrops from the storm today. This vine was climbing up into a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree, and you can see a few oak leaves on the right. In past years I've found the first flowers as early as mid-January, see this photo - but most flowers are late this year. I got this photo down by the Santa Ynez River today. Julie and I drove down to see how the river was doing after the rain today. It was running but nothing very exciting - it's now running at 127 cfs (cubic feet per second) into Lake Cachuma. Even last year it got to 13,000 cfs, though it dropped to 4,000 cfs by the time I got this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 March 2018)

File.Name: i322d_i318-034.jpg
Notes: 40961505691 This is an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa, Ericaceae) - presenting its rear end. I believe this fly is tribe Phthiriini and maybe genus Neacreothrichus - see this photo for the rest of the same fly. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2018)

File.Name: i323a_i320-036.jpg
Notes:
39169226390 I wish I knew for sure what this photo shows. I do know that It's a gall on a shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) that I believe is a galled flower - see this photo and this photo which shows a gall and a ripe berry at the same time. Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." I guess this is the one, and it's a flower NOT a berry. (Well, it may be the ovary of a flower.) I have more recent flowers of the galls here and here.

This photo shows an insect emerging from the gall - or at least the husk of an insect. Is it the pupal husk of an emerging Gall Midge (Cecidomyiinae, Diptera)? Here's where I'm confused as the insect looks headless, though flies in the genus Asphondylia do have small heads. I don't know if this critter got out of its shell and flew away, or whether it got stuck and perished. Any help with understanding this photo will be appreciated. I do believe it shows the remains of a fly, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2018)

Just two photos today as this was another jury day - I'm tired. We ended up with 5.35 inches of rain from our storm this week. That's enough to bring our rain total up to 17.55 inches, but that's still only 58% of normal - enough to bring us up to fifth place in the list of driest years. It was a good rain, but its still a very dry year.

Note from March 24 - I happened to read this on page 61 of Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) today, describing the ways of Gall Wasps (Cecidomyiidae): ". . . the mature pupae exit only about two-thirds of the way out, using the pressure of the last part of the passage to hold them firmly in place while they complete their transformation into adults." That pretty much perfectly describes what I see in my photo. I take it as settled that these coffeeberry galls are the work of Gall Midges (Cecidomyiinae, Diptera).

File.Name: i324a_i324-004.jpg
Notes:
39188476130 Here are two shots of a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) on a new leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) - with wings open and closed. With the wings open, you can see that there is no sign of a "wasp waist" - but sawflies are like that. There are many different kinds. Some of their larvae become real pests of our oak trees, see this photo, but I don't know which ones cause the damage. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2018)

I was hoping for interesting fungi and slime molds today, but I didn't find them. It was chilly and windy, with the sun in and out of the clouds. We ended up with 5.35 inches of rain from our storm this week, enough to bring our rain total up to 17.55 inches. That's still only 58% of normal - but 12.68 inches of that rain fell in the month of March which is still in progress! Do the math and you can see how bad it was before these March rains. This is the seventh highest rainfall in March since records began on San Marcos Pass in 1966. Of course the highest amount is the March Miracle rain of 21.56 inches in 1991 - now that was something!

It's a male as indicated by the swollen palps - see this photo and this photo for more info. Maybe this one is in the genus Xysticus or Ozyptila with those cryptic colors? (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2018)

File.Name: i324c_i324-038.jpg
Notes: 40997302481 Here is a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the first open flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The pretty flower buds form in September and then wait fully half a year to open in March, see this photo from December. I don't know how the spider managed to find the first open flowers before I did! This spider is pretty hairy, so I guess it's another spider in the genus Mecaphesa. The yellow color is unusual but not unheard of - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2018)

File.Name: i324d_i324-050.jpg
Notes: 40955425472 This is an odd succulent gall on a new leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe it is the gall of the Succulent Gall Wasp (Neuroterus fragilis) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera. The gall looks juicy, but it feels quite firm - kind of like a gummy bear. You can see a small oak catkin in the upper-right of my photo - it looks like a bunch of micro-bananas! - but the galls seem to inhibit the catkins. I don't notice these galls very often, and only when the oaks start to leaf out. These scrubby oaks are "semi-deciduous" - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones at the same time in the spring. New leaf galls like this one are just beginning to develop with the new leaves. There is another succulent gall that grows on the catkins of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) trees, see this photo. That one is different, and apparently an undescribed species. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2018)

File.Name: i325a_i325-022.jpg
Notes:
27148269518 This is another small wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flower of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's definitely different from the bee in my [Previous] photo on the same shrub. Is it another genus Andrena bee? I'm not sure, and I.D. help is always appreciated. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 March 2018)

It was quite windy today, so I went for a drive up East Camino Cielo road into the mountains. I dealt with the wind by taking lots of photos and managed to get a few keepers like this one. It sounds like we're in for another day or two of wind, but that's no problem because I have more jury duty coming up. *Sigh*

Notes: 26148418797 This is a small wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) with a big head and a skinny body on a flower cluster of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's got quite large mandibles hiding under its white beard. I won't try to guess what kind it, but help will be appreciated. It's definitely different from the bee in my [Next] photo on the same shrub. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 March 2018)

File.Name: i325c_i325-034.jpg
Notes: 41019000931 There are still lots of new flowers on this shrub of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It really is remarkable that one or another of these shrubs has been flowering since early October, see this photo - just about from equinox to equinox! The good rains this month seem to have brought out a whole new crop of the pretty buds. If you look close, you can see a tiny ant tasting the open flower on the left in this photo. (Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 March 2018)

File.Name: i325d_i325-006.jpg
Notes: 41018661981 Here are deep blue flowers of some horticultural variety of Ceanothus in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The individual flowers tiny, but there are lots of them. They are quite intricate - like they've been cut out with scissors, with a blue-green nectar disk at the base. Compare the white flowers of a different species in this photo. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "The Blues". I really wanted to find a native Ceanothus shrub in flower today, but the blue-flowered ones are just starting to flower up in the mountains, and I couldn't find one that was colored deeply enough. It was quite windy today when I got this photo, and I took dozens of photos to get this keeper. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2018)

File.Name: i326a_i325-038.jpg
Notes:
40146684055 Here are the first new flowers and leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Aceraceae plant family poking out of their buds on a winter-bare tree. Actually I've been seeing them for the past week near Cold Springs Tavern, but this is the first photo I've gotten. It's a winter-deciduous tree that is common in Oregon but unusual in our mountains. I think that every one of these deciduous trees and shrubs is a stumper - after all, winter is when our rains come and plants should start to grow! I have some discussion in my comments on this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 March 2018)

Just two photos today as this was another jury day - I'm tired. It was windy today anyway. I do have jury duty tomorrow, though not until the afternoon. I'll get out with my camera if it's not too windy. The good news is that Friday is a court holiday - Cesar Chavez Day not Good Friday. (I had to look it up.)

File.Name: i326b_i325-030.jpg
Notes: 40997389322 I noticed these (mostly) red-colored bumps on a shiny new leaf of native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family yesterday. I reckon they are galls, but I don't know whose galls they are. In his fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), author Ron Russo mentions "Erineum Galls" made by Eriophyid mites in the species Eriophyes breakeyi - but that one looks different in the monograph "An Illustrated Guide to Plant Abnormalities Caused by Eriophyid Mites in North America". I have another photo that shows a tiny wasp ovipositing on one of the galls - which at least confirms that they are galls with something living inside. Apart from the galls, these shrubs have beautiful foliage. (Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 March 2018)

File.Name: i327a_i327-039.jpg
Notes:
39253163200 Here is a handsome California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture from a broken sandstone rock. (Sandstone is porous enough to hold a lot of water.) I've seen a few of these butterflies in the air this year, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph. It looks newly emerged with that bright blue patch on its wing. These butterflies lose that blue patch with age, but it's an iridescence that only appears when the light is just so - note that the other wing doesn't show the color. As far as I know, these butterflies never feed, but they do take moisture. I guess they emerge with full bellies and fly until they're empty. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2018)

Just two photos again today as this was another jury day - I'm tired. But I didn't have my jury duty until the afternoon today, so I did get a few photos this morning - more than I can show. I do have a court day tomorrow, but then I'm free for a four-day weekend. Speaking as a retired person, you really don't appreciate your free time until you have commitments!

File.Name: i327b_i327-006.jpg
Notes: 41019685642 This is a handsome little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) guarding the throat of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). The swollen palps show that it's a male - see this photo and this photo for more info. Many of these male crab spiders have long legs and small bodies - sometimes carried to extremes like this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2018)

File.Name: i328a_i327-025.jpg
Notes:
41041303312 This is another Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera), though this one is more handsome that the usual black ones in this photo. It's on a leaflet (or leafy stipule?) of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. This one was moving around a lot, like it wanted to oviposit - if that darned photographer wasn't dogging its moves! I'm not sure what kind of sawfly it is - maybe genus Tenthredo, compare this photo at BugGuide. This wasp has no sign of a "wasp waist" - but sawflies are like that. There are many different kinds. Some of their larvae become real pests of our oak trees, see this photo, but I don't know which ones cause the damage. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2018)

Just two photos again today as this was another jury day - I'm tired. I don't have my jury duty until next Monday, so I'm looking forward to getting some photo time in the days ahead. Speaking as a retired person, you really don't appreciate your free time until you have commitments!

File.Name: i328b_i327-013.jpg
Notes: 41084184361 This is a small spider managing the lines that run to its orb web a little further over to the left. I believe this is a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) - an orb-weaving spider that makes a proper orb web and then builds a little retreat off to the side, see this photo and this photo and compare the markings. I don't see any retreat in this photo, but I guess it got washed away by the good rain last week. This one is touching the lines that run to its proper web - like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing, ready to strike. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2018)

File.Name: i329a_i329-025.jpg
Notes:
41104833881 This caterpillar looks like a stick! This is a well-camouflaged looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Geometrid caterpillars are distinctive with a base of just two pairs of prolegs. They have great camouflage - they stand out straight like little twigs. You can see a bit of "orange stuff" on both ends which seems to be part of the caterpillar - I'll post a closer "head shot" tomorrow. I've found these caterpillars before, try a search of my photos for "camouflage caterpillar Geometridae". I've found something similar on quite a few different plants. One thing I don't understand is that the caterpillar is large, but most of the moths are rather small. Have I missed something? (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2018)

It was warmer today, in the mid-70s with an annoying breeze. Hah, I could tell it was going to be warm from the moment I got up and saw that the ants swarming in the corner of our bedroom! See this photo from a few years ago, taken on the porch outside that corner of our bedroom. I believe they are California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale). They bite and sting - no fun. They're only a problem for a week or two in the spring, and then only in the morning. Maybe I'll try to get a photo - but the ants are really in control!

Notes: 26233002287 Here is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking at me on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This whole 1:1 macro photo is only about 1,680 pixels across as cropped, which comes to a bit over 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Sure, the spider is looking at me. There's something unnerving about making eye contact with such a tiny critter, but here it is. There are many kinds of these little jumpers that I can't tell apart, and this is one of them. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2018)

Maybe it's the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be the common species in central California, see BugGuide. The fly was gone a moment later, but I managed to get this one good shot of the fly's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2018)

File.Name: i330a_i330-008.jpg
Notes:
41125411611 Here is a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly resting for a moment on an old berry of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. (I think it was really interested in the Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata, Anacardiaceae) a few feet away.) This is the first one I've managed to photograph in this odd weather year. At first the butterfly looks plain brown, but when you look closer you see that it really has a rich palette of many colors like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (subfamily Theclinae) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2018)

It was another warm day, almost into the 80s. I'm not looking forward to it getting too warm, but maybe that will bring out more flowers and butterflies.

File.Name: i330d_i329-003.jpg
Notes: 41124534471 Here are two shots from either side of the head of the stick-like caterpillar in my [Previous] photo. I believe it's a well-camouflaged looper or inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These caterpillars stand on a base of just two pairs of prolegs, but they have three pairs of true legs on the head-end. My photo shows the circle of simple eyes of the caterpillar - I don't think they can see very well. This photo also shows that the "orange stuff" on the head is indeed part of the caterpillar - like a rooster's wattle? It also shows that this caterpillar is making webbing, though I'm not sure what for. The webbing has an interesting texture - like that of the cribillate spider in this photo. The caterpillar's spinnerets might be visible on the right - on the head rather than the tail like a spider, I'll make a note. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2018)

File.Name: i331a_i331-023.jpg
Notes:
27274205038 This is a new flower of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa, Ericaceae) that's been opened by an impatient critter that couldn't wait for the flower to open by itself. It reminds me of one of those fancy Easter eggs that can you look into - in this case, you can see the stamens inside. Happy Easter! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2018)

It felt like spring today - almost 80∞ again, though it was also foggy on the coast, and we had some annoying "fog winds" up here in the mountains. I guess the morning fog is a sign of spring too. I saw my first Shooting Star wildflowers today and Sara Orangetip butterflies. After our good March rains, I do believe we will have a proper spring!

File.Name: i331b_i331-034.jpg
Notes: 40252229445 This is looking into a new flower of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The walnut-sized flowers are packed with stamens surrounding two or three fat pistils with reddish stigmas at the tip. Usually the flowers point down towards the earth hiding their goodies, but I propped this one up with a stick for a photo. They are one of my favorite wildflowers, probably long-lasting fire-followers in this place that burned about 15 years ago. They are a true native that's related to the garden plants. I've read that the perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb - I'd rather find the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 31 March 2018)

File.Name: i331c_i330-017.jpg
Notes: 39337935070 This is a large foliose gall on stem of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that is just starting to flower. I believe these galls are actually flower buds that are altered beyond recognition. This might be the gall of a Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera, but it might also be one of its kin. I find these leafy galls on all of our Ceanothus shrubs with 3-veined leaves - including smaller ones on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus), see this photo; and larger ones on Chaparral Whitethorn (Ceanothus leucodermis), see this photo. I'm not sure if all three are caused by the same gall inducer, as there are visible difference between them - in size if nothing else. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2018)

File.Name: i331d_i329-017.jpg
Notes: 27273293558 This bee-size and bee-like insect is NOT a bee but a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably genus Abia. One sign that it's a wasp and not a bee is that it has no hairs on its legs - though this would be a better photo if you could see its rear legs. It also has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that. This one is on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They don't seem to be common in our woods, but I see them now and then at this time of year. They come from caterpillar-like larvae, see this photo for a picture and more info. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2018)

File.Name: i401a_i401-015.jpg
Notes:
40277399625 I believe this is a tiny Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) inserting its seed into a leaf bud of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) to make a gall. At least that's what I'd like to believe as I collect photos of plant galls! I suppose it might also be Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea) finding some prey inside the leaf bud, I'm not sure how to tell them apart. If it is a gall wasp, maybe it's the Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) that makes the gall in this photo. On the other hand, galls are actually gall communities which attract inquilines (that eat gall tissue), parasites (that eat the gall-inducer's larvae) and hyperparasites (that eat the larvae of the parasites) . . . it's complicated! Needless to say, the wasp is tiny - just 890 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Hah - I had to stand on a chair to get this shot! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2018)

Today was a fine day for getting photos - a few degrees cooler, still lots of high clouds to cut the glare, and not much breeze. *Sigh* It's back to the jury in the morning.

File.Name: i401b_i401-026.jpg
Notes: 40460252654 I realized something while looking at some undeveloped berries of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae) today. These berries have a a little "beak" just like the galled flowers of California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), see this photo. I wonder if these Toyon berries are also galled? This would be the work of a tiny Gall Midge (Cecidomyiinae, Diptera), see this photo. It's interesting that Toyon and Coffeeberry both have berries, but they are in very different plant families. Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2018)

File.Name: i401c_i331-016.jpg
Notes: 40276690095 This branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) is a regular gall hotel! It has three different kinds of galls on it, all the work of different Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). In the upper-right, there is a classic "oak apple gall" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus). This is a "detachable gall" that could be removed with some effort. On the other end of that stem, there are a couple of lumpy galls of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) with lots of exit holes. This is an integral stem gall that is not detachable. Finally, there are old galls that I believe come from the Fuzzy Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) - and a fresh one in the background. It almost looks like one of the fuzzy galls is growing on one of the spindle galls! If the scrub oak looks a little ragged right now, it's not because of the galls - it's about to get new leaves and flowers/catkins, and with the new leaves will come new galls! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2018)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Wasps; California Gall Wasp; Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Fuzzy Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Andricus chrysolepidicola; Disholcaspis washingtonensis
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Andricus chrysolepidicola; Disholcaspis washingtonensis

File.Name: i401d_i401-063.jpg
Notes: 40276248685 This is the type from my old typewriter - a Royal Portable Typewriter, a ringer for the one in these photos. I got this typewriter second hand back in the '60s when I was in high school, and it might actually date back to about 1930. I used it to type dozens of papers as a student at UCSB. Yep, it still works - but it wouldn't be my first choice for actually writing anything. I wonder what those other faint characters are on the type? This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Back in the Day" - HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2018)

File.Name: i402a_i401-037.jpg
Notes:
27324006368 Here is a white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) guarding the throat of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). This spider is pretty hairy, so I guess it's another spider in the genus Mecaphesa. This is still one of the only plants with flowers in our canyon so far this year, so I'm not surprised to find spiders waiting there. This is a lady with slender palps, see a male in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2018)

Just two photos again today as this was another jury day - I'm tired. I'm free tomorrow, then we're having full jury days for the rest of the week in an attempt to finish the case. Then it's time for our jury to actually do something. Marine-layer fog was spilling over the mountains as I left home this morning and blanketed us all day at court in Santa Barbara. I hope the clouds clear tomorrow - but even if they don't it will be nice to get outside. Speaking as a retired person, you really don't appreciate your free time until you have commitments!

File.Name: i402b_i401-031.jpg
Notes: 41198366971 Here is a golden Crab Spider (Thomisidae) guarding the throat of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). This spider is pretty hairy, so I guess it's another spider in the genus Mecaphesa. The yellow color is unusual but not unheard of - see this photo. The scattered pollen around the throat of the flower on the left shows that the flowers have had visitors. The missing front leg of the spider on the screen-left side shows that it's been going after the visitors! This is still one of the only plants with flower in our canyon so far this year, so I'm not surprised to find spiders waiting there. This is a lady with slender palps, see a male in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2018)

File.Name: i403a_i403-001.jpg
Notes:
40506583524 Here is a winged "alate" Ant (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) on our bathroom windowsill this morning. They seem to collect in the bathroom every day, probably because of the morning light from the skylight. I reckon this is a male ant, born to die - see the Wikipedia page on Nuptial flight. I saw the first ants swarming in our house a few days ago, but they've stopped now that the weather has cooled down again . Maybe this is the California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae) with a nasty temper and a strong unpleasant smell - see this photo. We also have the larger Carpenter Ants (Camponotus) in our house. I reckon this is one or the other of those. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2018)

The marine layer fog did a little dance this morning! The fog first spilled over the mountains from the ocean side with a little microburst of wind. Then it backed off, and the wind died down. Then an hour later, the fog moved up from the valley side - as quiet as can be, no wind at all. Then it backed off again. I noticed wind again an hour later, and sure enough, the fog was coming back from the ocean side! Finally, the fog backed off for good, though I could see it roiling around just over the mountains. I never did leave the canyon today to see what it looked like in town, but I can guess it was gloomy. I'll find out tomorrow when I report for my jury duty.
CommonNames: Ant; California Velvety Tree Ant; Carpenter Ants
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Camponotus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Camponotus

File.Name: i403d_i403-035.jpg
Notes: 41220125531 Here are two shots of the same little Orb Weaver (Araneidae) spider by its web in a tall cylindrical cactus in the garden. I guess it's genus Araneus with those pointed humps on its back, visible on the left, but cp. genus Neoscona. I got the photo showing the spider's top today, and the photo on the right of the spider's bottom two days ago. This spider is small, just 1180 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 4.6 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) That small size indicates that it might be Araneus bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo - but I don't think so! It looks more like a tiny version of one of our large bottlecap-size Halloween spiders, maybe Araneus gemmoides or Neoscona crucifera, see this photo. They both have that odd black and white patch under their abdomen, compare the shot on the right with this photo. Don't all large spiders have to start small?? Whatever it is, it's an impressive spider. I hope it stays around so I can keep an eye on it. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2018)

File.Name: i404a_i403-009.jpg
Notes:
27369290908 Who needs flowers to look beautiful! This is a gorgeous flower head of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family with very glandular bracts, but no flowers yet. For some reason, these glandular bracts attract tiny Midges in the family Chironomidae, Diptera. I'll make a note for one male midge in the upper-right that stands out, but I can see a number more on the bracts - at least in the larger original photo. Maybe the flies just like how it smells - I know I do! These plants and many more are flowering on the other side of Santa Ynez Valley, see this recent photo by Jeff Goddard. We've lagging behind on this side of the valley. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2018)

It was another gloomy day - in town at least. It did half-way clear up in the afternoon, but then the marine layer fog came back in, nearly to the top of the mountains. Just two photos today and for the next couple of days. The court is trying to finish my jury case on Friday, which puts me into deliberation next week. I'll be glad when this is all over.

File.Name: i407a_i407-012.jpg
Notes:
41264333352 Here is the spiky caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on foliage of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) - with raindrops from our little storm this morning. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example in this photo. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies and lay eggs. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. These are just about the only butterfly caterpillars I've been finding this spring. We'll have lots of checkerspot butterflies later this year, but I'm not sure what else. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2018)

They got several inches of rain in the Bay Area from our storm - we got 1/2 of 1/10 inch. That's how it is with these "Atmospheric River" storms - they are a narrow plume of moisture moving from west to east that's easy to miss if you are too far north or south. Oh well, any amount of rain is always appreciated. Today started with gloomy drizzle and ended up mostly sunny and windy with 20-30 mph gusts. I did manage to get a few photos before it got too windy.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i407b_i407-009.jpg
Notes: 41306525941 This moth has found shelter from the storm in a hideaway under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - at least its head is out of the rain today. Maybe it's a Noctuid Moth (Noctuidae, Lepidoptera)? We didn't get a lot of rain today, but it was definitely worth finding shelter. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2018)
CommonNames: moth; Coast Live Oak; Noctuid Moth

File.Name: i407c_i407-018.jpg
Notes: 41263953832 Here are new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family - with raindrops. We didn't get a lot of rain from this storm, but it came down as gentle drizzle this morning, just right for decorating the flowers. I guess it was too wet for Crab Spiders - see this photo without the raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2018)

File.Name: i407d_i407-039.jpg
Notes:
40592799794 Here are new flowers of Nuttall's Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) in the Rubiacae plant family, the first flowers I've seen this year. The colors in this photo are particularly striking, probably because my lens was inadvertently fogged up! This plant will end up with small white berries, see this photo from a couple of years ago. These plants climb over other shrubs, California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in this case. The leaves often turn red as the season goes on, and the plants look quite dead by the end of the year. But they come back. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2018)

Plants in the genus Galium are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny 4-merous flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds. They are one of my favorites - even if I get confused by them!
ScientificName: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Galium
associatedTaxa: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Galium

File.Name: i408a_i408-053.jpg
Notes:
26459354577 This is a little web-spinning caterpillar on a skeletonized leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I have additional photos of these caterpillars here and here. It's definitely the work of the caterpillar to skeletonize the oak leaf, but fungi also do this, see this photo. I don't know what kind of caterpillar this is, though they seem to be quite common. I reckon it's a moth - though that large head capsule does remind me of a duskywing skipper caterpillar, see this photo and lots more at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2018)

We've been under a NWS Wind Advisory all day with 45-55 mph winds expected . . . and it's been quite calm, go figure. Wind typically follows a storm - even a small storm - so this was a pleasant surprise. I'll be busy in town for the next few days, but soon I'll be able to get back to my photos full time.

File.Name: i408b_i408-046.jpg
Notes: 26459170667 Here is the nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) crawling over a flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. The nymphs are growing - this one is no longer tiny like the nymph in this photo. It's not as ornate as the Bush Katydid nymph in this photo from last summer, and I'm sure it will become a different sort of adult. It's probably the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2018)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: i408c_i408-055.jpg
Notes: 40434708745 I noticed this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) sprawled across a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. This spider is pretty hairy, so I guess it's another member of the genus Mecaphesa. I honestly don't know what prey it expects to find here, but I've seen several of these spiders on oak leaves, e.g. this one - there must be something! (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2018)

File.Name: i408d_i408-062.jpg
Notes: 26458742807 Here are lots of bubbles in a clear bowl of soapy water sitting on a dark background out in the sun today. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Circles". For some reason, the best circular bubbles are around that little clear "lake" on the edge of my photo. The larger bubbles aren't perfect, but I figure they aspire to circle-ness in a Platonic sense. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2018)

File.Name: i409a_i408-051.jpg
Notes:
26484174037 It feels like spring! This Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) was posed on this dead branch for just a moment in the woods - showing the brilliant blue mating colors on its belly. (These lizards get even more decorated for mating, see this photo!) I've mostly been seeing small lizards lately, but It's definitely warming up this week, and new flowers and lizards in a mating mood are on the loose! Those are new plants of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the background, see this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2018)

The wind finally did kick up last night - and died down this morning. I wish it was always so obliging! I was in town all day, and it was downright hot, in the 90s. That's hotter than it was in the mountains by about 10 degrees, maybe the work of those downslope "sundowner" winds last night. Just two photos again today, but I'll soon be back to normal.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Caterpillar Phacelia
ScientificName: Sceloporus occidentalis; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Sceloporus occidentalis; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae

File.Name: i409b_i408-001.jpg
Notes: 41354734291 Here are the first new flowers of California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family, growing in their usual habitat on a shady sandstone bank in a place where the sun rarely shines - though I got this photo early enough in the morning that there still was some sun on them. They are not common in our mountains. I usually find these flowers in February, see this photo. Everything is late (or absent) in this odd weather year, rescued from disaster by the good rains last month in March. That higher after-the-equinox sun is probably the reason I got this photo in the sun rather than the shade like all my older photos. Usually I find these plants growing with another plant in the same family, Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) - but I haven't seen that one this year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2018)

File.Name: i410a_i410-042.jpg
Notes:
27507786038 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) sprawled across a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today - probably the same spider I showed in this photo a couple of days ago, at least it's on the same leaf. This spider is pretty hairy, so I guess it's another member of the genus Mecaphesa. See my [Previous] photo for the kind of prey it can expect to find here, but this spider is also prey itself! Look close in the crease between the abdomen and the head/cephalothorax - you can see the orange tail of a critter that I believe is the spider-parasite larva of a mantidfly in the family Mantispidae, Neuroptera, see this photo. I'll make a note. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2018)

I had the morning free today, but it was a busy afternoon. It was still warm today, and now we might get a splash of rain tomorrow - windy too. Almost done!
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coast Live Oak; mantidfly
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Mantispidae; Neuroptera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Mantispidae; Neuroptera

File.Name: i410b_i410-015.jpg
Notes: 39568513390 This is a chubby little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with a bristly fly (Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's another fly that looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). The spider is pretty hairy, so I guess it's another member of the genus Mecaphesa. A couple of days ago, I posted this photo of another crab spider on an oak leaf and wondered what prey it expects to find here . . . well, flies for one thing! I've also been seeing more and more Common Sawfly (Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera) insects on the new oak leaves, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2018)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coast Live Oak; Tachinid Fly; Common Sawfly
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tachinidae; Mecaphesa; Tenthredinidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: i411a_i411-014.jpg
Notes:
27529369108 "Cute spider" is not an oxymoron!

This is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking at me on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The leaf looks odd because it has been partly skeletonized by the caterpillar in this photo. I believe the spider is a female of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus with those conspicuous dark areas that are barely visible on top of its head. I have another photo here. Many jumping spiders look alike, but this one is distinctive. It has punk eyebrows! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2018)

I've been sitting on a jury for the Santa Barbara Superior Court since February 23 - and we just finished our case today on April 11. We did reach a verdict on our third day of deliberation. *Whew* I understand that jury duty is part of citizenship, but it has cut into my photo time lately. I did get this photo today when I got home, and it will be a pleasure to get out every day with my camera again. Alas, it's quite windy tonight with 20-40 mph gusts, and we're under a NWS High Wind Warning for strong and damaging winds all day tomorrow. I hope the forecast is wrong - I'll get out when I can.
ScientificName: not; Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus
associatedTaxa: not; Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus

File.Name: i411b_i410-025.jpg
Notes: 40685422474 Here is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider sitting with prey in the inner circle of its web. The thick white cottony webbing running through the center of the web is the spider's stabilimentum. It's function is still a subject of debate, see the Wikipedia entry for web decorations. Usually these spiders decorate their stabilimentum with the bodies of their prey, but I guess this spider had to rebuild its web after the rain last weekend - and needs to redecorate! They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. Sure, I could post this to the Web Wednesday group. I already have my two photos for the group, but Happy Web Webnesday anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2018)

File.Name: i411c_i407-020.jpg
Notes: 41399137651 I guess this is the web of an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) between dead stems of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) - but look closer! The web has no cross-webbing except for a few loops in the very center. I got this photo in the morning after a small rainstorm last weekend, so I maybe the spider was interrupted before it finished. At least it had the good sense to get out of the rain! It's curious that I've seen his before - see this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2018)

File.Name: i411d_i407-036.jpg
Notes: 41356092722 Here is the web-spinning caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a dead-looking stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) - with raindrops from our little storm last Saturday. Yep, the webbing is from the caterpillar. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example in this photo, but they don't seem to spin such thick webs in the spring. I suspect the webbing in my present photo is an aid to molting, but webbing is webbing, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i412a_i412-021.jpg
Notes:
41421207631 Life is resilient! Here are new flowers - the first I've seen this year - on charred and stunted plants of Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) growing amid scorched chaparral shrubs in an area that was burned by the massive Thomas Fire last December. I got this photo along East Camino Cielo today, on the very edge of the burn area. Much of the burn area further inside is burned to the ground and still bare, just starting to show signs of new life. I hope to get back here regularly in the months ahead - when it's not as windy as it was today! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 April 2018)

There were crazy winds today of 30-45 mph as measured at La Cumbre Peak. It was too windy for my kind of photos at home, so I went for a drive up into the higher mountains and discovered that East Camino Cielo was no longer closed east of Gibraltar Road - at least it's not seriously closed. Our NWS High Wind Warning runs until noon on Friday with 60+ mph winds in the forecast tonight. I'll be glad when this wind event is over, but I did manage to get a few photos today - now that my jury duty is done.

File.Name: i412b_i411-007.jpg
Notes: 27549592878 This is a Common Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) ovipositing - laying eggs - on a new leaf bud of a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I have photos that show the whole insect better here. The eggs will hatch into caterpillar-like larvae that become real pests of our oak trees, see this photo. They only seem to become a problem in some years - maybe the larvae get the new leaves before they get too tough. I'll be keeping an eye on them. Meanwhile, this is a good butt shot - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2018)

File.Name: i412c_i408-047.jpg
Notes: 41378697252 This photo shows the rear end of the nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) crawling over a flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. It's crawling away from me, about to disappear into the shadows. A moment before, I got this photo which shows the whole nymph more clearly. This is probably the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum), see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2018)
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: i412d_i410-021.jpg
Notes: 26549271977 Here are two photos showing the rear ends of two tiny Jumping Spiders (Salticidae). I'm pretty sure the one on the right is a female of the same species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus that I show in my [Previous] photo. I'm not sure about the one on the left, but maybe it's a male of the species Pelegrina aeneola. I may have a shot of the female in this photo. Sure, this is a "butt shot" that's perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday (BBBT) group, but they have a limit of two photos per week, and I have two more photos for them - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2018)
ScientificName: Salticidae; Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus; Pelegrina aeneola
