File.Name: i413a_i413-047.jpg
Notes:
26571012697 Here are the first new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family fully open in the mountains today! I actually saw the first flowers a week ago, but these are nicer. This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers like this, edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves (here)! The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. They are pretty common on here San Marcos pass - both in woodland and chaparral. They are stunning flowers, worth hunting for. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2018)

The shrubs are unusual, a singleton in their family here in California, and endemic to the state. The specimen map at Calflora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There are a few records from San Luis Obispo county to the north and none from Ventura county to the south.

The wind didn't go away today, but it did drop to ordinary levels - not the "strong and damaging winds" we had yesterday. It was a lovely day . . . ruined by the turn of American foreign policy tonight. I'm deeply concerned. I'm glad I have wildflowers, insects, and spiders to think about tonight.

File.Name: i413b_i413-059.jpg
Notes: 41441566501 Here is a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on new yellow flowers of native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta, Apiaceae) that's just starting to flower. Good camouflage! I have a photo of the whole plant here. It's not obvious in my photo, but the spider is pretty hairy all over - so I guess it's another member of the genus Mecaphesa. If you look close, you can see that it's missing a leg on its screen-left. That's an occupational hazard for these spiders that grab their prey and hang on. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2018)

File.Name: i413c_i413-017.jpg
Notes: 40726981004 This is a tiny Psyllid (Psylloidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera ) insect on a leaf of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe this one is Trioza bakeri - see the comment by Chris Mallory on this photo. They are tiny - about 730 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Hah, they look like tiny cicadas! They are said to oviposit inside the folded leaf visible behind - which becomes a gall, see this photo and the earlier link. This gall is not described Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but I have no doubt that this psyllid is the cause. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2018)
ScientificName: Psylloidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Trioza bakeri
associatedTaxa: Psylloidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Trioza bakeri

File.Name: i413d_i410-027.jpg
Notes: 41398976832 This is a bristly little fly (Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae). This is another fly that looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), and it does seem to have a subscutellum which is said a useful character for identification of these flies, but who knows. Could it be the same kind of fly as shown in this photo? It's a nice fly whatever it is, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2018)
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Tachinidae; subscutellum

File.Name: i414a_i414-007.jpg
Notes:
27590535828 This sure looks like a gall on this stem of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family - normal leaves on the left and galled leaves on the right . It looks like one of the foliose galls on Ceanothus that is the work of a Gall Midge in the genus Asphondylia, family Cecidomyiidae,Diptera, see this photo - so maybe that's what it is? There's no mention of any galls on this plant in Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I looked close at other plants nearby and didn't find any more. Ron Russo says: "Each time I explore a new area, I find undescribed species. You may also find galls not described in this guide or any other reference. Please do not let that frustrate or discourage you. The world is full of discoveries yet to be made. If nothing else, this single idea encourages me, and I hope you, to keep looking and exploring." Good words. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2018)

It almost got up to 80∞ today, but there was an annoying breeze from the south just to remind me why taking my kind of photos is difficult. Checking at the Ventusky web site, I can see an eddy around Point Conception bringing winds from the south. I recommend this interesting site to see the big picture of the wind and other weather.
ScientificName: Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Asphondylia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Asphondylia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: i414b_i414-013.jpg
Notes: 41418452172 This is a brand new leaf of a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family - with a "leaf roller gall" (my name) on the lower margin. I'm pretty sure this is a real gall as it goes against the natural downward curve of the leaf, and it's made of "galled tissue" that looks different from normal leaf tissue. In time these galls will develop more color, see this photo. I don't know what critter's gall it is, and it's not listed in Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). We also seem to have a different "leaf roller gall" that rolls the leaves under the leaves rather than over, see this photo. Whatever critter induces this gall, it works fast - this leaf is fresh from the bud. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2018)

File.Name: i414c_i413-053.jpg
Notes: 40567822685 This appears to be a gall on a pistillate seed-bearing catkin of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. I've never noticed a gall on these plants before, but this is pretty unmistakable. Ron Russo describes such a gall in his fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). He identifies it as the gall of a Silk Tassel Gall Midge (Asphondylia garryae, Cecidomyiinae, Diptera). The only disparity is that he only lists the shrub species Garrya fremontii and G. buxifolia as hosts - to which we can now add G. veatchii. I'll keep my eye out for more of them. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2018)
CommonNames: Veatch Silk-Tassel; Silk Tassel Gall Midge
ScientificName: Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Asphondylia garryae; Cecidomyiinae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Asphondylia garryae; Cecidomyiinae; Diptera

File.Name: i414d_i413-014.jpg
Notes: 27589719158 Here are brand new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. The variety inezianus isn't recognized in Jepson, but the plants do seem distinct - larger, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. Very beautiful. They are a fire-follower, though these are growing in a place that was cleared - not burned - a few years ago. Sometimes that's enough. The plants are having a hard time in this odd drought year. I've only seen a few plants with flowers, and one of those was only about four inches tall - with flowers. Along with the Snowdrop Bush in my [Previous] photo, this is one of my favorite spring flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2018)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

File.Name: i415a_i415-011.jpg
Notes:
40593330525 Here are a pair of Blue-belly Lizards aka Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) - or more properly Coast Range Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii). But are they male and female courting or male and male facing off for a rumble? The one on top in my photo is definitely a male with those strong mating colors, but I'm not sure about the one below. See my [Previous] photo for separate shots of each lizard. I would appreciate any feedback on this - I've been wrong about this before, see this photo and Alice Abelia's comment. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)

There were clouds moving in today and a breeze, signs of a small storm approaching that might bring a splash of rain tomorrow. Then the forecast mostly calls for wind the rest of the week. This isn't my favorite weather, but at least it feels like spring.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizards; Western Fence Lizards; Coast Range Fence Lizards

File.Name: i415b_i415-013.jpg
Notes: 39677643900 Here are separate photos of the two lizards from my [next] photo. They are both Blue-belly Lizards aka Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) - or I believe more properly the Coast Range Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii). My question is whether they are male and female or are they both males? The California Herps web page for this species says that "Females have faint or absent blue markings on the belly, no blue or green color on the upper surfaces, and dark bars or crescents on the back" - which make it sound like these are both males, though if so the one on top is more mature. I would appreciate any feedback on this. I've been wrong about this before. see this photo and Alice Abelia's comment. At least I'm sure that's a male lizard on top - isn't it beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizards; Western Fence Lizards; Coast Range Fence Lizard

File.Name: i415c_i415-043.jpg
Notes: 26614370467 Just count the legs and divide by eight . . . . Here are a mating pair of Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spiders doing it in the air suspended by their webs. It was hard to get a sharp photo of these tiny spiders with the breeze today and their constant motion, but maybe this is sharp enough considering the subject. That's the male on the left and the female to the right - they look quite different, though they both have that characteristic hump on their backs. You can see something yellow down below that might be a drop of sperm. It was so interesting to watch this. There were actually four males paying attention to the one lady, and I saw two of them mate at different times. A male would make "plucking motions" on the web, and then the female would come out to meet him. One male spider advanced directly on the female, and she bounced him right off the web! I know, try not to anthropomorphize - but that's what I saw. I have more photos from this encounter that I'll post sometime soon. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)

File.Name: i415d_i415-065.jpg
Notes: 39676870420 This is a debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae,Neuroptera) on a gall on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see its impressive jaws if you look close. Apparently these debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter other predators - though I don't know what they find in woods that is so white. It's great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look closer! The galls are interesting too. I only find them on the just-opened new spring leaves of the oak. Like the galls in my previous two photos, this is another gall that is not listed in Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I've been calling them "Painted Toenail Galls" - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)

File.Name: i416a_i416-011.jpg
Notes:
39700089420 This is a pretty blue flower of native Tuberous Skullcap (Scutellaria tuberosa) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I usually find these near Lake Cachuma, but these are the first plants I've ever found up here in the mountains. It's curious though - this plant in the mountains is much hairier that the plants from the valley, compare this photo. It also has spots on the lip that the valley plants lack. These mountain plants look more wild and less like a groundcover. I reckon the species is highly variable - that's assuming they are the same plant, but I think so. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)

We did get our splash of rain from the storm last night, enough to get our outdoor seat cushions wet but not enough to measure. Mostly we got wind running 20+ mph. I went to a place with some shelter and got this photo of a new plant (for me) that happens to grow close to the ground where the wind isn't so bad. Our power will be out all day tomorrow for "scheduled maintenance" - I'll post if I can.

File.Name: i416b_i415-026.jpg
Notes: 41466773152 This is a male Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider - half of the mating pair I showed yesterday in this photo. It retreated to this place by that little tuft of webbing several times while the spiders were going at it, and I wonder if this is his "sperm web". As I understand it, the male deposits tiny drops of sperm on his special sperm web and then inflates the spermaphores on the end of his leg-like palps to pick it up. He then transfers his sperm to the female's epigynum. Of course that description leaves out all the romance and the finesse of courtship! You can definitely see the spider's inflated male palps in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)

File.Name: i416c_i415-077.jpg
Notes: 40794870324 Here is the odd cocoon of a Ribbed Casemaker Moth (genus Bucculatrix, family Bucculatricidae, Lepidoptera). The caterpillars are host-plant specific, and this is on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf, so it's probably the work of a Live Oak Ribbed Casemaker (Bucculatrix albertiella) moth. I wouldn't recognize the tiny moth, but the cocoons are distinctive with longitudinal ridges and surrounded by that odd silk "picket fence" - I wonder what it's for. I've found similar cocoons on Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) but without the picket fence, see this photo - the work of a different species in the same genus. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)
CommonNames: Ribbed Casemaker Moth; Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Ribbed Casemaker

File.Name: i416d_i416-019.jpg
Notes: 26636638767 Here is an opened Habanero Chile showing the seeds inside. This chile has formidable heat, concentrated mostly in the white placental material that surrounds the seeds. If you remove the seeds and the white material you can see running down the back of the pod, the heat is manageable. I usually eat them in the form of Marie Sharp's Habanero Pepper Sauce which is the salsa I've come to depend on - a proud product of Belize. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Condiment". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)
CommonNames: Habanero Chile; Marie Sharp's Habanero Pepper Sauce

File.Name: i417a_i417-052.jpg
Notes:
41489406992 I noticed this tiny insect on a catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) when I was looking for galls today. My first thought was that it's a tiny psyllid (Psylloidea, Hemiptera) insect - but after hitting the books, I believe it is a barklouse in the order Psocodea. It might well be the species Graphopsocus cruciatus which has distinctive wing marking, see the photos at BugGuide. Good heavens, was it really introduced from Asia or Europe in 1930 as Wikipedia says?? The reach of introduced species is remarkable and disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2018)

Just two photos today. Our power was off all day for scheduled maintenance. When it finally came back on, my computer wouldn't start up! No problem, it turned out to be a bad power cord, but it took me a while to isolate the problem. I'm ready to relax.
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Psocodea; Graphopsocus cruciatus
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Psocodea; Graphopsocus cruciatus

File.Name: i417b_i417-044.jpg
Notes: 41529504851 Here is a tiny wasp gall on a staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I only found a few of the galls. I'm still not sure whose galls these are, but they are definitely made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), see this photo of a different wasp. I believe it is the spring gall of a wasp that makes a different summer gall with two alternating generations. It might be the spring gall of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus), see this photo. Or it might be the spring gall of the Kernel Flower Gall Wasp (Callirhytis serricornis), see this photo on the right. The galls are tiny, about 1/8 inch long. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. It is an odd year. I found the first oak catkins way back in February, see this photo - and here they are still flowering two months later after getting a boost from our good March rains. I'm not surprised that the wasps are confused, compare this photo from a better year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Two-horned Gall Wasp; Kernel Flower Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Callirhytis serricornis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Callirhytis serricornis

File.Name: i418a_i418-001.jpg
Notes:
40656853545 Here is a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider sitting with prey in the inner circle of its web. It's the same spider I showed last week in this photo, though I got the present photo today. It shows what I call the "inner circle" of its web. The thick white cottony webbing running through the center of the web is the spider's stabilimentum, which these spiders usually decorate with the bodies of their prey - but this spider still needs to redecorate! I guess the recent winds blew its old web apart, and it had to start over. These spiders always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd, males and females both - see my photo two shots back. This time I will add my photo to the Web Wednesday group - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2018)

It was mostly overcast and breezy this morning when I was out getting my photos, then it mostly cleared up. I reckon this was the first sign of our next little storm that's due tonight. This looks to be another moisture-starved storm that will bring more wind than rain. Bah.

File.Name: i418b_i417-011.jpg
Notes: 27679531078 This is a humble Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly resting on the ground amid the weedy grasses that are its host plant. It's interesting that this appears to be the light-colored summer form of this butterfly - rather than the darker spring form shown in this photo which was also taken in April a few years ago. Butterflies have been scarce this spring, so maybe they're confused by the weather too! These butterflies have a weak flight like a moth, but that doesn't mean they are easy to approach. This is a tight crop, and I was happy to get the shot. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2018)

File.Name: i418c_i415-023.jpg
Notes: 40837844454 Here is another photo of mating pair of Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spiders doing it in the air suspended by their webs - see this photo taken at the same time. It was hard to get a sharp photo of these tiny spiders with the breeze and their constant motion, but maybe this is sharp enough considering the subject matter! That's the smaller male on the right and the female to the left - they look quite different, though they both have that characteristic hump on their backs. You can see a glint of something yellow down below that might be a drop of sperm. I have a closer shot of the male spider in this photo. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)

File.Name: i418d_i413-002.jpg
Notes: 41508149612 Here is a little beetle taking wing from a tuft of little spines on a prickly pear cactus in our garden. Those tiny cactus spines are really "glochids" - those irritating tufted spines that are nearly impossible to remove, though I suppose they're not so tiny to the beetle. It's quite a feat for the beetle to fold and unfold its wings under its hard wing-covers so quickly. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2018)

File.Name: i419a_i419-001.jpg
Notes:
40678581205 This is a deformed leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) which has been invaded by a rust fungus (Pucciniales, Basidiomycotina). This rust fungus sure looks like photos of Gymnoconia nitens or Arthuriomyces peckianus. The plants completely change when they are infected - the leaflets are thin and widely spaced, and the stems have no thorns, see this photo. I find this every year on a few plants in the canyon. It doesn't seem to be getting worse over the years. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2018)

We did get about 1/10 inch of rain last night, more than I was expecting. It was cold, in the mid-30s, not freezing but the higher mountains actually got a dusting of snow. The day started with drizzle and ended with 30-40 mph gusts of wind wind wind. Our NWS Wind Advisory ends early tomorrow morning, and I hope that's the end of it.

File.Name: i419b_i419-023.jpg
Notes:
40859856424 Here are three different galls on three different kinds of oaks. The galls have something in common - they are all succulent and red, growing with new leaves, and I don't know the cause of any of them. Maybe they are all made by little Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), see this photo - but maybe not. I don't think any of them is described in Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006).

On the left is the gall I call a "Painted Toenail Gall" on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia. I found that odd gall in the center on a stem of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis). I found the galls on the right on new leaves of an interesting oak in our mountains that is either Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei) or Quercus wislizeni. The gall grows along the central petiole of the new leaves. I repeat that I don't know the cause of any of these galls! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2018)

File.Name: i419c_i417-026.jpg
Notes: 41529762442 Here are new galls and leaves on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I believe these are the galls of the Fuzzy Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera. They are small "detachable galls" that often stay attached to the stems for years and turn black with fungi. It's curious that these galls are common in some years but skip other years. I have another photo of these galls at an earlier stage a month ago. The galls are stem galls. These scrubby oaks are "semi-deciduous" - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring. The gall wasps are attuned to this schedule, and most leaf galls don't start until after the new leaves appear. I'm sure they're getting started right now. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2018)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: i419d_i415-054.jpg
Notes: 40677677305 I found this furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a flower stalk of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) that's still in bud. It was a chilly morning, and I reckon the fly was trying to warm up. The fly is probably genus Bombylius, maybe the Greater Bee Fly (Bombylius major). This is a nice shot of the fly's rear end for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday and also a nice shot of the fly for Fly Day Friday. I reckon that's legal - today is still Thursday by my PDT local time but it's also Friday by Flickr/GMT/UTC time - so HBBBT and HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2018)

File.Name: i420a_i420-011.jpg
Notes:
39784315520 "Beautiful - but what is it??"

These are new leaves unrolling from a leaf bud of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). What's striking is that the new leaves seem to be already infected by the tumor-like galls of the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera - see this photo. They give the leaves their ear-like quality. Apparently the aphids get into the leaves while they are still in bud. These are familiar galls to me, but I'm still surprised by their life history. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2018)

It was warmer today, and the big winds died down. There was still a breeze, but not too bad. A nice day!

File.Name: i420b_i420-046.jpg
Notes: 39783966250 This is the real "California Poppy" in our neck of the woods - Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family. If you look close, you can see that this one lacks the "receptacle rim" or collar below the flowers that the real California Poppy (E. californica) has. Compare this photo and see this one for a comparison shot. These flowers are usually more yellow than the others. This one often grows as a fire-follower, see this photo for lots of the flowers in the Santa Ynez Valley. I found this single plant up here in the mountains along West Camino Cielo road today. I wonder how it got here? (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2018)

File.Name: i420c_i420-001.jpg
Notes: 40699978105 I believe these are tiny caterpillars of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae , Lepidoptera) emerging from a egg case on a concrete block retaining wall. It looks like you can see another egg inside the case? The caterpillars are really tiny - only about 500 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Hah - that's still larger than the tiny fly in my [Previous] photo! I'm not crazy about this photo, but it was loosing the light as I got this photo. I'll try again tomorrow - IF the caterpillars are still there. There's an excellent photo essay on "Life History of Western Tussock Moths, Orgyia vetusta" by Joyce Gross here. In time, the caterpillars will develop fanciful clown colors, but they start plain. I have another shot of hatchling caterpillars here. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2018)

File.Name: i420d_i419-027.jpg
Notes: 40880561114 I guess that this is a Gall Midge or a Wood Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae,Diptera. It's very tiny - only about 400 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.6 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I happened to notice it land on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) and got the photo as best I could. Hah - it's a 1:1 1200x800 crop from an 6000x4000 original! If it is a gall midge, them you can see the work of one in this photo - but I have no idea if this one is that kind of midge. It's hard to see, but it looks like its face is almost all eyes. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2018)

File.Name: i421a_i421-004.jpg
Notes:
41572131572 This is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" on a new flower of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. Many insects seem to know this trick, and it's the only way large bees like this could reach the nectar in these deep-seated flowers that are typically visited by hummingbirds. I've seen these large bees do this trick on many different flowers, but I believe this is the first time I've ever seen them on the Hummingbird Sage. I also believe this is a male bee with that white mane - see this photo for my evidence. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2018)

Nice day today - it was quite warm, in the low-80s. There was an annoying breeze from the south this morning that was just enough to keep everything in motion. I believe this is an "eddy wind" that forms as marine winds from the north wrap around Point Conception and come at us from the south. It's the wind that I've said feels like "fog winds" - without the fog.

File.Name: i421b_i421-047.jpg
Notes: 39803880570 I found this gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. I believe it's a Spiny Assassin Bug in the genus Sinea, though maybe not Sinea spinipes as I've called it before. It does have spiny front legs, though it's hard to see in my photo. These are tough-looking predators! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2018)

File.Name: i421c_i421-037.jpg
Notes: 40900846314 Here is an ant tending its aphids on a just-opened flower cluster of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). If you look on the flower to the right, you can see what the ant is protecting its aphids from - a tiny parasitic wasp! The wasp's "stinger" is really an ovipositor - designed to stick an aphid and deliver a wasp egg inside. (The larva will kill its host, so it's more properly a parasitoid wasp.) I guess the wasp is a Braconid Wasp (Braconidae, Hymenoptera), though are other possibilities. I have no idea about the ant or the aphids. I like this photo - it shows the cooperation and competition that make up nature, just right for Earth Day. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2018)

File.Name: i421d_i421-015.jpg
Notes:
40900685054 Here is another tiny caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae , Lepidoptera) that has just emerged from its egg case on a concrete block retaining wall. See my photo < href="https://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/40699978105/">yesterday that shows the whole nest. The caterpillars are really tiny - this one was about 600 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) In time, the caterpillars will develop fanciful clown colors, but they start plain like this. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2018)

(It's a pretty harsh landscape where they are, but there's a honeysuckle vine a few inches away. If they don't find it themselves, I'll help them out.)

File.Name: i422a_i422-023.jpg
Notes:
39828466000 These are odd new flowers on a shrub of native California Ash aka Two-petal Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. The flowers live up to their species name "dipetala" in having just two petals in addition to two large stamens that remind me of lemon popsicles. It's also kind of an odd shrub up here in the mountains. I usually find these shrubs near the Santa Ynez River, but there are a few plants along East Camino Cielo road in the mountains. They don't have a lot of flowers in this drought year, but there are some. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2018)

File.Name: i422b_i422-012.jpg
Notes: 41596136482 I was looking down into a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family, and I noticed the odd pentagonal structure in the photo on the left. Then I opened up a flower and got the photo on the right! You can see that the orange structure is made up of stamens like a brush to deliver pollen to any insect with a proboscis long enough to get past it to the nectar at the base - like this photo and this photo and this photo. Pretty cool! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2018)

File.Name: i422c_i421-021.jpg
Notes: 27766721218 I was trying to get a photo of this tiny fly on a leaf when it laid an egg! Look close at the tip of its abdomen on the right. The plant is a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I'm not sure what kind of fly this is, but one possibility Is a Leaf Miner Fly in the family Agromyzidae, compare this photo at BugGuide. That makes sense that it would lay a single egg. It's really tiny, about 675 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.6 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2018)

I believe these ants are the same California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae) that get into our home in the woods, see this photo. So far, they're not so bad this year. File.Name: i423a_i423-002.jpg
Notes:
27790504388 Here is a lizard with blue mating colors - and ticks! It's a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) - or more properly a Coast Range Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis bocourtii). The ticks are probably Western Black-legged Ticks (Ixodes pacificus), see this photo - but these are the six-legged nymphs. The lizards do a remarkable service with the ticks, protecting us from Lyme disease. As the web site CaliforniaHerps has it:

"In California, western black-legged ticks (deer ticks) are the primary carriers of Lyme disease. Very tiny nymphal deer ticks are more likely to carry the disease than adults. A protein in the blood of Western Fence Lizards kills the bacterium in these nymphal ticks when they attach themselves to a lizard and ingest the lizard's blood. This could explain why Lyme disease is less common in California than it is in some areas such as the Northeastern states, where it is epidemic."

Sound fantastic, but apparently it's true. I get ticked often enough that I'm grateful for the lizards - but aside from the ticks, I love the attitude of this guy! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2018)

It was another nice day, highs around 80 degrees and not much wind. I've switched over to shorts. Alas - with the nice weather, I feel like I'm coming down with a bug. *Blah*

File.Name: i423c_i422-009.jpg

File.Name: i423d_i423-028.jpg
Notes: 41655521871 This is a small bit of trail debris at the start of my trail today - some sort of plastic. The problem is that pieces of plastic break down by abrasion and weathering into smaller pieces, and these become micro pieces, and "so proceed ad infinitum" - as Jonathan Swift's poem ("On Poetry: A Rhapsody", 1733) has it, changing the context a bit. Please be careful with your trash! This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Plastic". The whole photo is about an inch across, and I avoided the temptation to "seed" a photo. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2018)

File.Name: i424a_i424-001.jpg
Notes:
26813805257 Here are two shots of a cute little Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). This is an adult with fully developed wings, the first one I've seen this year. It probably came from the nymph in this photo. I believe it is genus Danepteryx in the family Tropiduchidae. It's tiny, about 1/8 inch long. Those skinny wings can't be much help for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2018)

I was doing my duty when I got a couple of new photos today, but my heart wasn't in it. It was cooler today, and there was an annoying steady breeze from the south that kept everything in motion. I definitely feel crappy with some sort of fever bug.

File.Name: i424b_i424-017.jpg
Notes: 41642361042 These caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly are finishing off a leaf of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's been fun to watch - the caterpillars started at the top of the plant and are working their way down the stem leaving nothing. There's another untouched plant next to it, but I hope the caterpillars get their fill and leave that one alone to make flowers. (See this photo for the flowers.) It's interesting that this plant is still a real "scroph" the Scrophulariaceae plant family. This large family has been split up, but the caterpillars still prefer the larger group - like Climbing Penstemon and Bush Monkey Flower. See this photo for a list of host plants and their new families. Most of the caterpillars I've been finding this year are this kind. I expect a lot of checkerspot butterflies - but maybe not much else. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; California Figwort; California Bee Plant
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: i424c_i423-018.jpg
Notes: 40971792614 This is a large foliose gall on stem of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family with flowers and three-veined leaves. This is a variety of the shrub that is not at all hairy, known as Jim Brush (Ceanothus oliganthus var. sorediatus. The galls are quite large, probably the size of a ping pong ball. I believe this is the gall of a Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera, but it might also be one of its kin. See the discussion at this photo. For some reason, this one shrub has lots of these galls, but they are scarce otherwise. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2018)
CommonNames: Hairy Ceanothus; Jim Brush; Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge

File.Name: i424d_i423-021.jpg
Notes: 41641825362 I believe the bumps on this leaf are "Erineum Galls" made by Eriophyid mites in the family Eriophyidae of the Acari. I found them yesterday on a leaf of Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus, Styracaceae) - the same native shrub that has the beautiful white flowers in this photo. (You can see a flower bud hiding behind the leaf on the left.) This is another gall that's not described Ron Russo's fine "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). Hah, I'm starting to think that every tree and shrub (at least) has some sort of galls! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2018)

File.Name: i425a_i425-031.jpg
Notes:
41667054992 Here are a pair of small "true bugs" on a plant of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - a plant that the caterpillars haven't found yet, see this photo. They appear to be Stink Bugs in the family Pentatomidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera. At first I was worried that they were some horrible pest like Bagrada Bugs. After spending some time at BugGuide, I noticed the species Cosmopepla conspicillaris and jumped to a conclusion. Now I believe it is the similar Cosmopepla uhleri - a native bug that belongs here. Of course I might always be wrong! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2018)

I'm still feeling crummy with a nasty bug that's more damaging (to me) than the one in my photo. I did my duty and did get a couple of new photos today. I've been coughing so much that my ribs hurt. I hope my comments make sense. *Blah*

File.Name: i425b_i425-008.jpg
Notes: 41666828402 I found this neat little caterpillar on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) this morning. I'm pretty it's another caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriinae , Lepidoptera), though it's a later instar that the hatchling caterpillar in this photo a few days ago. That one was less than 1/10 inch, and this one is more like 1/4 inch. In time, the caterpillars will develop fanciful clown colors, but they start plain and slowly get their colors with successive molts. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Western Tussock Moth
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriinae ; Lepidoptera

File.Name: i425c_i424-009.jpg
Notes: 41666543292 This photo shows the end of the main structural "bridge thread" of the web of a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider's web where it attaches to a leaf. This is the thread that supports the whole web. The bridge seems to be made of multiple strands of silk that are individually anchored to a partially eaten leaf of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). Sure, simple threads fit together to make a stronger cable - but the support will be stronger with lots of small attachments, like velcro. I have another photo showing similar spider engineering. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2018)
CommonNames: Trashline Orb Weaver; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i425d_i420-018.jpg
Notes: 26838764957 Here is webbing on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) - but I'm pretty sure it's the webbing of a caterpillar and not a spider. If you look close, you can see an accumulation of frass (aka poop) inside the web. I even have a photo of the caterpillar that lives inside, see this photo. Spiders aren't the only ones, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2018)

File.Name: i426a_i426-006.jpg
Notes:
26859612787 It's easy to recognize a lupine (Fabaceae) with those "palmate leaves" like the fingers on you hand. It gets harder when you try to recognize species of these plants! Here are three different little lupines that hide among the weeds along the mountain roads. That's the Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) on the left, the most widespread of the three. In the middle is the Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) which is recognizable by its bunt-tipped leaflets. These plants grow quite luxuriously after wildfires, see this photo taken after the White Fire a few years ago in Paradise. The hairy lupine on the right is the smallest of these, and I really don't know what it is. I've called it the south coast variant of Coulter's Lupine (Lupinus sparsiflorus ssp. inopinatus) in the past, but I don't know if that's right. It's only a few inches tall. It grows in disturbed places along the road in our canyon. The raindrops are from a bit of rain on April 19 when I got this photo - even the tiny lupines catch raindrops! Help will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2018)

I'm still feeling crummy with a nasty bug. I did my duty and did get a couple of new photos today. I've been coughing so much that my ribs hurt. I hope my comments make sense. *Blah*
CommonNames: Miniature Lupine; Collar Lupine; Coulter's Lupine
ScientificName: Fabaceae; Lupinus bicolor; Lupinus truncatus; Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus
associatedTaxa: Fabaceae; Lupinus bicolor; Lupinus truncatus; Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus

File.Name: i426b_i426-005.jpg
Notes: 40828773925 I believe this is a cute little Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). There are many different kinds of these weevils, but maybe this one is species Thricolepis inornata? (Thanks Alice!). This one is small, about 940 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.7 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Needless to say, this is a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2018)

File.Name: i426c_i425-006.jpg
Notes: 41009762914 This is an impressive wasp with a super "stinger" that is really an ovipositor for laying eggs - like a flying hypodermic needle! I'm pretty sure it's an Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae). I found it in the morning on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Of course I saved it for . . . HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2018)

File.Name: i426d_i425-026.jpg
Notes: 41009372654 Here is another photo of one of the "true bugs" on a plant of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - see my [Previous] photo and Thijs de Graaf's comment. I believe he's right that this insect is Cosmopepla uhleri and not C. conspicillaris. The main difference is that the scutellum is uniformly dark with no pale margin around the tip - like the white "happy face" visible in this photo at BugGuide. I was taken in by the overwhelming record of the species C. conspicillaris in the California data display at BugGuide. It's also of note that several of the photos at BugGuide show the insects on the same plant. Well, I've been sick. This is a nice butt shot as well as the answer to a taxonomic question, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2018)
ScientificName: Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Cosmopepla uhleri; C. conspicillaris
associatedTaxa: Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Cosmopepla uhleri; C. conspicillaris

File.Name: i427a_i425-022.jpg
Notes:
26880250177 My red shirt was hanging over my chair by the (dry) creek, and this little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) took a liking to it, coming back again and again. It's a handsome fly with those speckled wings and grizzled abdomen. There are a number of such flies in the tribe Villini of the subfamily Anthracinae - but who knows. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2018)

Well, I did my duty today . . . not to get new photos, but to go to my doctor for this bug that's been getting me down this week. Now I have inhalers and antibiotics. The good news is that it's not pneumonia. It was too windy for photos by the time I got home, so I took a nap instead. I don't like feeling sick. I don't do it well.

File.Name: i427b_i421-054.jpg
Notes: 41748559161 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. It's another fly that looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but who knows. It's difficult enough getting photos of these flies, but the real challenge is to figure out what they're doing in the woods. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2018)

File.Name: i427c_421-038.jpg
Notes: 40848764025 Here is a colony of aphids on a new flower cluster of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The aphids come in all shapes and sizes - but the most interesting ones are at the light-colored ones at the very base of the stem. I believe these are "aphid mummies" that are carrying the larva of parasitic wasps inside that will eventually kill them - see this photo for the gruesome result. This is the same plant as this photo of the aphids, which also shows a parasitic wasp and an ant holding it off. This is how the ants earn their keep among the aphids, with the reward of sweet honeydew. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2018)

File.Name: i427d_i421-024.jpg
Notes: 40848639965 Here is another photo of tiny wasp gall on a catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm still not sure whose galls these are, but they are definitely made by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), see this photo of a different wasp. I believe this is the spring gall of a wasp that makes a different summer gall with two alternating generations. It might be the spring gall of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus), see this photo. Or it might (more likely) be the spring gall of the Kernel Flower Gall Wasp (Callirhytis serricornis), see this photo on the right. The galls are tiny, about 1/8 inch long. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. It's been an odd year. I found the first oak catkins way back in February, see this photo - and here they are still flowering two months later after getting a boost from our good March rains. I'm not surprised that the wasps are confused, compare this photo from a better year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Two-horned Gall Wasp; Kernel Flower Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Callirhytis serricornis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Callirhytis serricornis

File.Name: i428a_i428-023.jpg
Notes:
41727377322 This photo shows something remarkable - the pupal husk of an emerging two-winged Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) coming out of a gall on native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I first noticed something similar in this photo. Then I did some research and found this on page 61 of Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) today, describing the ways of Gall Wasps (Cecidomyiidae): ". . . the mature pupae exit only about two-thirds of the way out, using the pressure of the last part of the passage to hold them firmly in place while they complete their transformation into adults." That pretty much perfectly describes what I see in my photo. I take it as settled that these coffeeberry galls are the work of Gall Midges. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2018)

It was windy all night and day. The oaks are still in flower, and all the pollen in the air sure isn't helping my congestion. But despite the wind, I am feeling better - coughing less and sleeping better, maybe the meds help. Our NWS Wind Advisory runs through tomorrow night, but it would be helpful if the wind ended sooner!
ScientificName: Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i428b_i428-007.jpg
Notes: 26899856067 Here are a pair of new mushroom-like spring galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They must be new galls - they look new, but they are also growing on new leaves that weren't there a month ago. These are "bisexual galls" because the will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual "oak apple" galls, see this photo, which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that. It's part of what makes then so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2018)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i428c_i428-019.jpg
Notes: 39959896050 I guess this striking little beetle is some kind of Lady Beetle in the family Coccinellidae, but I don't recognize it. I found it on a flower of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). It seemed to be interested in that black "thing" on the calyx of the flower - which I can now see has legs, and is probably what's left of an aphid. I first thought of the Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle (genus Chilocorus) in this photo, but that one is larger and just has two spots. Another possibility is a Spurleg Lady Beetlein genus Brachiacantha. I don't know and help will be appreciated. This beetle seems to have amazing eyes with almost animal-like pupils. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2018)
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; Black Sage; Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle; Spurleg Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Chilocorus; Brachiacantha
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Chilocorus; Brachiacantha

File.Name: i428d_i428-027.jpg
Notes: 40868229875 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm happy to see these flowers because they are a favorite of the larger butterflies like swallowtails and checkerspots which I've had no luck photographing so far this year. And they are just cheerful-looking on a blustery day! The plants have been looking poorly for the last few of drought years, but they are looking better this year. I guess it's the combination of good rain last year followed by good rains in March. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2018)

File.Name: i429a_i429-021.jpg
Notes:
39984238590 This is looking at the upper wings of a gorgeous Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) in the woods today. See my [Previous] photo for a shot under the wings. I suddenly had a panic attack wondering if this butterfly wasn't maybe a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) which I haven't seen yet this year - but the antennae are quite different, more golden, especially on the tips. Panic averted!

This butterfly wasn't flying much, and I suspect it was newly emerged and still getting used to having wings. I tried not to approach it too close or too aggressively - but I had to get a couple of photos! This is the first one I've seen this year. I don't know much about these butterflies, though I see a few every year. My book says they host on composites and only have a single brood. It's interesting that I found this one in almost the same place as my first photo both last year and the year before. I guess these butterflies don't travel much - like me. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2018)

I'd be feeling better by now if the darn wind would go away, but it's been windy all day again and the air is still full of oak pollen. Our NWS Wind Advisory runs out early in the morning, so I'm hoping tomorrow will be better.
CommonNames: Gabb's Checkerspot; Canyon Lupine; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Chlosyne gabbii; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Chlosyne gabbii; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: i429c_i429-039.jpg
Notes: 39983686680 This is a little moth (Lepidoptera) trying to hide under the lip of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's small but not tiny, more than 1/4 inch long. I love that eye. I don't usually try to identify little moths, but it was nice to fond this one just in time for Moth Monday - so HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2018)

File.Name: i429d_i428-013.jpg
Notes: 41748826382 This is a Buprestid Beetle aka Metallic Wood-boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera) on a native head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). Maybe it's a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. There are many different kinds, try a BugGuide search on the genus name! This is the first one I've noticed this year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2018)
CommonNames: Buprestid Beetle; Metallic Wood-boring Beetle; Black Sage; Spotted Flower Buprestid

File.Name: i430a_i430-034.jpg
Notes:
41774219852 Here are lots of new flowers of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. All of the red flowers in my photo are actually galled - they will stay on the plant all year, see this photo for another example. It may be that these galls are similar to the galls I find on flowers of this plant's cousin California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica) which are induced by Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) insects - see this photo. The flowers and galls make an attractive combination. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2018)

The wind did ease up today, but it was chilly and cloudy in its place. I still feel congested, but I'm not letting it get me down. We may even get a splash of rain tomorrow!

File.Name: i430b_i430-046.jpg
Notes: 41773914742 There must be a story to explain this photo. It's a dead honeybee on a new flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. How did it die? Maybe it was beauty killed the bee! The flowers have been going for about two weeks now, see this photo from April 13, and they are still going strong with many more flowers still coming. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. They are pretty common on here on San Marcos Pass - both in woodland and chaparral. They are stunning flowers, worth finding - but not worth dying for! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2018)

File.Name: i430c_i430-038.jpg
Notes: 40917059515 Here are three shots of a large plant of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. They've been going for a couple of weeks now, see this photo of the first flowers on April 13. This plant typically grows as a fire-follower, but this one is growing with the weeds along West Camino Cielo road. This is right on the edge of land that did burn in the Gap Fire back in 2008, but I reckon that now they are just lingering in a disturbed place. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2018)

File.Name: i430d_i430-053.jpg
Notes: 40916635045 This is a handsome little caterpillar in the thick of a flower cluster of native Jim Brush (Ceanothus oliganthus var. sorediatus, Rhamnaceae). It's small and hairy, maybe 1/2 inch long. I believe this is the caterpillar of a Ceanothus Nola Moth (Nola minna) - I've shown one before in this photo on a different Ceanothus species. I wouldn't recognize it as an adult if it landed on my nose, but the caterpillar is quite attractive with that patchwork design. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2018)
