File.Name: i501a_i501-018.jpg
Notes:
27970097308 This is another gorgeous Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly trying to warm up on a small plant of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) surrounded by weedy annual grasses. I you compare this photo of another butterfly taken a few days ago, you can see why I was confused by that other one. They both have black and white antennae and a fringe around the wings. I guess they are the same kind, but they sure look different! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2018)

It was chilly and gloomy today . . . and then we did get about 3/10 inch of rain! That was a nice surprise, appropriate for May Day.

File.Name: i501b_i501-024.jpg
Notes:
26969749917 This is a Timema insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) today. I believe this is a female with symmetric cerci, though it's hard to see in this photo. This one is green with a white stripe down its back, but other are brown.

There's a story about this one. I've been looking in vain for these insects, but I saw one this morning climbing up the wall in our bedroom at home! It was right above where I dumped my clothes last night. I reckon I inadvertently swept it on up on my shirt yesterday and carried it home. I got a nice photo this morning in a white custard dish that I might post tomorrow. I then drove back to (probably) the same place where I picked it up, and released in on this Chamise branch - getting another photo. After this one ran away, I noticed the Cicada in my [Previous] photo in a nearby shrub. And then I noticed another Timema insect in a different shrub!

Timemas are atypical walkingstick insects. There's some discussion of them in Smithsonian Magazine, but it's better to go to the source of Christina Sandoval - the "cristinae" of the specific name. The Timema page at BugGuide is excellent. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2018)
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: i501c_i501-031.jpg
Notes: 40939223145 I found this handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) hiding on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) today. Maybe it's genus Platypedia? I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, they just make the clicking noise that we've been hearing up in the oaks. I believe we have different cicadas later in summer that do sing. (I have a hard time hearing cicadas. They sing at the the same frequency as my tinnitus, and I just can't pick them out. But it's not so bad to have a personal background chorus of cicadas!) I only found this critter because I was looking for Timema insects in the Chamise - see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2018)
ScientificName: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Platypedia
associatedTaxa: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Platypedia

File.Name: i501d_i501-007.jpg
Notes: 27969221318 Here is the first flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus, Liliaceae) that I've seen this year growing in the woodland along our road. It wasn't here yesterday, and here it is today. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily. They are a "tender trap" for pollinators - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those hairs without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. They are very elegant, one of my favorite flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2018)

File.Name: i502a_i502-025.jpg
Notes:
41816022552 Rain yesterday, termites today! We had a hatch of these winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) this morning. These are arising from the edge of our chip seal road on the other side of the canyon, well away from our house. Maybe they are Subterranean Termites (family Rhinotermitidae)? It was a small rain, only about 3/10 inch, so I reckon that means it's not so much the rain as the idea of rain that brings out the termites. I guess this is how they synchronize their swarms with other termite colonies so that they'll all emerge at the same time, thereby increasing the chance of mating across swarms. We see these termites every year, see this photo from last year for an especially large hatch at home. I don't believe these termites do structural damage. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2018)

It felt like more rain today, but cloudy and chilly is all we got. I'm very slowly getting some energy back from that nasty virus.

File.Name: i502b_i502-019.jpg
Notes: 40959901835 This is a tiny spider on a dead leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It looks something like an Orb Web Weaver spider in the family Araneidae, but I can't find it in my book. It's really tiny, about 870 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.4 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This spider seems to have a "glittery patch" on its back - maybe glandular? It's an attractive spider. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2018)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkeyflower; Orb Web Weaver

File.Name: i502c_i429-045.jpg
Notes: 41141037394 Here are three shots of a tiny (but interesting!) spider on the side of our house. I'm pretty sure that it's the Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers. Back in January, I was finding a relative of this spider known as a Triangle Web Spider (genus Hyptiotes) that spins a little triangular web, see this photo. The spider in my photo today is similar, though it spins a proper orb web that is mostly flat. You can barely see the dark crescent-shaped cribellum of the spider in my upper-left photo, see
CommonNames: Feather-legged Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Hackled Band Orb Weavers; Triangle Web Spider

File.Name: i502d_i501-001.jpg
Notes: 27989292888 This is the Timema insect (Timema cristinae, Timematidae, Phasmida) that I found climbing up the wall in our bedroom yesterday! It was right above where I dump my clothes at night, and I reckon I inadvertently swept it on up on my shirt and carried it home. I got this nice photo in a white custard dish - and then drove back to (probably) the same place where I picked it up, and released it onto a Chamise branch - see this photo for the whole story and some additional info. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2018)

File.Name: i503a_i503-039.jpg
Notes:
41834240722 Here is the first Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly I've managed to photograph this year. It's perched on a flowering plant of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti, Solanaceae) among the weedy grasses on the fire break I was walking today. This is the first one I've managed to photograph, but I suspect that many more are coming - the caterpillars have been very common this spring, see this photo and any more. Yep, this butterfly looks nothing like the odd Gabb's Checkerspot in this photo, look at those antennae - but it had me going for a few minutes! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2018)

Butterflies and wildflowers and insects in general have been scarce so far this year, but I reckon everything is happening at once now. It was 20 degrees warmer today than yesterday, in the mid-70s, with calm winds - very nice. It feels like spring!

File.Name: i503b_i503-041.jpg
Notes: 27008642007 It's already the month of May, but I believe these are mating March Flies (Bibionidae, Diptera) in a shrub of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae). They may be species Bibio albipennis with those long white hairs. I seem to suddenly have an abundance of fly photos to post! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2018)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Bibio albipennis
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Bibio albipennis

File.Name: i503c_i502-016.jpg
Notes: 40069166230 This is a golden Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) trying to warm up on a chilly day on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's the species Syrphus opinator which is said to be the common species in central California, see BugGuide. If you know what to look for, you can the "spurious veins" on its wings. The fly wasn't moving much, and I managed to get this good shot of its rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2018)

File.Name: i503d_i502-030.jpg
Notes: 41875950791 This is the rear end of a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) tucked in between leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) on a chilly day. At least it is bee-like. I suppose it could also be a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) like this one, though I've never seen one with those yellow colors before. Alas - I tried to move the leaves for a better photo, and the bee got away from me. I did manage to get one photo that isn't too bad, and I'll post it in a couple of days if I can figure out what it is. In the meantime, this is a fine butt shot - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Coast Live Oak; Cimbicid Sawfly
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cimbicidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cimbicidae

File.Name: i504a_i504-004.jpg
Notes:
40998079625 I was surprised to find this handsome Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly taking moisture from a garden path today. It must be newly emerged to have such brilliant colors! I usually find these butterflies on warm days in January and February, though I have found them before in July and August. The combination of chocolate brown wings with creamy yellow margins and blue highlights is spectacular! This is being a tough year for insects in general and butterflies in particular, but see my [Previous] photo - maybe things are looking up? (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2018)

It was another 10 degrees warmer today than yesterday, in the mid-80s, with mostly calm winds - very nice. It feels like spring!

File.Name: i504b_i504-019.jpg
Notes: 27028294227 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly ovipositing on a leaf of native Hollyleaf Cherry aka Islay (Prunus ilicifolia, Rosaceae). I've always seen them lay their eggs on shrubs in the Rhamnaceae plant family - shrubs like Ceanothus, Coffee Berry, and Redberry, see this photo. I looked it up in my book, and sure enough it's listed as a host plant. Jeffrey Caldwell also mentions it is in his excellent Notes on Lepidoptera Values of California Native Plants list. This is also the very first of these butterflies that I've managed to photograph this year! Like I said before, this is being a tough year for insects in general and butterflies in particular. On the other hand, see my [Next] photo - maybe things are looking up? (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2018)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i504c_i502-042.jpg
Notes: 27027971817 I believe from the round head and flattened proboscis that this is Dance Fly in the family Empididae, possibly genus Empis. It's perched on a leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). It's small, about 1010 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.0 mm, about 3/32 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I seem to find these flies either working the willow catkins by the creek, see this photo - or I find them living as predators, see this photo. (And this photo seems to show both modes!) This one seem larger that the flies in my other photos, and I wonder if it's a gravid female. It's an interesting fly, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2018)

File.Name: i504d_i501-004.jpg
Notes: 28026691178 This is looking close at a medium-size Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Tipulomorpha, Diptera) hanging onto a tall columnar cactus stem in the garden. They have wonderful eyes. What's odd is that this is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. Insects have generally been scarce this year, and I take these particular insects as emblematic of that. They re usually common in March and April, but I've only seen a few in his odd weather year that has been mostly dry with a wet March. At least I found this one, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2018)

File.Name: i505a_i505-002.jpg
Notes:
41018139455 This is the first flower I've seen this year of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. This plants came up on the wild edge of our garden, and so far the caterpillars haven't found it, see this photo! The flowers are more "interesting" than "beautiful" - a challenge to photograph, growing on five foot tall stems that seem to sway with the slightest breeze. Of course it's even better when you get a bee working the flower like this photo on the same plant last year! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2018)

It was another lovely day in the mid-80s, though there were enough high clouds that it felt cooler. There were mostly calm winds - with gusts of real wind. Today's photos are all wildflowers, and why not - It feels like spring!

File.Name: i505b_i504-026.jpg
Notes: 41017901535 Here are beautiful new flowers of the uncommon Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. The plants have whorls of very skinny leaves, like a lily on a diet. There are only a few plants with flowers in this place, but they are hanging on after our drought years. These plants are growing in a place where the sun rarely shines - but I managed to get just a hint of sunshine on the flower on the left by bending the plant over and holding it with a stick. (Sure, it recovered.) This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.) Like all of our native plants this year, this one is about one month late - last year I found the flowers on April 2, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2018)

File.Name: i505c_i503-008.jpg
Notes: 41199467164 Here are lovely flowers of native Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The seeds of this plant are nutritious and have a reputation for being an energy source, but this is not the chia that is available commercially. That one is Salvia hispanica which is native to Mexico and cultivated there. We had some seeds sprout last year and they finally flowered - deep blue just like our native. And yes, these are the seeds in those Chia Pets. See this page at Wikipedia for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2018)
ScientificName: Salvia columbariae; Lamiaceae; Salvia hispanica; Chia Pets
associatedTaxa: Salvia columbariae; Lamiaceae; Salvia hispanica; Chia Pets

File.Name: i505d_i503-026.jpg
Notes: 41917016131 These are shy new flowers of native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's one of a group of flowers known as Farewell-to-Spring, though this one prefers shady canyons rather than full sun like most of the others. They are considered harbingers of the end of spring, but it doesn't feel that way yet this year. I reckon the real farewell-to-spring comes when the weedy grasses start to release their nasty seeds that get stuck in your socks and shoes, and that hasn't happened yet. But it is an odd year for our flowering plants. We got most of our rain late this year, in the month of March. The result is that all of our plants are late, and you can find just about all of our flowering plants in the woods right now - native Milk Maids and Shooting Stars together with these Clarkia. I'm not complaining, a drought year with late rain seems to be better for the plants. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2018)

File.Name: i506a_i506-041.jpg
Notes:
28069954898 Look close at the hole in the center . . . here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) looking right at me from its nest in sandy soil! They make remarkable nests like little sand pedestals. I saw the bee go into the hole, set up my camera, and waited . . . and waited . . . until the bee appeared. I got several photos like this and then a blur as the bee flew off. I waited for a long while for it to come back, but I never did get a photo. It was interesting that when the bee was poised in the hole and I took a photo, the bee immediately disappeared back into its burrow. I guess they can hear the snap of my shutter? At first I only saw this one bee, but as I sat there I started to notice more. There are a dozen or more of the bees with individual burrows in this place. I'm not sure because I never did get a photo, but maybe they are genus Andrena, also known as Digger Bees? (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2018)

It was curious that we were under a NWS Wind Advisory this morning - and it was dead calm. There were enough clouds in the sky today to cut the heat a few degrees, but it was still quite pleasant. I need to go down into Los Angeles tomorrow, but I should be back in time to post a few photos.

File.Name: i506b_i506-025.jpg
Notes: 40133970010 This tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) has brought down a little nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) that's only a bit bigger than the spider is. The spider is only about 1320 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5.2 mm, less than 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Yeah, this is a brutal photo, but nature is sometimes like that. I never would have noticed this scene, but it was near the wild bee nest in my [Next] photo where I spent some time waiting . . . and so found something else! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2018)

File.Name: i506c_i504-040.jpg
Notes: 27071156237 Hah - you don't need any warning to keep your hands away from this spider! This is a female Western Black Widow (Latrodectus hesperus, Theridiidae) spider on her cobweb-like web. I don't see them very often, but I know they're out there - a good reason not to stick your hands into dark places! A couple of years ago I found a male of the species which looks quite different - see this photo and this photo. The second of these photos shows that the male also has a red hourglass-shaped mark under the abdomen, but it's not nearly so impressive as the lady's mark. Surely the whole point of that red hourglass is a warning to keep away! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2018)

File.Name: i506d_i503-023.jpg
Notes: 41940507621 This is a new stem with small toothed leaves growing from a trunk of native Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis, Fagaceae). This is an uncommon oak in our mountains, and it's interesting that the older leaves mostly have smooth margins. These new leaves look sharp and jagged, but they are less than an inch long and feel quite soft. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Jagged". The whole stem is only about two inches long - legal for the group. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2018)

File.Name: i507a_i504-031.jpg
Notes:
41965524801 Here are two tiny Midge Flies (Diptera) on the leafy phyllary of a new flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm not sure if these are male-female courting or male-male fighting - I suspect the latter since their rear ends look pretty similar. Maybe they are Gall Midges in the order Cecidomyiidae because I have found galls on these flowers, see this photo. On the other hand I've also found Tephritid Fruit Flies (Tephritidae) ovipositing on these flower buds, see this photo. Whatever they are, these midge flies are really tiny, about 440 pixels long in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.7 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I like the way their wings are fringed with tiny hairs. I'll be off camping in the desert later this week, but this might have been my photo for both the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday and Fly Day Friday groups - so HBBBT and HFDF in absentia! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2018)

Julie and I spent the day driving down to LA and returning with a rented reconditioned VW Rialta Camper. We got home late, so just two photos tonight. We're heading out to the desert for a few days to see how we like the van. This isn't really a photo trip, but I suspect I'll get few - I'm not expecting many wildflowers though, it's been that kind of year. I'm not sure yet if we're leaving tomorrow and returning on Friday or leaving on Wednesday and returning on Saturday. I'll know tomorrow morning, and maybe I'll have new photos tomorrow or maybe not.
CommonNames: Midge Flies; Canyon Sunflower; Gall Midges; Tephritid Fruit Flies
ScientificName: Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Cecidomyiidae; Tephritidae; in absentia
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Cecidomyiidae; Tephritidae; in absentia

File.Name: i507b_i504-006.jpg
Notes: 41965335411 I believe this little spider is a Long-jawed Orb Weaver in the Tetragnathidae family, probably genus Metellina. It has a humped back, very long front legs, and It spins a small perfect orb web that is distinctive for being mostly horizontal. This spider is sitting on its "inner web" - the web continues further out from the hub. I'll be off camping in the desert later this week, but this might have been my photo for both the Web Wednesday and the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday groups - so HWW and HBBBT in absentia! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2018)

File.Name: i508a_i508-041.jpg
Notes:
27117405397 Here is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) filling the bowl of a blue (oh-so-blue) flower of native Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii) in the Boraginaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see its long proboscis delving deep into the base of the flower. I was already focused on the flower when the bee suddenly appeared - I like it when that happens! I was surprised to find this beautiful flower in a meadow near home - it's an annual, and perennials are doing better in this drought year. But here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2018)

Julie and I are heading out to the desert in a rental reconditioned VW Rialta Camper tomorrow. This isn't really a photo trip, but I suspect I'll get few. I'm not expecting many wildflowers though, it's been that kind of year. We'll be back late on Saturday.

File.Name: i508b_i508-030.jpg
Notes: 41086138655 This is another galled catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but it's a different gall from the one I usually find like this photo. That one seems to form on individual anthers, but the one in this photo takes over the entire catkin so that only individual anthers protrude from the mass. I believe this is the gall of the Sausage Flower Gall Wasp (Callirhytis congregata, Cynipidae). Looking at my notes, I've only photographed this gall once before in 2004, see this old photo at Fotolog. I guess it's not very common. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but I believe this galled catkin will stay on the tree long past blooming. Yes, the catkin was really horizontal - not the usual orientation. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Sausage Flower Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis congregata; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis congregata; Cynipidae

File.Name: i508c_i508-036.jpg
Notes: 41085964265 Here are new flowers on a shrub of native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family. Like many of our early-flowering natives, this one is flowering again after first showing flowers in February. It's an uncommon plant in our mountains that I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines. The foliage is holly-like and beautiful - but very thin, like paper, not what its looks like. These plants haven't flowered for a few years of drought, but I reckon they are responding to last year's good rain and our good March rains this year. This plant was growing up an embankment, so I couldn't get closer. This is as open and beautiful as I've ever seen the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2018)

File.Name: i508d_i506-046.jpg
Notes: 41267082834 This is a wasp hiding under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - which makes a perfect light tent in the bright sunshine. I guess it's another Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae), though it doesn't have the singer/ovipositor of the wasp in this photo. Maybe it's a male? (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2018)

File.Name: i514a_i511-040.jpg
Notes:
40310118580 This is a new flower of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris) in the Cactaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see a little black-and-white striped wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) in the center of the flower. I only found a few of these flowers on our desert trip. These cacti lack large spines, but watch out for the annoying "glochids" - the irritating small spines that are impossible to remove. The flowers are beautiful. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 11 May 2018)

The desert was pleasant last week - until our last night when monster winds kicked up. The authorities closed the highway to big trucks and motor homes. We survived. But alas, I had trouble with my legs on this trip. First an injured toe set me to limping, and I guess the limping led to my other knee going out. I've been in considerable pain. It's *very slowly* getting better. I'm glad to have some trip photos to post, as I'm trying not to walk much. Hah - I don't have to try very hard!

File.Name: i514b_i510-048.jpg
Notes: 28244187118 Here are new flowers of Silver Cholla (Opuntia echinocarpa) cactus in the Cactaceae plant family. Green seems an unlikely color for flowers that want to stand out, but they actually stand out just fine in the sere desert. I only found a few of these cacti with flowers this year. Watch out for the live and dead stems in the desert - you sometimes need pliers to get the spines out! (Red Rock Canyon, Kern County, California, 10 May 2018)

File.Name: i514c_i511-021.jpg
Notes: 28243963318 Here are two shots of a tiny parasitic wasp with a relatively long ovipositor on the surface of a gall on a Saltbush (Atriplex, Chenopodiaceae) shrub, see my [Previous] photo for more info on the gall and plant. I believe this wasp is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea), probably in the family Torymidae which favor galls. I've found very similar wasps on oak galls at home, e.g. this photo. The wasp is trying to sense a larva inside the gall to get it with its long ovipositor. (That's not a stinger!) The gall contains a larva that feeds as a parasite on the shrub, so I reckon this wasp is a hyperparasite - a parasite whose host is a parasite. Actually it should be a hyperparasitoid since it will finally kill the parasite it infects. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 11 May 2018)
CommonNames: parasitic wasp; Chalcid Wasp; hyperparasite; hyperparasitoid

File.Name: i514d_i510-086.jpg
Notes: 42116538031 These are galls on a twig of a female pistillate shrub of Four-wing Saltbush (Atriplex canescens) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. I believe they are the galls of the Tumor Stem Gall Midge (Asphondylia atriplicis), a tiny fly in the Cecidomyiidae family of the order Diptera. If you look close, you can see the pupal husks of emerging midges stuck halfway out of the gall. I've seen this before with different midge galls, see this photo. Ron Russo describes this perfectly in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) in a passage describing the ways of Gall Wasps (Cecidomyiidae): ". . . the mature pupae exit only about two-thirds of the way out, using the pressure of the last part of the passage to hold them firmly in place while they complete their transformation into adults." This is the same gall as I show in my [Next] photo, only a very different color. Hah - the gall on top looks like a sideways Homer Simpson! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 10 May 2018)
CommonNames: Four-wing Saltbush; Tumor Stem Gall Midge
ScientificName: Atriplex canescens; Chenopodiaceae; Asphondylia atriplicis; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Atriplex canescens; Chenopodiaceae; Asphondylia atriplicis; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: i515a_i515-010.jpg
Notes:
42092603312 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae) on the wild edge of our garden. This one is a female with that long ovipositor. I believe it is genus Agulla with that "kite-like" head. They have subtle but beautiful colors. I know, the focus isn't quite right with this photo, but I was happy to get it with my bum knee and the breeze today! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2018)

I injured my knee on our desert trip last week. and I'm still in considerable pain. But I did get down the stairs and outside today - to the garden at least. I hope that's a sign that I'm on the mend. Aside from my knee, there was a steady breeze today, and I had to take many photos to get this keeper. I earned this photo!
ScientificName: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Agulla
associatedTaxa: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Agulla

File.Name: i515b_i511-048.jpg
Notes: 42139180621 Here are the beautiful new flowers of native Mojave Aster (Xylorhiza tortifolia var. tortifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you look close, you can see little caterpillars inside the flower on the right. I'm always surprised to find these flowers in the desert - especially in a drought year like this when little else is flowering. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 11 May 2018)

File.Name: i515c_i510-033.jpg
Notes: 42092046172 Here are small beetles munching a new flower of Wire Lettuce aka Desert Milk-Aster (Stephanomeria pauciflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. We have several different representatives of the genus in the mountains at home, like this one, and they all have those pretty flower parts in the center that look frosted. This plant is a subshrub with white stems and leaves that mostly wither away by the time the plant flowers. I have no idea what kind of beetles they are, but they are digging the flower! (Red Rock Canyon, Kern County, California, 10 May 2018)

File.Name: i515d_i510-011.jpg
Notes: 28266106728 This is common Creosote Bush (Larrea tridentata) in the unusual Zygophyllaceae plant family, showing a yellow flower and a foliose gall on the stem. The distribution of this shrub almost defines the Mojave Desert, and it is the staff of life for many desert critters. The walnut-sized gall is the work of the Ceosote Stem Gall Midge (Asphondylia auripila), family Cecidomyiidae in the order Diptera. I find similar galls made by different gall midges on different Ceanothus species, see this photo. There were lots of flowers on the shrubs at this place, but I only found old galls like this one - and the galls seemed to avoid the flowers. This was the closest I could get to showing both flower and gall in the same frame. (Red Rock Canyon, Kern County, California, 10 May 2018)
CommonNames: Creosote Bush; Ceosote Stem Gall Midge
ScientificName: Larrea tridentata; Zygophyllaceae; Asphondylia auripila; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Ceanothus
associatedTaxa: Larrea tridentata; Zygophyllaceae; Asphondylia auripila; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Ceanothus

File.Name: i516a_i516-030.jpg
Notes:
42160964641 This is a small wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a new flower of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, maybe genus Andrena or Lasioglossum? What's odd is that it seem to be ovipositing inside the flower, while most of these bees nest in the ground. Maybe it's just taking a dump?? I was lucky to get a photo even this good today, as there was a steady breeze that kept the head-high plant in constant motion. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2018)

My knee is still ailing, but I did get outside again today. There was one of those annoying breezes all day that doesn't blow your hat off but keeps everything in motion. I was lucky to get this photo.
CommonNames: wild bee; California Figwort; California Bee Plant
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae; Andrena; Lasioglossum

File.Name: i516b_i515-012.jpg
Notes: 42114079722 What a surprise - this is "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom! I'm not walking so well with my bum knee right now, but a friend found this one fruiting under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees yesterday and brought it to me. We posed in on the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. You can see the blushing color on the stalk from our holding it. This mushroom has a ring, but it doesn't have much of a volval cup on the base of the stem. It's the end of our mushroom season, but I also find them in the fall at the beginning of our season, see this photo. This confirms what David Arora says (in his book Mushrooms Demystified) that they usually fruit twice - "a small flush after the first fall rains, and a larger crop in the late winter and spring." We never did get an early fruiting this year, so I'm happy to find them now. I've never them before in May. These mushrooms are late like our flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2018)

File.Name: i517a_i517-003.jpg
Notes:
41460623194 Me and my exuviae . . .

Here are two photos of a little "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera that is emerging from its final molt. I didn't realize what I had when I got the photo on the left, and I'm glad I checked back about 10 minutes later to get the second shot. This is a teneral specimen of what I believe is the same critter I show in my [Previous] photo - meaning that it's still soft-bodied and pale compared to what it will become when it's had a chance to harden. It's on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). My suspicion is that it's in the family Miridae, see my [Previous] photo for more info. Could the husk in this photo really be the nymph in that photo?? (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2018)

My knee is still ailing, but it's definitely feeling better today. I can bend it again! Now my lower leg below the knee feels cramped, and it's still painful to walk. Darn, if it's not one thing it's another! I did get outside in the garden again today. It was nearly calm this morning, or I couldn't have got these photos. A real wind did pick up in the afternoon.

Notes: 28308414858 Here are two small "true bugs" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - a nymph and an adult - on old and new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). They look nothing alike, but I think they might be the same species - mostly because they are both so common in the oak trees right now. File.Name: i518a_i518-007.jpg
Notes:
42200365361 This is the remarkable early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on the leaf of a Mallow (Malvaceae) plant in the garden. This is the first one I've found this year, though I've been finding a fair number of our other kind of Katydid nymphs, see this photo. I checked my records for the last couple of years, and I find most of these nymphs in August - though there are outliers in April and October and every month in between. These little nymphs are a favorite of mine. They always remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2018)

My knee continues to mend. I actually took a short walk in the woods today which would have been unthinkable even yesterday. Well, I hobbled anyway. It was cloudy today until afternoon, a nice day.

I seem to find these flies either working the willow catkins by the creek, see this photo - or I find them living as predators, see this photo. (And this photo seems to show both modes!) It's an interesting fly, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2018)

File.Name: i519a_i519-012.jpg
Notes:
41320269565 I believe this is the beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom. A friend found this one under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees this morning and brought it to me, and we posed it on bare garden soil. These mushrooms are beautiful, but deadly poison. They are easy to recognize: pure white cap and gills, like a styrofoam cup in the woods; free gills; membranous ring or "skirt" on the stem; and the characteristic "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. Don't eat them! That goes for dogs too - a neighbor lost a beloved dog to one of these mushrooms. However, it's not dangerous to touch or admire them. They are probably the most elegant mushroom in our woods, very beautiful.

I haven't seen one of these mushrooms for over three years, see this photo. What's remarkable is we found this one in May instead of March. The oaks are still flowering, and the mushrooms are just as confused as our plants. I've been finding other mushrooms that should be out earlier in the year, see my [Previous] photo. The woods feel dry, but there is some moisture if you dig down, and I guess that's enough. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2018)

My knee continues to mend. I took a longer walk in the woods today down by the creek. It was another cloudy morning, followed by sun and breeze. Now I'm tired and sore. I don't want to overdo this.

File.Name: i519b_i518-015.jpg
Notes: 28347900728 What a surprise - I found this Agaricus mushroom in the woods yesterday! It has a mild smell and taste, and I suspect it's edible and delicious. It found it in the woodland under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I can see hints of brownish stains, so maybe it's the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens) mushroom? Or the yellow-staining Agaricus sylvicola group? I don't really care what it is, but what's remarkable is that it's out in May instead of February. The oaks are still flowering, and the mushrooms are just as confused as our plants. I've also found "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta) mushroom this week (here), and what I believe is the Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) in my [Next] photo. The woods feel dry, but there is some moisture if you dig down, and I guess that's enough. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2018)

Notes: 27372374967 Here is another mushroom in the woods today, though this one is not as surprising as the other late-season mushrooms I've been showing in my [Previous] photos. I believe this is some kind of Agrocybe mushroom, probably the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox). It's growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. This one has a brownish cracked cap and an evanescent veil that catches the brown spores as on the left. Sometimes these mushrooms come up in great numbers on wood chips, see this photo. I typically find these mushrooms in April when most other mushrooms are done. Here it is in May! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2018)

File.Name: i521b_i521-005.jpg
Notes: 41545717404 This is a bee-size wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) seemingly asleep in a flower of native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae plant family on a chilly day. The sun never did come out today, and the temperature stayed in the 50s. I looked at BugGuide, and apparently a number of different bees sleep in these flowers. I won't try to guess a name, but one candidate is genus Diadasia. The flowers close at night giving some protection to the bee inside. Does anyone else remember Robert Heinlein's classic sci-fi book "Red Planet" where the boys survive the frigid Martian night inside the cabbage-like plant that closes up to protect them against the cold? (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2018)

File.Name: i521c_i521-020.jpg
Notes: 27396323447 I guess this is a bee-like wasp (Hymenoptera) with its head buried in a new flower of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) on a chilly day. The sun never did come out today, and the temperature stayed in the 50s. I really thought this was a bee at first because of its chunky build and association with flowers, but there's no sign of hairs on it's rear legs, so I believe it's some kind of wasp. I tried to nudge it to show it's face, but it steadfastly refused - even when I poked it with a stick! I let it be. Any suggestions about ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2018)

File.Name: i522a_i522-004.jpg
Notes:
42243414402 I was surprised to find this Velvet Ant (Mutillidae, Pompiloidea, Hymenoptera) hurrying across a garden path this morning! I followed it and found that it has an uncanny ability to hide under the dead oak leaves. By picking up whole handfuls of leaves, I managed find it and get my photo. They are not really ants, but wingless wasps that are ectoparasitoids of other immature insects, esp. bees and solitary wasps. This must be a female, as the males have wings. Other local species are white in color, see this photo of the only other one I've managed to photograph. I guess this one is genus Dasymutilla or Pseudomethoca. They are said to have a painful sting. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2018)

File.Name: i522b_i522-032.jpg
Notes:
42289722861 The spider and the fly . . .

The spider was on the fly when I found it, but it backed off when I got closer. I suspect the spider is a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis, Linyphiidae), though it's web seemed pretty dishevelled. I believe the fly is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, Diptera - this is the first of these interesting flies that I've seen this year. And look at all those tiny flies between the spider and the large fly - the spider's pantry. I found this scene in a patch of prickly pear cactus in our garden. And yes, the spider did go back to the fly. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2018)

File.Name: i522c_i522-024.jpg

The flowers aren't much to look at, but the ribbed seeds are beautiful. I'll post photos of the flowers and unbroken seed heads in a day or three. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2018)

File.Name: i522d_i522-013.jpg
Notes: 42242446492 I found another surprising "May mushroom" in the woods today - a Chanterelle (Cantharellus californicus), though it's a week or two past its prime. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I've been finding several different mushrooms that I find more typically in March - see this photo and the several photos previous to it. I checked my records, and I've found Chanterelles as early as November and as late as April, with a peak in February - not counting today's specimen in May. The late rains in March have confused everything. The oaks are still flowering, and the mushrooms are just as confused as our plants. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2018)

File.Name: i523a_i523-006.jpg
Notes:
28439284828 I'm pretty sure this caterpillar-like creature is the larva of a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, maybe the species Abia americana. I have a recent photo of an adult here. I found it this morning on a vertical concrete-block retaining wall on the edge of our garden. That seems mysterious, but there's a vine of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) growing against the wall, and I've found the larvae before on this plant - see this photo. (That's the benefit of always recording the plant that I find insects on, I never would have remembered this.) That photo also shows the features that distinguish the larvae from real caterpillars - they have simple eye-spots whereas caterpillars usually have a broken ring of eyespots. Second, sawfly larvae always have more prolegs - six or more pairs - while caterpillars have five pairs or fewer. I still don't know what it's doing on this stone wall! (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2018)

I still didn't work on my ailing computer, but my work-around is still working - using "Safe Mode with Networking" which seems completely stable. I'll get to it *real soon now*. The weather has been in the grips of "June Gloom" lately - it never did clear out yesterday. Today was both gloomy and breezy. It's really been too chilly for insects, but I've been finding a few.

File.Name: i524a_i524-008.jpg
Notes:
40526604350 Here are the exerted pistil and stamens of a new flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family - with drops from the steady drizzle today reflecting the woods. What more can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2018)

We were deep in a marine-layer cloud this morning, with substantial drizzle. I had morning errands in the valley, and it was still drizzly when I got back home after noon. That's when I got a few photos, in the gloom with some breeze. It never did clear out today, and we actually ended up with an honest 1/10 inch of rain in the gauge!

File.Name: i524b_i524-010.jpg
Notes: 42333006631 One of our native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus, Liliaceae) flowers lost a petal which gives an opportunity to look inside. Here it is in the drizzle today. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily that are a "tender trap" for pollinators - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those long inward-pointing hairs without picking up a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. They are very elegant, one of my favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2018)

File.Name: i524c_i521-023.jpg
Notes: 42285363302 Here is another shot of the wasp (Hymenoptera) that I found a few days ago with its head buried in a new flower of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) on a chilly day. I thought it was a bee at first because of its chunky build and association with flowers, but there's no sign of hairs on it's rear legs, so I believe it's some kind of wasp. I tried to nudge it to show it's face, but it steadfastly refused - though it did open its wings and waggled its abdomen around! I don't know what kind of wasp it is, but I reckon it's good for HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2018)

File.Name: i524d_i518-010.jpg
Notes: 28459964458 This is another photo of the remarkable early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) that I showed last week in this photo. Here it is getting away from me on the leaf of a Mallow (Malvaceae) plant in the garden. This is the first one I've found this year, and I never did see it again. It has a cute caboose, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2018)

File.Name: i525a_i525-020.jpg
Notes:
42307900512 I guess this is a little Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae, Diptera) on a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in the canyon. We always leave a few of these weedy plants for the caterpillars, see this photo. The fly might be species Aciurina thoracica, see BugGuide - the furry thorax and dark abdomen seem distinctive. I was looking for caterpillars in the fennel foliage - I found them, but I also found this interesting fly. Those eyes! I figure today's photos are always the best, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2018)

I had a little scare tonight when my Win7 computer wouldn't power up - nothing, it wouldn't even boot to "Safe Mode with Networking" which I've been using lately. Finally it started. I'll see if I can help it this weekend, but I think it some needs some shop time. It's probably time for a new computer.

File.Name: i525b_i525-033.jpg
Notes: 27486244287 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) in a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). I believe the fly is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae), though you can't really see the distinctive doubly-bent proboscis in this shot - compare this photo of a different species. It's small, only a bit more than 1/8 inch long. I figure today's photos are always the best, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2018)

File.Name: i525c_i522-028.jpg
Notes: 27486110437 I'm pretty sure this is a Snipe Fly in the genus Rhagio, Rhagionidae, Diptera on a leaflet of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens, Polypodiaceae). It was a gloomy day, and this fly wasn't moving much. I was planning on posting this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2018)

File.Name: i525d_i522-007.jpg
Notes: 27485937457 It's close to the end of May, but I I found this March Fly (Bibionidae, Diptera) resting on a garden hose on a gloomy morning a few days ago. I was planning on posting this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2018)

File.Name: i526a_i526-008.jpg
Notes:
41473145765 This is a tiny Mosquito (Culicidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). It must be a male mosquito because of its feathery antennae, but also because it's sucking a flower and not my arm - only the females need a "blood meal" for protein to produce their eggs. It's interesting how the mouthparts of these mosquitoes do double duty on both flowers and legs! It looks like this one has an extra appendage by its proboscis, but maybe that's just its right-front leg? There are many different kinds of mosquitoes, but this one might actually be a Western Treehole Mosquito (Ochlerotatus sierrensis), see Alice Abelia's photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2018)

I'm leaving my computer on just to play it safe. Today started gloomy and finished clear and windy. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight, and I hope that's the end of it. Two days ago, I don't think the temperature ever got to 50∞; yesterday, it got up into the low 60s; it almost got to 70∞ today. That's still on the cool side, but I like the way this is going!

File.Name: i526b_i525-060.jpg
Notes: 41652092504 Look at those crab-like front claws - this must be the nymph of a Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). It's tiny. It's also curious that the adults are common, but I don't see the nymphs very often - I only have one other photo. I found this one on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). Yeah, the flowers are yellow - all too yellow. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2018)

File.Name: i526c_i525-016.jpg
Notes: 27505305837 Here are two different stages or instars of the caterpillars of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. I found them both on leaves of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) plant in the canyon, the first I've seen this year. The plants are invasive in our mountains, but we always leave a few for the caterpillars and try to remember to cut the flowers before they make seeds. The butterflies favor this non-native brood plant, but they must have found other Apiacea plants before fennel was introduced, maybe Sanicles or Tauschia, though I've never found a caterpillar on those plants. Yep, the two stages look completely different, and the mature caterpillars look different again, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2018)

File.Name: i526d_i525-002.jpg
Notes: 28501906688 I found this medium-size caterpillar happily munching a young shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) in the drizzle yesterday morning. There are a couple of out-of-focus ants in he picture too, they've been tending aphids in this shrub. The caterpillar is more than an inch long. I'm not sure what adult moth or butterfly it will become. I think I've seen this kind of caterpillar before, see this photo. I tried to find it again this morning, but I couldn't find it - it is pretty well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2018)

File.Name: i527a_i527-014.jpg
Notes:
27527376837 This is a handsome little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working a new flower of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). It's the first one I've seen this year. All butterflies have been scarce this year - at least they are late, like the wildflowers. I've also been laid up with a bum knee. That is feeling better now, but I still don't want to kneel - I might not be able to get up again! I've seen a few Swallowtails and Acmon Blues, and I hope some warmer days will bring out more of them. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2018)

It was windy last night, but it mostly died down today. It didn't get much past 70∞ today, still on the cool side for almost June.

File.Name: i527b_i525-054.jpg
Notes: 28524413058 Here is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). These butterflies are still not common, but I suspect many more are coming - the caterpillars have been quite common this spring, see this photo and any more. In time, I will have taken enough photos of these butterflies - but not yet! (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2018)

File.Name: i527c_i527-033.jpg
Notes: 42347731022 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a bristly new flower of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). I thought at first that it was taking nectar, but looking closer at my photo, I can see that it is collecting pollen under its abdomen. (If you look close, you can see a fifth stamen under its body.) This behavior places it in the family Megachilidae of Leaf-cutter Bees and Mason Bees, but I don't know beyond that. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Caterpillar Phacelia; Leaf-cutter Bees; Mason Bees

File.Name: i527d_i527-001.jpg
Notes: 40588937790 This is a leaf of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) that's being taken over by a rust fungus (Pucciniales, Basidiomycotina). I don't think I've ever noticed this before. This one vine had a number of infestations. I tried a google search for "rust fungus clematis" and this Flickr photo by Anne Elliott came up with a fungus that looks very similar, though her photo was taken in Alberta, Canada. I also find something similar on our Wild Blackberry, see this photo. I assume that's something different. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2018)

File.Name: i528a_i528-028.jpg
Notes:
27550437727 Here are two shots of a female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. In the photo on the right, you can see that it's ovipositing! You can tell that this is the same butterfly in both shots because it's missing a chunk of its right hindwing at the fourth spot - visible in both pictures. I've been seeing these butterflies in the woods, but this is the first time I've managed to get a photo. Actually, I photographed several of them today - all females with dark wings, though there must be males out there somewhere! We had warmer weather today, and it seems to be bringing out more butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2018)

I could tell it would be warmer today because the ants were swarming in our bedroom this morning - see this photo from a few years ago. It was indeed warmer today, into the 80s. That's just what we need to bring out some butterflies!

File.Name: i528b_i528-041.jpg
Notes: 28547123368 I'm pretty sure this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly perched for a moment in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It has a furry face, orange-tipped antennae, and hyaline (translucent) spots near the forewing tips. The Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) is larger and darker, see this photo which shows them both on the same flower. It's curious to find this one now as I still haven't managed to photograph an Umber Skipper, though I've seen a few - usually they appear first. It's been a screwy year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2018)
CommonNames: Rural Skipper; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

associatedTaxa: Ochlodes agricola; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: i528c_i528-017.jpg
Notes: 41517830275 Note the little wing buds. I believe this is the late-stage nymph of a Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). It's sucking juices from a bristly flower head of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). Not that this one has a little spine on its forehead. It sure looks different from the usual ones I find in the woodland, compare this photo. On the other hand, this photo shows another adult stilt bug on the same kind of Phacelia that lacks the spine, so maybe it's just a juvenile trait like the spotted legs? (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2018)

File.Name: i528d_i525-003.jpg
Notes: 41689743014 Here are raindrops on new flowers of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae). Actually, "raindrops" is pushing it - they are more like drizzle drops, though we did get 1/10 inch of drizzle on this wet morning a few days ago. Drizzle is perfect for leaving drops on flowers - real rain usually runs right off. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "AllNatural". Hah - most of my photos fit this theme. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2018)

File.Name: i529a_i529-041.jpg
Notes:
41720454974 I believe this is a common European Earwig (Forficula auricularia, Dermaptera) taking wing from a new flower head of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I say "common" - but these insects are not at all common up here in the mountains. I think of them as household pests, though I occasionally find them in the woods too - see this photo. Its wing is quite beautiful, fan-folded to tuck into the small space under its short wing covers - see this photo series to appreciate the fold. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 May 2018)

I went up into the higher mountains today mostly to find Copper butterflies - see this photo and this photo. I saw one male Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly in a place where I've found dozens in years past. *Sigh* It's been that kind of year. It was a few degrees cooler today, but still close to 80∞. The marine layer is expected to come back tonight bringing cooler temperatures, then back into the 80s next weekend.
Locality: Lycaena gorgon) butterfly in a place where I've found dozens in years past. *Sigh* It's been that kind of year. It was a few degrees cooler today

File.Name: i529b_i529-014.jpg
Notes:
41720174574 Here is a new flower of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. Here's an interesting phenomenon - the flowers mostly look blue when you see them, though they do an odd color shift and end up looking more magenta when you try to photograph them. I don't understand this. Sometimes the blue does come out, like this photo - which was also my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Pareidolia" - the word for the phenomenon of seeing faces where none really exists. Can you see the face now? This plant is called a "pen-stemon" for a reason - there is something strange about its fifth stamen. There are four normal stamens, with the mysterious namesake "fifth stamen" laying along the lower petal like a Rolling Stones tongue. It's a non-fertile "staminode". The receptive pistil is not visible on top of the flower. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 May 2018)

(My favorite example of pareidolia is my photo of the Naked lady Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) - a tree that looks like a lady . Hah - it's one of my most viewed photos, but probably just because its name pops up in so many searches!)

File.Name: i529c_i529-027.jpg
Notes: 41719993484 Here are two photos of the locally uncommon Wide-throated Yellow Monkeyflower (Mimulus brevipes) in the Phrymaceae plant family. This one is growing on the scree of a steep shale bank, but the plant was down low where I could get to it. I only found this one plant. I only know it from this one place in our mountains, but I seem to find it most years. We're at the northern edge of this species, and it's more common further south. Thanks to NatureShutterbug for finding these a few years ago. Now they are an ephemeral memorial, RIP Lynn. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 May 2018)

File.Name: i529d_i528-037.jpg
Notes: 42442767401 This is a remarkable and unusual Tmarus spider, probably Tmarus angulatus. It's a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that is less than 1/4 inch long - not counting its long front legs. I had to break my usual rule and use my on-camera flash for this photo - the spider would not get out of the shadow, and it seemed to disappear in the contrasty light. I also rotated the image 90∞ clockwise to make better use of my screen pixels. This one is one a green stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae), but you can imagine how well camouflaged it would be on a dark oak twig. I was confused for a while distinguishing these spiders from a couple of spiders in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers, see this photo and this photo. The differences are obvious when you can compare them directly - not so obvious when you see them in the wild one at a time! I've only found these a few times, but that might just be because they are so well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2018)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Greenbark Ceanothus; Cribellate Orb Weavers
ScientificName: Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus; Thomisidae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Uloboridae

File.Name: i530a_i530-033.jpg
Notes:
40656404440 Here are raindrops on the face of a medium-size Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Tipulomorpha, Diptera on an oak leaf. It was a chilly and drizzly day, and this crane fly wasn't going anywhere, so I tried to get close. Actually I got this photo after the fly moved, shedding some drops - it's still pretty cool. What else can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2018)

The morning NWS forecast called for "night and morning low clouds with patchy fog and drizzle". That's an understatement - it never did clear up today, and we actually got another 1/10 inch of drizzle in our rain gauge! It was another cool day that never got out of the 50s. Like this crane fly, the butterflies are waiting it out.

File.Name: i530b_i530-019.jpg
Notes: 27594321827 This is a bristly new flower head of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae) in the drizzle this morning. These flowers have a "scorpioid inflorescence" - coiled like a scorpion's tail or the neck of a violin. They will keep producing new flowers in the curl of the stem as long as they can, fading to seed pods behind - see this photo and this photo. The drizzle suits them. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2018)

File.Name: i530c_i530-014.jpg
Notes: 42413733432 Here is another surprising "May Mushroom" that's fruiting in the woods months later than it should be - it's almost June! This one is a Bolete - maybe the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus aka Xerocomellus chrysenteron) or kin. It's growing in leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. These mushrooms have reddish, minutely velvety caps, yellow and red stems, and rather large yellow pores which slowly stain blue with handling. It's said to be edible, and the rodent which nibbled the cap certainly agrees! This one also has a white "mold" growing over the pores which is the first sign of the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that is particularly attracted to these mushrooms - see this photo of the later stage of this interesting fungus. I usually find these boletes in January or February. Like the wildflowers and butterflies, they are late this year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2018)
CommonNames: Bolete; Oak-Loving Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Bolete Eater
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus chrysenteron; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Hypomyces chrysospermus
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Xerocomellus chrysenteron; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Hypomyces chrysospermus

File.Name: i530d_i530-005.jpg
Notes: 42464199621 This is a bridge of webbing in the cactus patch of the garden, with drops from the copious drizzle this morning. It runs between last year's ripe fruit and this year's flower bud. I reckon this web is the work of either a Sheet Web Weaver (Linyphiidae) or a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider - maybe the latter because the drops are relatively large? I like that one amber-colored drop. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2018)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Funnel Web Weaver

File.Name: i531a_i531-038.jpg
Notes:
42434053562 I found this Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly in a shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) that's just starting to flower. It's pretty tattered, but this is still the first one of these butterflies I've manage to photograph this year. Butterflies have been scarce, and the few there are don't always want to be photographed! These butterflies were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from the Appalachian and Ozark ranges. I believe this one is male with blue upper wings - but they are worn. These butterflies have no strong markings below. They should be one of our earliest butterflies - see this photo from 26 January 2016. Then again, I found one in July last year, see this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2018)

Today started gloomy and drizzly like it's been, but it mostly cleared up by noon - with some residual patchy clouds and "fog wind". There should be a warming trend over the next few days with temps in the 80s by Sunday. Maybe we get a break with the "May gray" done and the "June gloom" not yet begun??

File.Name: i531b_i531-026.jpg
Notes: 28612622338 This is a handsome little Beetle (Coleoptera) with red epaulettes munching new flower buds of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I'm pretty sure this is a Red-shouldered Leaf Beetle (Saxinis saucia) in the Chrysomelidae group of beetles, at least that is the genus. What I'm not so sure of is the color! I think of these little beetles as black, compare this photo which also shows the epaulettes better. Maybe the color is a reflection of the sky, but was there really that much blue in the cloudy sky this morning? Is it iridescence? (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2018)

File.Name: i531c_i531-057.jpg
Notes: 41762338384 I believe this is a little Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae, Cerambycidae, Coleoptera), though it's hiding its long horns (antennae) in the calyx of a spent flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. Yep, this remarkable shrub still has flowers - see this photo from mid-April. I've never seen this plant going so late, but it's been that kind of year. The beetle is hiding from me, but it is showing its rear end - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2018)

Notes: 42433501012 This is a little Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) as it leaves my presence in a bristly plant of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida, Boraginaceae). I believe it's a late-stage nymph with speckled legs and little wing buds - see this photo for the rest of the insect. It's a decent butt shot of an interesting insect, so HBBBT! File.Name: i601a_i601-012.jpg
Notes:
40697241150 Here is a particularly gorgeous Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's wings are crumpled, and I suspect that it's newly emerged and still not quite ready to fly. This shrub is one of it's host plants, but don't they usually crawl away before they pupate? In any case, it's very beautiful with especially striking colors. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2018)

It was sunny and clear today from the get-go. I had something to do in town, and by the time I got home it was still nice. There was an odd breeze mostly from the south, though it seemed to jump around - not a big wind, but enough to keep things in motion. It was in the 70s today, and the forecast is for ten degrees warmer tomorrow. I'm ready for some heat.

File.Name: i601b_i601-006.jpg
Notes: 27634191177 This is a tiny insect inside a new flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). Looking close, I'm sure it's a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - it has a sucking proboscis and four-segmented antennae. I was actually looking in the flowers for the handsome insect Closterocoris amoenus in the family Miridae which I have found inside before, see this photo. The insect in my photo today looks similar, but it's much smaller. I don't know what it is, but it may well be in the same family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2018)

File.Name: i601c_i601-034.jpg
Notes: 40696780290 This is an extraordinary gold-flecked Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a steel bridge railing in the canyon. I believe it is genus Thevenetimyia, very likely the species Thevenetimyia luctifera. Apparently the species name means "light-bearing" which is certainly appropriate. I've been seeing the same flies on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae), and I've seen them there before - see this photo and this photo from last year. HFDF! Notes: 28631654628 This is a tiny Mosquito (Culicidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). It must be a male mosquito because of its feathery antennae, but also because it's sucking a flower and not my arm - only the females need a "blood meal" for protein to produce their eggs. It's interesting how the mouthparts of these mosquitoes do double duty on both flowers and legs! I have another shot of the same mosquito in this photo that shows more of the proboscis - but I wanted to save this one for the Fly Day Friday group. There are many different kinds of mosquitoes, but this one might actually be a Western Treehole Mosquito (Ochlerotatus sierrensis), see Alice Abelia's photo at BugGuide. Julie had an interesting thought when she saw my photo. Look how the mosquito is dusted with pollen - they are pollinators! Does that mean that mosquito eradication programs are eradicating a pollinator? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2018)

File.Name: i602a_i602-039.jpg
Notes:
41623356845 Surely this is an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly going for nectar on these new flowers of native Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum, Plantaginaceae). (Never mind the weedy Wild Oats (Avena fatua, Poaceae) that are coming up through the snapdragon.) These butterflies are distinctive because the black on their forewings runs along the wing rather than across it like our other swallowtails. But for that, this one really looks more like a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) with those black and white colors - see this photo for the colors of a more typical butterfly. This one looks worn and tattered, but I doubt that's the whole story. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2018)

Today was a nice day - warmer than yesterday, in the 80s - but there was a cooling breeze from the south. Maybe it's an "eddy wind" that forms as marine winds from the north wrap around Point Conception and he Channel Islands and come at us from the south. It's the wind that I've said feels like "fog winds" - without the fog. It seems we've had a lot of these winds lately. Tomorrow might be ten degrees warmer than today!
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Sticky Snapdragon; Wild Oats; Pale Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Antirrhinum multiflorum; Plantaginaceae; Avena fatua; Poaceae; Papilio eurymedon
