File.Name: i602b_i602-035.jpg
Notes: 41802599534 Here is another non-native European Earwig (Forficula auricularia, Dermaptera) taking wing from foliage of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae). This is the second one I've found this year taking wing, see this photo. I hope they're not becoming more common in the mountains. I think of them as household pests, though I occasionally find them in the woods too - see this photo. Earwig wings are surprisingly beautiful, fan-folded to tuck into the small space under its short wing covers which you can see sticking out to the side. See this photo series to appreciate the fold. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2018)
ScientificName: Forficula auricularia; Dermaptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Forficula auricularia; Dermaptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: i602c_i602-001.jpg
Notes: 42525132591 Here is a small "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on the keel of a flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It seems to have four-segmented antennae, so maybe it's another Plant Bug in the Miridae family. If you look close, you can see its long proboscis going deep into the private parts of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2018)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Miridae

File.Name: i602d_i602-013.jpg
Notes: 42473256222 This is a small spider with its prey - a winged aphid! That should give a sense of scale, but I also got this photo at 1:1 magnification to I can measure the spider by counting pixels - it's about 860 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.4 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I believe this spider is a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis, Linyphiidae), though you can't really tell from this angle. For one thing, it has a "boxy" abdomen. This one seems to have male palps. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2018)

File.Name: i603a_i603-033.jpg
Notes:
42496422052 What a surprise! This must be a Phantom Orchid (Cephalanthera austiniae) in the Orchidaceae plant family. A friend found it yesterday in Kinevan Canyon about a mile from home, and we met there today to get photos and look for more. The pure white color of this plant is a sign that it has no chlorophyll. Instead, it's a mycotroph or myco-heterotroph. Ronald A. Coleman explains in his book on The Wild Orchids of California (Comstock Books, 1995) that a "compatible fungus invades the root mass of the orchid, and the orchid uses food already digested by the fungus. Although most, if not all, orchids need a mycorrhizal fungus to germinate their seeds, mycotrophic orchids never outgrow that need; if the fungus dies for any reason, the orchid also dies." What's even more interesting is that most records for this orchid are north of the bay area. There's only one other record from Santa Barbara County in May 2015 not far from where we found this one. We looked up and down the canyon today, but this is the only one we found. I'm going to "stealth" the location of this unusual plant, but I'll show the canyon to explore. (I also stealth our home, though I show the canyon.) Like I said, what a surprise! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2018)

It did get up in the 90s today, so what do I do - spend my day in a cool shady canyon looking for orchids! This was probably the warmest day of the year so far, and now it will start to cool down some. It was a very nice day - in addition to the heat, there was little breeze to frustrate taking photos.

File.Name: i603b_i603-047.jpg
Notes: 42548404031 This is a new flowering plant of native Hemp Dogbane aka Indian Hemp (Apocynum cannabinum) in the Apocynaceae plant family. That's hemp as in fiber, not smoking. It's an uncommon plant in our mountains that I only know from a few places. This was one of the most important native plants for the native American Chumash in the mountains - for string, rope, nets, and clothing. For more info, see Jan Timbrook's book on Chumash Ethnobotany (2007). It's curious that the plant is not more common. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2018)

File.Name: i603c_i603-031.jpg
Notes: 27677614737 These are ripening seed heads of a handsome native Sedge in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in Kinevan Canyon along the creek. The key for sedges goes on for 14 pages in the Jepson Manual, so I'm not sure what it is! Maybe it's the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae)? It does match the photos at CalFlora. "Santa Barbara Sedge" is an exaggeration though, as this one is found all over the state and into Oregon. It was an important basket fiber plant for native Americans. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2018)

File.Name: i603d_i603-049.jpg
Notes: 42495550972 A couple of days ago, these would have been surprising May mushrooms - now they are surprising June mushrooms! These are little yellow Bolbitius titubans aka Bolbitius vitellinus mushrooms coming up in the shady woods in Kinevan Canyon. They are quite ephemeral - not quite "here today and gone tomorrow", but close to it. The new caps on the right are quite pretty with that typical 'egg yolk yellow' color, but they soon loose their color like the mushrooms on the left. As with all my recent mushrooms, these are fruiting months later than they should be. I usually find these mushrooms in winter, see this photo from December a few years ago. Like the wildflowers and butterflies, they are late this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2018)

File.Name: i604a_i604-025.jpg
Notes:
41850220864 This is a handsome California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly trying to get water from the sandstone rock - look close, you can see its proboscis doing its thing! I'm used to seeing a few worn butterflies in mid-winter like this one - that makes sense because the butterflies overwinter as adults. But this specimen looks perfect - it must be newly emerged. I have found the caterpillars before in our mountains, see this photo taken a few miles away. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2018)

I had something important to do this afternoon, but I had some time for photos in the morning. I'm tired, so 'nuff said!

File.Name: i604b_i604-009.jpg
Notes: 28698565658 This is a handsome Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly trying to get moisture from damp soil near our wellhead. I often confuse these with the Mylitta Crescent butterfly in this photo, though they are completely different when you get them to stand still. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2018)

File.Name: i604c_i604-023.jpg
Notes: 28698221368 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) in the throat of a new flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's gathering pollen from the groves in the flower, but I'm not sure what kind of bee it is because it seems to have pollen-collecting scopae on both its hind legs and abdomen. (Look close, you can see hairs on both.) The stamens of the flower are yellow in the back. Above the bee is the flower's stigma, but it's hard to see because it's already folded up - compare this photo. The stigma of these flowers is touch-sensitive. If you take a grass stem and touch it, you can watch it close in a few seconds! I guess the wild bee in this photo already triggered this one. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; scopae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; scopae

File.Name: i604d_i604-001.jpg
Notes: 40763188030 I noticed this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on buds of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) that is just starting to flower. I figure it's a male because of those enlarged palps. Did you notice what's missing? It has lost all but one of its legs on the right side, a real hardship for these spiders that snatch their prey between their long front legs. Then again, maybe all that really matters for these male spiders are its palps. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2018)

File.Name: i605a_i605-025.jpg
Notes:
40798377250 Look at the torn tail of this butterfly and compare my [Previous] photo - this is the same Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working different hybrid flowers of "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden. This photo shows the upper side of the wings. These swallowtails look less pale as we move into summer - they get a cream color, and sometimes I confuse them with Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus). This is only the second of these usually common butterflies that I've managed to photograph this year, see this photo for the first one. They're out there, but not as common as I expect. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2018)

Like many Flickr users, I've been bombed by spam accounts favoriting my photos with a link to a porn site. Sure, I've sent in abuse reports and blocked the perpetrators. Some of the names are disappearing from my Recent Activity list - along with real activity from the last 12 hours. I suspect it's a bot going through the photos in certain groups. They seem to like my spider photos, so I'll be curious if they hit the new ones I posted today. It's no big deal, but it is a pain.

File.Name: i605c_i605-045.jpg
Notes: 40797901400 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey inside a new flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I reckon that's a good place to sit - I've been finding other insects inside these flowers lately, see this photo and this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2018)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: i605d_i605-040.jpg
Notes: 41704081075 Here are two different shots of the same little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). In the photo on the right, you can see the spider's fangs at the bottom of its face. In the photo on the left, I believe the spider is trying to "balloon" away - to hitch a ride on the wind using a length of webbing as a sail. If you look close, you can bee a bit of webbing coming from its tail. This matches the illustration of genus Phanias in R.J. Adams' Field Guide to the Spiders of California (UC Press, 2014) - a male, as you can see from its enlarged palps. I think I've seen this species before, always in an oak tree - see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2018)

File.Name: i606a_i606-054.jpg
Notes:
28758673458 I found this Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly working new flowers of native Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana) in the Fabaceae plant family. I don't associate these flowers with butterflies, though I have found a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the flowers - see this photo. (Jeffrey, this is another one for your records!) These butterflies were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from back east. These butterflies have no strong markings below. This one may be male - I can see just a hint of blue behind its head. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2018)

It was a gray day as "June gloom" weather has returned. I don't mind the gloom, but the "fog winds" can be killer. It did finally clear up today, but it stayed cool - in the 60s. I was surprised to find this butterfly.

File.Name: i606b_i606-018.jpg
Notes:
41731818645 Here is a little Ant (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) taking nectar from a new flower of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). I'm pretty sure the ant is Formica moki, see this photo and Alice Abelia's comment. There were quite a few ants tasting the flowers in this shrub - you can almost see a pool of nectar in the cup of each flower. These shrubs are important for pollinators. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2018)

I just realized that this photo shows the real petals of the flowers - not the yellowish petal-like sepals, but the tiny orange flaps around each stamen. It's interesting that Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) is different with just four sepals and no petals, see this photo. I guess this is why Coffeeberry and Redberry are no longer placed in the same genus.
Locality: Rhamnus ilicifolia) is different with just four sepals and no petals
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Formica moki

File.Name: i606c_i605-063.jpg
Notes: 41731558425 I'm pretty sure this is the web of a tiny Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers - with the spider sitting in the center! At first, I though it was just a bit of detritus like a leaf fragment in the center, but sure enough - it's the spider. I have more photos of the spider here. This spider spins a proper orb web that is mostly flat. It's not a proper orb weaver (family Araneidae), so I was surprised that it also spins a stabilimentum - that band of thicker webbing that runs across the web. The spider disappeared into the shadows before I could get a closer shot, but I'll keep trying. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2018)
CommonNames: Feather-legged Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Hackled Band Orb Weavers

File.Name: i606d_i604-014.jpg
Notes: 41731387625 This is a flower cluster of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) that is almost ready to flower - and it's bedecked with spider webs. The webbing is effective. You can see a little Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Tipulomorpha, Diptera) tangled up at the bottom of the web, and its legs separately tangled up the the main cluster of flowers. I think the spider itself is barely visible in the shadows on that green leaf closest to the center. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2018)
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Tipulidae; Tipulomorpha; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Tipulidae; Tipulomorpha; Diptera

File.Name: i607a_i607-037.jpg
Notes:
28785840928 This is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly perched on new flowers of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). It's the first one of these butterflies I've seen this year. These butterflies appear in spring or early summer which distinguishes them from the somewhat similar Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterflies that appear later in summer, see this photo. I also got photos of this butterfly working new flowers of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2018)

It was mostly sunny today, though we seemed to be right on the edge of a cloud bank, and there were some "fog winds". Despite the sun, it was still on the cool side, only in the low 70s. The forecast calls for warmer and windier - with a Fire Weather Watch in effect on Saturday night. Here we go again . . .

File.Name: i607b_i607-013.jpg
Notes: 42609037632 Here is a handsome California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly in a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It was a sunny morning that quickly turned chilly as marine layer clouds moved in, and I think this butterfly got stuck in the tree waiting for the sun to come back. I usually only manage to photograph these critters on the ground when they are "puddling" - taking moisture - so I was happy to get a photo up in a tree. These oaks are also their host plant, so it's fitting to show them together. I have a photo of their amazing caterpillars here. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2018)

File.Name: i607c_i607-051.jpg
Notes: 41758904385 This is a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) with a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the right and the throat of the flower and its interesting stigma on the left. I'm pretty sure the spider is genus Mecaphesa again. The stigma is the sexual part of the flower that receives pollen, and it's touch-sensitive in these flowers. If you take a grass stem and poke it, you can watch the flaps close in a few seconds - great fun with grandkids in the woods! If you look at my [Previous] photo, you can just barely see the stigma pushed against the top of the flower. It's more visible in this photo, and you can see it both open and closed in this photo. After a while, the flap seems to open up again - at least if the flower is not fertilized. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2018)
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Crab Spider
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Thomisidae; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Thomisidae; Mecaphesa

File.Name: i607d_i605-044.jpg
Notes: 28784696708 Here is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) in the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) - with its rear end sticking out! This bee looks similar to the bee in this photo, though it look larger using the throat of the flower as my ruler. I also got this photo on the next day. You can just barely see the stigma of the flower folded flat against the ceiling. See my [Next] photo for a different view. This certainly counts as a butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: i608a_i608-020.jpg
Notes:
41964648044 *Sigh* The things we do for a photo...

This biting fly is drawing blood from my arm, but look at those antennae - it's not a Deer Fly (Tabanidae). I believe this is a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia of the Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) family. It's related to our common Snipe Flies in this photo, but this one has annoying biting habits. We actually have (at least) two biting flies in this genus living in our mountains. The other one is orange in color, see this photo. That one is a real bloodsucker that flies in and out faster than you can swat, but the one in this photo is different. It's slower for one thing. I noticed it on my arm and fumbled around with my tripod and finding a place to get the photo ... and it was still on my arm. It has impressive mouthparts that are visible in this photo from last year. This one was biting my upper arm, and it was a real stretch to get focus with my 105mm macro lens. This is the first one I've seen (and been bit by) this year. It is a fly, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2018)

It was sunny and warmer today, but the wind from the north started to kick up before noon, and it was soon too windy for my kind of photos. It sound like we're in for a hot and windy weekend, including a Fire Weather Watch on Saturday. Bah.
CommonNames: Rocky Mountain Bite Fly; Snipe Fly

File.Name: i608b_i608-014.jpg
Notes: 42682531341 This is another remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). I believe it is genus Thevenetimyia, maybe the same Thevenetimyia luctifera species that I showed in this photo, though this one has less gold. I was going to post this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2018)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia; Thevenetimyia luctifera
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia; Thevenetimyia luctifera

File.Name: i608c_i606-027.jpg
Notes: 41964188964 I don't know what kind of little fly this is, but what amazing eyes it has! That's a trait I associate with Tephritid Fruit Flies (Tephritidae) and Deer Flies (Tabanidae), but this fly doesn't appear to be either. It's tiny, about 800 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I found it on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) which is just starting to flower. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2018)

File.Name: i608d_i603-003.jpg
Notes: 40873157790 This is a shy little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) seemingly hiding behind a flower of California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. What amazing eyes it has! See this photo for the rest of the flower. The flowers attract lots of bees and flies, but they are small and they grow on a higher-than-head-high plant that sways in the slightest breeze that makes getting photos a challenge. I was planning on posting this to the Fly Day Friday group, but they have a two photo limit. I won't add this one, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2018)
CommonNames: Syrphid Fly; California Figwort; California Bee Plant

File.Name: i609a_i609-042.jpg
Notes:
42702963311 I believe this is a plain Gold-hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly working new flowers of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). It's the plainest of our "plain brown hairstreaks" - and you mostly recognize it by the process of elimination. It doesn't have obvious white postmedian lines and rusty-orange upper wings like the Hedgerow Hairstreak, and it doesn't have "white frosting" like the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak. This is the first one I've seen this year, a month later than last year, compare this photo on the same plant. It's possible I have this ID wrong, so please let me know. I love that name "Gold-hunter's Hairstreak" - I wonder where it comes from? (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2018)

The expected winds kicked up early today, but I managed to get some photos in the morning before it got too windy. We've had 45 mph gusts this afternoon! Now we're under both a NWS Wind Advisory and a Red Flag Warning until Monday morning. The good news is that it's been cooler than expected today, only in the 70s.
ScientificName: Satyrium auretorum; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Satyrium auretorum; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i609b_i609-008.jpg
Notes: 27833698207 This is a gorgeous nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia) on a petal of what I believe is Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica, Onagraceae) - though these flowers are not at all speckled. They do have erect wiry stems, nodding flower buds, and erect buds. The species Clarkia bottae is very similar. It was quite windy today, and getting this photo was a challenge as the flower was dancing around. I like the shadow! The flowers are one of a group of flowers known as Farewell-to-Spring. They're late this year and almost on schedule - the summer solstice and the beginning of summer is not too far off. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2018)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Clarkia cylindrica; Onagraceae; Clarkia bottae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Clarkia cylindrica; Onagraceae; Clarkia bottae

File.Name: i609c_i609-021.jpg
Notes: 40892745090 Here is a lovely little Damselfly (Zygoptera, Odonata) perched on the chipseal road in our canyon today. I don't really know much about damselflies, and I can't identify it - but I'm always happy to photograph one. As far as I know, our creek is dry for miles - I wonder where this one came from? (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2018)

File.Name: i609d_i609-018.jpg
Notes: 42653128532 I caught this Deer Fly (family Tabanidae, order Diptera) hovering in the air today! I wasn't close to the fly - this is actually a 1:1 crop, a 800x1070 crop from my 4000x6000 original. The fly about the size of a honey bee, and just as furry. I believe it is genus Stonemyia which are not biters, maybe S. californica. Unlike most other tabanid species, none of the females of this genus are known to take a blood meal. They visit flowers instead, like the one in this photo. Those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2018)

File.Name: i610a_i610-014.jpg
Notes:
28850796518 Here is a well-camouflaged little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) - with a little Beetle (Coleoptera) perilously close! The spider actually stepped down onto the spider, and the spider brushed it away with its hind leg! I was so startled by this that I twitched and the photos are not as sharp as they should be - I'm not sure if I'll post them, but I have them. I'm pretty sure the spider is genus Mecaphesa again because of its general hairiness. I have another photo of a yellow crab spider on yellow flowers of Golden Yarrow that might be the same kind of spider. I believe they are slow color shifters. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2018)

It was warmer today and breezy from the get-go. It wasn't the best day for new photos, but I got in few in the morning before the winds got too bad. But then in the afternoon the wind started to back off. We're still under both a NWS Wind Advisory and a Red Flag Warning until Monday morning. Hopefully, this will be the end of it.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Coleoptera; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Coleoptera; Mecaphesa

File.Name: i610b_i610-003.jpg
Notes: 42006077964 I believe this is a little Brown Lacewing in the family Hemerobiidae of the Neuroptera. I haven't seen one of these for years, and I don't know much about them. I found this one in a shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that is just starting to flower. They have interesting eyes that resemble those of their more common cousins, the Green Lacewings (Chrysopidae) - see this photo for an extreme example. According to BugGuide, both adults and larvae are predaceous; females attach eggs directly to leaves and not on stalks like this; and their larvae do not carry debris on their backs like this. They are small and easy not to notice. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2018)
CommonNames: Brown Lacewing; California Buckwheat; Green Lacewings
ScientificName: Hemerobiidae; Neuroptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Chrysopidae
associatedTaxa: Hemerobiidae; Neuroptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Chrysopidae

File.Name: i610c_i610-021.jpg
Notes: 28850144778 This is the handsome chrysalis of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, see this photo. It's hanging from a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). That's one of the plants the caterpillars favor. These caterpillars like to change plants when they pupate, but this one didn't travel very far - or maybe it moved over from the Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) that grows right next to it? Sometimes the caterpillars are consumed by parasitic wasps before they can form their pupae, see this photo. They seem vulnerable in this stage as pupa, but I reckon the hard shell of the chrysalis provides some protection. We have a fair number of the butterflies right now, but I still expect a lot more. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella
ScientificName: chrysalis; Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: i610d_i609-026.jpg
Notes: 41823787245 This is a fasciated stem of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. The stem is flat like a ribbon, but the plant is still producing a few seeds. You can see normal round stems in the background, as well as stems of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). Many plants get fasciated, some often and others (like this one) rarely. There's some info at Wikipedia. They are unusual and worth collecting - in photos at least. There is even a low-traffic Flickr group on Fascinating Fasciations to which I'll add this one. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2018)
ScientificName: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: i611a_i611-043.jpg
Notes:
42744094871 This is a female Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly perched on her host plant - native Longstem Buckwheat aka Wand Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I know it's a female because I saw her ovipositing on another plant a few moments before. I also got a photo of the male butterfly today visiting flowers of Golden Yarrow, but I like this one better. Unfortunately, neither photo shows the pretty upper wings - see this photo for the upper wings of male and female together. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 June 2018)

The winds finally died down today, leaving us with a gorgeous day. I saw hundreds of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies today, as well as an unusual Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) and the first California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis) of the year. No photos yet, but a quest begins!
Locality: Hemaris thetis) of the year. No photos yet
CommonNames: Gorgon Copper; Longstem Buckwheat; Wand Buckwheat; Variable Checkerspot; Callippe Fritillary; California Clearwing Moth

File.Name: i611b_i611-005.jpg
Notes: 40933150040 This is another California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). These butterflies appear in spring which distinguishes them from the somewhat similar Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterflies that appear later in summer, see this photo. They are similar, but there are differences when you look close. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2018)
CommonNames: California Hairstreak; Golden Yarrow; Gray Hairstreak
ScientificName: Satyrium californica; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Strymon melinus

File.Name: i611c_i611-039.jpg
Notes: 40933002330 Here are two shots of a little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. They never actually land. This fly looks like a small version of the larger genus Bombylius flies that we get in mid-winter, see this photo. Maybe it's the same genus - or at least the same tribe Bombyliini of the subfamily Bombyliinae. The proboscis is probably as long as the fly, see this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 June 2018)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius; Bombyliini; Bombyliinae

File.Name: i611d_i611-027.jpg
Notes: 27874533207 This is looking across at a flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. It's the first one that I've seen this year, flowering at the top of a six foot tall stalk so I couldn't look into it. This is the first of these fine thistles that I've seen this year. It should have a swallowtail butterfly working the flower like this photo or this photo - maybe next time. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 June 2018)
CommonNames: Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle

File.Name: i612a_i612-002.jpg
Notes:
40954411930 "It's a dirty job, but someone's gotta do it!"

Here is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) deeply engaged in a prickly pear (Opuntia) cactus flower in the garden. The bees almost seem to get intoxicated by the pollen and need some quiet time before they can fly back to the hive. This cactus has short spines. We may have collected a piece of this in Baja many years ago. It seems quite happy here, and it's grown. A neighbor was a retired authority on local cacti, and he thinks this may be a hybrid. See this photo of the little "cactus corner" in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2018)

It was another nice day, though it was breezier than yesterday. I'm a little distracted right now with a bum foot and upcoming events, but I'll get over it!

File.Name: i612b_i612-019.jpg
Notes: 42715958872 These appear to be two different galled flowers on the same shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The gall on the left has a beak, and it's made by a tiny two-winged Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) - see this photo and this photo with show the husk of the midge. The galled flower on the right looks different, but it's clearly a gall. Maybe it's the work of another gall midge - but maybe not. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2018)

File.Name: i612c_i611-035.jpg
Notes: 42047901074 Here are a mating pair of little beetles "doing it" on (and in!) a glandular new flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I have no idea what kind of beetles they are, but they're tiny. This is my photo for the Insect Hump Day group though it's not quite hump day by local time - but It is hump day by UTC/GMT/Flickr time, so here it is. What is it about food and sex? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 June 2018)

File.Name: i612d_i611-033.jpg
Notes: 27895850177 Here is an unfortunate Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that has been captured by a Jumping Spider (Salticidae). If you take a moment to take it in, you can see that the spider has the butterfly by the proboscis. (I've seen this before - see this photo.) The spider is probably genus Phidippus with its red and black colors and its relatively large size. The plant is a native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 June 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Jumping Spider; Golden Yarrow
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Salticidae; Phidippus; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Salticidae; Phidippus; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: i613a_i613-006.jpg
Notes:
42786489321 This is a particularly handsome Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly resting in partial shade on viny stems of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). The butterfly was working new honeysuckle flowers on a different plant a moment before. I reckon this one is newly emerged. The colors on its rear end are gorgeous. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2018)

It was another nice day, though it was still breezier. It was in the 80s again today, but it's expected to cool down some over the coming weekend.

File.Name: i613b_i613-025.jpg
Notes: 42068672594 Thistle mandala! This is a brand new opening flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family showing its first color. I usually call it the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. The cobwebby hairs are part of the plant, and give the plant its other common name of Cobweb Thistle. We only have a few flowers on stunted plants in this drought year, but I've been waiting for this one. Unlike our non-native weedy thistles, this one is truly beautiful. All it needs is the usual butterfly, but I'm sure they'll come as soon as it opens completely - maybe tomorrow. Hah - this photo makes me think of old Busby Berkeley musicals! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2018)

File.Name: i613c_i610-008.jpg
Notes: 42735889932 Here is a mating pair of little beetles "doing it" on a a flower of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) - with a tiny midge fly (Diptera) ovipositing on a different flower. Maybe the fly belongs to the Cecidomyiidae family, but as a wood midge and not a gall midge. Cause and effect? Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2018)

File.Name: i613d_i609-036.jpg
Notes:
42785702641 "The spider is in!"

This is the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider - with the spider lurking in the entrance. The spiders sit in the opening and run out in the blink of your eye to snag any prey that bumps into its web. (But they can usually tell the difference between a grass stem and real prey; they are not easy to fool.) This might be an insect's idea of the entrance to hell. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2018)

File.Name: i614a_i614-005.jpg
Notes:
28932181198 This is a handsome Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil on a garden path at home this morning. These butterflies are quite variable, compare this photo and this photo. And of course age adds to this variability as individual butterflies show their wear and tear as we all do. This butterfly is worn, but still beautiful. I've been seeing quite a few of these butterflies this year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2018)

It was another nice day, though it was quite breezy from the south - eddy winds coming around Point Conception? It was in the 80s again today, but it's expected to cool down into the 60s over the next couple of days. There might even be some drizzle on Saturday night.

File.Name: i614b_i614-010.jpg
Notes: 27936584537 I was trying to get another photo of the little Bee Flies on this plant (like this), when this Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly landed right next to be. I whipped my camera around and got the photo - over-exposed and not quite sharp, but it has an "aliveness" that I like. If you look close, you can see that the butterfly's proboscis is dusted with sticky pollen. This is how butterflies carry pollen around for their pollination service. The shrub is Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family, which is especially attractive to these checkerspot butterflies. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 June 2018)

File.Name: i614c_i614-018.jpg
Notes: 28931707728 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. The bee is collecting pollen by rubbing its abdomen back and forth across the flower tips. This behavior places it in the family Megachilidae of Leaf-cutter Bees and Mason Bees, but I'm not sure beyond that. The bee is metallic blue on its front half, so maybe it's genus Osmia? I'll post another photo in a day or two that shows more of the bee, but I like this photo of the bee's behind - so HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 June 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Leaf-cutter Bees; Mason Bees
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Megachilidae; Osmia

File.Name: i614d_i612-025.jpg
Notes: 42805005121 This is the beautiful bug butt of a little Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) that's digging into a flower of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) on the other end. I believe this one is a Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens), though you can't see why from this angle, and it hardly matters anyway. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2018)
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; California Coffeeberry; Convergent Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Hippodamia convergens

File.Name: i615a_i615-039.jpg
Notes:
28952238198 I believe this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly working brand new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that have hardly opened yet. One distinguishing feature of these butterflies is that they have hyaline (translucent) spots near the forewing tips - I'll add a note. This butterfly seems especially soft and beautiful - I suspect it's newly emerged. It's easy to just skip skippers, but they do have their charms! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2018)

It was another nice day, though it was quite breezy again from the south - eddy winds coming around Point Conception? It did get up to almost 80∞ today, but it's expected to cool down into the 60s over the next couple of days. There might even be some drizzle on Saturday night. Our grandkids are arriving tomorrow to visit for the week. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: i615b_i615-041.jpg
Notes: 28952124748 Here is another plain Gold-hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It's the plainest of our "plain brown hairstreaks" - and you mostly recognize it by the process of elimination. This is the second one I've photographed this year, see this photo. That one does have more distinct markings than this one, but I believe they are both the same. Look how the butterfly's proboscis is covered with sticky pollen. This is how butterflies carry pollen around for their pollination service. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2018)

File.Name: i615c_i615-020.jpg
Notes: 42776496542 This is a neat little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) with a furry body and patterned wings. I found it on the ground today, perched for a moment on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe it's genus Conophorus? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2018)

File.Name: i615d_i614-007.jpg
Notes: 41924171985 Here are two more shots of little Bee Flies (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. They never actually land. This fly looks like a small version of the larger genus Bombylius flies that we get in mid-winter, see this photo. Maybe it's the same genus - or at least the same tribe Bombyliini of the subfamily Bombyliinae. The proboscis is probably as long as the fly, see this photo. If my photo looks familiar, see this photo from a few days ago. I'll keep trying to get sharper photos, but in the meantime . . . HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 June 2018)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius; Bombyliini; Bombyliinae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius; Bombyliini; Bombyliinae

File.Name: i616a_i616-005.jpg
Notes:
41942977015 This is an impressive Bee-like Robber Fly in the genus Laphria of the family Asilidae. It's a large fly, maybe 5/8 inch long, with dragonfly-like eyes and bumblebee-like yellow and black hairs. They are predators that catch their prey in the air like dragonflies - hence the wonderful eyes. They don't visit flowers, so I don't see them very often - this is only the second one I've managed to photograph in my time at Flickr, see this photo of what appears to be the same kind of fly from 2013. This one was grounded by the gloomy weather today. I found it on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). The "Bee Killers" (genus Mallophora) are even more impressive flies - they have a thin terminal final antenna segment that these lack, but this is still an impressive fly. Sure, I saved a shot for next week's Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2018)

It was overcast and gloomy all day today - it never got out of the 50s. There was some drizzle last night and more is expected tonight. Our house is full of kids and grandkids! I'll do what I can for new photos.
ScientificName: Laphria; Asilidae; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae; Mallophora
associatedTaxa: Laphria; Asilidae; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae; Mallophora

File.Name: i616b_i616-010.jpg
Notes: 28971089988 Here is an unusually symmetrical new flower cluster of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). I'm looking down on these flowers, but the plant is a vine that sometimes grows high up into the oak trees. They are just starting to flower - late this year like most of our wildflowers. They are a favorite of swallowtail butterflies , see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2018)

File.Name: i616c_i615-010.jpg
Notes: 27974831767 Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) - with the sun behind. It's a beautiful combination. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2018)

File.Name: i616d_i615-026.jpg
Notes: 41942665775 This is looking down on a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly next to a new plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. I suspect the butterfly is ovipositing - at least it would be if the darned photographer would leave so it can get on with was doing. They are said to use plants in the snapdragon family (Scrophulariaceae) and the plantain family (Plantaginaceae), and the plant in this photo was formerly considered a "scroph". That's interesting because Penstemon plants that used to be considered scrophs are now considered to be in the plaintain family! I've seen this connection before, see this photo. This is the first photo I've managed to get of a Buckeye butterfly this year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2018)
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Penstemon
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Penstemon

File.Name: i617a_i617-004.jpg
Notes:
41055738720 Here's another shot of the impressive Bee-like Robber Fly in the genus Laphria of the family Asilidae - with raindrops from the light rain this morning. This is the same fly that I showed yesterday in this photo, perched on the same leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). It's been chilly, and the fly hasn't budged, though it has pivoted on the leaf a bit. It's a large fly, maybe 5/8 inch long, with dragonfly-like eyes and bumblebee-like yellow and black hairs. It's an exciting find! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2018)

We actually had almost 2/10 inch of light rain and drizzle this morning! It was overcast and gloomy all day today, and it never got out of the 50s again - chilly enough to keep this fly grounded for the second day. *Sigh* This unusual weather is probably not so good for the Live Oak Music Festival which is happening this weekend. Julie took the kids to the festival today . . . and I went for a walk in the woods.

File.Name: i617b_i617-023.jpg
Notes: 42865742341 Here is a remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera staying out of the rain today under a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I believe this is the nymph of the bug Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) that I sometimes find inside the flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) - see this photo from last year in May. These nymphs are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, move like ants, and even have a faux "nodal spine" like some ants have for real. You can see its long proboscis folded under its body, which real ants don't have. This is the first nymph or adult that I've seen this year, and I only found it because the light rain this morning bent the long white sage stems down closer to the ground. You can find more shots of these interesting critters if you search my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2018)
CommonNames: White Sage; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: i617c_i617-029.jpg
Notes: 27996956767 This is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider sitting in the entrance to its web. These spiders sit in the opening and run out in the blink of your eye to snag any prey that bumps into the web. (But they can usually tell the difference between a grass stem and real prey; they are not easy to fool.) I just posted another photo of such a web, but this shot shows the spider better. This might be an insect's idea of the entrance to hell! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2018)

File.Name: i617d_i615-030.jpg
Notes: 42817090542 Here is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the lip of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I'm pretty sure the spider is genus Mecaphesa again. This might actually be the same spider as in this photo taken a week ago - I got the photo in the same place, though it's probably not the same flower. I got this photo a couple of days ago when it was still warm and the sun was shining, but it's been gloomy since then. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2018)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Mecaphesa

File.Name: i618a_i618-028.jpg
Notes:
41078006440 Here is another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). Along with their pretty flowers, these plants always have that that umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf that is visible behind the flowers. I've been looking under the wings of these swallowtails for signs of Humboldt Lily pollen, see this photo. The lilies must be getting close to flowering, but not yet - in our canyon at least. The lilies are a favorite of these butterflies, but the honeysuckle will do! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2018)

What a change in the weather! Today was sunny and mostly clear unlike the two days of gloom and drizzle and 50s weather over the weekend. It got up into the 70s today, and it's expected to be back in the 80s tomorrow. Wind might be an issue.

File.Name: i618b_i618-043.jpg
Notes: 42888165001 This is another gorgeous nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia) munching the stamens of new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). They don't seem at all fussy about what they eat, and I find them on many different plants. These little nymphs are a favorite of mine. They always remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2018)

File.Name: i618c_i618-004.jpg
Notes: 41987556565 Note the long proboscis folded under its head. This is another photo of a remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera near a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). I got this photo today, but it's the same critter I showed yesterday in this photo - a nymph of the true bug Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae). These nymphs are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, move like ants, and even have a faux "nodal spine" like some ants have for real. I noticed several of these nymphs today. I still haven't seen any adults. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2018)
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae

File.Name: i618d_i614-025.jpg
Notes: 41070221390 This photo shows seed dispersal of native Mountain Dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. One seed is about to be blown away by the wind coming from the right - an effective method of seed transport. These seeds resemble common dandelions, but they are much larger. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Transportation". Humans aren't the only creatures that need to get around! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 June 2018)

File.Name: i619a_i619-038.jpg
Notes:
28042097087 I'm pretty sure this is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly on a flowering shrub of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It has more obvious markings on its wings that the Gold-hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly in this photo and lacks the "white frosting" of the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly in this photo. This is the first one I've seen his year. Butterflies and wildflowers have both been late. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2018)

What a nice day! Today was sunny, in the 80s, and wind was not an issue. What's not to like?
(Well the news actually - but that's another story.)
ScientificName: Satyrium saepium; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Satyrium saepium; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i619b_i619-027.jpg
Notes: 42192170504 What an odd insect! I'm pretty sure this is a male Scale Insect or Mealybug (Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) - maybe a Giant Mealybug in the family Putoidae, genus Puto. See this photo at BugGuide, and this photo by Alice Abelia which look very similar. The eyes of his insect are very strange, I added a closer crop. This critter is tiny, about 1/8 inch long without its tails - and 1/2 inch with. The long white tails are really just long hairs covered by a waxy exudate. I made a grab for one a few years ago, and the white stuff came off in my fingers leaving just the filament. I guess the whole insect is covered with the same waxy exudate. If this is a male, I have no idea what the females look like - but they must be out there. I don't see these critters every year, but I did see one last year also in June - see this photo. I found this one on a leaf of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae), but I'm sure that was just where it happened to land. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2018)
CommonNames: Scale Insect; Mealybug; Giant Mealybug; Toyon
ScientificName: Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Putoidae; Puto; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Putoidae; Puto; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae

File.Name: i619c_i619-004.jpg
Notes: 42009241965 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). It's taking nectar in this photo, but it was also collecting pollen by rubbing its abdomen on the stamen tips. This behavior places it in the family Megachilidae of Leaf-cutter Bees and Mason Bees. Maybe the bee is genus Chelostoma? The interesting flowers are originally folded up so that small bees like this can't get into them. Large bees like Carpenter Bees force the flowers open and make them available to other insects. I've been trying to get new photos to show this, but in he meantime see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; White Sage; Leaf-cutter Bees; Mason Bees
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Megachilidae; Chelostoma
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Megachilidae; Chelostoma

File.Name: i619d_i618-024.jpg
Notes: 29037697028 Here is another photo of the pretty new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). The plant is a vine that sometimes grows high up into the oak trees. They are just starting to flower - late this year like most of our wildflowers. They are a favorite of swallowtail butterflies , see my [Previous] photo. They are a favorite of mine too! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2018)

File.Name: i620a_i620-023.jpg
Notes:
28061226187 I should really close the book on new photos of these Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies - but this is a two-fer in an interesting pose! They are feeding on a shrub of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I was actually trying for a three-fer with no luck - but see this photo from last year, a five-fer! These checkerspots probably outnumber all our other butterflies by ten to one. Not that I'm complaining - they are beautiful and fairly docile as butterflies go. They are a good butterfly to practice on when you are just learning that you can really photograph them. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 June 2018)

It was another very nice day, but it actually got hot - up to 90 degrees! Hah, it didn't get up to 60∞ on Sunday; then into the 70s on Monday; and into the 80s on Tuesday . . . and now it's made it up to 90 degrees on Wednesday. Notice a pattern? The summer solstice is also officially tomorrow on June 21 at 3:07 a.m. PDT. It's the first day of summer when the sun is highest above the ecliptic and the path of the sun is highest in the sky. Happy solstice!

File.Name: i620b_i620-026.jpg
Notes: 42028396495 Here's another photo of a little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I've been trying to get a reasonably sharp photo for some time, see this photo and this photo. I finally got it right - better at least! The flies never actually land. They look like a small version of the larger genus Bombylius flies that we get in mid-winter, see this photo. Maybe it's the same genus - or at least the same tribe Bombyliini of the subfamily Bombyliinae. The proboscis is probably as long as the fly, see this photo - long enough to reach the bottom of the flower. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 June 2018)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius; Bombyliini; Bombyliinae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius; Bombyliini; Bombyliinae

File.Name: i620c_i617-023.jpg
Notes: 41119553550 Here are pair of mating Crane Flies (Tipulidae, Tipulomorpha, Diptera) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek. It was a gloomy day, and the flies didn't want to move - even when I arranged the leaves a bit. I assume that's the larger female on the left and the make on the right - larger because she contains eggs. That's quite an elaborate coupling they have between them! These flies have actually been scarce this year so I hope they are successful in their endeavor to make more flies. Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2018)
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Tipulomorpha; Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Tipulomorpha; Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: i620d_i615-047.jpg
Notes: 42028054475 This is the inner web of a tiny Feather-legged Spider (Uloborus diversus) in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers - with the spider sitting in the center. It seems to have a wrapped prey of some sort. This spider spins a proper orb web that is mostly flat, though it's not a proper orb weaver in the family Araneidae. Sometimes it adds a stabilimentum - a band of thicker webbing that runs across the web that's visible in this photo - but not this time. What I most like about this photo is the way you can follow the spiral of its web starting in the upper right. The spiral finally gets lost in the mesh of the inner web. I have more photos of this interesting spider here. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2018)
CommonNames: Feather-legged Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Hackled Band Orb Weavers

File.Name: i621a_i621-036.jpg
Notes:
41135064220 Here are brilliant new flowers of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) in Kinevan Canyon today, the first I've seen this year. I know they weren't here two days ago, but they might have opened yesterday. These plants along the road are vulnerable to drive-by pickers, and I hope they get some respect this year. I worry about the weekends. Of course there are other canyons, and I'll have to do some hunting! We have many plants in our canyon at home, but I don't think we'll have any flowers again this year as deer and a mystery grub that eats the flower buds have taken them down. They are beautiful flowers, almost too pretty to be native, but they are. There should be butterflies here, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2018)

Today was another very nice day, up to 90 degrees again. It sounds like we're in for a couple more hot days before it cools down some on Sunday.

File.Name: i621b_i621-021.jpg
Notes: 42896040982 I got photobombed today in a good way. I was in close getting a photo of the wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on the right when I heard the rustle of wings close to my ear - a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the same flower. I keep a chair in this place near the creek, so I scooched it back as far as I could and got this photo. The flower is native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I usually call it the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. I did get a close shot of the bee, see my [Previous] photo. Note that this butterfly is quite cream-colored. They seem to do that now that summer is here. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Pale Swallowtail; California Thistle
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Papilio eurymedon; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Papilio eurymedon; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: i621c_i621-025.jpg
Notes: 41134713560 This is a bee-sized wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I usually call this the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is - maybe genus Anthophora or kin? Those large bushy pollen baskets on her hind legs remind me of the Wooly Chaps that some cowboys used to wear. This is one of two "beautiful bee butt" shots today, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2018)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Anthophora
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Anthophora

File.Name: i621d_i618-011.jpg
Notes: 28075825937 This is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) working a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). They adopt what I think of as a "copulatory position" when they mount the flowers. You can see that the bee is not heavy enough to bring down the stamens and pistil. That takes a heavier Bumble Bee or Carpenter Bee. This is one of two "beautiful bee butt" shots today, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2018)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: i622a_i622-028.jpg
Notes:
42058335605 Here is a little Snakefly (family Raphidiidae, order Raphidioptera) insect on a flower of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe it is genus Agulla with that "kite-shaped" head and female with that long ovipositor. (See this photo for a male.) It's odd because I think of these insects as complete predators, but maybe even they need an occasional hit of sugary nectar? (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2018)

Today was another warm one, up into the 90s again. It sounds like we're in for one more hot day before it cools down some on Sunday.
ScientificName: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Agulla
associatedTaxa: Raphidiidae; Raphidioptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Agulla

File.Name: i622b_i622-015.jpg
Notes: 28090730677 *Sigh* We don't always get the photo we want, but at least I got something today! Here is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family - with a striking Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) flying away behind it. These wasps have remarkable goggle-like eyes - which means they can see really well, and they don't like being photographed! They also have a remarkably long proboscis which they use to get nectar from flowers. (I believe they also feed on flies and other insects, but I'm not sure how.) This is the first one I've seen this year, and I hope I get a do-over for the photo. I have had pretty good luck photographing these camera-shy wasps before, try searching my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; California Thistle; Bembecine Sand Wasp
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera

File.Name: i622c_i620-002.jpg
Notes: 28090662777 This is a little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) ovipositing (laying eggs) on a mature flower bud of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). The eggs better develop fast because this flower is nearly ready to open! I did see aphids on the shrub which might be the target. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2018)

File.Name: i622d_i616-007.jpg
Notes: 29087175868 This is looking face-to-face with an impressive Bee-like Robber Fly in the genus Laphria of the family Asilidae. It's a large fly, maybe 5/8 inch long, with dragonfly-like eyes and bumblebee-like yellow and black hairs. They are predators that catch their prey in the air like dragonflies - hence the wonderful eyes. This one was grounded by the gloomy weather last weekend - it was cold and it let me get close, see this photo and this photo. I found it on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). Sure, I saved this shot for Fly Day Friday - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2018)

File.Name: i623a_i623-015.jpg
Notes:
41164898880 This is a gorgeous Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). This plant has just started to flower in the last few days. This photo shows the upper wings of the butterfly, see my [Previous] photo for a peek below. I believe this one is Comstock's Fritillary (Speyeria callippe comstocki). These large butterflies are uncommon in our mountains, and I don't see them every year. I mostly find them on this Monardella plant - with just one exception that I found on Golden Yarrow, see this photo. This butterfly looks young and fresh, but the ones I've found in the past are often pale and worn - compare this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 June 2018)

Today started sunny and warm, but the wind picked up early and marine layer fog started spilling over mountains. I went high to get above it and got today's nice butterfly shots. We stayed in the fog most of the day at home, in the 60s rather than the 90s like yesterday.
CommonNames: Callippe Fritillary; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Comstock's Fritillary
ScientificName: Speyeria callippe; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Speyeria callippe comstocki
associatedTaxa: Speyeria callippe; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Speyeria callippe comstocki

File.Name: i623b_i623-027.jpg
Notes: 42256011714 This is a gorgeous Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). This plant has just started to flower in the last few days. This photo shows the silvered spots below the butterfly's wings. I believe this one is Comstock's Fritillary (Speyeria callippe comstocki). These large butterflies are uncommon in our mountains,and I don't see them every year. I mostly find them on this Monardella plant - with just one exception that I found on Golden Yarrow, see this photo. See my [Next] photo for a better look at its upper wings. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 June 2018)

File.Name: i623c_i623-056.jpg
Notes: 42255915314 Here are a pair of Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterflies sharing a flower cluster of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) - a female on the left and the copper-colored male on the right. The different genders look very different on their upper wings. I don't think they're courting or anything, it was just my good luck to find them together today. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 June 2018)

File.Name: i623d_i623-033.jpg
Notes: 42925008592 Such a small butterfly on such a large flower seems like overkill, but I often see this combination. I believe this is a small Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly working a new flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The butterfly is actually a good fit for the flower's petals, but the stamens and pistil keep going way beyond its reach. It actually kind of a stumper which pollinator the flowers are adapted for. I show one possibility in this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 June 2018)

File.Name: i624a_i624-020.jpg
Notes:
29119539948 It was cold and drizzly this morning, so I went into our woods to find butterflies - and I found one! It's an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on a furry stem of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae). I'm looking almost straight down on the butterfly in this photo, which is actually sticking out nearly perpendicular from the stem like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays on a chilly day. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2018)

We were back in a cloud this morning, with temps in the 50s and drizzle. I stayed in the canyon, and had some luck finding insects like this butterfly that were grounded by the chill. Our kids went back home to Oregon yesterday. It was a good visit, but peace and quiet is also a good thing!
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: i624b_i624-018.jpg
Notes: 42273018464 It's hard to tell from the photo, but this is the beautiful golden behind of an interesting fly that's hiding in a flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family on a cold and drizzly day. It's a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. These flies seem made for the flowers - just the right diameter to fit inside and a long proboscis to reach the nectary at the flower's base. You can see the tip of its proboscis extending back past its tail, but see this photo and this photo of flies approaching a flower in the air to really appreciate what that proboscis is! I hear these flies more often than I see them, as they fly with a high-pitched whine that is distinctive. I'll keep trying to get a better photo, but in the meantime you can search my photo for few more shots. These flies are fast and erratic so I was happy to find this one at rest today. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2018)
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Metallic Small-Headed Fly
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Diptera; Eulonchus smaragdinus
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Diptera; Eulonchus smaragdinus

File.Name: i624c_i624-005.jpg
Notes: 42272874474 This is another remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera staying out of the drizzle today in a flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I believe this is the nymph of the bug Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) that I also find among the flowers of White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) - see this photo from last week. These nymphs are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, and move like ants, but they have the proboscis of a true bug folded under its body. I found an adult in a nearby flower today, but it got away from me before I got a decent photo. Like ants, these bugs are fast! You can find more shots of these interesting critters if you search my photos.
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; White Sage
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: i624d_i624-012.jpg
Notes: 29119011408 Here are a pair of new mushroom-like spring galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - with drizzle drops. The green color is interesting as these galls are often reddish, see this photo. These are "bisexual galls" because the will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual "oak apple" galls, see this photo, which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that. It's part of what makes then so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2018)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i625a_i625-053.jpg
Notes:
42961813632 Here are two shots of a little hairstreak butterfly tilted towards the sun like a living solar collector, trying to catch some rays on a chilly day. Actually the sun was out by then, and it wasn't especially chilly - but here it is! Compare this photo of a different butterfly doing the same thing yesterday on a genuinely chilly morning. Good enough, but what kind of butterfly is it? It looks plain enough to be a Gold-hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum), but I believe it's really a worn Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly - look how you can see a hint of its rusty-orange upper wings in that place were the wing is torn. I found this one on a flowering shrub of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2018)

Today started gloomy and chilly like it's been, but the sun came out in the morning, and it warmed up nicely into the 70s. As soon as we lost the morning fog, we also lost the breeze. Nice day!
ScientificName: Satyrium saepium; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Satyrium saepium; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i625b_i625-034.jpg
Notes: 42292824654 This is a tiny diurnal (daytime) micro-moth (Lepidoptera) working a new flower of native Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plants have just started flowering, and I suspect the moths were waiting for them - I don't see them anywhere else except on these flowers. They are quite pretty with iridescent gold/bronze scales when the light is just right. I don't know what kind of moth it is. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2018)

File.Name: i625c_i625-059.jpg
Notes: 42292573794 I believe this is a Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae, Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) that I found on a leaf of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata, Anacardiaceae). It's larger and hairier than the usual beetles I find in his group like the one in this photo. Maybe it's the species Strophiona tigrina? It's a handsome beetle. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2018)
ScientificName: Lepturinae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Strophiona tigrina
associatedTaxa: Lepturinae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Strophiona tigrina

File.Name: i625d_i625-019.jpg
Notes:
42961048472 "Please Don't Pick The Flowers!"

*Sigh* I suppose it was inevitable. Some thoughtless "drive-by picker" got this native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) that was growing along West Camino Cielo road in Kinevan Canyon over the weekend. See this photo from last Thursday of what they got, and it had eight more flower buds! There are other plants still coming, but this one was the biggest and most productive of the plants along the road. I've seen this before, see this photo and this photo. At least they did it right this time - a diagonal cut with a sharp tool to maximize water intake in their vase. Bah! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2018)

Please, don't pick the wildflowers. Leave them for the butterflies and to make seeds.

File.Name: i626a_i626-006.jpg
Notes:
42314345124 I know, I said I was closing the book on these Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies, but this one is doing something interesting by working flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). Look how perfectly the butterfly is inserting its proboscis under the flap of the sage flower! I thought it was unique to find this butterfly on White Sage, but then I did a search and found this photo from 2015 in my collection! This is still pretty unusual and worth a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2018)

Today started sunny for the first time in a few gloomy days, but there was an annoying breeze for most of the day. It was curious as well as annoying because the breeze kept changing direction back and forth between south/east to north/west. I believe this is the result of an eddy wind from the south meeting our usual wind from the north.

File.Name: i626b_i626-016.jpg
Notes: 42130429335 Here is a mating pair of little Leafhoppers (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a branch of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica, Rhamnaceae) that's just starting to flower. I say "little" leafhoppers, but they are actually larger than most. They have a pretty aquamarine color. Hah, there are about 3,000 described species in North America, and I haven't tried to identify these. It's not quite Wednesday by local time, but it is by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2018)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i626c_i626-002.jpg
Notes: 28163798147 This is a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. It's one of those bees in the family Megachilidae that collects pollen on the underside of its abdomen, though this one was only interested in nectar. Maybe the bee is genus Chelostoma? The flowers only last for less than a day before they shrivel up, but there are lots of new flowers that keep on coming. It's interesting how the stamen filaments especially become twisted as they dry out, compare my [Previous] photo. This perennial plant is a native that has become happily established on the wild edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2018)
