File.Name: i626d_i625-003.jpg
Notes: 42982503512 I found this little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on a spent flower of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. What look like twisting worms in the bowl of the flower are really the originally straight filaments (stalks) of the stamens - compare my [Next] photo. It's a striking little fly, maybe genus Geron with that round head and humped back. Another possibility is the little fly in this photo that may be genus Acreophthiria in the tribe Phthiriini. I'm not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2018)

File.Name: i627a_i627-025.jpg
Notes:
42151908525 Here is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These moths are fast, and they never stop moving, hovering in place as they work the flowers - a challenge to photograph! I find the moths on a number of different flowers, but these mints might be their favorites. They are also one of my favorites - they look like spring flowers, but they pick the hot and dry time of the year to flower. All of my butterfly photos today are on this plant. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 June 2018)

Today was a nice day - the fog stayed along the beach, and the breeze was not as bad as yesterday. I went back to the place I call "FAA Peak" - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. There's a colony of these monardella plants up there that are magnets for butterflies. There's another colony in our canyon at home that always seem to flower later. That gives me two chances to photograph the butterflies and other insects that love these flowers.

File.Name: i627b_i627-038.jpg
Notes: 28185092587 This is an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). It is somewhat tattered, but in better shape than the pale specimen in this photo. These butterflies are distinctive because the black on their forewings runs along the wing rather than across it like our other swallowtails. These butterflies mostly use non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) as their host plant. I have found a few caterpillars this year, but only a few - see this photo and follow the link. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 June 2018)

File.Name: i627c_i627-074.jpg
Notes: 42335227874 Here is another Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) - with another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on an adjacent flower. If you compare the specimen in this photo from a few days ago, you can appreciate how worn this one is and how brilliant the other one is. This is actually how I usually find them, and it took me a while to figure out what they are. I believe this one is Comstock's Fritillary (Speyeria callippe comstocki). These large butterflies are uncommon in our mountains, though I saw several of these worn ones today on Monardella plants. They seem to be having a good year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 June 2018)

File.Name: i627d_i627-002.jpg
Notes: 29181574418 This is a large spider web outside our upstairs bathroom window. I admire this web every morning when I sit on the throne, and I finally removed the window screen to get the photo. It's a couple of feet across - not huge, but the rigging lines span a good twenty feet which helps create a sense of size. This is one of those webs than can span a road that you don't want to walk into at night - where's the spider?! I've never seen the spider on this web, but it repairs the web every night. I'm sure the spider is genus Araneus or Neoscona in the family of Orb Web Weavers (Araneidae). This would be a good Halloween photo, but here it is in June. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2018)
ScientificName: where's the spider?!; Araneus; Neoscona; Araneidae
associatedTaxa: where's the spider?!; Araneus; Neoscona; Araneidae

File.Name: i628a_i628-015.jpg
Notes:
28210423517 I found this handsome Beetle (Coleoptera) on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) today. I'm not sure what kind of beetle it is, but if I had to guess I would suggest a Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae, maybe genus Nemognatha - see BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2018)

Today was another nice day once the morning fog burned off. We're at 2500 feet elevation, so that was still a thick marine layer! It ended up being warm but not hot, and the breeze was not too bad.

File.Name: i628b_i628-027.jpg
Notes:
43028661292 This is a tiny gall with the pupal husk of an emerging two-winged Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) coming out of the side.I found this gall today on native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). Just so there's no confusion, that's a new berry of the Coffeeberry right behind the gall. I've noticed something similar in this photo and this photo and this photo. I did some research and found this on page 61 of Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) today, describing the ways of Gall Wasps (Cecidomyiidae): ". . . the mature pupae exit only about two-thirds of the way out, using the pressure of the last part of the passage to hold them firmly in place while they complete their transformation into adults." That pretty much perfectly describes what I see in my photo.

It's curious though . . . This gall seem smaller than the usual Coffeeberry galls that I find, about 850 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. It's also appeared not long after flowering, whereas I usually find hem the next year. Based on where I found it, this my even be the gall that I show on the left in this photo earlier this month. Lots of questions! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2018)
ScientificName: Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i628c_i627-052.jpg
Notes: 42174183925 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a new flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's reaching out for a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that is collecting pollen on it's abdomen from one of the long-exerted stamens. Curiously, the spider couldn't quite grasp the bee, and the bee didnt' seem conerned at all and got away with no problem. It's one of those bees in the family Megachilidae that collects pollen on the underside of its abdomen, maybe genus Chelostoma again? This photo isn't particularly close, but I reckon it counts as a "double butt shot" of both spider and bee - so HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 June 2018)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Woolly Blue Curls; wild bee
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Megachilidae; Chelostoma
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Megachilidae; Chelostoma

File.Name: i628d_i627-079.jpg
Notes: 43028292502 This little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) is working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) by hovering in the air as it flies from flower to flower. This is the same kind of fly I've been showing on Trask's Yerba Santa, e.g. in this photo. They look like a small version of the larger genus Bombylius flies that we get in mid-winter, see this photo. Maybe it's the same genus - or at least the same tribe Bombyliini of the subfamily Bombyliinae. Sure it's a tight crop - how could it not be? It's also a nice shot of the fly's rear end - with mutton-chop whiskers! HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 June 2018)

File.Name: i629a_i629-024.jpg
Notes:
41287855950 I was prefocussed on the flower trying to get a photo of the Bembecine Sand Wasp that was occasionally visiting, see my [Previous] photo. I heard a noise and saw a blur through my viewfinder . . . I managed to push the shutter button and got this photo! It's just about full frame across, but scrunched down towards the bottom of the frame - hence the non-standard aspect. This is a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) visiting a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I was sitting about two feet away! A blur, and then it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2018)

We were in a cloud for much of the day, and it was cooler, only about 70∞ even after the sun finally came out. There was some breeze as the fog cleared - those pesky "fog winds".
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: i629b_i629-021.jpg
Notes: 29226611598 This is a striking Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I usually call it the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. These wasps have remarkable goggle-like eyes - which means they can see really well, and they don't much like being photographed! They also have a remarkably long proboscis which they use to get nectar from flowers. (I believe they also feed on flies and other insects, but I'm not sure how they manage with that proboscis.) This wasp is covered with sticky pollen from the flower. I had to wait quite a while to get this photo, but I got my [Next] photo while I was waiting! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2018)
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: i629c_i629-009.jpg
Notes: 29226462908 Here is a cute little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) collecting pollen from a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides, Asteraceae). There are many different kinds of these pretty flies, so I'll leave it at that. It was a chilly morning with cloud cover, so I was surprised to find this one actively feeding. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2018)

File.Name: i629d_i626-037.jpg
Notes: 28229726607 This is a small dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae) in the woods. It might be a Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini), at least it's similar to that species. These little flies come in bigger and smaller versions, maybe a gender difference? They are the probably the most common "interesting flies" in the woods right now, but they're not easy to photograph. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2018)

Notes:
41308045660 Here is another photo of a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These moths are fast, and they never stop moving, hovering in place as they work the flowers - note that in this photo the moth is not as sharp as the flowers! I find these moths on a number of different flowers, but these mints might be their favorites. They are also one of my favorites - they look like spring flowers, but they pick the hot and dry time of the year to flower. All of my butterfly photos today are on this plant, which I show by itself in this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2018)

Today was much like yesterday - morning fog and only about 70∞ temps even after the sun finally came out. I went into the higher mountains to get away from the fog, and I could see the 3,000 foot thick marine layer sitting off the coast. There was wind from the south today trying to blow the fog ashore, but it didn't quite make it. (Not yet, anyway.)

File.Name: i630b_i630-053.jpg
Notes: 28249790197 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I also saw a male flying by, but he didn't want to pose. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year, though I did get this photo last December 28. I believe they overwinter as adults, and the new brood is just appearing now. These females are handsome butterflies, though they are plain compared to the impressive males - compare this photo. The pointed "falcate" forewings are distinctive among our similar species. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2018)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: i630c_i630-087.jpg
Notes: 42214382315 Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Note that this butterfly isn't as pale as some are - they sometimes get a cream color during summer. This one is in good condition except for a missing chunk of its tail - the wound is symmetrical, and I guess some bird took a bite when the butterfly's wings were closed. I'm glad it got away and was able to pose for me. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2018)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: i630d_i630-093.jpg
Notes: 42400269704 Here is a whole plant of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - with lots of flowers and even a few butterflies. I've been posting so many photos of butterflies on these flowers lately, that I figured it was time to show the plant. It's a perennial that favors disturbed areas but not exclusively so. Several of its cousins in the same genus (like this one) have a wonderful peppermint smell, but this one smells musty and not quite pleasant. These plants are growing in the place I call "FAA Peak" - the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro that hosts a FAA radio transmitter. There's another colony in our canyon at home that always seems to flower later. That gives me two chances to photograph the butterflies and other insects that are attracted to the flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2018)

File.Name: i701a_i701-036.jpg
Notes:
42422863614 I found his handsome male Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly by native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. The female butterflies have brown wings on top, see this photo from a month ago. This is actually the first male I've been able to photograph this year. These butterflies have been slow to appear this year, but that matches their Deerweed host plant which has also been late - and it's still flowering beyond its normal time. It's been a most peculiar year with most of our rain (such as it is) coming late in the month of March, and most of our flowers and insects coming after. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2018)

Today was much like yesterday, but a tad warmer - morning fog and temps in the upper 70s. It was windy enough to be annoying, with chaotic winds from both the north and south. The forecast is that it will warm up substantially . . . after the Fourth. That's good news as the connection between illegal fireworks and wildfire spooks me every year. We don't need a heat advisory to make the threat worse.

File.Name: i701b_i701-037.jpg
Notes: 28272069357 This is a Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly on a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). These butterflies were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from back east. These butterflies have no strong markings below. This one is male - I could see its handsome blue upper wings when it took off. I think of these as one of our earliest butterflies, see this photo from February a few years ago. I never did see an early brood this year, but this must be the second brood that is appearing now. The earliest ones must have slipped past me. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2018)
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Celastrina echo; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Celastrina echo; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: i701c_i701-062.jpg
Notes: 42422394774 Here is another striking Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Actually both wasp and flower are equally striking - the flower looks like something under the sea! I usually call it the "violet thistle" to distinguish it from the "white thistle" that grows higher in the mountains - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. These flowers are a magnet for the wasps, but this is our last flower - at least for a while. The wasps do visit other flowers, but nothing so large and photogenic as this thistle. I have to get photos while I can! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2018)
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: i701d_i701-041.jpg
Notes: 42236882535 I found this odd spider on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon today. It was sitting curled up like a kitten - though at one moment it stretched its legs, and I got the photo. I wonder if this is the first step in molting? I've only seen spiders in the later stages of a molt (like this), so I'm not sure how it happens. In this photo, it does look like the skin of its abdomen is tearing loose revealing the oddly colored skin underneath. The spider is small but not tiny. I suspect it's genus Araneus - maybe a juvenile Araneus andrewsi because of its dark colors and bristly legs. I'll check back on this spider in a day or two if it's still there. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2018)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus; Araneus andrewsi
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Araneus; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: i702a_i702-014.jpg
Notes:
43163849451 Here is a feisty little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) driving off a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) from a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is the "white thistle" as opposed to the "violet thistle" that I've been showing - they are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. The wild bee is remarkable! Once I noticed what it was doing, I saw it chase the Honey Bee away several times. When it wasn't chasing the bee, it was perched on a flower looking and waiting to pounce again. I'm not sure what kind of wild bee it is, but it has an attitude! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2018)

Today was much like yesterday again, morning fog and temps in the 70s. The day started clear and calm, though a tongue of fog spilled through the mountain pass above us and the fog winds immediately kicked up. I went back into the higher mountains and rose above it. I was really looking for red, white, and blue flowers today for an Independence Day triptych.
CommonNames: wild bee; Honey Bee; California Thistle
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Apis mellifera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: i702b_i702-045.jpg
Notes: 43114119562 Here are male and female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterflies on native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I just show my first male blue yesterday, and here are a courting pair. The female on the right with brown upper wings is pretending to feed, but I'm sure she's paying attention to to the male showing off his handsome blue wings! It's always tough getting a photo with two subjects - this time neither butterfly is quite sharp, but it has a nice balance. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2018)

File.Name: i702c_i702-024.jpg
Notes: 29292360298 This is a female Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly showing her unusual upper wings that are completely different from her mate's - see this photo for male and female together. This lady is perched on a shrub of native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica, Asteraceae), but she's really interested in the Longstem Buckwheat aka Wand Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family - her host plant - which is growing where I'm standing. She was ovipositing on the buckwheat. I got a photo, but I like this one better. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2018)

File.Name: i702d_i702-065.jpg
Notes: 28293750687 This is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly starting to work its way down a flower cluster of locally uncommon native False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. The shrubs have a light and airy look, like mesquite or acacia in the desert, but here they are in the mountain chaparral. They are said to be the host plant for California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies, but I don't believe they're the only host plant - the butterflies are common on San Marcos Pass where I live, but the shrubs don't grow there. They grow on Refugio Pass and Arroyo Burro Pass but for some reason skip San Marcos Pass - as far as I know. This particular shrub took a hit last summer when it was cut to the ground by an over-zealous crew trimming the mountain road. The shrub has stump-sprouted and is recovering nicely. I have another close shot of the interesting flowers in this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2018)
CommonNames: California Hairstreak; False Indigo; California Dogface
ScientificName: Satyrium californica; Amorpha californica; Fabaceae; Colias eurydice
associatedTaxa: Satyrium californica; Amorpha californica; Fabaceae; Colias eurydice

File.Name: i703a_i703-019.jpg
Notes:
42467344514 This is a remarkable inflorescence of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) in Kinevan Canyon today - I count 22 buds on this plant that is just starting to flower! I hope that this plant doesn't share the fate of this lily - shown here. I hope it survives the holiday tomorrow. Please, don't pick the wildflowers! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2018)

(That tree in the background is an unusual Incense Cedar (Calocedrus decurrens, Cupressaceae). I believe these trees were introduced long ago, though they've been seeding and proliferating in Kinevan Canyon, and they are native in the backcountry.)

The marine-layer fog was deeper today and lasted longer, though it did finally clear up. Tomorrow should be one last mild day before the triple-digit heat comes on Friday and Saturday. Damn, it sounds like there will be wind with that heat. The good news is that the heat comes after Independence Day - the connection between illegal fireworks and wildfire spooks me every year.
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae
associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae

File.Name: i703b_i702-028.jpg
Notes:
41374829250 Here are red, white, and not-quite blue native thistles for Independence Day tomorrow! It curious that all three have the same binomial scientific name, and all can be called by the same common name - Cobwebby Thistle. Here are:

Red - Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family;
White - White Thistle aka California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family; and
Not-quite Blue - Lavender Thistle aka California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae

Yeah, I've done this before - and why not? See this photo from 2016. I'll post more photos of red, white, and real blue flowers tomorrow on the fourth of July. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2018)
CommonNames: Cobwebby Thistle; Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; White Thistle; California Thistle; Lavender Thistle; California Thistle

File.Name: i703c_i702-038.jpg
Notes: 43185066671 This is a stunning wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) emerging from within a flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess that the bee is a Green Sweat Bee (genus Agapostmon, Halictidae), though there are other metallic green bees. This one did have pollen-collecting scopae on its hind legs. The bee would spend whole minutes deep within the flower, come up for air, and go down again . . . and again. One thing they didn't do is stand still for a photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Green Sweat Bee

associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Agapostmon; Halictidae

File.Name: i703d_i628-009.jpg
Notes: 43135130372 Here are a mating pair of little beetles (Coleoptera) on flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). Or more correctly, they are thinking about mating - despite being in mating position, I don't see any intimate connection between them. Maybe this is a kind of "mate guarding" in which males ride on the backs of females for some time to keep other males away - see BugGuide. Or maybe they're just taking a break? I like the little dollops of pollen on their noses! It's not quite Wednesday by local time, but it is by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, so happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2018)

File.Name: i704a_i704-015.jpg
Notes:
42486826504 This is a mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It's nicely posed on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) where it was waiting by the flowers for prey. The fog winds were blowing at the time, and I had to take many photos to get this keeper. I wish there was a hint of blue in its mostly black body so that it could be red, white, and blue for Independence Day . . . but there isn't. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2018)

We had our usual marine-layer fog this morning with the usual fog winds that gave me some trouble getting this photo as it cleared up. It was mild today for Independence Day, but now it will get hot. We're under a NWS Excessive Heat Watch and a Fire Weather Watch starting on Friday. Yipes - the forecast calls for 104∞ with 40 mph winds! There's more info at the California Weather Blog.
CommonNames: California Bee Assassin; Assassin Bug; California Coffeeberry
ScientificName: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i704b_i702-083.jpg
Notes:
43155722572 Here are red, white, and blue native flowers for Independence Day!

Red - Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The plants in the higher mountains like this one are just starting to flower.

White - Chaparral Yucca (Hesperoyucca whipplei) in the Agavaceae plant family aka Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae. Usually they are fading by July, but there are still new stalks that haven't flowered yet.

Blue - Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. True blue flowers are the hardest to find at this time of year, and these have some magenta in them. Violet is more common.

Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to remember our revolutionary heritage. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2018)
CommonNames: Scarlet Larkspur; Chaparral Yucca; Chaparral Penstemon
ScientificName: Delphinium cardinale; Ranunculaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Penstemon heterophyllus; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: i704c_i702-086.jpg
Notes: 42301014025 Who needs fireworks for the Fourth of July with wildflowers like this in the mountains! These are stunning new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They even look like little rockets with that spur in the back! This is a summer wildflower that is not common in our mountains, but it's well worth looking for. The plants sometimes grow quite tall, up to eight feet in a more favorable year. It's another plant where you have to pinch yourself and remember that it is native. I haven't always been able to find these flowers for my Fourth of July triptych because they are all finished y now in some years, but this year most of the flowers are still waiting to open. Like I've said before, everything is late this year. Most of the plants are higher in the mountains, but I found these near Knapp's Castle. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2018)

File.Name: i704d_i702-001.jpg
Notes: 42300821135 Here are two shots of the same small spider in its tube-like retreat on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). The spider has another layer of webbing over the whole leaf, so these shots are taken through two layers of diaphanous webbing. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but I thought at the time that it looked like a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider with a slightly different web, the funnel becomes a tube. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2018)

File.Name: i705a_i705-019.jpg
Notes:
29354661728 This is an interesting Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I found it today up over my head in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. It's interesting for me because I don't recognize it - and this is a group I like! This bug has a uniform tan color, no spines on its front legs, and a pair of short bumps on its pronotum. It's fairly large, maybe an inch long. I tried to relocate it after I got this photo, but it took off for parts unknown. I don't know what it is, and I would appreciate any suggestions. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2018)

We had weather from two different directions today. It was hot and sunny and dry from the north. But it was cooler, cloudy, and muggy from the south. There were periods of both. Overall, there was a nice tropical feel to the day, but it sounds like tomorrow will be more of a desert day. We're now under a NWS Wind Advisory and an Excessive Heat Warning and a Red Flag Warning starting tonight. The watches yesterday have become warnings tonight. Yipes - the forecast calls for 106∞ with 45 mph winds! There's more info at the California Weather Blog.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i705b_i705-033.jpg
Notes: 43176502502 This is a galled leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe it's the work of a Midrib Gall Moth (Sorhagenia nimbosa) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the Lepidoptera. I've only found a few of these this year, always on shrubs that have buds or are flowering or just finished. It's a real gall, not just a leaf-roller. You can see how the cell structure of the leaf is changed by the caterpillar inside, and it's interesting that the tip of the leaf is unaffected beyond the pinched part. I wouldn't recognize the moth, but there are photos at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2018)
ScientificName: Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Sorhagenia nimbosa; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Sorhagenia nimbosa; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: i705c_i702-051.jpg
Notes: 43225789371 Here are two different wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. These bees have different strategies for working the flower. The bee on top is after pollen, which it collects in special hairs under its abdomen. It's a deep metallic blue color that almost looks black. Maybe it's genus Osmia? It mostly stays on top of the flower collecting pollen with its rear legs. The green bee burrows into the flower and completely disappears for minutes at a time. It collects pollen on its hind legs, but I think it's after nectar here. I have another shot of this bee emerging from within in this photo. This bee might be a Green Sweat Bee (genus Agapostmon, Halictidae). They are both decent butt shots, so HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2018)
CommonNames: wild bees; Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Green Sweat Bee

File.Name: i705d_i701-058.jpg
Notes: 29353893548 This is the behind of a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) visiting a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This wasp looks pretty ordinary from behind like this, but the have remarkable goggle-like eyes and a long proboscis which they use to get nectar from flowers - see this photo. If you look close at the end of this wasp's abdomen, you can see what sure looks like a stinger. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2018)
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: i706a_i706-004.jpg
Notes:
42528309644 This photo is a bit underexposed, but it shows an interesting Bumble Bee (Bombus, Apidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I think it might be the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) - see this photo from last year for a brighter shot of what I think is the same bee. Last summer, I found it working new flowers of the Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae), but that plant is not flowering yet. This phacelia seems like a poor substitute! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2018)

It's too damn hot . . . listen! I reckon it was too damn hot for most insects today - and it was certainly too hot for insect photographers, though I did get a few photos in the morning before it got bad. We got up to 106∞ today by my thermometer on the upstairs porch - in the shade of course. It was 112∞ down by the river, and a friend in Mission Canyon also recorded 112∞ at their house. That makes sense, as the winds from the north heat by compression as they blow down the mountains towards the coast, so it should be hotter. It's still over 90∞ as I write this at 6:30 pm. It might be a few degrees cooler tomorrow, but that's still in triple digits. The good news is that the wind hasn't been bad. though we're still under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. They do call them "sundowner winds" for a reason, so maybe they're still coming?

(I spoke too soon. The winds did kick up last night, mostly below us on the coast side of he mountains. There was a wildfire in the Goleta foothills which destroyed several houses. Bummer.)
Locality: I spoke too soon.  The winds did kick up last night
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Branching Phacelia; Morrison Bumble Bee; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily

File.Name: i706b_i706-006.jpg
Notes: 43246369631 Here are two small wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. (It happens to be the same flower.) Both bees look the same at first, but look closer! The bee on the left is one of those bees in the family Megachilidae that collects pollen on the underside of its abdomen, though this one was mostly interested in nectar. (Maybe it's genus Chelostoma?) The bee on the right is different because it has pollen-collecting hairs on its hind legs. (Maybe it's genus Ceratina?) There are other small black bees that lack pollen-collecting hairs altogether. Bees are complicated like that - especially these tiny black ones! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2018)

File.Name: i706c_i705-004.jpg
Notes: 43197240672 Here is a tiny Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) on the long-exerted stamens of a native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. the fly really is tiny - pixels - it's about 740 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.9 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I've seen this fly before on this plant, see this photo. That time I suggested the fly might be genus Geron or Acreophthiria. I'm still not sure. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2018)

File.Name: i706d_i705-013.jpg
Notes: 42342138885 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I suspect that it's a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae), though it's smaller than most. It has reddish eyes and a red tip on its tail. Just a fly . . . and then it was gone. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2018)

File.Name: i707a_i707-032.jpg
Notes:
41456865400 This is a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. Or is it a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) that uses buckwheat as a host plant? (Yeah, the name is confusing, apparently a reference to the color of lupine flowers.) See this photo for some discussion. I guess I don't really care what it is - it's a handsome butterfly! This isn't the sharpest photo, but I like the colors - especially the orange on the butterfly's wing, the flowers, and the background. I got this photo in partial shade today - a sensible place to be on a very hot day. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2018)

It was forecast to be a few degrees cooler today, but if anything it was hotter - I logged 108∞ today by my thermometer on the upstairs porch, in the shade of course. Thankfully, the winds have been pretty calm today - though that's what I said last night before the sundowner winds kicked up. There was a wildfire in the foothills above Goleta last night. The Holiday Fire is now 80% contained after burning about 100 acres with maybe 20 houses/structures burned. That's our first significant wildfire of the year, but I'm sure there it's not the last. The forecast calls for "only" 99∞ tomorrow.
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; California Buckwheat; Lupine Blue
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Plebejus  lupini

File.Name: i707b_i707-002.jpg
Notes: 43217106792 Here is the tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a flower cluster of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. The plant is glandular and bristly, yet the nymphs like it - I saw several of them on the plants today. The flowers grow in a "scorpioid inflorescence" - coiled like a scorpion's tail or the neck of a fiddle. They will keep producing new flowers in the curl of the stem as long as they can, fading to seed pods behind - see this photo and this photo of different Phacelias. If you look close, you can see that the nymph is missing something important - see my [Previous] photo for a closer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2018)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Boraginaceae

File.Name: i707c_i707-011.jpg
Notes: 43216860802 Here is the tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a flower cluster of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. The plant is glandular and bristly, yet the nymphs like it - I saw several of them on the plants today. (There seem to be two different kinds, though I'm still trying to get a photo of the other ones. They're fast!) Did you notice that this nymph is missing a hind leg? That must be pretty important for these little hoppers. I wonder if they can regenerate that missing leg on a future molt?? See my [Next] photo for another wider shot of the same cricket that shows more of the plant. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2018)

File.Name: i707d_i707-034.jpg
Notes: 41456343710 Starting from the upper-left, here's a flower, another flower . . .and a well-camouflaged Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for prey! The flowers are native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It was over 100∞ when I got this photo, and insects were scarce - though maybe that's a little bee in the first flower? I notice the Ambush Bug is in the shade, a sensible place to be. This is the first adult I've seen this year, though I have seen nymphs - see this photo taken on a different flower in the same place. This seems late to see the first adults, but then most everything is late this year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2018)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: i708a_i708-025.jpg
Notes:
28418688247 This is a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) flying into a new flower of non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare, Asteraceae) that has just started to flower in the canyon. We'll have to remember to remove this weed before it produces seed - but not for a while. These wasps have a remarkably long proboscis which they use to get nectar from flowers. They also have remarkable goggle-like eyes - which means they can see really well, and they don't much like being photographed! This one also has black and red mark on its left eye - and they all have a remarkable "third eye" between their two compound eyes. See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot. They are a challenge to photograph, but quite satisfying when you get it right. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2018)

It was forecast to be cooler today, and it was - about 98∞ on the upstairs porch, which is about 10∞ cooler than yesterday. The humidity was higher which may have made it feel about the same! It sounds like we'll continue hot for a few days, losing a few degrees every day this week after tomorrow. Our last NWS Heat Advisory will expire tonight. I'm looking forward to cooler days and nights.
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium vulgare; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium vulgare; Asteraceae

File.Name: i708b_i708-032.jpg
Notes: 29417973928 Here are two shots of a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) in my [Next] photo - showing the remarkable "third eye" in the space between its compound eyes and above its antennae. What a odd little structure it is - almost spooky. I suspect these are actually two different wasps, since the one on the right also has black and red mark on its screen-right eye that the other one seems to lack. The wasp in my [Next] photo is the one on the right that has the mark. This time, the wasp is visiting a non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare, Asteraceae) that has just started to flower in the canyon. We'll have to remember to remove this weed before it produces seed - but not for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2018)
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium vulgare; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium vulgare; Asteraceae

File.Name: i708c_i708-007.jpg
Notes: 42383709665 Here is another tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a stem of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. Yesterday, I showed this photo of a different cricket nymph on the same plant. This one is greener and larger and it's starting to develop wing buds. They are hard to photograph as they hide "butts up" in the flower clusters and scoot away at the slightest disturbance. I know that we have at least two different kinds of crickets that these may represent - a "thermometer cricket" that sings with a pulsing call that speeds up on warmer nights, and another one that has a steady trill. It's the combination of the two that creates their wonderful song jams all night. See my photos here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2018)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Boraginaceae

File.Name: i708d_i707-010.jpg
Notes: 29417414648 I found this little Brown Lacewing (Hemerobiidae, Neuroptera) on a bristly stem of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Boraginaceae plant family - deceased. I photographed another one a month ago, see this photo. Even dead, this one has interesting eyes that resemble those of their more common cousins, the Green Lacewings (Chrysopidae) - see this photo for an extreme example. They are small and easy not to notice. I don't know much about them, including what happened to this one. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2018)

File.Name: i709a_i709-020.jpg
Notes:
43261840802 I noticed this caterpillar on a stem with flower buds of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. Hah - I was staring right at it when I realized that it wasn't just a plant stem, that's pretty good camouflage! This caterpillar is about two inches long, and I have no idea what it will become. The caterpillar in my [Previous] photo is only half as long and a different color, but it also has a yellow stripe on the side, so I figure there's a chance that they are different instars of the same creature. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2018)

It was only in the 90s today, but it was humid enough to make it feel just as hot as it's been. It was actually quite cloudy all morning which did cut the heat. I'm getting weary of this hot weather, but the forecast is only for the 80s tomorrow and maybe even the 70s for the rest of the week. I'm ready.

File.Name: i709b_i709-026.jpg
Notes: 41501579540 I found this green caterpillar munching a flower bud of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) in the woods today. It's a striking little caterpillar, about an inch long. I have no idea what it will become. The caterpillar in my [Next] photo is twice as long and a different color, but it also has a yellow stripe on the side, so I figure there's a chance that they are different instars of the same creature. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2018)

File.Name: i709c_i709-024.jpg
Notes: 43261289802 Here are new caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on foliage of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some of their work in my photo. It's very odd that the butterflies have been common this year, but this is the only nest I've found . The butterflies are still on the wing, so maybe there are lots more coming? The new caterpillars are social and probably all come from one egg mass, see this photo. They may kill a branch or two of the plants, but they do not do too much damage. The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year! I'm not happy with this photo, and I hope to get a do-over tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i709d_i709-039.jpg
Notes: 42406585385 This is looking into a flower cluster of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. (I first learned this plant as Penstemon cordifolius which explains the names.) The flowers grow on the end of downward-pointing branches, but they point back upwards again, see this photo. Hah, that photo is from April 5 a few years ago - a sign of how late all our flowers are this year. All of the caterpillars in my next few photos are on this kind of plant. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2018)

File.Name: i710a_i710-020.jpg
Notes:
43332412031 Here are new caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly munching a leaf of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. You can see caterpillars working on on both sides of the leaf. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some of their work in my photo. (I'll have another photo tomorrow for Web Wednesdays.) It's very odd that the butterflies have been common this year, but these are still the only caterpillars I've seen. The new caterpillars are social and probably all come from one egg mass, see this photo. They may kill a branch or two of the plants, but they do not usually kill the plants. The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2018)

It was only about 90∞ today, but it was humid enough to make it feel hotter. There were some clouds again this morning which cut the heat. I did go to town today, and I was surprised to see a fog bank lying along the coast. The forecast is for 80s for the rest of the week - still warm, but reasonable for the season.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i710b_i710-047.jpg
Notes: 28463356797 First you notice the wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) in the new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. The black and white colors make it stand out. Then you notice the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) under the bee. Finally you notice the eyes of the spider staring at the belly of the bee! Life and death in the woods . . . (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2018)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Acourtia microcephala; Asteraceae; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Acourtia microcephala; Asteraceae; Thomisidae

File.Name: i710c_i710-040.jpg
Notes: 43332008961 These are new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains - with a little wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working the flower in the lower-left. This is an unusual perennial composite with an interesting common name. It has heads of pretty two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. Many plants with composite flowers that have the appearance of being a single flower, but these Sacapellote flowers look more like groups of individual flowers. It's also interesting that it's starting to flower in mid-July - a month after it normally flowers in early June. I'm not surprised that it's flowering late this year - it seems everything is late because of our late March rains. I will always associate this plant with the start of summer because it normally flowers just when the school where I used to teach starts summer vacation. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2018)

File.Name: i710d_i710-034.jpg
Notes: 43282009862 I believe these are aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. You can see lots of the aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the flowers, including the one completely open flower on the left. Compare this photo of the normal flowers from last June. You can see the umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf at the base of the flowers that these plants have. These abnormal flowers are caused by aphids. The aphids completely change the flowers - the infested flowers look different and stay on the plant much longer than normal. I reckon they qualify as galls, as they are surely "abnormal growths of plant tissues". It's curious that I haven't seen (noticed) these galled flowers for a few years. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2018)
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: i711a_i711-067.jpg
Notes:
42636530294 This is a mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) with some kind of wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a summer flower of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. This is a brutal picture as the assassin lives up to its name. These are large Assassin Bugs in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. You can see its spike-like proboscis. I thought the pattern of black and white on its broad abdomen looks like genus Anthidium, but Bernhard Jacobi says it's an interesting Cuckoo Bee in the genus Triepeolus. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 July 2018)

Today was a nice day with temps only in the mid-80s. It was still humid, and there was one of those annoying breezes from the south that kept everything in motion. If we could just get rid of the breeze and reduce the humidity, it would be very nice indeed.
CommonNames: California Bee Assassin; wild bee; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Assassin Bugs; Cuckoo Bee
ScientificName: Apiomerus californicus; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Anthidium

File.Name: i711b_i711-004.jpg
Notes:
42449207135 "Don't get your feet all muddy when you go puddling today!"

Here is a handsome little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly having a drink from a garden path this morning. That fallen Madrone leaf makes a nice platform! Apparently the butterflies are named for their blue color above and not for any affinity with the ocean. I've noticed these before this year, but this is my first photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2018)

File.Name: i711c_i711-012.jpg
Notes: 42449022745 Here are a pair of new flowers of the Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The flowers are highly variable, and I want to photograph every one I see because they are so different! These fantastic summer flowers attract lots of life - did you notice the little red and black beetle on the flower to the left? These are the first open flowers I've seen this year. I was in this place before the weekend, and there were no flowers. Now there are. I found the first flowers on June 25 last year. They are late this year, but not as late as some other plants. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 July 2018)

File.Name: i711d_i710-017.jpg
Notes: 42448854205 This is a web-shrouded stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. The webbing is made by caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly who eat the outer layer of the leaves inside. The caterpillars are out right now munching fresh leaves, but you can see some frass (caterpillar poop) in the webbing. I show them in my [Previous] photo (here). The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2018)
CommonNames: Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella; Variable Checkerspot

File.Name: i712a_i712-041.jpg
Notes:
28504728357 Here is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). These impressive wasps are maybe 2 inches long - and they seem larger when you actually see them! They are large and a bit ungainly as they clamber over the flowers that are too small for them. This one looks like it has a blue head and a black body, but the black is really a blue that is so deep that it looks black - at least on a cloudy day like this. They can coil their antennae, but they can straighten hem out too. I have another photo of the wasp's hindquarters as my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 July 2018)

It was cool and cloudy this morning as we had some summer monsoon weather. We actually had a 10% chance of rain this morning, but nothing developed. The clouds were a nice change, but there was also a pretty good breeze coming from the south. It still warmed up in the afternoon as the clouds disappeared.
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae
associatedTaxa: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae

File.Name: i712b_i712-034.jpg
Notes: 42469507245 This is the mighty rear end of a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). They are about 2 inches long - and they seem larger when you see them! They are large an a bit ungainly as they clamber over the flowers that are too small for them. They are blue in color, but it's a blue that is so deep it's nearly black - at least on a cloudy day like this. I have a photo of the whole wasp as my [Next] shot (here), but this one is for HBBBT! (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 July 2018)

File.Name: i712c_i712-001.jpg
Notes: 42469141645 Well, I'll be darned . . . this appear to be a different Ambush Bug (Phymatidae) waiting for prey on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. It's small, about 1110 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.3 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It seem spikier than our usual Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica), compare this photo. It's also in a completely different habitat - woodland instead of sunny flowers. It may actually be a nymph, but it still looks different to me. I haven't found a match at BugGuide, so maybe I'm just confused? I know it's not the best photo, but it's the best I could do on a cloudy morning with a breeze. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2018)

File.Name: i712d_i711-050.jpg
Notes: 43373405841 Hah - the elephants trunk is really a fly's butt! Here are two shots of a remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) - probably Geminaria canalis. It has real eyes on its head, buried in the flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. What's most remarkable are the those dark "eye spots" on its back that reflect light just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. I reckon that's camouflage. This fly digs into flowers with its long proboscis, and sticks its butt in the air - and it sure looks like a long-nosed creature from above. I'll post a photo of the whole fly tomorrow for Fly Day Friday, here. I also have an old photo from the same location here. These flies aren't common in our mountains - the last time I found them here was in 2010. I have seen the same flies in the high desert near Lone Pine, see this photo from 2015. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 July 2018)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Jumping Spider
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Salticidae

File.Name: i713a_i713-008.jpg
Notes:
42489528055 Here is a two-fer of Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies sharing a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden. Hah - they were attacking each other a few minutes earlier, but I guess they worked it out. Like the butterfly in my [Previous] photo, these butterflies don't look so pale - they sometimes get a cream color during summer. Did you notice the tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flying by above the butterfly's wing on the very left? These butterflies do like the Butterfly Bush in my previous photo, but they seem to prefer this Salvia. The two plants are growing side by side in the garden, and I'm sure they spent more time here. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2018)

It was sunny and warmer today, up to almost 90∞ again. There was a pretty good breeze coming mostly from the south. I could do without the wind, but there's always something to complain about. If not the wind, then it might be something worse!
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Waverly Sage; wild bee

associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Salvia x 'Waverly'; Lamiaceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera

File.Name: i713b_i713-020.jpg
Notes: 29523044948 Here is a handsome Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. This butterfly doesn't look so pale with this backlight, but they sometimes get a cream color during summer. They are never as bright as Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus), but sometimes they are close, and I get confused. This one looks fresh, but it's already missing a bird-sized chunk from its hind wing. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2018)

File.Name: i713c_i713-021.jpg
Notes: 29522853378 I found this little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a neighbor's house number sign along our road. It seems pretty plain for a Bee Fly. Maybe it's genus Conophorus, though it doesn't see hairy enough. It really seemed to be attached to that metal house number, and kept coming back to it again and again. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2018)

File.Name: i713d_i711-043.jpg
Notes: 28523607207 Here is another shot of the remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that I showed yesterday in this photo - the whole fly and not just its rear end! I believe this fly is Geminaria canalis, see the info page at BugGuide. It has real eyes and a long proboscis on its head, but when it's buried in the flower with its butt sticking up, you see those dark "eye spots" on its back that reflect light just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. That's some camouflage. This fly digs into flowers with its long proboscis, and sticks its butt in the air - and it sure looks like a long-nosed creature from above. I got this photo on a sandstone outcrop a few feet from the monardella flowers in my other photo. These flies aren't common in our mountains - the last time I found them here was in 2010. I have seen the same flies in the high desert near Lone Pine, see this photo from 2015. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 July 2018)

File.Name: i713e_i713-052.jpg
Notes:
29542698878 I believe this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly working one of the last flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. These butterflies are very similar to the Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) in this photo, but they have three little hyaline (translucent) spots near the forewing tips which distinguish them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2018)

It was a little bit warmer today, in the low 90s. The wind was not exactly calm, but not bad either. Nice day!

File.Name: i713f_i713-051.jpg
Notes: 28543398747 Here are two shots of a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. Or is it a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) that uses buckwheat as a host plant? (Yeah, the name is confusing, apparently a reference to the color of lupine flowers.) See this photo for some discussion of these very similar butterflies. I don't really care what it is - it's a handsome butterfly! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2018)

File.Name: i714a_i714-009.jpg
Notes: 29542443608 This is a Spread-wing Skipper (Pyrginae) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden - but I'm not sure what kind of butterfly it is. The ID depends on the fringe of its hindwings. It might be a dark-fringed Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius), or a white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) whose fringe has worn away. I suspect it's the former, but I'm not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2018)

File.Name: i714b_i714-021.jpg
Notes: 43412731711 Here is a nice photo of a Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) on a leaf of Coffeeberry that was flowering. These tiny predators have remarkable armor that reminds me of crabs or traditional Japanese armor. Now look at the other Ambush Bug I found in this photo. That one might be a nymph, but even so - are they the same or different? (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2018)

File.Name: i714c_i714-030.jpg
Notes: 43406550961 We had a close encounter with this fledgling California Spotted Owl aka Southern Spotted Owl (Strix occidentalis occidentalis) on the upstairs porch last night! First it landed the porch railing and then it moved to the roof - and ended up perched on our satellite TV dish! It was almost dark at about 8:45 pm, darker than it looks in my photo. I got my camera on a tripod and set the ISO to 1600 - and got a few photos. The owls have been around for a few weeks, but this is my first photo. We could hear it squeaking in high-pitched tones, and I heard the parents answer from the nearby trees. I guess they are still feeding it. I have another photo of a younger fledgling from 2013, see my photos here and here. That owl is furrier than this one. These owls are quite uncommon, and it's a treat to see one up close like this. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2018)
CommonNames: California Spotted Owl; Southern Spotted Owl

File.Name: i715a_i715-006.jpg
Notes:
41627725510 Here is another photo of a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera). This one is working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. They never actually land, this one is just stabilizing itself with its front feet while hovering. Hah - about half way between those feet and the left edge, there's a little spider huddling out of the way of the monster moth! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2018)

Our days are being much the same lately, temps about 90∞ with some breeze. Can't complain!

File.Name: i715b_i715-003.jpg
Notes: 43387379112 I believe this is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. They have three translucent spots on the tips of their forewings like the Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) in this photo, but they also have a series of larger spots in the middle of the wing. They also seem to have darker antennae. I'm always surprised by how deep the Buddleia flowers are - not many insects other than butterflies can get to the bottom of them! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2018)

File.Name: i715c_i715-017.jpg
Notes: 42718928634 Here is a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) prying open a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. The flowers start in a "closed position" - closed by that flap of skin at the base of the stamens. But once the flower is opened up by a large bee, then other insects can get in. See this photo for both open and closed positions. This wild bee isn't so large, but I guess it's large enough to open the flower - note that it's using all its weight to leverage open the flower in the shot on the left! (That said, it seems to be missing the floral tube on the right and going straight in - a kind of nectar theft.) I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, maybe a Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora. We seem to have lots of these bees right now on all sorts of flowers in the garden. Those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; White Sage; Mining Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Anthophora
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Anthophora

File.Name: i715d_i715-033.jpg
Notes: 29564168678 Here is another little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for prey on on flower of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's remarkable how the predator's armored claw is actually holding one of the flower's stamens. (I imagine him wiggling it - as bait!) The flowers are interesting too. They have five thick outer sepals and five thin petals inside - those little flaps that seem to enclose the stamens. It's interesting that Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) is different with just four sepals and no petals, see this photo. I guess this is why Coffeeberry and Redberry are no longer placed in the same genus. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2018)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i716a_i716-046.jpg
Notes:
43410083972 This is looking close at a tiny "Calochortus Bee" (my name) on the petal of a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. These bees are quite shy, and I took many photos to get this keeper. It was interesting that the bees seem quite attached to particular flowers. They don't leave when I get too close, but they do hide among the stamens! I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera, maybe Perdita californica. I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. (See this photo from last year of a bee waiting for an unopened flower bud to open.) I was looking for these bees today, but I didn't find many. I'll go back to this place in the next day or two and try again. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2018)

It was a few degrees cooler today, but it felt about the same with temps in the mid-80s. There was an annoying breeze again - mostly from the south and west.

File.Name: i716c_i716-011.jpg
Notes: 28571603697 Here is another new flower of the Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The flowers are highly variable, and I want to photograph every one I see because they are so different! I picked a couple of my photos pretty much at random today to post - there's no way to decide! They've only been flowering for a week now (since July 11) up here along East Camino Cielo, but they are already showing signs of finishing for the summer. On the other hand, different populations on West Camino Cielo and elsewhere still haven't started flowering, so they will be around for a while longer - in one place or another. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2018)

File.Name: i717a_i717-055.jpg
Notes:
42574038845 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a new flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's not the best photo, but it's interesting. The flowers have very long stamens and styles that extend way beyond the reach of the petals, so what pollinator are they adapted for? I show one possibility in this photo, and I guess my photo here shows another possibility. The flower parts seem to be a perfect fit for the butterfly's wings to transport pollen. I also saw a male butterfly working the same flowers. They are definitely interested. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2018)

Today was another pleasant day with temps in the high-80s. There was an annoying breeze again - mostly from the south and west. I guess these are eddy winds from marine winds wrapping around Point Conception - see Ventusky for a visualization. I'm coming to appreciate how important these eddy winds are for our weather.

File.Name: i717b_i717-057.jpg
Notes: 43478614021 Hah - I was getting a photo of this galled leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family when this tiny fly flew into my photo! You can see it right in the center of my photo above the hole in the leaf. I'm pretty sure it's a tiny Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) - maybe genus Geron, though it hardly matters. I believe the galled leaf is the work of a Midrib Gall Moth (Sorhagenia nimbosa) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the Lepidoptera. This one has an opening, but I'm not sure if the moth came out or a predator went in. I've only found a few of these galls this year, always on shrubs that have buds or are flowering or just finished. It's a real gall, not just a leaf-roller - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2018)

File.Name: i717c_i717-039.jpg
Notes: 42761854984 This is an adult Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper - those distinctive little stubs are all the wings it will ever get. The colors of these grasshoppers sure is confusing. This one is tan , but others are quite dark - compare this photo. (Hah - and compare this photo at BugGuide by Alice Abelia of a completely black individual!) This tan one is on last year's dead stem of native (but weedy) Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). I haven't been bending down too much as I'm still having some trouble with my knee, so I was happy to find this hopper at waist-level! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2018)

File.Name: i717d_i716-022.jpg
Notes: 43478239931 Here is a lovely Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working flowers of weedy Black Mustard (Brassica nigra, Brassicaceae) growing along East Camino Cielo road. There's some dried out Wild Oats in there too, another common weed. This is the first one of these summer hairstreaks that I've managed to photograph this year, though I've been seeing lots of the different California Hairstreaks in this photo. The gray upper wings of this summer butterfly are surprising and beautiful, see this photo. They seem uncommonly scarce so far this year, but maybe they are just late. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2018)

File.Name: i718a_i718-003.jpg
Notes:
29628017758 Here are two photos of a golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Apidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. I believe this is the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni) - see this photo from last year for a another shot of what I think is the same bee. It was moving quickly between the flowers, hard to photograph. This sage plant is like the Energizer Bunny - it keeps going and going with new flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2018)

There was a breeze from the south today, and none of my photos is quite sharp. The day started sunny, but monsoon clouds soon moved in - cutting the heat a bit, but increasing the humidity. We have a 20% chance of thunderstorms through Friday.

File.Name: i718b_i718-013.jpg
Notes: 28612453457 Here is another wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. But look closer. The flower opens to the right. The bee isn't heavy enough to leverage the flower open, so it's entering the flower by holding it flat and entering from behind. Yep, it's a different kind of "nectar theft" - entering the flower in such a way that pollination doesn't happen. (Not that pollination could happen - these bees aren't large enough to engage the stamens to collect pollen.) I have another photo of a similar bee doing nectar theft in a different way. This bee might be a Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora. We seem to have lots of these bees right now on all sorts of flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; White Sage; Mining Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Anthophora

File.Name: i718c_i717-046.jpg
Notes: 41690608580 This is the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis, Linyphiidae) or maybe the similar Neriene digna. The web is interesting because it comes in layers! As I understand it, the web consists of three main parts. The main part is "the bowl" of non-sticky webbing. The spider hangs below this and grabs its prey from underneath. Hanging below this is "the doily" which seems to be like an oil pan to catch falling prey. Above the bowl is a fine network of random webbing that knocks flying insects into the bowl. I found this web in a shrub of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa, Ericaceae). Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2018)
CommonNames: Bowl and Doily Spider; Eastwood Manzanita
ScientificName: Frontinella communis; Linyphiidae; Neriene digna; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Frontinella communis; Linyphiidae; Neriene digna; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Ericaceae

File.Name: i718d_i713-038.jpg
Notes: 43450818322 Here are more new caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a web-shrouded stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and they eat the leaves as they cover them. The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now mean butterflies next year. It's odd that the butterflies have been common this year, but the new caterpillars are rather scarce. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i719a_i719-005.jpg
Notes:
41710772710 Here is an impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This one might be species Stenopogon californiae. It's close to an inch long and has impressive whiskers! These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing between their bristly front legs, like the dragonflies they resemble. They don't visit flowers, so there's no easy way to find them. I've seen this kind before on the same plant, see this photo. It's not quite Friday by local time, but it is by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2018)

Today was less cloudy than yesterday, but there was still a haze in the air. There was a steady breeze from the south again that made it difficult to get sharp photos. It was warm but not hot, in the mid-80s. There's still some humidity in the air, but no threat of storms.
