File.Name: i720a_i720-017.jpg
Notes:
41730713400 This is a handsome little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched on a dead weedy grass stem along our road, probably Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus, Poaceae). I see these butterflies more than I photograph them - they are fast, and fly close to the ground. They seem to spend most of their time on a perch like this and flying off to chase any other insect - or photographer - that comes by. They are watchful and hard to get close to. That iridescence on its thorax is surprising and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2018)

There was a steady breeze from the south again today that getting photos difficult. I mostly stayed close to the ground. It was warm but not hot, in the low-80s. That will seem like heaven in a few days as hot weather returns.

File.Name: i720b_i720-007.jpg
Notes: 41730553410 This sleek and sexy-looking "true bug" is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). Here it is hiding in a flower cluster of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. This is the adult that comes from the remarkable ant-mimic nymph on the same plant in this photo from last month. It's interesting that they lose their ant-like qualities as they make the transition to adulthood. This insect has a long proboscis which it keep folded under it's head when not in use. It uses it to suck plant juices. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2018)

File.Name: i720c_i720-020.jpg
Notes: 42633577765 Here are two shots of a tiny Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working a new flower of native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in our canyon. It's an interesting fly because I don't think I've seen it anywhere except on these summer flowers. It looks like a small version of one of the larger bee-size flies that I find later in the summer like this one, so maybe it's in the tribe Villini? This fly does have a long proboscis, but you can see just a bit of it entering the flower on the left. The two shots are probably different flies - male and female? The plant is interesting too. Despite the common name, it is no relation to gillyweed in Harry Potter 4! (I've never actually heard anyone use this name.) It's also called Sticky False Gilia. It's a remarkable summer plant that will continue to flower until September or later - on no water at all. Hah - I was trying to get a photo of the flowers on a breezy day when the fly flew right into my photo - and then it happened again! I've already used up my two photo limit for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2018)

43557864001 We had lunch with friends near the Santa Ynez River today, and I found this red dragonfly by a small pond that catches the overflow from our hosts' water tank. I couldn't get very close, so this is a tight crop. I believe this is a Cardinal Meadowhawk (Sympetrum illotum) dragonfly - family Libellulidae, suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata. It's rather small, and perches with its wings held forward. (That's not the wind!) Here it is perched on a stem of a Rush (Juncaceae). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 July 2018)

As the NWS Forecast Discussion had it this morning, "Nothing but hot for the extended period." We're under both an Excessive Heat Warning and a Fire Weather Watch next week.

Notes: 29686009778 Here are handsome new flowers of native Dunn's Lobelia (Lobelia dunnii var. serrata) in the Campanulaceae plant family. It's uncommon in our mountains, but only because it's habitat is so uncommon - perennial creeks that flow in our dry summer. We had lunch with friends near the Santa Ynez River today, and I found this plant by a small pond that catches the overflow from our hosts' water tank. File.Name: i721d_i719-035.jpg
Notes:
42839513084 Here is a new acorn of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) that's being pushed out of its cup by a flow of real oak honey coming from the acorn cups. It's sweet and sticky, I've tasted it. Insects like it. It's not uncommon to hear a buzz of bees from the oaks in the summer, and this is why. It's curious that I find these honey flows on both Scrub Oak and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), see this photo.

I'm not sure what causes this. One possibility (at least on the Coast Live Oak) is that it's the work of the Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but it would be highly unusual for the same gall to effect both oaks. Another possibility is that it's the work of Drippy Nut - a bacterial infection caused by Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina, which I don't know anything about. There's some info on p.72 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. Either way, the honey flow starts, the acorn turns dark, and it's finally pushed out of its cup by the pressure of the honey. This affliction sometimes accounts for half the acorns on a tree or shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2018)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Drippy Nut
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina

File.Name: i722a_i722-032.jpg
Notes:
41771595650 This is a tiny Robber Fly (family Asilidae, Diptera) on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It has an even tinier prey - but what is it? I thought it was an aphid at the time, but that doesn't seem right looking at it closer. Hah - it looks like a marine crustacean, but it does have the beak of a Hemiptera. By counting pixels, I figure the fly is about 6.7 mm long, about 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) By the same reckoning, its prey is less than 1.0 mm long. The fly took off before I could reposition for a perfect shot - but I'm not complaining. It's interesting to compare this fly with the much larger robber fly in this photo - like horses and poodles! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2018)

Today was warmer than expected, in the low-90s. The good news is that there was no annoying breeze this morning like we've had for a few days. Now we're due for "Nothing but hot for the extended period" as the NWS Forecast Discussion had it yesterday morning. We're under both an Excessive Heat Warning and a Fire Weather Watch next week. We'll be in triple-digits again.

File.Name: i722b_i722-003.jpg
Notes: 41771366400 Here are two shots of tiny wild bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) collecting pollen from anthers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The anthers are 2-3 mm long, and the bees about twice that. They look like the same kind of bee to me. These bees are clearly collecting pollen on their hind legs, but they also seemed to have some stashed under their abdomen - unfortunately not visible in these photos. I'm not sure what kind of bees they are. There seem to be lots of these little black bees, so any help with an ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2018)

File.Name: i723a_i723-013.jpg
Notes:
43604523491 This is looking close at a tiny "Calochortus Bee" (my name) on the stamen of a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Did I say "one bee"? It's really a mating pair of bees, though the place where one bee ends and the other begins is hidden behind the anther. I'll post another photo of the mating pair tomorrow for almost-hump day. Look at the very full pollen baskets (or scopae) on the bee's hind legs! The packed pollen looks like wax. I believe this is the female bee, and only she will collect pollen to provision a nest. I believe these bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera, maybe Perdita californica. I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. Maybe that's why they are so intent on "doing it"? (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2018)

I apologize for disappearing so abruptly tonight. Our power went out for some time tonight. I figured it was the wind, but apparently a transformer went bad down in town. The power came back on, but my computer didn't. I tried to stay calm, and it finally came alive after a couple of hours. This has happened before - and I'm still running in "Safe mode with networking". *Sigh* I think it's time for a new computer. Oh yeah, it's been hot - and windy tonight. I've been jumpy enough without computer woes!

Notes: 28716875617 Here is another new flower of the Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family - though this one is flowering along West Camino Cielo rather than East Camino Cielo road like the ones I've been showing. Notes: 43604259941 Here are two shots of a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) snuggling into a new late-season flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. It looks a lot like the photos at BugGuide of a genus Melanoliarus hopper. It's only about 800 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.1 mm, about 1/8 inch. File.Name: i724a_i724-036.jpg
Notes:
43575256542 This is a little drama in the woods involving a flower and three insects. The pretty blue flower belongs to native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The butterfly is a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae), though this one has seen better days. Always investigate when you find butterflies and other insects in odd positions! I suspected a Crab Spider, but the perpetrator is a Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) hidden in the shadows. That's a big prey for a little predator! The wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) is flying in to work the now-vacant flower. I suspect it's a Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora. There's a lot happening in this small scene! (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2018)

It was seriously hot today - I figure just under 100∞ on our upstairs deck, but a neighbor recorded 103.5∞ on his online weather station. Fortunately it's not as windy as it was yesterday - mostly from the south, though now it's coming from the north as sundown approaches. Now we're under a NWS Wind Advisory, an Excessive Heat Warning, and a Red Flag Warning for the next few days.

That's the female bee on the left with the very full pollen baskets (or scopae) - only she will collect pollen to provision a nest. I believe these bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera, maybe Perdita californica. I don't find these bees anywhere else than these flowers, and the flowers don't last very long. Maybe that's why they are so intent on "doing it"? It's not quite Wednesday by local time, but it is by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2018)
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; scopae; Pygoperdita; Perdita; Andrenidae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Perdita californica
associatedTaxa: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; scopae; Pygoperdita; Perdita; Andrenidae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Perdita californica

File.Name: i725a_i725-026.jpg
Notes:
41835390910 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) has captured a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a summer-flowering plant of Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae). The spider might be Peucetia viridans. It's prey might be the same genus Anthophora bees I've been finding at home, see this photo from yesterday. Other bees were working the flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 July 2018)

We did get into triple-digits today, around 100∞ or a bit more on the upstairs porch, without much wind when we could have appreciated it. What breeze there was came mostly from the south in the morning, turning from the north in the afternoon. I suspect it was too hot for most insects today, and I know it was too hot for insect photographers!
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; wild bee; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Hieracium argutum; Asteraceae; Peucetia viridans; Anthophora

File.Name: i725b_i725-001.jpg
Notes: 41835188740 I noticed this little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) lurking under a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. No doubt it's waiting for a bee to come by, like this one yesterday, or maybe something smaller like this one. Yes, the flowers are starting to look a bit wilted. These 100 degree days have something to do with that, but this shrub has also had a long run of flowering - it's due for a rest. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2018)

File.Name: i725c_i725-005.jpg
Notes: 43595617382 I apologize for how gruesome this shot is, but sometimes nature is like that. The spider has captured the fly and is doing what spiders do. The spider might actually be a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae), though I can't really see enough of it to be sure. There's actually another spider nearby, you can see a bit of its legs in the upper-left of my photo. I gave up trying to get all three in focus and just cropped it out for this shot. I found this scene in the cactus patch of the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2018)

File.Name: i725d_i722-047.jpg
Notes: 29772443998 Here is another clutch of web-spinning caterpillars on a stem of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I reckon they are the same caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that I've been showing, e.g. in this photo. But It's curious. I've always found these caterpillars on a "scroph" - the old family of Scrophulariaceae which has now been broken up into a number of other families. This group includes plants like Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae) and Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) that I've shown many times. So it's very curious to find them on a mint relative like this! They seem to be thriving, and they are certainly making webbing - so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2018)
CommonNames: Woolly Blue Curls; Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: i726a_i726-002.jpg
Notes:
41854384030 Here is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil on a garden path at home this morning. The male butterflies get all the glory - see why in this photo - but the ladies are quite beautiful in their own right. The pointed forewings are distinctive. Now to photograph the male! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2018)

It never got out of the 80s all last night - that's hot! Today was a few degrees cooler than yesterday, but still in the upper-90s. Maybe that's because it was quite hazy all day to cut the heat a bit. The haze was unnerving because there was also a faint smell of smoke in the air - or did I just imagine it because it's so much on my mind lately? There's no fires nearby, but could it come from the Cranston Fire down south/east in the San Jacinto Mountains? That's a long ways away, but there was a good breeze from the southeast all morning. There wasn't any mention of smoke on the local Edhat blog where someone usually notices something like that.

File.Name: i726b_i726-006.jpg
Notes: 43615378452 This is a handsome little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched on the spent flower/seedpod of a succulent plant in the garden, maybe genus Kalanchoe, Crassulaceae. This photo looks peaceful, but the butterfly is actually darting forth from its perch to defend its territory from all other passing butterflies. (Does that mean its male?) It was hard to get close, but I tricked it by leaving my camera set up and then retreating. Like a dragonfly, the butterfly came back to the same perch and I moved in to get my photo. It didn't want to open its wings for me, but I like this photo - the colors of summer. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2018)

File.Name: i726c_i725-039.jpg
Notes: 28773615787 Here is another photo of a remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have a face with eyes on its rear end - probably Geminaria canalis. It has real eyes on its head, buried in the flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. What's most interesting are the those dark "eye spots" on its back that shine just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. I reckon that's camouflage! This fly digs into flowers with its long proboscis, and sticks its butt in the air - and it sure looks like a long-nosed creature from above. These flies aren't common in our mountains, at least I rarely notice them. I found another one two weeks ago near this place, see my photos here and here. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 July 2018)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Jumping Spider
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Salticidae

File.Name: i726d_i722-016.jpg
Notes: 41853707830 This is the behind of a handsome wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) digging into a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. Hah - the neighboring flower is digging into the bee too - look how its long stamen is poking the bee in the rear! This bee might be another Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora - I've been showing a lot of them lately, e.g. this photo and this photo, not to mention my [Previous] photo of a bee in less happy circumstances. My photo here shows a beautiful bee butt, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; White Sage; Mining Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Anthophora
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Anthophora

File.Name: i727a_i727-042.jpg
Notes:
43682385251 Here is a grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. I believe this is another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper - you can see its small "angel wings" that are all the wings it will ever get. I don't know if its feeding on the flower or just lounging in a beautiful place. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2018)

Today was much like yesterday - still hot in the mid-90s, and still quite hazy. I didn't smell any smoke this morning, but I still wonder if the haze is actually smoke from far-away wildfires. It did cool into the 70s last night. Our NWS Excessive Heat Warning expires tonight - the last of our watches and warnings - but it will continue hot for a few more days.
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus

File.Name: i727b_i727-038.jpg
Notes: 41873975250 This is the nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia) on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that's finished flowering. This is a later-stage nymph that looks plainer and greener from the early-stage nymphs I've been showing, e.g. in this photo. It will get even plainer and greener when it finally becomes a fully-winged adult like this. Looking at all my photos, I don't seem to find this stage very often - maybe because they are so well camouflaged? This one will become a female - you can see her ovipositor at her rear end. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2018)

File.Name: i727c_i726-019.jpg
Notes: 29812190268 This is some kind of Green Bottle Fly (genus Lucilia?, Calliphoridae, Diptera) tucked in between leaves of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). It was near 100 degrees when I got this photo, and I'll wager the fly was just chilling in a cool place. It didn't budge even when I got close with my tripod, so I reckon this is where insects go when it's "too hot for insects" like it's been lately. I got this photo near a neighbor's house. Sure, it's a trash fly - but a handsome one. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2018)
ScientificName: Lucilia; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Lucilia; Calliphoridae; Diptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: i727d_i725-033.jpg
Notes: 42778840575 Here is another photo of a remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have a face with eyes on its rear end - probably Geminaria canalis. In this photo you can see its real eyes and proboscis on its head, and the dark "eye spots" on its back that shine just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes aren't so obvious. See this photo for the surprising effect! This one is digging into a new flower of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family, the only place I've found them locally. These flies aren't common in our mountains, at least I rarely notice them. I found another one two weeks ago near this place, see my photos here and here. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 July 2018)

File.Name: i728a_i728-001.jpg
Notes:
42798106045 Here is a handsome California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil on a garden path this morning. It looks newly emerged with that bright blue on its forewings. They will lose that blue patch with age, and it's an iridescence that only appears when the light is just so. This photo looks composed, but they actually flick their wings open and closed constantly - timing is everything! As far as I know, these butterflies never feed or visit flowers, but they do take moisture. I guess they emerge with full bellies and fly until they're empty, mostly soaring high in the oak trees. These butterflies have been relatively scarce this year, so I'm happy to see a fresh one like this. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2018)

It was noticeably cooler today, barely up to the 90∞ mark on our upstairs porch. That's a pleasure after a week in the high-90s or higher! I believe we have had smoke in the air from far-away wildfires, and now there is a daily smoke map on the web. I can't find an archive, but John Wiley has the map from a few days ago on his SBitz web group - the first item in the "Fire Season" topic. Thanks John, this is maybe when I was smelling smoke. I'm grateful that the air seems clearer now.

File.Name: i728b_i728-012.jpg
Notes: 41892528290 I believe this is an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the canyon at home. These summer wildflowers are just starting to bloom in the woodland. The butterflies have three translucent spots on the tips of their forewings like the Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) in this photo, but they also have larger spots in the middle of the wing and darker antennae. I believe this is now their second brood of the year. Their first brood last spring was quite sparse, and I never did get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2018)
CommonNames: Umber Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Rural Skipper
ScientificName: Poanes melane; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes agricola
associatedTaxa: Poanes melane; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes agricola

File.Name: i728c_i728-005.jpg
Notes: 42797797265 Here is another tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. This nymph has quite pretty patterns, almost like a katydid nymph. These are the crickets that "sing" on warm summer nights, and I know by listening that we have several different kinds - a "thermometer cricket" that sings with a pulsing call that speeds up on warmer nights, and another one that has a steady trill. They are most active in last summer, though I've just started to hear their wonderful all night song jams. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2018)

File.Name: i728d_i727-049.jpg
Notes: 41892133710 This is a handsome "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera, maybe the Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens) though there are other possibilities. If they are that species, they don't always have that red line across the thorax - in fact, sometimes they look completely brown. This one is on a stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). This is the first of these handsome bugs that I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2018)
CommonNames: Green Stink Bug; Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug; Deerweed
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Thyanta pallidovirens; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Thyanta pallidovirens; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: i729a_i729-064.jpg
Notes:
42822280235 Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly hanging from a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden. Actually, this one doesn't look very pale, but they usually get a cream color during summer. They are never as bright as Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus), but sometimes they are close, and I get confused. This one is missing a tail that allows me to identify it. It's been hanging out in the garden for a couple of days now, and I hope it sticks around. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2018)

Today was much like yesterday - barely up to the 90∞ mark on our upstairs porch, a bit breezy from the south/east. It's still hot - but not debilitating.

File.Name: i729b_i729-006.jpg
Notes: 42821915185 This is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" on a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden. It's robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. Many insects seem to know this trick, and I've seen these large bees do the trick on many different flowers. Hah if you look close at the neighboring flowers, you can see where other bees have done the same thing! I also believe this is a male bee with that white mane that's worn but still visible - see this photo for my evidence. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2018)

File.Name: i729c_i729-029.jpg
Notes: 43009466404 This is another wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) getting nectar from a new flower of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Maybe it's the same kind of bee I've been showing lately, e.g. in this photo, that I've been tentatively calling genus Anthophora. But it looks subtly different from those bees - smaller and longer antennae for starters. Another possibility is a "Long-horn Bee" in the genus Eucera in the same Apidae family. Whatever it is, these bees are aggressively territorial, trying to drive away all other insects from "their" flowers - hah, they bump them off! It's a handsome bee. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Anthophora; Eucera; Apidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Anthophora; Eucera; Apidae

File.Name: i729d_i729-042.jpg
Notes: 43725370221 Here are two shots of a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) collecting pollen from flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The bee already has a pretty good load of pollen, but there's always room for more! I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, but maybe it is genus Lasioglossum (Dialictus) - a small metallic sweat bee in the family Halictidae? It really is tiny - compare my [Next] photo of a different wild bee on the same flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2018)
CommonNames: wild bee; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; sweat bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Lasioglossum; Dialictus; Halictidae

File.Name: i730a_i730-014.jpg
Notes:
29882050358 Here are three shots zooming in on an uncommon native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family - in full flower. This is the first flowering plant I've seen this year. It was hiding in plain sight nearby, but in a different place than I usually find them. Good eyes, Julie! This one is growing in the woodland next to a Madrone sapling that's visible on the left. The basal leaves completely shrivel up by the time they flower, leaving a bare asparagus-like stalk a foot or three tall. See this photo for the early basal leaves. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. Believe me - if you drive over San Marcos Pass on Highway 154 at the end of July, the last thing you would think of is orchids and lilies in the summer-dry woods... but here are the orchids, and the lilies are out there! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2018)

Today was cooler than yesterday with monsoon clouds to cut the heat a bit, only in the high-80s. It was also quite hazy and breezy from the south/east again. Not bad.

File.Name: i730b_i730-037.jpg
Notes: 43036300824 This is another wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) inside a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The bee is suckling at a gland on the petal - see this photo for a close shot. I'm not sure what kind of bee this is, but look at those shaggy pollen baskets on her legs that remind me of the Woolly Chaps that some cowboys used to wear. That's a quality I associate with the genus Melissodes - but I'm in over my head with bee IDs! It's a handsome wild bee - 'nuff said. Hah - there's another tiny bee on the stamen to the right - out of focus but visible. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 July 2018)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Melissodes
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Melissodes

File.Name: i730c_i730-032.jpg
Notes: 42847819595 Here is a tiny Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a new summer flower of Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae). I believe this fly is genus Geron, there's more info at BugGuide. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane! Julie calls it a "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 July 2018)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Hieracium argutum; Asteraceae; Geron
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Hieracium argutum; Asteraceae; Geron

File.Name: i730d_i730-031.jpg
Notes: 41943415860 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) seems to have a untidy nest in a summer-flowering plant of Yellow-flowered Hawkweed aka Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum, Asteraceae). This is probably the same spider I showed with prey a few days in this photo. I'm just starting to find the mature spiders. I usually find them guarding their egg sacs in October when Arachtoberis going on. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 July 2018)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed; Southern Hawkweed

File.Name: i731a_i731-020.jpg
Notes:
43057760374 Here is another lovely female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon. The male butterflies get all the glory - see why in this photo - but the ladies are quite beautiful in their own right. The pointed forewings are distinctive. I've been seeing the males on the same flowers, but they don't want to pose for me . . . yet! (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2018)

It was a fairly cool morning, but then it turned hot in the afternoon, up to about 96∞ on the upstairs porch. We didn't have any clouds or haze today, that might have something to do with it. The breeze is manageable. I don't know if the bump in temperature is a sign of things to come or a flash from the past.
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: i731b_i731-001.jpg
Notes: 43773971351 This is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) showing the right way to enter a flower of White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). The bee is using its mass to fully open the flower, and going in through the proper throat of the flower so that pollination can occur. Smaller bees aren't heavy enough to leverage the flowers open, so they have to find other ways - compare this photo where the bee is bending the flower over backwards. This White Sage shrub is running low on new flowers, but it keeps putting out a few at a time. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2018)

File.Name: i731c_i731-003.jpg
Notes: 41964845490 Here are two shots of tiny "parasitic wasps" (""Parasitica", Hymenoptera) on seed husks of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. These wasps are really tiny, about 520 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots without the ovipositor, which comes to 2.0 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) The wasps sure look like genus Torymus in the group of Chalcid Wasps (Chalcidoidea), see this photo. But BugGuide says that "...90% of Torymus with known hosts are ectoparasitic upon gall-forming insects of the families Cecidomyiidae and Cynipidae" - and this is not a gall. I have found similar wasps on galls, see this photo. I've found similar wasps on seed husks before, see this photo of a wasp on a different shrub. These wasps typically use that long "stinger" - really an ovipositor - to lay eggs on the gall wasp larva inside. I'm not sure what the wasps in this photo are after, maybe critters like weevils living in the developing seeds? (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2018)
ScientificName: Parasitica; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Torymus; Chalcidoidea
associatedTaxa: Parasitica; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Torymus; Chalcidoidea

File.Name: i731d_i729-050.jpg
Notes:
43725237662 Even armored predators need love!

Here are a mating pair of tiny Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) on a leaf of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This particular shrub still has a few flowers, and lots of these predators - I have another photo of one from the same shrub. They remind me of shogun-era samurai warriors. The color is interesting. I've always assumed they are color-shifters that change to match their flowers, but I saw three different mating pairs today, and they all had a yellow male riding a white female. Is there something else going on? It's not quite Wednesday by local time, but it is by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, so Happy Hump Day! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2018)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: i801a_i801-005.jpg
Notes:
43793878941 This is another shot of a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) working into a new late-season flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. These summer-flowering plants are just starting to flower, and they seem to be magnets for these wasps - see this photo for another shot a week ago. The goggle-eyed wasps are so fast that they are a challenge to photograph - but it's great fun to try. See this photo to appreciate how long its proboscis is! (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2018)

When the stats come in, July 2018 is going to go down as a hot one. It wasn't quite as hot today as yesterday, but it was still in the low 90s - hot enough! There was a nagging breeze from the south today that kept everything in motion and made getting photos difficult. I reckon today - August 1 - is our real midsummer day - Lammas or Lughnasadh, the cross-quarter day midway between the summer solstice and the autumnal equinox. Damn, six more weeks of summer heat??
CommonNames: Bembecine Sand Wasp; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae

File.Name: i801b_i731-038.jpg
Notes: 43077431554 Here are two shots of a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly perched for a moment on a dead stem in the woods. The blue upper wings show that this one is male. These butterflies are getting scarce now that their main food and host plant as stopped flowering - that's native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. I was actually surprised to find this one looking so fresh. It must be tough for all insects when their food plants stops flowering - time to adapt or die. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2018)

File.Name: i801c_i731-012.jpg
Notes: 43793436131 Here is a tiny orb web on a cluster of what I believe are aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. See this photo for more info about the flowers and why I think they're galled. I assume this web belongs to a Orb Web Weaver in the family Araneidae). The web is tiny, only an inch across. It appears to consist of the "inner web" of a spider - and nothing else. The debris stuck in the web resembles ash from a wildfire, see this photo from last December when he Thomas Fire was getting too close to home. But it's not ash - these are the molted skins ("exuviae") of the aphids that cause the abnormal flowers! Pretty interesting find, and grounds for a Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2018)

File.Name: i801d_i729-020.jpg
Notes: 28857162347 This is a small spider on its sheet-like webbing. The webbing actually comes in two layers, like a sandwich with the spider inside. It has a retreat in that dead leaf at the bottom where it disappears at the slightest bump. It's hard to see the similarity in the photos, but I believe this is the same spider I showed in this photo a month ago. At that time, I thought it it looked like a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider with a slightly different web, compare this photo at BugGuide, but now I'm not so sure. This is a nice web, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2018)

File.Name: i802a_i802-008.jpg
Notes:
43814454581 I finally got a photo of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a new summer flower of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) today - my first for the year. It's a bit beat up, but you can sense that it has *something* hidden between its wings - see this photo. These fine butterflies always close their wings at rest, so it's a challenge to capture their upper wings. This is our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why. I'll keep trying for a better photo of the upper wings, but this is a start. (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2018)

It was a little cooler today, but also a little breezier. The weather is much the same from day to day. Can't complain!
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: i802b_i802-017.jpg
Notes: 29944330608 Here is a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) ovipositing on a ripening berry of native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. What odd divisions the wasp has in its body! If you know the plant, then the berry can be your ruler. The wasp is about 900 pixels across in this in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.5 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) That's about all I know for sure about this photo. The wasp looks like the illustration of a Gall Wasp (family Cynipidae) on page 67 of Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), though the berry isn't a gall. Another (maybe more likely) possibility is a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea) - see this photo at BugGuide of a genus Eurytoma wasp. Either way, it's still considered a "Parasitic Wasp" - though I can't see it hitting any tiny critter in the berry with that short ovipositor, compare this photo. I'm surprised that I got such a decent photo on a breezy day when the branch was rocking back and forth! (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2018)
CommonNames: wasp; California Coffeeberry; Gall Wasp; Chalcid Wasp

File.Name: i802c_i802-004.jpg
Notes: 29944125288 This is another wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) perched on new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure this is the same kind of bee I showed in this photo, maybe genus Anthophora or Eucera. It might even be the same bee. Hah - this bee seems to spend it's time flying around it's small domain of three flowering plants trying to bump off any other insects that come by! Note that it's not feeding in this photo, just giving me the eye - I'm too big to bump! I'm pretty sure this one is a territorial male since it has no "pollen baskets" on its hind legs. (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2018)

I believe it's another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper, though its small "angel wings" are obscured by its leg. I have another shot of the same grasshopper in the same flower - see this photo. File.Name: i803a_i803-006.jpg
Notes:
28897051137 This is a Swallowtail (genus Papilio) butterfly hanging from a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. But what kind of swallowtail is it? It seems yellow enough to be a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus), but I still suspect that its a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) that has developed more than the usual summer color. Is there a way to distinguish these butterflies apart from their color? Hah - if you look close, you can see that the butterfly is doing "nectar theft" just like the Carpenter Bee in this photo - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. I spent some time watching this butterfly this morning (and getting photos), and I'm sure it was doing nectar theft on most of the flowers. Not that it matters with this hybrid plant - it probably doesn't produce viable seed anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2018)

It was a few degrees warmer than its been today, and tomorrow will probably be a few degrees warmer again. It sounds like we're heading for another heat wave next week. Unfortunately, there are sundowner winds in that forecast too.

File.Name: i803b_i803-027.jpg
Notes: 42929629255 Here is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. This isn't the first one I've photographed this year, but it is only the the second one. (The first one is here.) I've been seeing them, but they don't like to pose. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2018)

File.Name: i803c_i803-016.jpg
Notes: 42024644490 Here is a handsome little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil on a garden path at home this morning. These butterflies seem to spend their time darting forth from a perch to defend its territory from all other passing butterflies. I guess they take a time out for a drink on a hot day. I don't blame them! See this photo for a look under their wings. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2018)

43852205491 "Now you see it, now you don't."

Here are two more photos of a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) perched on flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) taken in quick succession. The bee's proboscis seems to magically disappear! I believe the bee folds it under its head. You can see just a hint of it in this photo. Today was much like yesterday, highs in the low 90s and a breeze from the south. I still expect it to warm up tomorrow as we head for another heat wave next week peaking on Monday and Tuesday. There are sundowner winds in the forecast too, but it's not bad tonight - yet. We're under a NWS Fire Weather Watch starting tomorrow evening and an Excessive Heat Watch starting on the following morning.

File.Name: i805a_i805-042.jpg
Notes:
28938132247 At first, I wasn't going to post this photo at all, but it does show the upper wing of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly in flight - so why not. It looks like it's flying into this non-native Lantana flower in the Verbenaceae plant family in the garden, but as I recall, it's actually backing away from the flower after visiting it. One of the reasons these butterflies are so hard to photograph is that they take off from flowers so fast that my reflexes and the camera delay just aren't up to it. This isn't the dogface photo I want, but it will do until I get something better. This is surely the holy grail of local butterfly photographers! If you search my photos, you can see more of my attempts to get this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2018)

It was downright hot today, just under the century mark of 100∞ on the upstairs porch. The winds haven't been too bad this evening, but they are a concern in this heat wave that's just starting. It sounds like it will be a hot week - with the peak on Tuesday. Now we're under a NWS Wind Advisory, a Red Flag Warning, and an Excessive Heat Warning for the next few days. Warnings are worse than watches!

File.Name: i805b_i805-047.jpg
Notes: 42065090980 Here is a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a composite Zinnia flower in the garden today. This is a male, and the real challenge is to photograph the upper side of its wings to see the hidden "dog face" pattern that's only visible when they fly. My [Next] photo is my best shot so far this year. Even with its wings together, it's slightly different from the female butterfly in this photo - the male has stronger colors and markings than the female, like that striking figure-8 on its hindwing. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2018)

Notes: 43156183674 This is a slender little Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). These slender bugs are small but alert - almost always when I go to get a photo they immediately amble off, usually disappearing under the leaf they are on. Once I notice one, I usually notice more. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2018)

42969350525 Waiting for dinner service?

This is a tiny Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae) waiting for a meal at a flower of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae) in the woods today. Of course it won't get service - meals here are strictly self-serve, which it is entirely capable of. The flowers are nearly done, but a few are hanging on. They have flat stamen filaments that remind me of those wide matches that some restaurants used to give out when I was a kid. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2018)

Notes:
42991599715 This is a white-fringed Duskywing butterfly working summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I was trying to get a different shot, when this butterfly suddenly appeared too close to my camera - I got a photo, but barely! I'm not sure, but this one might be the Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly that is more black than brown, compare this photo of the similar Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis). This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2018)

It was really too hot for insects or insect photographers today - about 103∞ on the upstairs porch by my old-school thermometer, though a neighbor recorded 107.4∞ and the Los Prietos weather station recorded 110∞. The good news is that the wind hasn't been too bad - at least not yet. It sounds like Tuesday will be the hottest day, but it will be hot all week.

File.Name: i806b_i806-036.jpg
Notes: 42991395835 These are brilliant red berries of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. This shrub is scattered in the chaparral and woodland, nowhere really common. The berries are juicy when ripe. They taste quite bitter to me, but maybe I haven't collected them when they are dead ripe? See this article for a different view and caveat emptor! It's interesting that most of the plants have no berries in this drought year - but this shrub is covered. The berries have an odd texture - my first thought is a juicy sci-fi alien with translucent skin! (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2018)

File.Name: i806c_i806-025.jpg
Notes: 42991076035 Here are two shots of acorn of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) that are exuding a flow of real oak honey from the acorn cups. It's sweet and sticky, I've tasted it. Insects like it. It's not uncommon to hear a buzz of bees from the oaks in the summer, and this is why. It's curious that I find these honey flows on both Scrub Oak and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), see this photo. I'm not sure what causes this, but I suspect it is not a gall because it would be highly unusual for the same gall to effect both oaks. I now believe this is the work of Drippy Nut - a bacterial infection caused by Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina, which I know nothing about. There's some info on p.72 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. Another source of information is the article Living Among the Oaks Creates a Sticky Situation from the UC Oak Woodland Management website. Once the honey flow starts, the aborted acorn turns dark, and it's finally pushed out of its cup by the pressure of the honey within. This affliction sometimes accounts for half the acorns on a tree or shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2018)

File.Name: i806d_i805-005.jpg
Notes: 43895319471 Here is another photo of what I believe are aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. You can see lots of the aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the flowers. Compare this photo of the normal flowers from last June. These abnormal flowers are caused by aphids, and the aphids completely change the flowers - the infested flowers look different and stay on the plant much longer than normal. I reckon they qualify as galls, as they are surely "abnormal growths of plant tissues". I have another photo of these galled flowers here from a month ago. They are still here looking about the same. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2018)
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: i807a_i807-006.jpg
Notes:
43917115371 Here are three shots of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly flying into a non-native Haworthia flower of some kind in the garden. Hah, I couldn't get one great shot so maybe three shots will make up for it . . . Nah, but I'll keep trying to get something better. This is surely the holy grail of local butterfly photographers! If you search my photos, you can see more of my attempts to get this photo. This is our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why - until they see it with wings open! The glory kind of disappears when it closes its wings, though it is still a handsome butterfly on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2018)

Maybe it was a few degrees cooler today, but it still got up into triple digits - too hot for insects or insect photographers. It was quite hazy today, which I believe was smoke from the huge wildfires in Northern California hundreds of miles away. You can see our smoke on the daily smoke map on the web, but I can't find an archive so look fast! Our NWS warnings and watches end tonight, and tomorrow will hopefully be a few degrees cooler.

File.Name: i807b_i807-001.jpg
Notes: 43916551651 This is a Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) with its face buried in a non-native Haworthia flower of some kind in the garden. You can see the wasp's "third eye" between its goggle-like real eyes - see this photo for a closer shot! These remarkable wasps seem to show up wherever there are summer flowers - in the garden as well as in nature. They are not uncommon, but they are so fast and alert that it's hard to get decent photos of them. See this photo to appreciate how deep into the flower its proboscis goes! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2018)

File.Name: i807c_i807-013.jpg
Notes: 28979581507 This is a mating pair of "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a brand new flower of native Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. Like it's cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), this shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants - this one is male. There are a number of these red and black bugs which I always get confused, but I believe this one is a Redcoat Seed Bug (Melanopleurus belfragei, Lygaeidae). I have a list of other "red bugs" in my description for this photo. It's not quite Wednesday by local time, but it is by Flickr/GMT/UTC time, so Happy Hump Day! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 August 2018)

File.Name: i808a_i808-005.jpg
Notes:
43031799335 This is another attempt to photograph a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly with its wings open. Well, it looks pretty good in the thumbnail at least! The plant is a non-native Haworthia in the Asphodelaceae plant family in the garden - probably the Zebra Plant (Haworthia fasciata). It looks like the butterfly is flying towards the flowers on the right, but I believe it is actually backing away from the flowers after visiting them. One of the reasons these butterflies are so darn hard to photograph is that they push off from flowers so fast that my reflexes and the camera delay just aren't up to it. If you search my photos, you can see more of my attempts to get this photo right. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2018)

It was definitely cooler today, down to about 90 degrees. I appreciate that, but it was also quite breezy with chaotic winds 10-20 mph coming from all directions, but mostly from the south. Wind kind of takes the fun out of my kind of photos.

File.Name: i808b_i808-013.jpg
Notes: 43888610592 Here is a lovely little Damselfly (Zygoptera, Odonata) perched a dead twig of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in the woods today. I don't really know much about damselflies, and I can't identify it - but I'm always happy to photograph one. I've been seeing dragonflies in the woods too. As far as I know, the creeks are dry for miles - I wonder where they come from? (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2018)

File.Name: i808c_i806-002.jpg
Notes: 30067673428 Here is another photo of caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a web-shrouded stem of Climbing Penstemon aka Heart-leaved Keckiella (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. You can see a few of the caterpillars along the bottom edge of the web. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and they eat the leaves as they cover them. They spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. Finally they emerge, eat and get fat, and pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now mean butterflies next year. It's actually kind of late in the season to be finding them on this shrub, as most of the caterpillars on other shrubs in the woods have already dropped to the ground. Spiders aren't the only ones, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2018)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Heart-leaved Keckiella

File.Name: i808d_i805-036.jpg
Notes: 43031425705 This looks like the tornado that took Dorothy to Oz! This is looking under the web of a fairly large Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider that's being turned up by the wind. It's probably the same kind of spider that I show in this photo and this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2018)

File.Name: i809a_i809-004.jpg
Notes:
30087105848 Here is a flawless California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil on a garden path this morning. It looks newly emerged with that bright blue on its forewings. They will lose that blue patch with age, and it's an iridescence that only appears when the light is just so. This photo looks static, but they actually flick their wings open and closed constantly - timing is everything! As far as I know, these butterflies never feed or visit flowers, but they do take moisture. I guess they emerge with full bellies and fly until they're empty, mostly soaring high in the oak trees. These butterflies have been relatively scarce this year, so I'm happy to see fresh ones like this. I have another recent photo that looks very similar to his one. Well, why not? (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2018)

It was hotter again today, up to about 95 degrees. It was also quite breezy in the morning with chaotic winds mostly coming from the south. Wind makes my kind of photos difficult, and I mostly shot close to the ground today to avoid the worst of it.

File.Name: i809b_i809-012.jpg
Notes: 43907424592 Here is another shot of the little "digging wasp" in my [Previous] photo. It's excavating a nest in rocky soil that's mixed with gravel - it's brought up all the rocks that you see there. See my [Next] photo for another photo. You can see a bit of the thread-like petiole that attaches its thorax to abdomen, so I'm pretty sure that it's a Thread-waisted Wasp in the family Sphecidae, maybe genus Prionyx. I saw several of these today on the flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) growing a foot away. I also what looks like a larger version of this wasp, but that one has a dark tip to its abdomen - maybe genus Sphex. I hoped to get a photo of this one actually hauling a rock, but it was moving too fast for me. BugGuide says tersely: "Adults feed on flower nectar. Female wasps secure adult grasshoppers (Acrididae) as live, paralyzed food for their offspring. Prey is stored in a burrow, an egg laid upon it, and the burrow sealed." Yipes! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2018)
ScientificName: Sphecidae; Prionyx; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Sphex
associatedTaxa: Sphecidae; Prionyx; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Sphex

File.Name: i809c_i809-013.jpg
Notes: 29017577127 This "digging wasp" has been excavating a nest in rocky soil that's mixed with gravel - it's brought up all the rocks that you see there. See my [Next] photo for another photo. I'm sure that it's a Thread-waisted Wasp in the family Sphecidae, maybe genus Prionyx. I saw several of these today on the flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) nearby. I also what looks like a larger version of this wasp, but that one has a dark tip to its abdomen - maybe genus Sphex. Hah, if you look close you can see that this wasp has black whiskers on its orange butt - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2018)
ScientificName: Sphecidae; Prionyx; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Sphex

File.Name: i809d_i807-026.jpg
Notes: 43050065425 This is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working new staminate flowers of native Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. Like it's cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), this shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants - this one is male. You can see a bit of the butterfly's charcoal-colored upper wings, see this photo. This one is also missing a chunk of its hindwings, including its tails. The butterfly is exposing its handsome orange abdomen - just right for HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 August 2018)
CommonNames: Gray Hairstreak; Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Strymon melinus; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; B. pilularis; dioecious

File.Name: i810a_i810-002.jpg
Notes:
43065518015 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on an almost ready-to-flower spike of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. The spider has a tiny caterpillar for prey - caterpillar-like anyway. This spider is really tiny, about 520 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I like the tiny water drops on the unopened mint flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2018)

It was cooler today, only in the upper 80s. It was also humid, and we had some monsoon clouds which probably cut the heat a bit. It was quite breezy again, with winds coming from the south. A least the winds were gusty, with almost calm periods in between the gusts. That was my chance for new photos.

File.Name: i810b_i810-010.jpg
Notes: 43970795651 Here is a tiny Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on the first new flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe this fly is genus Geron, there's more info at BugGuide. The name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane! I'm not sure of that ID though. Compare the tiny fly in this photo, especially its abdomen. These flies have much in common, but they appear different. Maybe it's just a gender difference? Julie calls these "Kokopelli Flies" - which is perfect. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2018)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae; Geron
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae; Geron

File.Name: i810c_i810-016.jpg
Notes: 43065223425 Here is another one of those remarkable Bee Flies (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have eyes on its rear end - probably Geminaria canalis, see this photo. It's working a flower cluster of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The remarkable faux eyes are obscured by the flower, but it's a good shot of its real eyes and the odd division across them - as though they were adapted to seeing at water level. I have another nice shot of the same fly on the same flowers in my [Previous] photo, here. I've only seen these flies on this native plant around here. These are probably the last of these flowers to appear. It must be tough for the flies when their host plant finishes. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 August 2018)
CommonNames: Bee Flies; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: i810d_i810-013.jpg
Notes: 30101930728 This is a flower cluster of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I got this photo higher in the mountains where I first found this plant, and I suspect these are the last flowers. I didn't notice when I took the photo, but there's a neat little fly on the left - one of those remarkable Bee Flies (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have eyes on its rear end - probably Geminaria canalis, see this photo. By the time I noticed the fly, it got away - but it did come back, see my [Next] photo. I've only seen these flies on this native plant around here. It must be tough for them when their plant finishes. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 August 2018)
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Bee Flies
ScientificName: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis
associatedTaxa: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis

File.Name: i811a_i811-005.jpg
Notes:
43268724914 Here is another flawless California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly perched on a plant in the garden - actually a house plant that's spending the summer outside. It looks newly emerged with that bright blue on its forewings. They will lose that blue patch with age, and it's an iridescence that only appears when the light is just so. This photo looks static, but they actually flick their wings open and closed constantly - timing is everything! As far as I know, these butterflies never feed or visit flowers, but they do take moisture. I guess they emerge with full bellies and fly until they're empty, mostly soaring high in the oak trees. I haven't seen one of their fantastic caterpillars yet this summer, but they're out there - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2018)

It was noticeable cooler today, only in the lower 80s. Unfortunately, there was a steady breeze from the south that kept everything in motion. Maybe that breeze from the south/ocean side of the mountains kept everything cooler today. I hope it lasts for a while.

File.Name: i811b_i811-027.jpg
Notes: 43081469745 There are still fresh flowers of the fantastic native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in our mountains - at least in our canyon near home. They're near the end of their season. There's a tiny "Calochortus Bee" (my name) on the stamen in the upper-right, see this photo for info and a better view. This flower appears to be growing from a garland of Coffeeberry leaves, but that's just how the plant came up through the shrub. I haven't been posting all my photos, but I have been shooting just about every flower I see - they are all so different from one another. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2018)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Calochortus Bee; Coffeeberry

File.Name: i811c_i811-036.jpg
Notes: 43268224334 Here is a little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) in a flower cluster of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. At least I think the plant is flowering - sometimes you can see actual white petals in-between the bristly green bracts like this, but other times you have to wonder. I suppose this spider is indirect evidence that the plant is really flowering since flowers attract insects which are spider food! I have another photo of a pollinator bee on such a hidden flower. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2018)
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Cordylanthus rigidus; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: i811d_i809-022.jpg
Notes: 29049014317 I found this little "looper caterpillar" of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It reminds me of the fantastic Emerald moth caterpillars in the genus Synchlora that decorate themselves with flower bits, see this photo and this photo. This one has no decorations, and the caterpillar looks different too - longer for one thing. Even without the decorations, this caterpillar is pretty well camouflaged - look how its colors match the new and old colors of the buckwheat. The only way to spot them is to look for movement. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2018)
CommonNames: Geometrid Moth; California Buckwheat; Emerald moth
ScientificName: Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Synchlora
associatedTaxa: Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Synchlora

File.Name: i812a_i812-048.jpg
Notes:
43956992322 This is another shot of the remarkable fly in my [Previous] photo doing what it does best - sticking its head and long proboscis deep into a flower and sticking its butt up in the air with its faux eyes looking for all the world like the real thing! The flower is native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). I believe the fly is Geminaria canalis, an atypical Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) - see my [Previous] photo for some more info. I watched this fly for some time today as I was getting my photos, and it would sit in this position for whole minutes at a time without moving - trying to trick me who was getting too close. Remarkable mimicry. I have some more photos which I'll save for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2018)

It was even cooler today than yesterday, but just as windy from the south again. I gave up trying to get photos in the morning wind at home and went back up into the higher mountains to where this flower cluster of Coyote Mint is hanging on. It was just as windy up there, but the interesting fly was still here working the same flowers that it was in this photo two days ago. This fly seems to live in a small world, but a pretty one - at least until the flowers give out.
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Bee Fly
ScientificName: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Geminaria canalis; Bombyliidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Geminaria canalis; Bombyliidae; Diptera

File.Name: i812b_i812-022.jpg
Notes: 43286558904 Here is another one of those remarkable Bee Flies (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have eyes on its rear end - probably Geminaria canalis, see this photo. This one was working a flower cluster of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) a few feet away, but it would retreat to this sandstone outcrop when I got too close. I spent some time photographing the fly, and it retreated to this rock several times. I finally had a chance to measure the fly in another photo today taken at 1:1 magnification - it's about 1680 pixels long, which comes to 6.6 mm, about 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) Hah - its proboscis is another 1060 pixels long, which comes to 4.2 mm - almost another 3/16 inch! The faux eyes on its rear end really are remarkable. They shine just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes, and even have dark areas on the side to suggest more of the spider's eight eyes. And the fly's colors are pretty good camouflage on this sandstone rock all alone. I almost forgot, that black "thing" above the fly that looks like charcoal is a small crustose lichen. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2018)
CommonNames: Bee Flies; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Jumping Spider
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Salticidae

File.Name: i812c_i812-005.jpg
Notes: 43956254862 I found this small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in the cactus patch of our garden sitting on the edge of its orb web. I believe it's in the genus Araneus - an "angulate orb weaver" with two humps across the front of its abdomen. I find these spiders hard to identify because they are so variable, but maybe this is a juvenile Araneus andrewsi because of its dark colors and bristly legs? This one is still less than 1/2 inch across, but I reckon it will get larger in time for Arachtober! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2018)

File.Name: i812d_i812-063.jpg
Notes: 43098726985 This is an empty seed capsule of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family - catching the light just right from behind. Calochortus beauty indeed, and not just the flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2018)

File.Name: i813a_i813-036.jpg
Notes:
30154516018 I believe this is a somewhat-tattered Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working new flowers in the mint patch of the garden. These butterflies have three translucent spots on the tips of their forewings like the Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) in this photo, but they also have larger spots in the middle of the wing and darker antennae. This is now their second brood of the year. Their first brood last spring was quite sparse, and I never did get a photo. This skipper sure has great control of its proboscis - swish! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2018)

It was another pleasantly cool day, with high temps only about 80 degrees - but it was still windy from the south all morning. I stayed in the garden for today's photos. I'm getting pretty distracted anyway, as we're heading up to Oregon this weekend for a few weeks. I've also got the new newsletter of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to put together before we leave. I'll do what I can for new photos . . . and then we're gone.

File.Name: i813b_i813-007.jpg
Notes: 42213775120 Here is a neat little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) with its face buried in a new flower in the mint patch of the garden. It's interesting that this fly is apparently going for nectar, while the fly in my [Previous] photo is going for pollen. I believe this one is genus Villa. It's another fly that I see every year, for example this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2018)

File.Name: i813c_i813-026.jpg
Notes: 43974012192 This is an interesting little Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on flowers in the mint patch of the garden. It's been lapping pollen from the stamens and is about to go for a new one in a kind of kiss with its proboscis. This fly has Popeye-like enlarged legs! I believe it is genus Syritta in the subfamily Eristalinae, though genus Tropidia looks somewhat similar. I seem to see these little flies every year, see this photo from last year. They fly like little cargo helicopters! It's interesting that the Bee Fly in my [Next] photo seems to be getting nectar instead of pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2018)

File.Name: i813d_i813-002.jpg
Notes: 44022584401 Here are two photos of a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) getting pollen from the new mint flowers in the garden. The bee is tiny, about 1000 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 3.9 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) There are too many tiny black bees! I'm not sure what kind of bee this is, and ID help will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2018)

File.Name: i814a_i814-029.jpg
Notes:
43322391124 This is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sac in a flowering plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). This is the first one that I've seen this year tending eggs. Maybe this is Peucetia longipalpis because its abdomen has white hairs and lacks the usual chevron patterns of Peucetia viridans - see BugGuide and compare this photo. If so, it's interesting because it suggests that P. longipalpis raises its family first, before P. viridans that I usually find in autumn. I have another photo that might also be P. longipalpis taken last July - also early. This is an interesting observation. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2018)

It was another nice day - not too hot, the breeze could be worse. I'm getting increasingly distracted as we're heading up to Oregon this weekend for a few weeks. I've also got the new newsletter of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to put together before we leave. I'll do what I can for new photos . . . and then we're gone.

File.Name: i814b_i814-043.jpg
Notes: 44040780351 This is a goofy-looking net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I'm pretty sure it's in the genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. This is the first one I've noticed this year, though I have found a different planthopper in the same family, see this photo. They're small, less than 1/4 inch long. Julie says they look like cartoon insects. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2018)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae
