File.Name: i814c_i814-005.jpg
Notes: 43992254542 Here is another wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working new mint flowers in the garden. This is probably the same wild bee I've been showing lately on different flowers, maybe genus Anthophora or Eucera or Habropoda, all in the family Apidae. It's a handsome bee with wonderful eyes. There are only a few plants with flowers yet in the garden mint patch, but my half-dozen previous photos show some of the many insects they are attracting. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2018)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Anthophora; Eucera; Habropoda; Apidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Anthophora; Eucera; Habropoda; Apidae

File.Name: i814d_i813-024.jpg
Notes: 30172456198 I believe this photo shows a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea, Hymenoptera) on the mint flowers in the garden. The "double abdomen" of these wasps is distinctive. Maybe it's genus Eumenes? They make neat little mud nests mostly on plant stems. A couple of years ago, one of them made a nest on the antennae of my truck - see this photo. I haven't found any nests yet this year, but the wasps are out there. I have another photo of these wasps that shows a little more of it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2018)

File.Name: i815a_i814-011.jpg
Notes:
43154633555 This is webbing between old flower (now seed) whorls of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae) in the woods. The plant was originally standing up, but I bent it over for my photo to better use my screen space. Look close at the texture of the webbing - it's frizzy and comprised of very fine threads that are spun into strands, different from ordinary spider webbing. I believe this is a cribellate spider web. I've seen a few of these cribellate spiders in the family Uloboridae of Cribellate Orb Weavers aka Hackled Band Orb Weavers, see my photos here and here. But this spider seems to be something else, making columns rather than org webs. There's something similar in this photo. There's more info at Wikipedia and a nice article in The Atlantic. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2018)

Just two photos today, but maybe I'll do better tomorrow. I've been working all day putting together the new newsletter of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society, and I'm pretty fried. The good news is that I'm almost done with the rough stuff. I'll do some fine-tuning tomorrow and send it out for comments. Then off to the printer on Friday, and we're off to Oregon on Saturday! At least that's the plan . . . you know, frass happens!
CommonNames: Wood Mint; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Hackled Band Orb Weavers

File.Name: i815b_i809-019.jpg
Notes: 44012343342 Here is an acorn cup full of webbing. I guess it's a spider nest, but I really don't know. The acorns belong to a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. There are webs everywhere, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2018)

File.Name: i816a_i814-032.jpg
Notes:
30213371028 Here is another shot of a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sac in a flowering plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) - see this photo from a couple of days ago. In my comment to that photo, I explain why I believe this spider is Peucetia longipalpis rather than P. viridans. Sure, this is a "butt shot" that's perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday (BBBT) group, but they have a limit of two photos per week, and I've already posted them - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2018)

My newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society is done, and I've sent it out to a few board members for proofreading. I'll get it off to the printers tomorrow, and then we're off to Oregon on Saturday! At least that's the plan . . . you know, frass happens!

File.Name: i816b_i814-007.jpg
Notes: 43174990375 This is another wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working new mint flowers in the garden. This is probably the same kind of wild bee I've been showing lately on different flowers, maybe genus Anthophora or Eucera or Habropoda, all in the family Apidae. It's a handsome bee with wonderful eyes - and a impressive rear end. Sure, this is a "butt shot" that's perfect for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday (BBBT) group, but they have a limit of two photos per week, and I've already posted them - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2018)

File.Name: i816c_i813-011.jpg
Notes: 44032855632 Here is another shot of a neat little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) in the mint patch of the garden - showing its rear end. Compare this photo taken on the same day. I believe this fly is genus Villa. It's funny that some insects always turn and face the camera when I get close, but these flies usually face away. The result is that I have lots of good butt shots, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2018)

File.Name: i816d_i812-054.jpg
Notes: 43362533964 Here are two more shots of that remarkable Bee Flies (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have eyes on its rear end - probably Geminaria canalis, see this photo and this photo for the whole fly and see this photo for another "butt shot". This one is working still-new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae), and this is just how it looks when its head is buried in a flower. The faux eyes on its rear end shine just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes, though it looks more like a furry mouse to me. That's mimicry! These photos are pretty much right from my camera, though I did rotate the left one another 15 degrees clockwise for symmetry. The focus is what it is - there's never enough DOF. It's a remarkable rear end - so HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2018)

File.Name: i817a_i816-002.jpg
Notes:
42292399130 This is a small (but not tiny) wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. This shrub has basically been done flowering for a few weeks, but it keeps putting out occasional new flowers - and the bees always find them right away. I believe this bee is in the family Megachilidae that collects pollen on the underside of its abdomen, but I won't guess beyond that. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2018)

My newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society is done and delivered to the printers. We're heading off to Oregon tomorrow/Saturday, and I'll be pretty much off the grid for a few weeks. Sure I'll have some new photos to share when we get back. Now to do some packing . . .

File.Name: i817b_i816-007.jpg
Notes: 44052713042 Here is a small wasp (Hymenoptera) that I can't identify on new flower buds in the mint patch of the garden. In other photos, it's working the open flowers. I thought at first that it was another Potter Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea) like the one in this photo. It's about the same small size, but it lacks the distinctive "double abdomen" so I don't know. Any help with an ID will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2018)

File.Name: i817c_i814-008.jpg
Notes: 44052572582 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a dead stem of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) down by the creek. I'm pretty sure that it's a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. Its reddish eyes and the barely-visible red tip on its tail seem to suggest this family. The plant was actually standing vertically, and I rotated the image to make better use of my screen space. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2018)

File.Name: i817d_i812-037.jpg
Notes: 44052397662 Here is another shot of that remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) that seems to have eyes on its rear end - probably Geminaria canalis, see this photo for the whole fly. It's faux eyes are obscured in this photo, but the fly's face is also remarkable with those divided eyes and that very long proboscis. This one is working summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae). This plant is the only place where I've noticed these flies. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2018)

File.Name: i912a_i907-153.jpg
Notes:
42837416260 Here are a mating pair of pollen-dusted beetles on a flower of non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare, Asteraceae) in a clearing in the Oregon woods. I believe they are Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera. The abdomen of these beetles is slightly longer than the wing covers like many in this group. Yeah, it's a non-native plant, but the non-natives were where I found most of my insects on this Oregon trip. Happy Hump Day! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 August 2018)

I'd rather show butterflies on my first day back from our Oregon trip, but today is Hump Day and Web Wednesday and there will be time for butterflies in days ahead. We got home on Monday, delayed a day by the wildfires in Northern California. I got home to a dead computer - it wouldn't power on at all. I took it to the shop yesterday and got it back on the same day with a new power supply and video card. I'm not running in safe mode any more! I came back from Oregon with 1,000+ photos. I still haven't gone through them all. It's good to be home.

File.Name: i912b_i907-361.jpg
Notes: 42836943390 This is looking close at the spider that makes the odd triangular web in my [Previous] photo. The spider is probably species Hyptiotes gertschi in the family Uloboridae of the Cribellate Orb Weavers. Here it is sitting close to its anchor on a leaf of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the Ericaceae plant family in the Oregon woods, with the web extending off to the apex of the triangular web off to the right. The most peculiar thing about these spiders is that they break the web with their bodies - look close and you can see that the spider is holding its web on both ends and not in between! I'm not sure how they move, but they certainly do. You can also see the spider's cribellum near its rear end, see this photo for some discussion. There were quite a few of these spiders in the shrubs nearby. Maybe they are common everywhere, but they are small and inconspicuous and hard to spot. These tiny spiders have become one of my favorites. I've also found them at home but only in winter, see this photo taken this year on New Year's Day. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 September 2018)
ScientificName: Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloboridae; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; cribellum
associatedTaxa: Hyptiotes gertschi; Uloboridae; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; cribellum

File.Name: i912c_i907-341.jpg
Notes: 43927574874 Here is the odd triangular web of a Triangle Web Spider (genus Hyptiotes) in the family Uloboridae of the Cribellate Orb Weavers - probably species Hyptiotes gertschi. They make these odd webs shaped like a pie-slice of a complete web. The spiders hang upside-down on the thread that goes off to the right in this photo. I got his photo in front of our wood shed in the Oregon woods. There were quite a few of these spiders in the shrubs nearby, see my [Next] photo, this photo. Happy Web Webnesday! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 September 2018)
CommonNames: Triangle Web Spider; Cribellate Orb Weavers

File.Name: i912d_i907-273.jpg
Notes: 44596336702 This is the web of a little Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae - either the Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata) or the Sierra Dome Spider (Neriene litigiosa). Some other Linyphiid spiders pull their webs into inverted bowl shapes like this photo. Maybe you can see the spider at the top of the dome - I'll have a better photo of the spider tomorrow. The fallen needles belong to the Douglas Fir trees overhead. Happy Web Webnesday! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 September 2018)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Filmy Dome Spider; Sierra Dome Spider

File.Name: i913a_i913-008.jpg
Notes:
44617270742 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

Here is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working late-season flowers of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. The flowers are usually hidden inside the greenish bracts, but sometimes the white petals stick out. We're approaching the end of our hot and dry summer, and there are still flowers and butterflies in the woods at home. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2018)

We got home on Monday after a three-week stay at our cabin near the North Umqpua River in Oregon. After a slight delay caused by my computer playing dead, it was great to get out into my home woods again with my camera! Something interesting happened while we were gone - it feels like autumn now with the hint of a chill in the morning air. We even got a bit of rain while we were in Oregon. I still have to post many of my Oregon photos, and I also plan to get photos of more of our late-season flowering plants and insects in the days ahead. It's a great time of year!

File.Name: i913b_i907-170.jpg
Notes: 43947868614 This is a Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) balanced on a plant stem in the Oregon woods - named for the leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs. It might well be the Western Conifer Seed Bug (Leptoglossus occidentalis). The Western Leaf-footed Bug (L. clypealis) is similar, though it has a small spine at the tip of its head and more of a white line across its wings. I've also found this one at home, see this photo. It looks a bit like a large assassin bug (Reduviidae), but they are strictly herbivorous and use their sharp beaks to suck plant juices instead of other insects. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 August 2018)
CommonNames: Leaf-footed Bug; Western Conifer Seed Bug; Western Leaf-footed Bug
ScientificName: Coreidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Leptoglossus occidentalis; L. clypealis; Reduviidae
associatedTaxa: Coreidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Leptoglossus occidentalis; L. clypealis; Reduviidae

File.Name: i913c_i907-283.jpg
Notes: 43756895205 I found this little Harvestman (Opiliones) spider in the same shrub of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) where I found the spider in my [Previous] photo near our cabin in the Oregon woods. I believe this one is superfamily Phalangioidea of the suborder Eupnoi - a proper Daddy-long-legs. I'll post another photo of the whole spider in a few days. I say "little" to describe the spider's body, but its long legs go on for several inches making it look larger than it is. These harvestmen are built differently from true spiders with just one body part and only one pair of eyes. This is looking at the spider's rear end - it has a goofy face with fat lips, so HBBBT! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 September 2018)
ScientificName: Opiliones; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; Phalangioidea; Eupnoi
associatedTaxa: Opiliones; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; Phalangioidea; Eupnoi

File.Name: i913d_i907-260.jpg
Notes: 43947357744 I believe this is one of the Sheet Web Weaver spiders in the family Linyphiidae that make the dome webs in the Oregon woods that I showed in this photo - either the Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata) or the Sierra Dome Spider (Neriene litigiosa). This one doesn't seem to have a web yet, though it does have a "safety line" extending from its abdomen. (Maybe it's substituting an arch of foliage for a dome of webbing?) I found this one in a shrub of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) near our cabin where I drank my coffee every morning. I found a lot of spiders here during the three weeks we were there, no doubt because I was in no hurry and looking close for day after day. This is a pretty good butt shot, so HBBBT! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 September 2018)

File.Name: i914a_i914-002.jpg
Notes:
43967262964 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

Here is a late-stage nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on the flower scar at the top of a ripening fruit of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus in the Cactaceae plant family in the garden. They like to hide face-down in these cactus fruit, and I've found them here before - try searching my photos for "Oecanthus cactus". I've been hearing them singing at night, so the adults are out here. With those short immature wings, this one is nearly ready to join the jam! (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2018)

This photo is from home today, and the rest of my photos today are flies from our time near the North Umpqua River in Oregon over the last month. It's been cool here for the last couple of days, but it was warmer today. The same thing happened in Oregon in reverse - warm at first but then cooler. That's the way it is when autumn approaches - it swings . . . back and forth!

File.Name: i914b_i907-088.jpg
Notes: 43776451355 Here is an impressive Bee-like Robber Fly in the genus Laphria of the family Asilidae on a leaf in the Oregon woods. Nice whiskers! It's a large fly, maybe 5/8 inch long, with dragonfly-like eyes and bumblebee-like yellow and black hairs. They are predators that catch their prey in the air like dragonflies - hence the wonderful eyes. Insects were actually pretty scarce in the Oregon woods during our time there, and predators like this fly and spiders are part of the reason why. HFDF! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2018)

File.Name: i914c_i907-287.jpg
Notes: 42875468740 This is "just a fly" watching me from a leaf of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) in my usual morning place in the Oregon woods. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it's handsome with those orange colors. It's a good sharp photo of the fly's face - which I usually seem to miss with such narrow DoF at this magnification. I've already used up my two photo limit for the Fly Day Friday group, but HFDF anyway! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 September 2018)

File.Name: i914d_i907-275.jpg
Notes: 44684930291 This is "just a fly" hanging upside-down under a leaf of native Vine Maple (Acer circinatum, Aceraceae) in the Oregon woods. This fly (or one just like it) would rest under the leaves every afternoon when the leaves start to glow with light from the setting sun - and I finally got the photo. I don't know (or care) what kind of fly it is. It took a couple of weeks to successfully get this photo, so HFDF! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 September 2018)

File.Name: i915a_i915-019.jpg
Notes:
44657050252 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera on a bare stem of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. The little hopper is missing its right-side hopping leg, but that didn't keep it from getting away from me before I could get a closer shot. I guess it's less than 1/2 inch long. I'm not sure how to distinguish nymph and adult as both are wingless, but this one is small enough to be a nymph. They are remarkably variable as you can see if you search all my photos for "Morsea californica". This is the first and only one I've see this year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2018)

It was warm again today, but there was still a chill in the air when I got out of the sun - a sign that autumn is approaching. The Autumnal Equinox happens in a week. We're starting to lose morning sunlight in the garden at home on the north-facing side of the canyon, but it's interesting that we're actually getting more sunlight under the trees on the other side of the canyon - the sun is lower, so it lets sunlight in under the leaves. Window eaves work the same way.

File.Name: i915b_i907-232.jpg
Notes: 42895715950 Here are male and female Fritillary (genus Speyeria) butterflies in the Oregon woods - male on the left, then a female, then both together on the same flower. These butterflies exhibit remarkable sexual dimorphism - meaning that males and females look totally different. I'm not sure, but these might be the Leto Fritillary (Speyeria cybele leto) which is a subspecies of the Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele) butterfly. That one is known from this part of Oregon and is known for its extreme sexual dimorphism - like my photo shows. I believe the flowers belong to non-native Spotted Knapweed (Centaurea maculosa, Asteraceae) growing at about 1500 feet elevation in the Oregon woods. The flowers are rather pretty, but this is a nasty non-native weed that takes over whole meadows in the woodland. At least it's not it's not thorny like the related Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) at home. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2018)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes
CommonNames: Fritillary; Leto Fritillary; Great Spangled Fritillary; Spotted Knapweed; Yellow Star Thistle
ScientificName: Speyeria; Speyeria cybele leto; Speyeria cybele; Centaurea maculosa; Asteraceae; Centaurea solstitialis
associatedTaxa: Speyeria; Speyeria cybele leto; Speyeria cybele; Centaurea maculosa; Asteraceae; Centaurea solstitialis

File.Name: i915c_i907-254.jpg
Notes: 44705116611 I believe this is a Common Wood Nymph (Cercyonis pegala) butterfly perched on a frond of Western Sword Fern (Polystichum munitum, Dryopteridaceae) in the Oregon woods. They have two eyespots under their forewings that are about the same size. (The lower eye is partially hidden, but you can see that it's there.) This distinguishes them from the Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) that we have at home, with the lower eye smaller. These butterflies (both of them) are hard to photograph. They don't visit flowers and they don't pose for photos. I was lucky with this one that kept coming back to its perch. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 September 2018)
CommonNames: Common Wood Nymph; Western Sword Fern; Great Basin Wood-Nymph
ScientificName: Cercyonis pegala; Polystichum munitum; Dryopteridaceae; Cercyonis sthenele
associatedTaxa: Cercyonis pegala; Polystichum munitum; Dryopteridaceae; Cercyonis sthenele

File.Name: i915d_i907-075.jpg
Notes: 29768102417 This is a little Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly dipping its long proboscis into a flower of non-native Perennial Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius, Fabaceae) along our driveway in the Oregon woods. The butterfly has to make a few turns in there - like a plumber's snake! I'm not sure what kind of skipper it is, but it might be a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) like we have at home. The flowers are kind of a weed in our part of Oregon, but at least it's pretty and useful for wildlife and doesn't seem to spread away from the roads. Non-natives like this were where I found most of my insects on this Oregon trip. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2018)
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Perennial Sweet Pea; Woodland Skipper
ScientificName: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: i916a_i916-017.jpg
Notes:
44678277472 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

Here are separate photos of a Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly on the left and a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on the right. Hah - I actually got both photos today! The Admiral is perched on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree by the creek, but it's really interested in the nearby willow that is still in the shade. The Sister is taking moisture from damp soil on a garden path at home. The butterflies are quite similar, but there are differences - the Admiral's orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings, while the Sister has dark border. It's curious that this is the first Lorquin's Admiral that I've seen this year - it seems late for them. They seem to spend most of their time chasing other butterflies - especially the similar California Sisters! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2018)

Today was another nice day - not too hot, though there was an annoying breeze in the morning. I'll keep posting Oregon photos for maybe another week with a "today's photo" as a lead, but I'll soon end up with too many new photos to post! I'm actually happy to have all these photos in reserve as late-summer and autumn are the most difficult times to get new photos every day.

I'm not sure, but this might be the Leto Fritillary (Speyeria cybele leto) which is a subspecies of the Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele) butterfly. That one is known from this part of Oregon and is known for its extreme sexual dimorphism. The flower is Spotted Knapweed (Centaurea maculosa, Asteraceae) again, an all-too-common weed in the Oregon woods. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 August 2018)

File.Name: i916d_i907-204.jpg
Notes: 43818090695 This is another little Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly working a non-native flower of Spotted Knapweed (Centaurea maculosa, Asteraceae) growing at about 1500 feet elevation in the Oregon woods. I believe this one is a Juba Skipper, species Hesperia juba. The greenish color of the butterfly is different, though it is orange on the upper wings. The Common Branded Skipper (Hesperia comma) is similar, but it has a slightly different markings - a comma-shaped mark closest to its body. These flowers are rather pretty, but this is a nasty non-native weed that takes over whole meadows in the woodland. At least it's not it's not thorny like the related Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) at home. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 August 2018)

File.Name: i917a_i917-038.jpg
Notes:
30880706498 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). With its legs extended like this, it is 2600 pixels from toe to toe in this 1:1 macro shot - which comes to 10.0 mm, about 4/10 inch. Its face is less than 1/16 inch across. (See here for how I figured.) I've been looking for these spiders. They seem late this year, but they better hurry up or they'll miss the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2018)

Today's photos all feature spiders, both at home and in the Oregon woods during our trip last month. I've been looking for spiders since we got back last week as a prep for Arachtober which starts in just two weeks! The challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day. It's almost autumn, and this is the time for spiders to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good. I'm staking them out to get ready!

File.Name: i917b_i907-127.jpg
Notes: 44701753842 Here are two shots of a large bottlecap-size "Halloween spider" in the Oregon woods, genus Araneus or Neoscona in the Araneidae family, see this photo and Alicia Jordan's comment. Maybe it's the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides), but I can't see that it has proper "horns" on the shoulders of its abdomen. It's a striking spider - and its partially digested prey on the right is even more striking. I got a closer photo of the prey, but I'm not sure I want to show it - its pretty disturbing. I've seen something similar before, see this photo. I suspect this is a kind of external digestion to liquify their prey before sucking it up. Brrrr! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 August 2018)

File.Name: i917c_i907-037.jpg
Notes: 44031653254 This is another Sheet Web Weaver spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging at the top of its dome-like web in the Oregon woods. I believe that it is either the Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata) or the Sierra Dome Spider (Neriene litigiosa). I have a shot of a different spider's dome web in this photo and another shot of the spider in this photo. These were probably the most common webs and spiders that I saw during our time in Oregon. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2018)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Filmy Dome Spider; Sierra Dome Spider

File.Name: i917d_i907-059.jpg
Notes: 42940474310 This is a different little Harvestman (Opiliones) spider on a frond of Western Sword Fern (Polystichum munitum, Dryopteridaceae) in the Oregon woods. That is, it's different from the one in this photo, though it appears to be the same kind. I believe it is superfamily Phalangioidea of the suborder Eupnoi - a proper Daddy-long-legs. I say "little" to describe the spider's body, but its long legs go on for several inches making it look much larger than it is - long enough there there wouldn't be much actual spider visible in a photo. These harvestmen are built differently from true spiders with just one body part and only one pair of eyes. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 August 2018)

File.Name: i918a_i918-008.jpg
Notes:
44053819094 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

Here is a colony of Large Milkweed Bugs (Oncopeltus fasciatus, Lygaeidae) on a spent seedpod of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). It sure looks like a family group with nymphs and protective adults, but I don't think they do that - do they?? I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley this morning, so I made one of my usual stops along the highway and got this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 September 2018)

We're lucky that this relatively cool weather is continuing. It's still warm in the sun, but there's a chill in the air when I get out of the sun - a sign that autumn is approaching. The word is that there's a weak El NiÒo in the works, but that doesn't mean that it will be a wet winter. There's more info at the new post at the California Weather Blog.
ScientificName: Oncopeltus fasciatus; Lygaeidae; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae

File.Name: i918b_i907-108.jpg
Notes: 44053116524 WTF? I found this odd exuviae - the shed skin of an insect or spider molt - in the Oregon woods. At first, it was in a low-hanging conifer, but I managed to displace it into a shrub of Salal (Gaultheria shallon, Ericaceae). Hah - I'm still not sure what it is! At first, I thought it was a spider skin despite the fact that it only has six legs. It appears to have just one body part. My best guess now is that it's the exuviae of a true bug in the Heteroptera, Hemiptera. If you look at the photo on the right, you can see something extending under its body from the upper-left which I reckon is an antenna with maybe four segments. I don't know what the strings are for, but I've seen them before. There's also a little white thing of its back which I suspect is the egg of a parasitoid Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera). What a nice mystery! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2018)
ScientificName: Gaultheria shallon; Ericaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Tachinidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Gaultheria shallon; Ericaceae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Tachinidae; Diptera

File.Name: i918c_i907-188.jpg
Notes: 30900807488 Here is a lovely gray Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera balanced (for the moment) on a couple of old berries of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae). It's a nice pose, but it didn't last! This true bug has five-segment antennae unlike the four-segment antennae of the true bug in my [Previous] photo, hence the family name Penta-tomidae. This one might be the Consperse Stink Bug (Euschistus conspersus). There's more info and photos at BugGuide. I believe I've found this same species at home, compare this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 August 2018)

File.Name: i918d_i907-168.jpg
Notes: 29835008697 This is another shot of the Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that I showed a few days ago in this photo. It might well be the Western Conifer Seed Bug (Leptoglossus occidentalis). This shot doesn't show the leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs very well that give it its name, but it does show its impressive proboscis that it keeps folded under its head. They are strictly herbivorous and use their sharp beaks to suck plant juices. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 August 2018)

File.Name: i919a_i919-015.jpg
Notes:
44793617031 Here is a nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's just starting to flower. I believe it's a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) - named for the size of it's prey. Even the nymph has a mighty proboscis that it keeps folded under its head. This is the first one I've found since January - at least it's the first one I've photographed since then. It seems to be in a good place for a predator - in a shrub just starting to flower that attracts many insects. If the leaf looks a bit odd because it's upside-down, that's because I rotated my image 90 degrees to make better use of my screen pixels. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2018)

Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are all from home in the Santa Barbara mountains taken during the last week. I've been getting too many nice photos lately! Darn, I've still got lots of good Oregon photos to go. This happens after every trip, and I'll do my best to show both trip photos and home photos.

File.Name: i919b_i919-001.jpg
Notes: 43883156065 Spider webs have charms of heir own, but we must never forget that their purpose is to catch insects - like this! This bee-size fly (Diptera) has gotten caught up in (I believe) the tangled web of a Cobweb Weaver spider in the family Theridiidae. It looks like the fly has been here for some time, and it appears to be untouched. I believe the fly is a non-biting Deer Fly in the family Tabanidae, probably genus Stonemyia - see this photo for a close shot of a living fly. The bright green eyes of the living fly are much prettier that the black eyes of the fly in my photo. I usually find these flies in June, so this one may have been hanging here for some time. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2018)

File.Name: i919c_i913-025.jpg
Notes: 29856621727 Here is a spider's egg sac on an old flower/seed head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). By zooming into the original photo, I can see a few exuviae (shed skins) of the spiderling molts, but they're probably not visible in his reduction. There's also webbing between the seed balls that looks a little fuzzy and might be the work of a cribellate spider, see this photo for another example that also runs between whorls of flowers. The egg sac is webbing too, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2018)

File.Name: i919d_i913-029.jpg
Notes: 44073205804 I believe this is the composite web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). I have another shot of both webs here and a photo of the spider in its retreat here. File.Name: i920a_i920-021.jpg

43904537155 I found this handsome Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly in the mountains today - it's probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). The autumnal equinox happened today while I was posting these photos, at 6:54 pm PDT. Many web sites also say that today is the first day of fall/autumn . . . but maybe not! That time 6:54 PDT is also 1:54 GMT/UTC which happens to be early in the morning on September 23 in England where time starts, and that's why autumn really starts tomorrow on Sunday!

Whenever autumn starts, this seems like a good time to do an inventory of what's still flowering in our summer-dry woods. This photo and my other photos today are a start. I'll show more plants tomorrow.

File.Name: i923a_i923-023.jpg
Notes:
43064219920 Here is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" on an autumn flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. Many insects seem to know this trick, and it's the only way large bees like this could reach the nectar in these flowers that are typically visited by hummingbirds. If you look close at the flower on the left, you can see another slit in the base of the flower where another bee got in first. The bees seem to reuse these holes again and again. Note the beautiful large pollen grains on the flower with the bee. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 August 2018)

The autumnal equinox happened yesterday, but today is the first day of autumn - in our PDT time zone at least, see my comment for this photo yesterday. This seems like a good time to do an inventory of what's still flowering in our summer-dry woods. I started yesterday, and added two more pages today. There are a few more like Toyon, and Hawkweed, and Mexican Pink that are on the way out with just a few flowers left, while most of the plants I've been showing are just getting started. There are also a few weedy species like Horseweed that are believed to be native. And there are more plants down by the river. I'll get back to this project, but 20 species isn't bad for a start!

Notes: 31025652778 I found this interesting fly working new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) today. What a striking face! It seems to have four antennae, which it keeps in constant motion. I believe this one is a Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), maybe Copestylum marginatum or something similar. The "double antennae" are actually ordinary antennae with a thickened arista - that usually hair-like bristle that arises from the base of the antennae of many flies. If I'm right, then this fly is a cousin of the Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) that I show in this photo. Yep, I saved a photo for Fly Day Friday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2018)

File.Name: i924d_i924-035.jpg
Notes: 29962039487 Autumn brought us this early mushroom as well as end-of-season flowers. This is an odd cap of a Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius aka P. arhizus) "mushroom" coming up in the place where we park our cars at home. This one looks like a puffball on a stalk. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" - the fungus gets its scientific name from the pea-shaped peridioles or spore capsules which are somewhat visible in my photo - and more so in this recent photo of a fresher specimen. They very slowly break down to release the spores. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. There are rumors of it being used as a seasoning and a medicine, though I'm not tempted to try it. It's not really fleshy, but it is a fungus at an odd time of year. Our real mushroom season won't begin until real rains start. I'm thinking positive! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2018)
ScientificName: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus
associatedTaxa: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus

I don't know what the caterpillar on the bottom will become. It's on a weedy plant of Prickly Lettuce (Lactuca serriola, Asteraceae) that's pretty much gone to seed. File.Name: i925d_i922-013.jpg
Notes: 44198302774 My [Previous] photo from yesterday shows a damselfly on a large fallen summer gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. These gall wasps also make much smaller spring galls shown in this photo on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These smaller galls are bisexual galls because they will give rise to both male and female wasps that will mate and produce the summer unisexual oak apple galls in my [Previous] photo which contain only female wasps. Those ladies will emerge next spring and produce another crop of bisexual galls like the one in my photo, and the cycle of alternating generations repeats. At least that's the story. But it's curious that I'm finding both spring and summer galls now in autumn. I looked close, and the spring galls in this photo do not have exit holes. I wonder if they can delay emerging for a year or longer e.g. for a drought year? Sorry, but galls are complicated like this. It's part of what makes then so interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2018)

Notes:
44219508464 Here is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. Note that these flowers are male or staminate unlike the female pistillate flowers in my [Previous] photo. If you look close, you can see that the bee is dipping its proboscis into the well at the base of the individual pollen - after nectar rather than pollen. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is. It has a little white badge on its face that is not visible in this photo. I've seen these bees before, see this photo. The genus Ceratina comes to mind, but it might be too hairy for that. Maybe it's genus Lasioglossum (family Halictidae)? There are too many small dark bees! (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2018)

It was warmer today with temps in the upper 80s. Tomorrow is expected to be another warm one, and then it will cool down again back into the 70s. There was a breeze again this morning, though it became calm in the afternoon. Hah - I notice a "slight chance of showers" in the forecast for early next week from Tropical Storm Rosa - that's still many days away, but it's nice to think about!

File.Name: i926b_i926-026.jpg
Notes: 30002695777 Here are two photos of a fairly large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flowering branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) today. Note that these flowers are female or pistillate, unlike the male staminate flowers in my [Next] photo. This shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants. The spider is mostly hairless, which separates it from the more common Mecaphesa spiders I've been showing. This one might a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes) with a ridge between its chelicerae and anterior eyes, though the band of this one is yellow rather than white. Does that matter? The Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia) is similar, and I may have them mixed up. This one also has an oddly deflated abdomen like it's emaciated or just laid eggs. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2018)

File.Name: i926c_i922-026.jpg
Notes: 44939416331 This is the web of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis, Linyphiidae) hanging between branches of a native California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica, Rhamnaceae) shrub. These webs come in layers, see my comments on this photo for some discussion. These bowl-shaped webs are common in our mountains, but they are replaced in Oregon by the dome-shaped webs in this photo. After I got today's photo on my computer screen, I noticed three little Stilt Bugs (Berytidae) crawling on the web. (I'll make a note.) I think of these bugs as herbivores, so I wonder what they're after? Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2018)

File.Name: i926d_i921-001.jpg
Notes: 30002292497 This is a feather caught in a spider web, probably the tangled web of a Cobweb Weaver spider in the family Theridiidae. See this photo for something else caught in the same kind of web. What can I say . . . except Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2018)

File.Name: i927a_i927-007.jpg
Notes:
44910509322 I found this adult Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It was actually hiding under the overhanging leaf when I found it, but it came partly out as I pestered it. I believe it's a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) - named for the tiny size of it's prey. This one is slightly different from the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus) in my [Previous] photo. It's much more common in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2018)

I was a little distracted today - like much of the country, I suspect - but I did get out for my usual walk in the woods. It was quite warm today - in the 90s. There was a breeze in the morning again, but it disappeared in the afternoon when it would have done some good. It is supposed to cool down for the weekend, and there is still an interesting chance of showers for early next week.

File.Name: i927b_i922-033.jpg
Notes: 30021665657 I found this late-stage nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) hiding under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I had to open up my exposure a couple of stops to get the shot. What interesting antennae it has! I can see that this one is different from the adult in my [Next] photo, even though it's still a nymph. I believe this is a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus), see this photo of an adult and this photo of another nymph with the same goofy antennae. It's similar to the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) in my [Next] photo, but different. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2018)

File.Name: i927c_i924-002.jpg
Notes: 30021486857 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is hiding between two flowers of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe that this spider is genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen,compare yesterday's photo of a different spider. I don't think of these spiders as web-spinners, but this one does have a safety line! If you look close, you can see openings at the base of each flower where Carpenter Bees have gotten in for nectar theft - see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2018)

File.Name: i927d_i922-003.jpg
Notes: 44958663731 Here is a wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working brand new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) - showing its rear end. The bee might be a male Anthophora urbana in the family Apidae, see Bernhard Jacobi's comment on this photo of he same bee. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2018)

File.Name: i928a_i928-017.jpg
Notes:
44929540972 It was windy today, so I was looking close to the ground for something to photograph and found this detached insect wing with an iridescent sheen in a dusty spider web. I believe it's the wing of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae,Neuroptera) insect, see this photo. There's always something to photograph in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2018)

It got up into the 80s this morning, but then the temperature dropped back into the mid-70s after noon. It was quite windy today, really too windy for my kind of photos. That's a sign that the weather is changing. We now have a 40% chance of rain on Monday night and "rain likely" on Tuesday! This will be our first rain of the season if it comes through.

File.Name: i928b_i925-025.jpg
Notes: 44977674841 I happened to notice (and photograph!) this really tiny fly of the flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) a few days ago. I believe it's a Frit Fly aka Grass Fly in the family Chloropidae - genus Thaumatomyia in particular. I've briefly seen these flies before, but this is my first photo. I didn't get a good sideways shot of the fly to measure it by, but a better ruler is the flower itself - compare this photo of an already tiny bee on similar flowers! It's a tiny fly that's actually attractive, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2018)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Frit Fly; Grass Fly

File.Name: i928c_i925-023.jpg
Notes: 44258584074 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I'm pretty sure that it's another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. I've already used up my two photo limit for the Fly Day Friday group today, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2018)

File.Name: i928d_i924-007.jpg
Notes: 31104920468 Here is another photo of an interesting fly working new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) a few days ago - see this photo. It seems to have four antennae, which it keeps in constant motion. I believe this one is a Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), maybe Copestylum marginatum or something similar. The "double antennae" are actually ordinary antennae with a thickened arista - that usually hair-like bristle that arises from the base of the antennae of many flies. My other photo may show the antennae better, but this one shows the whole fly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2018)

File.Name: i929a_i929-034.jpg
Notes:
44949136942 This is the nymph of a Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on an unopened seed capsule of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. You can see the same critter on the left in my [Previous] photo if you look close. This green nymph looks quite different from the brown adults I see at home, but it may be the same thing. There's a smaller exuviae (shed skin) right in front of it maybe its own from a previous molt? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 September 2018)

We were in a thick marine-layer cloud at home today, so I went for drive up East Camino Cielo road looking for some sun. I ended up near the FAA radio station on the high place between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro, about 3600 feet - still in the fog! The sun finally did break through after noon, and I got a few photos. It was in the 60s for most of the day, finally getting up to the low 70s. There was the usual "fog wind" that happens at the top of marine layer, though I've seen worse than we had today. The fog today is another sign that the weather is changing. We now have a 60% chance of rain on Tuesday! This will be our first rain of the season if it happens.

associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: i929b_i929-030.jpg
Notes: 30061347007 Here is a stem of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family that's going to seed, with other red flowers in the background. This is the one time when this plant really looks like it belongs in the genus Epilobium! If you look close at the unopened seed capsule to the left, you can spot the tiny Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) nymph that I feature in my [Next] photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 September 2018)

File.Name: i929c_i929-018.jpg
Notes: 44948785752 These are the odd new flowers of a native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. What look like skinny petals are really club-shaped style branches. I don't hear much about this shrub, but the flowers are quite striking when they are in full bloom like this. It's one of those late-season composite flowers in our woods that are are important for keeping the insects alive. I find it so interesting that there's always something flowering in our woods. The late-season composites and others like the California Fuchsia in my [Next] photos overlap the early flowers of Chaparral Currant and Bigberry Manzanita in a way that almost seems like design to keep the pollinators alive. Could this be an adaptation of the ecosystem? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 September 2018)

File.Name: i929d_i929-023.jpg
Notes: 31124451238 This is another Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius aka P. arhizus) "mushroom" coming up in bare ground along the short road to the FAA radio on the high place between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" - the fungus gets its scientific name from the pea-shaped peridioles or spore capsules which are visible in my photo. They very slowly break down to release the spores. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. There are rumors of it being used as a seasoning and a medicine, though I'm not tempted to try it. These fungi are highly variable in shape, compare this recent photo from home. It's not really fleshy, but it is a fungus at an odd time of year. Our real mushroom season won't begin until real rains start - probably not next week. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 September 2018)
ScientificName: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus
associatedTaxa: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus

File.Name: i930a_i930-041.jpg
Notes:
31148048718 I found this broad-winged male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding in plain sight in a boat-like fallen oak leaf that's caught in a spider web. Hah, "if I can't see you, then you can't see me" - but it is peeking at me! Both male and female crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but only the males sing. See this action shot of a male doing its thing. The females have narrow wings and don't sing, compare this photo. The crickets have been pretty quiet with the cooler weather lately, but they are still out there. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2018)

What a difference a day makes! Yesterday was foggy and cool, and today was sunny and warmer - but the warmer weather won't last. Our chance of rain next week is sounding better and better - now we're up to a 70% chance on Tuesday and Tuesday night! I first thought this weather was connected to Hurricane Rosa, but most of what's left of that storm will pass south and east of us. Our rain is apparently coming from a real Pacific storm that will tap into some tropical moisture and might well become a proper storm. Our first storm of the rain-year is always exciting! There's more info in the new post at the California Weather Blog.

File.Name: i930b_i930-036.jpg
Notes: 31147918488 Here is another adult Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods today. I believe it's a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) - named for the tiny size of it's prey, not any resemblance. These are the most common Assassin Bugs in our woods, but they're still scarce enough that I get excited when I find one - at least early in their season like this. You can see part of its powerful proboscis folded under its narrow head. I wonder if the faint aquamarine or turquoise colors mean that it's just emerged from its final molt? (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2018)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry; Leafhopper Assassin Bug

File.Name: i930c_i930-017.jpg
Notes: 44109135185 Here is a handsome little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) lapping up *something* from a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I was under the impression that it's something sticky, but I never did touch it - maybe it's just moisture from the morning dew. The fly was interested and kept coming back to this leaf. One guess for this fly it is would be genus Sphaerophoria, but I really don't know the group very well, and there seem to be many different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2018)

File.Name: i930d_i930-022.jpg
Notes: 30083472157 This is a furry little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. We seem to have bigger and smaller ones of these, and this is one of the smaller ones. Maybe it is genus Villa in the tribe Villini - and maybe even species Villa lateralis which is said to have clear wings unlike most members of the group. It's a cute little fly! (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2018)

File.Name: ia01a_ia01-003.jpg
Notes:
30108304777 Here is a fairly large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) lounging on a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's in a shrub that is flowering up above, and later in the morning the spider did take up its position waiting for prey by the flowers. The spider is mostly hairless, which separates it from the more common Mecaphesa crab spiders in our woods. This one might a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes) with a ridge between its chelicerae and anterior eyes, though the band of this one is yellow rather than white. Does that matter? The Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia) is similar, and I may have them mixed up. I have more photos of the same spider here. Arachtober 1. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2018)

Apparently our storm is still coming, but you sure couldn't tell by looking around today. It was mostly sunny and warm. The storm has been delayed until Wednesday, and I hope it's still going to happen. Oh well, the month of Arachtober has finally arrived! The group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day. It's autumn, and this is the time for spiders to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good. It's their time.

File.Name: ia01b_ia01-004.jpg
Notes: 44995640372 This is another goofy-looking net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. They're small, less than 1/4 inch long - but once you notice one, they kind of stand out. Julie says they look like cartoon insects. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2018)

File.Name: ia01c_ia01-042.jpg
Notes: 43232004400 I found this handsome Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on gravel near our mailbox today. I can't really see its hindwings, but I did notice that it had greenish wings when it took off. I believe it's genus Trimerotropis and maybe the Fontana Grasshopper (Trimerotropis fontana) with those light colored bands on its inner femur. It's a handsome critter, and that's about all I know about it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2018)

File.Name: ia01d_ia01-046.jpg
Notes: 31170097088 I found this narrow-winged female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis, Caprifoliaceae). Both male and female crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, and only the males sing at night. The ladies are silent, but they are a receptive audience. I found this lady just a few feet away from the male in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2018)

File.Name: ia02a_ia02-008.jpg
Notes:
44345771214 Here is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sac in a flowering plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). I've shown this spider before, in this photo and this photo. I'm hoping for spiderlings any day now! Arachtober 2. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2018)

I wasn't really planning on posting all spiders today, but spiders have been on my mind lately, and that's what I have. There were clouds coming in from the south today along with the wind. That's our usual "rain wind" so I hope this is an omen for the predicted rain tomorrow. There's a rumor that we might get an inch of rain up here in the mountains, but confidence isn't high. The first storm of the rain-year is always exciting!

File.Name: ia02b_ia02-007.jpg
Notes: 45017419162 Here is another photo of the fairly large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in a shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) taken today. Compare this photo of the same spider from yesterday. I believe this spider is a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), though the band under its eyes is yellow rather than white. (See Eric Eaton's comment on yesterday's photo.) Here it is waiting for prey by the flowers rather than lounging on a leaf. Note that these flowers are female or pistillate, unlike the male staminate flowers I've been mostly showing e.g. in this photo. This shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants, and the two look quite different. Sure, this photo would do for the Arachtober group, but they have a limit (for now) of one photo per day - HA anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2018)

File.Name: ia02c_i929-012.jpg
Notes: 30128572777 This moth (Lepidoptera) has fallen prey to a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf. Sorry, I can remember what kind of leaf it is, but it hardly matters. The spider is no doubt genus Mecaphesa, see my [Previous] photo for a shot of a different one in better focus. It's remarkable how these little spiders can bring down much larger prey. I do find quite a few of them with missing front legs, a sign that their prey can be too big! Sure, this photo would do for the Arachtober group, but they have a limit (for now) of one photo per day - HA anyway! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 September 2018)

File.Name: ia02d_i925-004.jpg
Notes: 30128477447 Here is another shot of a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) under a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe this spider is genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen, compare my [Previous] photo of a different crab spider. If you look close, you can see openings at the base of the flower where Carpenter Bees have gotten in for nectar theft - see this photo. This photo is quite similar to this photo I posted last week, but I actually got it on the following day. Sure, this photo would do for the Arachtober group, but they have a limit (for now) of one photo per day - HA anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2018)

File.Name: ia03a_ia03-008.jpg
Notes:
45038280232 Here is a Silver Argiope spider aka Silver Garden Spider (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) hanging from its web in a shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) - with raindrops from our storm today. Hah - the rain was picking up when I got this photo, and a moment later I started worrying about my camera and gave up. The spider looks like it's wearing a demon mask on its abdomen! Now why would a spider have a face on its back that looks scary to us humans?? This spider seems uncommonly dark - compare this photo of a much lighter one. You can also see part of the spider's stabilimentum - those thick white zig-zag webs on the diagonals, see my [Previous] photo for the whole thing. The common name of this spider comes from the white/silver part on the spider's top side, but a better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. Arachtober 3. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2018)

We did indeed get some real rain today, about 1/2 inch in our rain gauge. We didn't get this much rain last year until January of the new year! That was unusual, but it's also unusual to get this much rain so early in the rain year. I hope it's an omen for the coming winter!

45037854082 Here are new flowers of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family catching sun in the woods. I'll leave some spoiler space before I give it away. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2018)

File.Name: ia03d_i925-020.jpg
Notes: 44174899345 This is an older egg sac of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that has done its work and is now abandoned. If you look close, you can see the exuviae or cast off molts of the spiderlings near the bottom of the sac. I found this in the same native plant of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae) as in my [Previous] photo - though that one still the spider guarding it. I figure both the strands of webbing and the egg sac are made of spider silk - so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2018)

File.Name: ia04a_ia04-015.jpg
Notes:
45106932791 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This is a different spider than the one in my [Previous] photo (here), but I'm sure it's the same kind. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen. It sure has good camouflage on these late-season flowers, but the flowers are fading, and these spiders will soon be out of luck. Arachtober 4. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2018)

We had more rain showers today bringing our total up to 0.63 inches here on the Pass. This showery rain brought very different amounts to different parts of the county: 1-1/2 inches near Lompoc, 2/10 inch in Carpinteria and 1 whole inch in the city of Santa Barbara which had a downpour this morning. Here are a few things I noticed on my walk today:

* There was not much wind after his storm, but tomorrow might be different.
* There were a few termites in the air, I'll bet there will be more tomorrow.
* The liverworts have greened up!
* Some wilted plants like Hummingbird Sage already look revived.
* Goldback Ferns already look revived.
* The Earthstar fungi are revived!
* The buckwheat in this photo suddenly looks faded.
* There are no mushrooms yet, but I'll be looking for them!

File.Name: ia04b_ia04-013.jpg
Notes: 43292814770 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa with that hairy abdomen. The spider is tiny. I couldn't get a photo of the whole spider, but its abdomen is only about 630 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.5 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It sure has good camouflage on these late-season flowers. This is a nice photo of the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2018)

File.Name: ia04c_ia01-015.jpg
Notes: 45106402701 Here is another fairly large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). This spider was just a few feet away from the yellow Crab Spider in this photo. Even though they are completely different colors, I believe they are both the same kind of spider - a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes) with a ridge between its chelicerae and anterior eyes. I think of these spiders as color-shifters that can change between white and yellow depending on the color of their surroundings, but then why would the yellow spider be on the white flowers in this photo? I tried to get another photo of this spider today, but it's nowhere to be found. I hope it comes back. This is a nice photo of the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2018)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coyote Bush; White-banded Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Misumenoides formosipes
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Misumenoides formosipes

File.Name: ia04d_ia03-005.jpg
Notes: 45057042462 I'm not sure - and there's probably no way to tell from my photo - but I think this may be the male spider that goes with the female Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider in my [Previous] photo. It doesn't have the ornamentation of the lady and it's much smaller, but it definitely has male palps and its web was right next to the lady's larger web - in fact, the webs shared an anchor point. (You can see a few strands of it blurred in the foreground of my photo two shots back.) It was starting to rain pretty good when I got this photo, and I'm surprised it came out as good as it is. I went back to the place today, but the spider wasn't there. I'll try again tomorrow. Yeah, this is a pretty good "butt shot" for the BBBT group, but they have a limit of two photos per week, and I've already posted them - but HBBBT and HA anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2018)

File.Name: ia05a_ia05-015.jpg
Notes:
31252251058 I had an encounter with this gorgeous Jumping Spider (Salticidae) today. I found it on faded flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This jumper is larger than many, but it's still quite small. It's abdomen is brick red. I'm pretty sure this one is genus Phidippus, possibly a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). Look, you can see its blue chelicerae hiding behind its palps. Isn't it cute! Arachtober 5. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2018)

Today was sunny, warm, and calm after the morning clouds burned off. I was expecting serious winds from the north after our recent rain, but it didn't happen. There was a breeze in the late afternoon, but it wasn't too bad. I'll be smiling if we get a few more days like this!
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; California Buckwheat; Johnson Jumper
ScientificName: Salticidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Phidippus; Phidippus johnsoni
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Phidippus; Phidippus johnsoni

File.Name: ia05b_ia04-044.jpg
Notes: 44213762265 We just had the first rain of the new season after our long dry summer - and there's already a new mushroom today?? I'm not sure if this one is new or an old one that has revived, but it sure looks new with that powdery spore mass. I believe this is the Hygroscopic Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus), though it seems larger and more patterned that that species usually is. These fungi are built with an inner puffball or spore sack surrounded by an outer rind. The rind splits to make a star, and the inner sack opens to release spores. This one is just starting. The rays of the outer rind open and close depending on the humidity. The inner puffball is usually ephemeral, but the outer rinds are tough and last for years. I found this one growing in the same place last January, and this one the year before. All have the same interesting textures. Happy Fungi Friday! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2018)

File.Name: ia05c_ia04-021.jpg
Notes: 43312075030 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. There are too many kinds of flies! Sometimes it seems like almost every fly I see is a different kind! Whatever this one is, I believe it's a lady with those wide-spaced compound eyes. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2018)

File.Name: ia05d_i930-034.jpg
Notes: 44213556785 Here is another little Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) working staminate (pollen bearing) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). These shrubs are is dioecious with separate male and female plants, and the two look quite different. I'm not sure exactly what kind of fly this is, but it is handsome. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2018)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious

File.Name: ia06a_ia06-007.jpg
Notes:
43331738230 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. Hah - it's resting on top of a spring gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae) which looks like a little golf tee, see this photo. The spider is probably Araneus bispinosus or Araneus montereyensis, see this photo for more info. It really is tiny - about 510 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's remarkable that the spider looks so much like a miniature of one of our large Halloween spiders, like this one! I had to use my on-camera flash to get this photo as the spider was hidden in the shadow of the leaf with sunlight coming from the left. I rarely use flash, but sometimes it really helps. Arachtober 6. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2018)

We did finally get some wind today, which is to be expected after a storm - even a small storm like we had a couple of days ago. The wind started this morning as a little breeze, and worked up to a pretty good blow by the afternoon. I'm happy that I took my walk in the morning. The wind seems to be dying down now as the sun sets, so maybe we'll be spared tomorrow.

File.Name: ia06b_ia06-013.jpg
Notes: 30207029907 I found this gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. Hah - it's got bumps and spines like a little dinosaur! I believe it's a Spiny Assassin Bug in the genus Sinea, though maybe not Sinea spinipes as I've called it before. It does have spiny front legs. These are tough-looking predators, though they are shy and retreating in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2018)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Spiny Assassin Bug

File.Name: ia06c_ia05-041.jpg
Notes: 31271355028 Here are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Actually, these are the first flowers of any native plant this year. This is an early bloomer that sometimes flowers even before the winter rains start - which is remarkable given our summer-dry Mediterranean climate. Hah, this year they got beat by an early rain that came the day before I found the first flowers. In some years, I've found the flowers as early as the autumnal equinox in September - see this photo from 2013. I'll be on the lookout for other early flowering shrubs like Bigberry Manzanita. It's a new year! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2018)

File.Name: ia06d_ia05-027.jpg
Notes: 31271243588 Here's another photo of the gorgeous Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in my [Previous] photo showing its brick red abdomen. I found it on faded flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I'm pretty sure this one is genus Phidippus, possibly a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2018)

File.Name: ia07a_ia07-017.jpg
Notes:
30230493937 Here is another mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sac amongst the faded flowers in a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). The is a different spider than the ones I've been showing, though it's still waiting and waiting for her eggs to hatch - like this one. I'm waiting too! This photo is focused on its spiny legs. I spotted it up a steep hill near the FAA radio station on the high place between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro, and I only got the photo with tripod fully extended and standing on my tiptoes. Not bad, considering. Arachtober 7. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 October 2018)

The wind did die down this morning, but it started to come back in he afternoon - this time from the south/west rather than the north. Otherwise it was just about an ideal autumn day.

File.Name: ia07b_ia07-009.jpg
Notes: 31294809358 I believe this is a white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly working no-longer-new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The flowers are faded - especially after the small rain we had a few days ago - but the butterfly is still able to find the fresh ones that have pink stamens, just follow its proboscis! These butterflies are mostly brown which distinguishes them from the Funereal Duskywing (E. funeralis) which are mostly black, compare this photo. Sounds simple, but I still get them confused! Butterflies are getting scarce as we move into autumn, but they are still a few out there. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2018)
CommonNames: Mournful Duskywing; California Buckwheat; Funereal Duskywing
