File.Name: ia07c_ia07-034.jpg
Notes: 43353973200 You should probably look at my [Previous] photo before you study this one. I was getting that photo of green Coast Live Oak acorns from the upstairs deck today, but I kept noticing a Yellowjacket (Vespula, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) wasp that was intently doing *something* hidden from view. It actually left and came back several times. The branch was a few feet away, so I couldn't get any closer to the tree, but here are a couple of tight crops that show what the wasp is doing - getting sweet honeydew from a gall! You can see the wasp hunched over the gall on the left and the actual gall on the right. I believe this is the gall of the Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, compare this photo of mine and this photo by Joyce Gross. I've been confused by these in the past and attributed the galls to the work of Drippy Nut - a bacterial infection, see this photo. Both of these conditions do cause an oak honey flow, so I have some excuse. I don't find these galls very often, but maybe the trick to finding them is to follow the wasps! These aren't the only oak galls that produce honeydew, see this photo of a different wasp on a different gall. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2018)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Yellowjacket; Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Drippy Nut
ScientificName: Vespula; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Vespula; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: ia07d_ia07-037.jpg
Notes: 44445515184 I was getting this photo of this nice group of green Coast Live Oak acorns from the upstairs deck today - but I kept noticing that Yellowjacket (Vespula, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) wasp on the separate branch to the left. It was intently doing *something* hidden from view. The acorns were a few feet away, so I couldn't get any closer to the tree, but I kept taking photos to try and figure it out. See my [Next] photo for a couple of tight crops. This is a nice photo of the acorns too! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2018)

File.Name: ia08a_ia08-001.jpg
Notes:
43377489300 Here is a little spider crawling across the window sill in our bathroom this morning. I'm pretty sure it's a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider. It has a proper funnel web in the corner of the window, but its web also extends out across the sill. That's why this photo looks kind of blurry - the spider is under a sheet web. We had another spider in the same place last year that had a striking web - see this photo. This spider's web isn't so impressive this year. Its web comes and goes depending on when we last opened the window, which disturbs it. Usually it's hiding down in the funnel during the day - I got lucky this morning! Arachtober 8. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2018)

Today was another nice autumn day, but there was the same annoying wind from the south for most of the day. I guess this is an "eddy wind" wrapping around Point Conception and the Channel Islands. Fortunately, it was gusty - with quiet moments between the gusts for photos.

File.Name: ia08b_ia08-026.jpg
Notes: 45141614902 This is another gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. It might actually be the same individual I showed in this photo a few days ago - at least it's in the same small tree. I believe it's a Spiny Assassin Bug in the genus Sinea. It does have spiny front legs, though to my mind the bumps on its head and back are too short to be called proper spines. These are tough-looking predators, though they are shy and retreating in my experience. It's interesting that I've been finding these predators in the month of "Arachtober" when I'm also finding so many spiders. Predators like these have the job of picking off all the pollinators before winter comes and kills them anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2018)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Spiny Assassin Bug

File.Name: ia08c_ia08-016.jpg
Notes: 44279609735 This is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding in plain sight on an old flower of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) today. This one is female with narrow wings and a long ovipositor. Both male and female crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, and only the males sing at night. They are still having their nightly music jams, but they are slowing down as the autumn nights get cooler. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2018)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: ia08d_ia08-025.jpg
Notes: 45190093401 Here are handsome multicolored berries of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. There are a pair of large seeds inside each berry that resemble coffee beans and give the shrub it's name - see this photo. The berries start green, then turn yellow, then red, and finally black when they are dead ripe. Birds love the ripe ones, but they usually wait until their time comes. I've never actually tried eating the ripe berries, as this plant is related to native Cascara Sagrada (Rhamnus purshiana) which has a strong laxative effect. That's a good way to spread seeds though! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2018)
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus purshiana
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus purshiana

File.Name: ia09a_ia09-015.jpg
Notes:
45164117022 Here are two shots of a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on faded flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Hah - in the photo of the right the spider is about to balloon away! I believe this one is genus Xysticus or Ozyptila with those cryptic colors. It really is tiny - about 515 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.0 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I was lucky to get these photos in the wind today. Arachtober 9. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2018)

We started out in a marine layer cloud today. The fog started to disperse, but then the fog winds started started to kick in. And it stalled that way until mid-afternoon - clouds blowing over the mountains and winds propelling them. It wasn't a great day for taking photos, but I managed to get a few keepers.

File.Name: ia09b_ia09-010.jpg
Notes: 44492468214 I found this small fly (Diptera) today working no-longer-new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The flowers are faded but there are still a few fresh ones in the flower clusters. It's an interesting fly - maybe a Root-Maggot Fly in the family Anthomyiidae? I'll save another photo for Fly Day Friday. The morning fog was just clearing, and it was still quite cool - this was just about the only insect I saw. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2018)

File.Name: ia09c_ia09-032.jpg
Notes: 44302725485 I found this odd insect exuvia or "pupal husk" under a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Curiously, I've seen these before - see this photo and this photo. I'm pretty sure this is the pupal husk of a Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle (genus Chilocorus, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) - follow the second link above for a photo of the adult. Great name! I found all on them on Scrub Oak. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2018)

File.Name: ia09d_ia08-008.jpg
Notes: 44302601755 Here are beautiful new berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. You can see the distinctive umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf that subtends the berries. The berries look tasty, but they taste quite bitter, even when dead ripe. Even the birds seem to ignore them. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2018)

File.Name: ia10a_ia10-028.jpg
Notes:
45186073792 Here are two shots of the tiny spider in my [Previous] photo (here). I believe it's a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider. It has the right shape to its abdomen, but it's curious that it has no sign of a "trash line" of its prey down the center - so it might be something else. This spider is really tiny - about 640 pixels long in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 2.5 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I've been trying to get decent photos for a few days, but the slightest breeze gets their web to swaying. I got the photo on the left today and the photo on the right a few days ago - different days, different light. Arachtober 10. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2018)

We were in a marine layer cloud all day today that only started to clear up as the sun was going down. I'm glad that we didn't get the annoying "fog winds" that we had yesterday that happen at the top of the cloud layer. Maybe the fog was too thick for that? It was calm but gloomy - a good day to bump up my ISO and try to capture this tiny spider.

File.Name: ia10b_ia05-005.jpg
Notes: 30296547637 This spider web may look large, but the whole thing is about the size of my open hand, and the spider itself hanging in the center is tiny. I have two shots of the same spider in my [Next] photo (here). I'm pretty sure this is a Trashline Orb Weaver (Cyclosa, Araneidae). It has the right shape to its abdomen, but it's curious that it has no sign of a "trash line" of its prey down the center. If you look close, you can see something remarkable about its web - its inner web makes a clockwise spiral, while its outer web makes a counter-clockwise spiral. I've never noticed this before, and I wonder if its universal. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2018)

File.Name: ia10c_ia09-022.jpg
Notes: 45185673152 See the spider? It's hiding in this flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in that opening in the center. It's sticking its legs out like a spider striptease! (See this photo for another.) The spider is another well-camouflaged Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae). The buckwheat flowers are fading, but there are still new flowers among the old. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2018)

File.Name: ia10d_ia08-004.jpg
Notes: 31361418978 This is part of the web of the Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. It lives on our bathroom widow sill, and it has decorated its web with its past exuviae - the shed skins after molting. You can see the funnel opening of the spider's web in the background. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2018)

File.Name: ia11a_ia11-019.jpg
Notes:
30319324537 Here are two shots of a really tiny spider hanging from the spine of an oak leaf. It was doing some aerobatics - hanging on a line and then going back to the point again. This spider is even tinier than the spider I showed yesterday - about 450 pixels across in these 1:1 macro shots, which comes to 1.8 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I only noticed it because it was hanging from its web line. I have no idea what kind of spider it is. Is it an adult or a juvenile? What does it eat? Arachtober 11. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2018)

Today started gloomy like yesterday - we even got almost 1/10 inch of drizzle in the rain gauge! It started to clear up before noon, but then the fog winds started started to kick in. And it stalled that way most of the day - clouds blowing over the mountains and winds propelling them. It wasn't a great day for taking photos, but I managed to get a few keepers.

File.Name: ia11b_ia11-008.jpg
Notes: 43443920200 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a plant of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). This one is female with narrow wings and a long ovipositor. Both male and female crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, so I'm surprised to find this one right out in the open like this. Only the broad-winged males sing at night, see this action shot of a male doing its thing. Yeah, this is a decent "butt shot" for the BBBT group, but they have a limit of two photos per week, and I've already posted them - but HBBBT anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2018)

Notes: 31383733468 This is the hairy red behind of a gorgeous Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I found on faded flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). See this photo for the other end of the spider. I'm pretty sure this one is genus Phidippus, possibly a Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2018)

File.Name: ia11d_ia04-006.jpg
Notes: 43443617810 Hah - this is the rear end of the same fairly large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I show in this photo and this photo. It's posing for me on a flowering shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). I believe this spider is a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), though you can hardly tell from this angle. It is certainly is a good angle to see its rear end so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2018)

File.Name: ia12a_ia12-009.jpg
Notes:
45230614122 I've been showing tiny spiders for the Arachtober group lately (here and here), but here's a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae)! I noticed its empty web in the woods today, and followed one of the web support lines back to the spider - hiding in its retreat in a small oak tree. (The leaf was probably skeletonized by the caterpillar in this photo.) I believe it is genus Araneus, maybe Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It's a proper Halloween spider - the first I've seen this year in the woods at home, and about time as Halloween is getting close! I've been looking for these spiders, and I'll visit this one again. The Arachtober group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. So far I've been successful, but it's getting harder! Arachtober 12. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2018)

Today started sunny for the first time in a few days, and it was almost dead calm. Look at the specs for this photo: 1/10 sec. @ f/16 with no flash! It was warmer today too, into the 70s again after a few days in the 50s. It will get even warmer next week as Santa Ana winds come into play, but those devil winds mostly effect LA and Ventura counties south of us. It was curious that even though it was warmer today, cumulous clouds were building up over the backcountry mountains. There's even a 20% chance of thunderstorms tonight!
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; A. gemmoides

File.Name: ia12b_ia12-019.jpg
Notes: 44367862535 I witnessed this odd wasp behavior today. It's a Yellowjacket-type wasp (Vespula, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) curled up under a leaf of of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's grasping some part of an insect - maybe the thorax of a bee? - turning it around and around. It reminded me of a wasp wrapping its prey. I watched it for a couple of minutes and took my eye off it for a moment to follow a different fly - and when I looked back, the wasp was gone. Most peculiar, I wonder what was going on? This isn't the best photo, but I wanted a record of what I saw. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2018)
ScientificName: Vespula; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Vespula; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ia12c_ia09-013.jpg
Notes: 30340985977 This small fly (Diptera) has been working the working no-longer-new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). The flowers are faded, but there are still a few fresh ones in the flower clusters. Maybe it's Root-Maggot Fly in the family Anthomyiidae? I have another photo of the same fly here. It has a remarkably ugly proboscis. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2018)

File.Name: ia12d_ia08-012.jpg
Notes: 43464973710 Here is a small dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a dead oak leaf in the woods. I believe this is a Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini), at least it's similar to that species. These little flies come in bigger and smaller versions, maybe a gender difference? They are fairly common but not easy to photograph. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2018)

File.Name: ia13a_ia13-028.jpg
Notes:
44579569014 Here is a little Jumping Spider stalking a goofy-looking Planthopper insect on an oak leaf. I was trying to get a close photo of the insect when I noticed the spider creeping up like a cat after its prey. The spider jumped, the hopper hopped . . . and got away. The spider is a female of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus, instantly recognizable with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes - see my previous photo. Note that the spider has a safety line. The would-be prey is a net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera), genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae - see my twice-previous photo. What a treat to witness this little drama. Arachtober 13. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2018)

We were in and out of the clouds today, and also in and out of the breeze which came and went all day long. We didn't get any rain here, but Ventura and Santa Monica southeast of us did get 1/4 inch or so. I'd feel cheated, but they'll also get Santa Ana winds over the next few days that we won't get. That more than makes up for it.
ScientificName: Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus; Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae
associatedTaxa: Thiodina hespera; Colonus hesperus; Fulgoroidea; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Neaethus; Tropiduchidae

File.Name: ia13b_ia13-032.jpg
Notes: 45252678422 This cute little spider is a female of the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus on an oak leaf in the woods. This is the same spider I show stalking a planthopper in my [Next] photo, on the same leaf. (The sun was in and out of the clouds today, so the colors look different.) It's one Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that you can recognize even without seeing its face with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes. I have another photo here. Many jumping spiders look alike, but this one is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2018)

File.Name: ia13c_ia13-023.jpg
Notes: 30362373717 This is another goofy-looking net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Tropiduchidae. They're small, less than 1/4 inch long - but once you notice one, they kind of stand out. This is not the one I show being stalked two photos ahead, but it's the same kind found about 20 feet away. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2018)

File.Name: ia13d_ia12-014.jpg
Notes: 43486459850 Here is another adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a plant of dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This one is male with broad wings, compare this photo of a female with narrow wings taken a couple of days ago. Both male and female crickets work the night shift and try to hide during the day, and only the broad-winged males sing at night - see this action shot of a male doing its thing. I enjoy finding and photographing these crickets. One thing I like about this photo is that you can see how long its antennae really are! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2018)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ia14a_ia14-018.jpg
Notes:
45325918081 Here is another small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on staminate (pollen bearing) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and the two look quite different. The flowers are nearly done for the year, but there are still a few fresh ones like this - and the spider found them. I got another photo of maybe the same spider yesterday on a cloudy morning, see my previous photo. I went back to the same shrub today for this photo. I'm not sure which photo I like best, but I'll stick with today's photo for the Arachtober group. The group challenge is to post spider photos every day during October, and I like to treat it like a scavenger hunt to find new spiders every day of the month. So far I've been successful, but it's getting harder. Arachtober 14. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2018)

It was quite breezy today with winds from the south and east, but I don't believe this is connected with the Santa Ana winds that are expected south of us. Our winds shouldn't be so bad, but we're still under a Red Flag Warning through Tuesday evening.
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious

File.Name: ia14b_ia13-008.jpg
Notes: 43510015480 This is a small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) waiting for prey on staminate (pollen bearing) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and the two look quite different. The flowers are nearly done for the year, but there are still a few fresh ones like this - and the spider found them. I got this photo yesterday on a cloudy morning. I went back to the same shrub today and got my [Next] photo. I'm not sure which photo I like best, but I'll stick with today's photo for the Arachtober group. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2018)

File.Name: ia14c_ia14-009.jpg
Notes: 43509753700 I found this impressive caterpillar in a small tree of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek today. It's fairly large, about the size and shape of my index finger. I guess it's the caterpillar of a Sphinx moth (Sphingidae) with that tail, but I'm not sure what kind. Note that tiny insect near the caterpillar's head - a wasp? I may have another shot of the same kind of caterpillar in this photo from 2016, though that one is a darker shade of green and has some color stripes that this one lacks. It seems the wrong time of year to find this caterpillar with winter approaching, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2018)

File.Name: ia14d_ia14-005.jpg
Notes: 44602187574 Here is a Yellowjacket-type wasp (Vespula, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) deconstructing a moth in the gravel along our chipseal road today. The wasp literally took the moth apart and then flew off with one of the thorax - minus the legs and wings. After a while, it and another one came back for the rest of the moth. Look how the wasp is covered with moth scales. I know this isn't the best photo, but it's the only photo I got that shows the faces of both wasp and moth. This reminds me of the "odd wasp behavior" I recorded a couple of days ago in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2018)

File.Name: ia15a_ia15-040.jpg
Notes:
31476457708 Here is another little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on a fading flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). If you look close, you can see that this spider has the swollen palps of a male. The flowers are fading, but these spiders have an unerring knack for finding the still-fresh flowers in the cluster and parking by them as they wait for prey to come by. They are one kind of spider that I can always find at this time of year. Arachtober 15a. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2018)

It was warm in the sun today, but there's still an autumn chill in the air when you move into the shade. We had normal breeze with no sign of Santa Ana winds. We don't get those here in Santa Barbara because there's no path to the desert like the Santa Clara River in Ventura county just down the coast. We are under a a Red Flag Warning today and tomorrow. Darn, I was hoping we were done with that!

File.Name: ia15b_ia15-026.jpg
Notes: 30411849937 Here is a tiny Syrphid Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) visiting a fuzzy violet flower of (I think) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. It's sticking its remarkably long proboscis into the throat of the flower - though you can't see it in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2018)

File.Name: ia15c_ia15-018.jpg
Notes: 30411622437 This wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) was doing "nectar theft" on this flower of non-native hybrid Waverly Sage (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen, see this photo. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is, but it's bee-sized and quite attractive - maybe genus Halictus or Lasioglossum? (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2018)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia x 'Waverly'; Lamiaceae; Halictus; Lasioglossum
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia x 'Waverly'; Lamiaceae; Halictus; Lasioglossum

File.Name: ia15d_i907-285.jpg
Notes:
45350439621 This is looking at the underbelly of an interesting spider in the Oregon woods during our time up there a month or two ago. These spiders are Triangle Web Spiders (genus Hyptiotes) in the family Uloboridae of the Cribellate Orb Weavers. They make the odd triangular webs that I show in this photo. The spider is probably species Hyptiotes gertschi. Here it is sitting close to its anchor on a leaf of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the Ericaceae plant family, with the webbing extending off to the apex of the triangular web off to the right. The most peculiar thing about these spiders is that they break the web with their bodies - look close and you can see that the spider is holding its web on both ends and not in between! I'm not sure how they move, but they certainly do. You can also see the spider's cribellum near its rear end, see this photo for some discussion. I found quite a few of these spiders in the shrubs near our house. Maybe they are common everywhere, but they are small and inconspicuous and hard to spot. These tiny spiders have become one of my favorites. I've also found them at home but only in winter, see this photo taken this year on New Year's Day. We also have Feather-legged Spiders (Uloborus diversus) at home which look similar but make proper orb webs, see this photo. Arachtober 15b. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 September 2018)

The Arachtober group is now allowing two posts a day. This will allow me to show some older photos that I never got around to posting, starting with more photos from our trip to Oregon.
CommonNames: Triangle Web Spiders; Cribellate Orb Weavers; Wild Huckleberry; Feather-legged Spiders
ScientificName: Hyptiotes; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes gertschi; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; cribellum; Uloborus diversus
associatedTaxa: Hyptiotes; Uloboridae; Hyptiotes gertschi; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae; cribellum; Uloborus diversus

File.Name: ia16a_ia16-033.jpg
Notes:
44651186874 Here is another photo of the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) still guarding her egg sac in plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). I've been checking in on this spider every couple of days waiting for spiderlings, see this photo. She has moved her egg sac around several times, and she's slowly turning from a Green Lynx into a Red Lynx! At this point, I'm trying not to worry about her delivery date . . . but I know that some egg sacs fail. Arachtober 16a. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2018)

Today was another beautiful autumn day with no sign of Santa Ana winds. It was either calm with occasional gusts of wind, or breezy with moment of calm - depending on whether you're a glass half-full or half-empty kind of person. Our Red Flag Warning had expired and the forecast sound benign for the next few days.

File.Name: ia16b_ia16-019.jpg
Notes: 44650849464 I believe this is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, Diptera in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree by the creek, probably genus Thereva, see this BugGuide info page. I've seen these large and handsome flies before, see this photo from last year, also in October. I got photos of another larger specimen in a different tree today that I suspect is a gravid female, but I'll save those photos for Fly Day Friday. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2018)
ScientificName: Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thereva
associatedTaxa: Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thereva

File.Name: ia16c_ia16-005.jpg
Notes: 45374704151 I found this gorgeous little Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a fading leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family this morning. I believe it is genus Scaphytopius and subgenus Cloanthanus. Hah - someone at BugGuide describes a similar insect as a "a peacock among hoppers" which is entirely appropriate. The hopper is tiny - about 1180 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.6 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's tiny, but worth getting close to. I hope it's not some kind of horrible pest! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2018)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Scaphytopius; Cloanthanus
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Scaphytopius; Cloanthanus

File.Name: ia16d_i907-339.jpg
Notes: 44650231544 I believe this is one of the Sheet Web Weaver spiders in the family Linyphiidae that make the dome webs in the Oregon woods that I show in this photo and this photo - either the Filmy Dome Spider (Neriene radiata) or the Sierra Dome Spider (Neriene litigiosa). This one doesn't seem to have a web yet, but maybe it's substituting an arch of foliage for a dome of webbing? I found this one in a shrub of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) near our cabin where I drank my coffee every morning. I found a lot of spiders there during the three weeks we were there in September, no doubt because I was in no hurry and looking close for day after day. And no, I didn't use flash for this photo, that's just a beam of morning light in the dark woods. The Arachtober group is now allowing two posts a day, which gives me a chance to show some older photos that I never got around to posting. Arachtober 16b. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 September 2018)
CommonNames: Sheet Web Weaver; Filmy Dome Spider; Sierra Dome Spider; Wild Huckleberry
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Neriene radiata; Neriene litigiosa; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; Neriene radiata; Neriene litigiosa; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae

File.Name: ia17a_ia17-028.jpg
Notes:
45397023301 I found this small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider in the cactus patch of our garden today, curled up among the tiny (but annoying) glochid spines that these Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cacti have. I believe it is in the genus Araneus - an "angulate orb weaver" with two humps across the front of its abdomen. The spider is small but not tiny - about 1600 pixels across in another 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 6.3 mm, about 1/4 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It's nearby web is a couple of feet across - much larger that the small web I showed two photos back, so it's not the tiny Araneus spider I showed in this photo. I have seen similar small spiders before, see this photo. Maybe it's a juvenile Araneus andrewsi because of its dark colors and bristly legs? Arachtober 17a. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2018)

Today was another beautiful autumn day that was much like yesterday and (probably) much like tomorrow. Well, if we're going to be stuck in a rut, this is a pleasant one.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Opuntia; Araneus; Araneus; Araneus andrewsi
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Opuntia; Araneus; Araneus; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: ia17b_ia17-005.jpg
Notes: 45346102792 Here is a tiny dandelion-like composite seed that's caught in a spider web in the cactus patch of our garden. It was spinning around and around in the very slight breeze this morning, and I took lots of photos to get this keeper. I don't know what kind of seed it is, but it's not a dandelion. I could wish that the web was more obvious in my photo - but I won't quibble! Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2018)

File.Name: ia17c_ia16-039.jpg
Notes: 30456895567 This is a small spider web among old flower/seed heads of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). The seed heads are about an inch across, which give an idea of the size of the web. This might be the web of the tiny Araneus spider that I show in this photo, but there are other tiny Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae) spiders that could be responsible. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2018)

File.Name: ia17d_i907-351.jpg
Notes: 45345519652 Here are two shots of a spider that is carrying a passenger in the Oregon woods! The spider is a small Jumping Spider (Salticidae), and the passenger is that orange girdle in the space between abdomen and cephalothorax. I wouldn't be surprised to find mites on a spider, but this is something else. I believe it's the larva of a mantidfly in the family Mantispidae of the Neuroptera, compare this photo at BugGuide. I've also found something similar at home, see this photo. I have seen an adult mantidfly at home, so I know they're around but not common - see this photo. Any info about this will be appreciated. This is another spider on a shrub of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum, Ericaceae) near our cabin in Oregon where I drank my coffee every morning. Arachtober 17b. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 September 2018)
ScientificName: Salticidae; Mantispidae; Neuroptera; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Mantispidae; Neuroptera; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae

File.Name: ia18a_ia18-027.jpg
Notes:
43600992180 I found this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I believe this one is genus Xysticus or Ozyptila with those cryptic colors, compare this photo of a similar spider taken last week. It really is tiny. The spines on the oak leaf give a hint, but I can also measure it by counting pixels - it's about 550 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figured.) This one is getting ready to balloon away on a silk thread. It was gone a moment later. Arachtober 18a. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2018)

Today was another beautiful autumn day that was much like yesterday and (probably) much like tomorrow. Well, if we're going to be stuck in a rut, this is a pleasant one. Hah - this is the same weather report I had yesterday!
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Xysticus; Ozyptila

File.Name: ia18b_ia16-044.jpg
Notes: 45366407012 This is another small dark Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched for a moment on a dead oak leaf in the woods. I believe this is a Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa, Villini), at least it's similar to that species. Lat week, I posted a similar fly for Fly Day Friday also taken on a dead oak leaf. This is a photo of a different fly taken on a different day for a different group, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2018)

File.Name: ia18c_ia16-013.jpg
Notes: 31542088328 Here is the bristly behind of a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. The combination of reddish eyes and the reddish tip of its tail seem to be pretty good field marks for these flies. I found this one on an oak leaf down by the dry creek. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2018)

File.Name: ia18d_i718-019.jpg
Notes: 44693167624 I found this small Orb Web Weaver (family Araneidae) spider on an orb web in my truck last July! I managed to coax it onto the handle of my walking pole to take it outside, and got this photo before turning it loose in the cactus patch of our garden. I believe it is in the genus Araneus - and possibly the same kind as I show in my previous photo. I doubt if it's the same spider, but it might well be the same kind of spider. Its markings are similar though not exactly the same. Maybe both spiders are juvenile Araneus andrewsi because of their dark colors and bristly legs? I never posted this photo before, and this seems like a good time. Arachtober 18b. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2018)

File.Name: ia19a_ia19-007.jpg
Notes:
45386111162 Here is today's shot of the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) that I found in the woods last week, see this photo. I first noticed an empty web, and followed one of the web support lines back to the spider - hiding in its retreat in a small oak tree. The leaf was probably skeletonized by the caterpillar in this photo. I believe the spider is genus Araneus, maybe Araneus gemma or A. gemmoides. (These species are known to cross.) It's a proper Halloween spider, and still the only one I've found this year in the woods at home. I still haven't seen this one out on its web - that would be a nice treat for Halloween! Arachtober 19a. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2018)

Today's weather isn't always the same as yesterday's. It was warmer today, up into the 80s, and also breezier than it's been, mostly from the south. There were still moment of relative calm to get a few photos. Areas south of us like Los Angeles and Ventura Counties are under a NWS Red Flag Warning tonight.
